Examples are in pre-Columbian cultures where it was believed that living spirits inhabit inanimate objects, and that damaging or destroying the object can release the spirit.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious events > beatifications
Description: Refers to the penultimate stage in the formal process that leads to the canonization of a saint in the Roman Catholic Church. At this stage, the pope grants the title 'Blessed' to a Roman Catholic of exemplary faith and life who is considered worthy of public veneration after his or her death. After this stage, such a person can be locally honored. If there is evidence of two miracles then the canonization can proceed, leading to the person becoming a universally recognized saint.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > communions (Christian ceremonies) > solemn communions
Description: Special Christian eucharistic ceremonies that are accompanied by ritual and prayers outside the usual, often including the renewal of baptismal vows.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > eucharists > Lord's Suppers
Synonyms: Lord's Supper | Domenical Suppers | coena dominica
Description: Generally, the Christian sacrament in which the bread and wine are consecrated. The belief surrounding the nature of the sacrament differs in various denominations: for example, the Baptist view is memorialism, the Reformed view is spiritual presence, the Lutheran view is consubstantiation, and the Roman Catholic view is transubstantiation. Specifically, the term is often used to distinguish Protestant liturgies from the Catholic mass.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions > baptizing
Description: Ceremonially immersing in or applying water to someone as an initiation rite or sacrament of the Christian church.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious events > canonizations
Description: Official placements of deceased persons within the canon of saints.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > abhiṣekas
Description: Hindu and Buddhist prayers, religious consecrations, or initiation rituals. Examples include where an individual is sprinkled with water or other liquid; may symbolize a change in status.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > baptisms
Description: Christian rituals or sacraments that vary by denomination, but always involve the immersion of a person in water, or the application of water by pouring or sprinkling; baptisms betoken the initiation of the baptized into the church. Baptism is symbolic of moral or spiritual purification or regeneration. When the baptized is a baby, it includes the naming of the infant.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious events > excommunications
Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions > blessing (religious function)
Description: Consecrating or sanctifying by a holy rite; also, invoking God's favor upon a person.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > eucharists > masses (eucharists)
Description: Services comprising a complex of prayers and ceremonies commemorating the crucifixion of Jesus Christ in the Roman Catholic rites. The term is derived from the rite’s Latin formula of dismissal, "Ite, missa est" (“Go, it is ended”). The term is also used to refer to the Eucharist in certain Protestant sects.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > bar mitzvahs (religious ceremonies)
Synonyms: bar mitzvah (religious ceremony) | Bar-Mitzvahs | bar mitzvoths | bar mitzvots
Description: Confirmation ceremonies held in a synagogue on the occasion of a Jewish boy having reached the age of thirteen, regarded as the age of religious responsibility for males.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions > ministering
Description: Providing religious guidance.

Hierarchy_Path: Objects Facet > Visual and Verbal Communication (hierarchy name) > Literary and Performing Arts Works (hierarchy name) > dances (performing arts compositions) > Native American dances > ghost dances
Description: Ritual group dances performed by Native Americans in the late 19th century, as part of a religious movement to promote the return of the dead, the restoration of Indian lands, food supplies, and way of life, and generally rehabilitate the traditional Indian culture.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies
Description: Observances or rites related to religious practice.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > bat mitzvahs (religious ceremonies)
Synonyms: bat mitzvah (religious ceremony) | Bat Mitzvah | bath mitvah (religious ceremony) | bas mitzvahs (religious ceremonies) | bath mitzvahs (religious ceremonies)
Description: Confirmation ceremonies held on the occasion of a Jewish girl having reached the age of twelve, regarded as the age of religious majority for females.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions > praying
Description: Generally, the act of making a request in a humble manner. Specifically, the act of addressing a deity or spirit with supplication, adoration, confession, or thanksgiving.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > benedictions (religious ceremonies)
Description: Orally delivered solemn invocations of blessedness pronounced by an ecclesiastical functionary.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions > preaching
Description: The act of delivering a sermon or religious address.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > bhogs
Description: Ceremonies used in Indian religions, particularly by Sikhs. In the Sikh tradition, bhogs are used at the ceremonial end of worship and other important occasions, especially at the end of complete readings of the 'Guru Granth Shahib.' The term, which literally means 'pleasure,' symbolizes the enjoyment of faithful worship and this feeling is often given material form as special food that is distributed.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions > tithing
Description: Paying or exacting as payment a tenth part of income, crops, or other material resources.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > chrismation
Description: Ceremonial anointing with chrism, especially immediately after baptisms in the Orthodox Church.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > circumcisions
Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > communions (Christian ceremonies)
Description: Ceremonies involving the reception of the consecrated bread and wine by Christians, usually in the context of a eucharistic ceremony.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > conversions (activity)
Description: Acts of bringing of a person from lack of belief or another belief over to a specified religious faith, profession, or party.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Functions (hierarchy name) > functions (activities) > <functions by specific context> > religious functions
Description: Functions or performances that are religious in nature.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > confirmations (religious ceremonies)
Description: Rites or sacraments administered as a reaffirmation of a particular religious faith. Examples are the rites administered to baptized persons in various Christian churches, intended to bestow a special grace upon the recipient that confirms or strengthens their Christian faith.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > consecrations
Description: Formal dedications and settings apart by a cleric or other religious dignitary of buildings, plots of land, or objects for ceremonial or religious purposes. For the bestowings of a sacred office upon a person, use "ordinations."

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > deconsecrations
Description: Acts or ceremonies of removing the sacred character of a thing or place.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > eucharists
Description: Christian ceremonies or sacraments involving of the consecration of bread and wine by the minister or priest and its distribution to the worshippers. The term 'eucharist' derives from the Greek 'eucharistia' meaning 'thanksgiving.' It is the central act of worship for most Christian denominations, commemorating the Biblical story of the Last Supper, when Jesus referred to the wine and bread as his blood and body.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > extreme unction
Synonyms: unction, extreme | sacrament of the sick | unction of the sick | anointing of the sick | last rites | last sacrament
Description: Christian sacrament or ritual consisting of the anointment with oil or chrism, along with other activities such as the sprinkling of holy water, reception of confession, blessings, and recitation of prayer. Originally in the Roman church it was administered by a priest to a person in imminent danger of dying. The modern sacrament is usually called the "sacrament of the sick" or other such name, and is available for all who are seriously ill or elderly, not only those who are at the point of death.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > lustrations (ceremonies)
Synonyms: lustration (ceremony) | lustrating | lustral ceremonies | lustral ceremony
Description: Ceremonial performances of a purificatory rite by washing, usually with water; may also be an expiatory sacrifice.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > novenas
Description: Christian private or public devotions, special prayers, or services that are observed on nine successive days, or on the same day for nine successive weeks. The purpose of novenas is to request special graces of God or to express mourning; a novena is not, strictly speaking, a ceremony or liturgical event. The practice derives from Jewish tradition, although Jewish prayer periods were of seven days rather than nine. The tradition of nine days derives from the ancient Romans, who had official religious ceremonies that were nine days in duration.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > ordinations (religious ceremonies)
Description: Actions or processes surrounding the conferring of holy orders, or otherwise appointing or admitting a person to the ministry of a priesthood of the Christian church another another religion.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > purifications (ceremonies)
Description: Ceremonies that make a person or thing ritually clean. An example is the ritual cleansing of a woman after childbirth in accordance with Jewish religious laws.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > ring shouts
Synonyms: ring shout | ring-shouts | shoutings (religious ceremonies) | shouts (ring shouts) | shouts, ring
Description: Religious ceremonies featuring music and dance traditionally performed by African Americans in coastal Georgia and South Carolina, derived from African practices.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > sacraments
Description: Certain solemn ceremonies, rites, or religious acts belonging to the institutions of the Christian church. The number and nature of rites considered sacraments varies by Christian denomination; examples include the even sacraments: baptism, confirmation, the eucharist, penance, last rites, holy orders, and matrimony; other traditions recognize only two sacraments, baptism and the Lord's Supper.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > sacrifices (religious ceremonies)
Synonyms: sacrifice (religious ceremony) | sacrificial rites | sacrificial rite | sacrifice ceremonies | sacrifice ceremony
Description: Offerings of an animal, plant, or human life or of some material possession to a divinity or personage of great religious significance in order to establish, maintain, or restore a right relationship of a human being or community to the sacred order, such as through propitiation or homage.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > Seders
Description: Jewish ritual services and ceremonial dinners held on the first evening of the Passover and repeated on the second evening by Orthodox Jews outside Israel.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > religious ceremonies > tea ceremonies
Description: In Japan, ceremonies for the preparation and consumption of green tea, according to strict rules of ceremony as an expression of Zen Buddhist philosophy.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious events
Description: Events, separate from holidays and ceremonies, that relate to religious life.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > institutions (organizations) > <institutions by activity> > religious institutions > churches (institutions)
Description: Institutions that maintain places for public Christian worship.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > institutions (organizations) > <institutions by activity> > religious institutions > monasteries (institutions)
Description: Institutions comprising a community living under religious vows, particularly a community of monks.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > institutions (organizations) > <institutions by activity> > religious institutions > mosques (institutions)
Description: Institutions that oversee Muslim places of worship.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > institutions (organizations) > <institutions by activity> > religious institutions > synagogues (institutions)
Description: Institutions conducting the regular assembly or congregation of Jews for liturgical services, assembly, and study apart from the service of the temple.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > institutions (organizations) > <institutions by activity> > religious institutions > temples (institutions)
Description: Institutions focused on the worship of a deity or deities.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > institutions (organizations) > <institutions by activity> > religious institutions
Description: Institutions exclusively or primarily serving or sponsored by a particular religious group.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion (discipline)
Description: Study of religious beliefs and traditions in human life and culture from the perspective of the humanistic disciplines.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion (discipline) > anthropology of religion
Description: Study of religion using the methods and theories of anthropology.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > biblical studies
Synonyms: Bible studies | biblical criticism | biblical study | criticism, biblical | studies, Bible | studies, biblical | study, biblical
Description: Critical study of the Bible and biblical writings, including such approaches as textual analysis, historical study, exegesis, hermeneutics, and literary and linguistic criticism.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines
Description: Branches of learning devoted to religion.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion (discipline) > comparative religion
Description: Comparative study of the characteristics of religious systems in different societies that focuses on the origin and evolution of religious beliefs and practices from earlier or less urbanized societies to modern ones, aims to develop a universal evolutionary continuum of religions, and stresses a scientific methodology. For the comparative study of religions that largely disregards evolutionary schemes and features a primarily historical methodology, use "religious history."

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > church history
Synonyms: church-history | ecclesiastical history | history, church | history, ecclesiastical
Description: Study of the history of the Christian Church and its sects and branches.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > philosophy > metaphysics > process philosophy
Description: Refers to the theistic philosophy that views creative becoming as the universal form of reality and emphasizes the relational nature of all being.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion (discipline) > philosophy of religion
Description: The philosophical study of religion, especially concerning the rationality of religious beliefs, and the descriptive analysis and elucidation of religious language, belief, and practice.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > ecclesiology
Description: Study of the doctrines of the church, including the study of ecclesiastical art, antiquities, and the adornment and equipment of churches.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion (discipline) > psychology of religion
Description: Branch of religious study in which religious thought, experience, and behavior are studied using the concepts and methodology of psychology.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > eschatology
Description: The science or teachings concerned with last things, such as the end of history or of the present world, the fate of the soul after death, the final judgment of individuals, and the creation or restoration of a perfect world.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion (discipline) > sociology of religion
Description: Study of religion using the sociological method of approach.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > religious ethics
Synonyms: ethics, religious | ethics, theological | theological ethics
Description: Study of ethics inspired and informed by religious doctrine, faith, and experience, also the study of religious doctrine and commitment with reference to ethics.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > religious history
Synonyms: history, religious | history of religions | history of religion
Description: Refers to the comparative historical study of the nature and structure of the world's religions and their diverse manifestations over time in different societies and cultures. For the comparative study of the characteristics of religious systems in different societies that aims to develop a universal evolutionary continuum of religions and stresses a scientific methodology, see "comparative religion."

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines> > religion-related disciplines > theology
Description: Study of religious doctrines and practices as understood within a particular religious tradition, usually from the viewpoint of committed religious believers.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > humanities > <religion and related disciplines>
Description: The discipline of religion and closely related disciplines.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > divination > geomancy
Description: Divination by means of signs derived from configurations of earth, such as from a handful of earth thrown upon a surface, or from arrangements of dots jotted down on paper at random. It was popular throughout Africa and Europe in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Processes and Techniques (hierarchy name) > <processes and techniques by specific type> > <exploring and investigating techniques> > dowsing
Description: Refers to the technique of detecting underground water, minerals, treasure, archaeological remains, and even dead bodies by feeling the motion of a divining rod. A Y-shaped twig is the traditional divining rod, its two prongs twisting or dipping in the hand of the dowser to indicate the nearby presence of the material sought; alternatively metal rods are held parallel a few inches apart and swing across each other. The nature of the stimuli causing these reactions is unexplained, but most dowsers deny that it is a matter of divination.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > alchemy
Description: A form of chemistry and speculative philosophy practiced in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance and concerned principally with discovering methods for transmuting base metal into gold and with finding a universal solvent and an elixir of life.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > divination > interpretive divination
Description: Method of divination in which the diviner creates a pattern to interpret rather than looking for omens in nature or other existing phenomena or things. Examples of interpretive divination include casting lots and reading tea leaves.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > astrology
Description: The study that assumes and attempts to interpret and foretell the influence of the stars, moon, and planets upon human affairs.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > divination
Description: Art or practice of obtaining secret knowledge or foretelling future events by employing supernatural or magical sources, omens, or oracles; it is based on the belief that revelations are offered to humans in extrarational forms of knowledge.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > divination > tetzahuitl
Description: Signs or signals perceived as forewarning of a future event, usually ominous.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > magic (occult science)
Description: Study and practice of ritual activities intended to control or influence human or natural events through the invocation of external and impersonal mystical forces beyond the ordinary human sphere; typical ritual activities include the manipulation of special objects and the recitation of spells.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences > witchcraft
Description: Study and exercise of supernatural and natural powers for benevolent or malevolent ends, encompassing in varying degrees magic, psychic powers, herbalism, divination, and communication with good or evil spirits.

Hierarchy_Path: Associated Concepts Facet > Associated Concepts (hierarchy name) > <concepts in the arts and humanities> > artistic concepts > forms of expression (artistic concept) > occultism
Description: Includes references to or interest in hidden, mysterious, or supernatural powers, as manifested in all fields of human endeavor or creativity. Excludes occult sciences themselves.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Disciplines (hierarchy name) > disciplines (concept) > occult sciences
Synonyms: occult science | arts, occult | occult arts | occult, the | sciences, occult
Description: Range of studies, theories, and practices involving a belief in and knowledge or use of supernatural forces or beings, the goal of practice being the manipulation or subversion of natural laws to achieve some desired purpose.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > ceremonies > cultural ceremonies > christenings
Description: Ceremonial namings of people, animals, or things, such as ships.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Pacific Islander holidays > Matariki
Description: Period of the Māori New Year and the rising of the Pleiades star cluster (Maramataka).

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Precolumbian holidays > Day of the Dead
Description: Holiday in Mexico, honoring dead loved ones and making peace with the eventuality of death. Also observed to a lesser extent in other areas of Latin America and in the United States. The holiday is derived from the rituals of the pre-Hispanic peoples of Mexico. Led by the goddess Mictecacihuatl, known as “Lady of the Dead,” the celebration lasted a month. After Christianity was established in Mexico, this feast was merged with All Souls' Day and All Saints Day on the Catholic calendar, November 1 and 2. An example of celebration is for families to adorn grave sites or altars with candles, marigolds, and the favorite foods of deceased relatives. Today, the festivities are often characterized by black humor. Toys and food, including breads and candies, are created in the shape of symbols of death such as skulls and skeletons.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > <holidays of ancient religions> > Flooding of the Nile (holiday)
Description: Egyptian holiday with ancient origins, celebrating the natural cycle of the Nile; it is held during two weeks annually starting August 15. In Ancient Egypt, the flooding was associated with the tears of Isis for Osiris, her deceased husband. In the Coptic Church the ritual includes ceremonially throwing a martyr's relic into the river. With the construction of High Dam at Aswan, the annual flooding cycle in Egypt came to an end in 1970.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Hindu holidays > Divali (Hindu holiday)
Synonyms: Deepavali | Dīpāvali | Dīvālī (Hindu holiday) | Diwali (Hindu holiday) | Dipavālī
Description: The Hindu festival of lights, an important festival celebrated throughout India for four or five days in October/November (from the thirteenth day of the dark half of the lunar month Asvina to the second day of the light half of Karttika). The festival, honoring Lakshmi, the goddess of wealth and good fortune, is observed with particular enthusiasm by merchants who begin new account books at this time (the goddess Kali is worshipped in Bengal). Worshippers place small earthenware oil lamps in rows along the parapets of temples and houses; the lamps are also set adrift on rivers and streams. This commemorates the return from exile and coronation of Rama, an incarnation of the god Vishnu. Ornate patterns of lotuses are drawn on the floor near the doors of houses as a way of inviting Lakshmi in. During the festival, people visit one another, exchange gifts, feast, and wear new clothes. The fourth day of the festival marks the beginning of the new year according to the Vikrama calendar.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jain holidays > Diwali (Jain holiday)
Description: Refers to an important Jain festival that ends the ritual year. The lights of the Hindu festival of the same name are reinterpreted as a commemoration of the passing into nirvana of Mahavira, the most recent of the Jain Tirthankaras, or saints. The lighting of the lamps is seen as a material substitute for the light of holy knowledge that was extinguished with Mhavira's death. The festival occurs on the full-moon day of the month of Karttika (October-November).

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Islamic holidays > Id al Adha
Synonyms: 'Id al-Adha | al-'Id al-Kabir | Kurban Bayram | Sacrificial Feast | Major Festival | ʻId al-Aḍḥā
Description: One of the two canonical festivals of Islam. This festival occurs at the end of the period of the Mecca pilgrimage, on the tenth and following three days of the last month of the year, Dhu al-Hijjah. The festival commemorates the willingness of Abraham to sacrifice his son. Abraham's sacrifice is believed by Muslims to have occurred at Mina outside of Mecca. Muhammad started this festival during his second year at Medina when it was impossible for early Muslims to observe the pilgrimage to Mecca. Id al Adha is marked by rejoicing, feasting, and the wearing of new clothes. All who can afford to are supposed to sacrifice a legal animal (sheep, goats, camels, or cattle) and then equally distribute the meat among themselves, friends, neighbors, and the poor.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Buddhist holidays > Wesak
Description: Refers to an important Buddhist festival commemorating the birth, enlightenment, and death of the Buddha. It is particularly important in Theravada Buddhism. This festival is held on the full moon in the lunar month of Vesakha (falling in April or May). Celebrations include special devotional services and meritorious deeds such as offering food or alms to monks. Captive birds are sometimes released in memory of the Buddha's compassion. This day is observed as a public holiday in many countries in Southeast Asia.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > All Saints' Day
Synonyms: Feast of All Saints | All Hallows' Day | Hallowmas
Description: In the Christian church, a day commemorating all the saints of the church, both known and unknown, celebrated on November 1 in the Western churches and on the 1st Sunday after Pentecost in the Eastern churches.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays > Passover
Synonyms: Pasch (Jewish holiday) | Pesach | Festival of Unleavened Bread
Description: A Jewish festival and holiday commemorating the first and most momentous event in Jewish history: the liberation of the Hebrews from bondage in Egypt and the "passing over" of God of the houses of the Isrealites during the tenth plague of Egypt (when the first-born children of the Egyptians were killed). Passover begins on the 15th and ends on the 21st or 22nd day of the month of Nisan (March or April). On these seven (or eight) days, also called the Festival of Unleavened Bread, all leaven is prohibited, and only unleavened bread (matzo) may be eaten. The matzo symbolizes the Hebrews' suffering while in slavery and the haste with which they left Egypt during the Exodus. After the destruction of the Temple in Jerusalem, Passover celebrations became home-based. The first night of Passover is particularly celebratory, with a special family meal called the seder. Symbolic foods commemorating the Hebrews' liberation are eaten at the seder and prayers and traditional recitations are performed. Custom also calls for a place to be set for Elijah, the herald of the messiah. Though Passover is one of great rejoicing, strict dietary laws must be observed, and there are certain restrictions regarding work at the beginning and end of the festival.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > celebrations > festivals (celebrations) > Carnival (pre-Lenten festival) > Mardi Gras
Synonyms: Fasnacht | Fast Eve | Fastnacht | Fat Tuesday | Shrove Tuesday
Description: Refers to the final climactic day of Carnival festivities, occurring on the last day before Lent. In some places, such as New Orleans, the term is applied to the entire Carnival season.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Buddhist holidays
Description: Days of celebration observed by followers of Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > <holidays of ancient religions> > Yule (ancient holiday)
Description: The Germanic and Anglo-Saxon feast of the winter solstice, observed in December in pre-Christian Britain and northern Europe; later Christianized as Christmas.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > All Souls' Day
Synonyms: Feast of All Souls | Commemoration of All the Faithful Departed
Description: In the Christian church, a day for commemoration of all the faithful departed, meaning those baptized Christians who are believed to be in purgatory because they have died with the guilt of only lesser sins on their souls. It is celebrated on November 2. In the early days of Christianity the names of the departed brethren were entered in the diptychs. Later, in the sixth century, it was customary in Benedictine monasteries to hold a commemoration of the deceased members at Whitsuntide. The intention was honored in various ways over the centuries; in 1888 Pope Leo XIII ordered a special Requiem in the Roman Catholic Church; it is celebrated on different days in different Christian sects. Anglicans, Lutherans, and others view All Souls' Day as an extension of the observance of All Saints' Day. In many cultures, the remembrance takes on traditions of pre-Christian ancestor worship.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays > Purim
Description: Jewish holiday commemorating the deliverance of the Jews in the 5th century BCE by Mordecai and Esther from a plot to have them massacred. The plot was the work of Haman, the chief minister of the Persian King Ahasuerus. The story is recorded in the Old Testament book of Esther. Its name derives from the lots cast to determine in what month the killing would take place. It is celebrated on 14 Adar (about March 1). The holiday was well established by the 2nd century CE. The day preceding Purim, Ta'anit Esther (Fast of Esther), is a day of fasting. On Purim the 'Scroll of Esther' is read in the synagogue. A festive and carnival atmosphere includes the customs of dressing up, exchanging gifts, and making donations to the poor. Three-cornered pastries called hamantaschen ("Haman's ears") are baked and Purim plays, which became popular in the 17th century, are performed on this holiday.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Hindu holidays > Ganesha Chaturthi
Synonyms: Ganeṣa Catūrthī | Ganesha-caturthi | Ganesa-caturthi | Ganesh Chaturthi
Description: A Hindu festival celebrating the birth of the elephant-headed god Ganesha. This festival, lasting seven to ten days, culminates on the fourth day of the lunar month of Bhadra (August-September). It is particularly popular in the Maharashtra state in India. Worshippers place instruments of work before Ganesha, god of wisdom and prosperity, in order for them to be blessed. This joyous festival features many statues and images of Ganesha and culminates in a spectacular parade called Ganesha Visarjana. Worshippers are forbidden to look at the moon on this day because the moon laughed at Ganesha when he fell from his mount, the rat.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Islamic holidays > Id al-Fitr
Synonyms: 'Id al-Fitr | al-'Id as-Saghir | Küçük Bayram | Festival of Breaking Fast | Minor Festival | ʻĪd al-Fiṭr
Description: One of the two canonical festivals of Islam. Falling on the first three days of the month of Shawwal, the festival immediately follows the fasting month of Ramadan. The festival is celebrated with the communal saying of the special Id prayer and special alms are given. These three days are a time for visiting friends, presenting gifts, wearing new clothes, feasting, congratulating one another, and visiting the graves of relatives. Official celebratory receptions are also held. This festival is also a public holiday throughout much of the Islamic world.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious seasons > Hanukkah (religious season)
Synonyms: Chanukah | Chanukkah | Hanukah | Hanukka | Festival of Lights | Feast of Lights | Feast of Dedication | Dedication, Feast of | Feast of the Maccabees
Description: Jewish religious season commemorating the rededication of the Second Temple of Jerusalem after its desecration. Marked by the rite of lighting the menorah, symbolic of the Talmud story in which the small supply of nondesecrated oil, enough for one day, miraculously burned in the Temple for eight full days. Gifts are also exchanged and children play holiday games.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > celebrations > festivals (celebrations) > Carnival (pre-Lenten festival)
Description: The festive period of balls, masquerades, parades, and general revelry held in the pre-Lenten season, over periods of time ranging from between Christmas and Shrove Tuesday to the last few days before Lent, varying from one country or city to another. For traveling or seasonal amusement enterprises consisting of sideshows, games of chance, rides, and other similar entertainments, use "carnivals."

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays
Synonyms: Christian holiday | holidays, Christian | Christian holy days | Christian holy day
Description: Days of celebration observed by followers of Christianity.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > feast days
Description: Religious anniversaries that are typically observed with rejoicing or reflection rather than sorrow, in commemoration of a given event or in honor of a saint or other personage.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Ascension Day
Synonyms: Ascension Days | Feast of the Ascension
Description: Refers to a major Christian festival commemorating Christ's ascension into heaven. The festival has been observed 40 days (i.e. the fifth Thursday) after Easter in both the Eastern and Western churches since the 4th century. Ascension Day celebrates the kingship of Christ, Christ's entry into heaven with human nature glorified, and the completion of the work of the salvation of believers in Christ (through the final redemptive act of the Ascension). In the Middle Ages the festival included a procession to remember Christ's journey to the Mount of Olives, the traditional site of the Ascension. Another early practice was to raise a crucifix or a statue of the risen Christ through an opening in the church roof; this was sometimes accompanied by a figure of the devil made to descend at the same time. A distinctive feature of the Western liturgy of Ascension Day is the extinguishing of the Paschal candle after the Gospel has been read. The Paschal candle, which is first lit on Easter, is extinguished as a symbol of Christ's leaving the earth.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays > Rosh Hashanah
Synonyms: Rosh Hanikra | Jewish New Year | New Year, Jewish | Rosh Hashana | Day of Judgment | Day of Remembrance
Description: The Jewish New Year, celebrated on 1 Tishri (in September or October). According to Jewish tradition, this is considered the day on which all humans are judged and it ushers in a 10-day period of self-examination and penitence. During these ten days, all Jews should review his or her relationship with God, the Supreme Judge. During the service for this holiday a shofar is blown as a call for spiritual renewal and repentance. As the holiday is also known as the Day of Remembrance, it is a time for Jews to commemorate the creation of the world (created during the month of Tishri) and to recall their responsibilities as God's chosen people. It is customary to eat sweets as good omens of the sweet year ahead.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Hindu holidays > Holi
Description: Refers to an annual spring festival that is the last festival of the Hindu calendar, occuring on the full-moon day of Phalguna in February or March. The festival is probably ancient in origin and is characterized by its sense of playfulness and reversal of normal codes of behavior. It is only on this day that the typical restrictions of caste, sex, age, and status are set aside. Celebrants throw colored waters and powders on one another and celebrations are known for their ribald language and behavior. Some participants may drink 'bhang,' an intoxicating beverage made with hemp. Bonfires are lit the preceding evening to signify the triumph of good over evil and the end of winter; a straw figure of the witch Holikā may be burnt in the fires. The festival is especially associated with Krishna, the young god known for his frivolity. The Dolayatra (swinging of the god) festival takes place during Holi; images of the gods on decorated platforms are swung to the accompaniment of special songs.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Islamic holidays > Laylat al-Qadr
Description: Islamic holiday generally falling on the 27th of the month of Ramaḍān. Some Muslims spend this night in prayer or retreat.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Hindu holidays
Description: Days of celebration observed by followers of Hinduism.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Hindu holidays > Mahashivaratri
Synonyms: Mahashivratri | Mahāśivarātri | Maha-sivaratri | Great Night of Siva | Great Night of Shiva | Śivarātri | Sivaratri | Shiva Ratri | Shiva-Ratri | Shivaratri | Maha Shivratri
Description: An annual Hindu festival dedicated to the worship of the god Shiva, celebrated on the night before the new moon in February or March. The thirteenth night of the dark half of every lunar month is sacred to Shiva but this night in the month of Magha or Phalguna is when devotions to the deity reach a climax. The festival commemorates the night Shiva is said to have performed the Tandava, the celestial dance of primordial creation, preservation, and destruction. The festival includes fasting, worship every three hours, and the annointing of the linga, a phallic emblem that is the principal symbolic representation of Shiva. The day after the all-night vigil is celebrated with feasting and festival fairs.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Islamic holidays > Mawlid al-Nabī
Description: The Prophet Muhammad's birthday, which traditionally falls on the 12th day of the month of Rabīʻ al-Awwal.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Ash Wednesday
Description: Refers to the first day of Lent, occuring 6 1/2 weeks before Easter. The name comes from the practice of marking the foreheads of clergy and worshippers with ash. Typically the ash comes from burning the palms used on the previous Palm Sunday. It is the only other day besides Good Friday for which fasting is prescribed by the Roman Catholic Church. Ash Wednesday is also observed in the Anglican, Lutheran, and some other Protestant churches. Eastern Orthodox churches do not observe Ash Wednesday because Lent begins on a Monday in their calendar.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays > Shavuot
Synonyms: Feast of Weeks | Pentecost (Jewish holiday) | Shabuot | Shabuoth | Shaḇuot | Shavu'ot | Shavuoth | Hag Shavuot | Ḥag Shaḇuot | Hag Ha-Shavuot | Yom ha-Biqqurim | Yom haBikkurim | Hag Habikurim | Yom ha-Bikkurim | Hag Matan Torah | Ḥag haKatzir | Hag Hakatzir | Hag ha-Qazir | Ḥag ha-Qatsir | Atzeret | Zeman Matan Toratenu | Day of the First Fruits | Day of Firstfruits | Festival of Reaping | Festival of First Fruits | Feast of the Harvest | Season of the Giving of Our Torah | Feast of the Giving of the Law
Description: A Jewish holiday observed on 6 Sivan (May or June) to commemorate God's giving of the Law to Moses on Mount Sinai. Second of the three Jewish pilgrim festivals, it falls fifty days after the first day of Passover and originally marked the end of the barley harvest and the beginning of the wheat harvest. The traditional agricultural aspect of the festival is reflected in the continuing custom of decorating synagogues with flowers, green plants, and fruits. In the Middle Ages, children first attended Hebrew school on this day; in modern times, confirmation often occurs on Shavuot. Customary readings include the Book of Ruth and the Ten Commandments. Dairy products are traditionally consumed on Shavuot because Song of Songs compares the Torah with milk.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Assumption (feast day)
Synonyms: Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary
Description: Christian feast day celebrated on August 15 in the western tradition. The feast honors the corporeal taking up into heaven of the Virgin Mary at her death.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays > Sukkot (Jewish holiday)
Synonyms: Feast of Tabernacles | Feast of the Tabernacles | Succos | Sukkoth (holiday) | Sukos | Tabernacles, Feast of | Succoth | Festival of Booths
Description: The Jewish autumn festival celebrated in September or October (15-21 Tishri) as a festival of thanksgiving. Sukkot is one of three Jewish pilgrim festivals. In biblical times the festival was associated with the agricultural year. Following the tradition established in Leviticus (23: 42), 'booths' or temporary shelters are built in homes, gardens, and synagogues in memory of the huts used by the Israelites after leaving Egypt.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Islamic holidays
Description: Days of celebration observed by followers of Islam.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Candlemas
Synonyms: Candlemases | Presentation of the Lord | Presentation of Christ in the Temple | Purification of the Virgin Mary | Purification of the Blessed Virgin | Feast of the Presentation of Christ in the Temple | The Coming of the Son of God into the Temple | Meeting of the Lord and Simeon | Purification of the Blessed Virgin Mary
Description: Refers to a Christian festival and feast most often observed on February 2 or forty days after Christmas. It commemorates the Virgin Mary's visit to the Temple in Jerusalem forty days after the birth of Jesus (Luke 2, 22-39). In accordance with Jewish law, Mary was purified and presented her son to God as her firstborn. In the Roman Catholic church the festival is currently called the Presentation of the Lord while the Anglican church calls it the Presentation of Christ in the Temple. Hypapante, the name for the festival in the Greek church, means 'meeting' and refers to the meeting of Jesus with Simeon and Anna in the Temple. The earliest reference to the festival comes from Jerusalem in the late 4th century. It quickly spread to other Eastern cities while it spread more slowly in the West. Justinian I decreed that it be observed on February 2 in 542. The festival was instituted in Rome probably before the time of Pope Sergius I (687-701) and the procession seems to have originated there, perhaps even before the feast itself was accepted. In the Western church it was mainly a celebration of the Virgin Mary (until the 1969 calendar reform) while in the Eastern church it was primarily a celebration of Christ. The name Candlemas comes from the tradition, in place by the mid-5th century, of observing the festival with candles. In the West, this procession with lit candles symbolizes Christ as the light of the world and his entry into the Temple.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays > Yom Kippur
Synonyms: Day of Atonement | Atonement, Day of | Shabbat Shabbaton
Description: The most important and solemn holiday in the Jewish liturgical calendar, observed on 10 Tishri (September or October). This day, which concludes the "ten days of repentance" that begin with Rosh Hoshanah, is a day of atonement and reconciliation with God. Jews must abstain from food, drink, and sex and all work must cease. In the Bible, Yom Kippur is referred to as "Shabbat Shabbaton," meaning "Sabbath of Solemn Rest" or "Sabbath of Sabbaths." Extremely Orthodox Jews may wear long white robes called kippelot and may forbid the wearing of leather and the anointing with oil. The evening before and the entire day of Yom Kippur are spent in prayer and meditation. Asking for and obtaining forgiveness from others signifies the forgiveness of God. Yom Kippur services are concluded with closing prayers and the blowing of the shofar. Before the destruction of the Temple in Jerusalem, the high priest performed an elaborate sacrificial ceremony that ended with a goat (the scapegoat) being driven into the wilderness, symbolically carrying the sins of Israel.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jain holidays
Description: Days of celebration observed by followers of Jainism.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Christmas
Synonyms: Christmases | Mass of Christ | Christ's Mass | Yule (Christmas)
Description: Refers to the Christian feast and festival observed on December 25 to celebrate the birth of Jesus Christ. Its observance is first documented in Rome in 336. The practice of celebrating on December 25 began in the 4th century in the Western church as a Christian replacement for the pagan festival held on the winter solstice to celebrate the birth of the unconquered sun. The East originally gave the date of January 6 for the nativity but the date of December 25 was generally accepted by the 5th century; the Armenian Church, however, still celebrates on January 6. Christmas took on the festivity (i.e. decorations and gift-giving) of the Roman Saturnalia and other pagan festivals of that time of year. Christmas has continued to accumulate traditions over the centuries; many of the customs associated with the holiday are of non-Christian origin. Evergreens, for example, are symbols of survival and have been associated with Christmas ever since the European Middle Ages. Christmas is traditionally regarded as a festival of the family and of children. In many countries presents are exchanged in the name of or in the spirit of the holiday's patron, Saint Nicholas, or Santa Claus.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Jewish holidays
Description: Days of celebration observed by followers of Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Corpus Christi
Description: Refers to a Christian feast and festival commemorating the origin of the eucharist and in honor of the presence of the body of Jesus Christ in the eucharist. The Latin term means 'body of Christ.' It is usually celebrated on the Thursday after Trinity Sunday in the Western church. Its origins lie in the Middle Ages when there was increased devotion to the Blessed Sacrament, especially after the Fourth Lateran Council of 1215. In 1246, Robert de Torote, bishop of Liège, first celebrated the feast in his diocese after being persuaded to do so by Blessed Juliana, prioress of Mont Cornillon near Liège (1222-58), who had experienced a vision. The feast did not spread until Jacques Pantaléon, formerly archdeacon of Liège, became Pope Urban IV; a bull of 1264 commanded its observance. Over time it became more accepted and in the 15th century it became, in effect, the main feast of the church. Corpus Christi has been celebrated by carrying the host in procession since the 14th century. The feast's procession became a pageant in which sovereigns, princes, magistrates, and guild members took part. In the 15th century the procession was often followed by performances of miracle plays and mystery plays by members of guilds. The feast was suppressed in Protestant churches after the doctrine of transubstantiation was rejected during the Reformation.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Midsummer’s Eve
Description: European celebration centered upon the summer solstice, which takes place on an evening between June 21 and June 24. It is typically accompanied by gathering flowers and greenery, lighting of fires, and feasting. It was originally related to ancient fertility practices and ceremonies performed to ensure a successful harvest. With the advent of Christianity, St. John the Baptist's Day was set during this same period.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Easter
Synonyms: Easters | Feast of the Resurrection | Eâster | Eâstron | Pasch (Christian holiday)
Description: Refers to the principal festival and feast of the Christian church in which the resurrection of Jesus Christ is celebrated. In Western churches it is observed between March 22 and April 25 depending on the date of the first full moon after the vernal equinox. Orthodox churches use the Julian date for the equinox and so usually celebrate Easter on a different, later date. The name may derive from 'Eostre,' the name of an Anglo-Saxon spring goddess. Some traditions associated with Easter (e.g. Easter eggs, the Easter rabbit) have pagan origins. Easter evolved from the primitive Christian feast known as the Pasch of the 2nd and 3rd centuries; it was the Christian counterpart to the Jewish festival of Passover and it commemorated both Christ's death and Christ's resurrection. The paschal vigil is the main celebration of Easter in liturgical churches and is held on the night before; the paschal candle was introduced in about the 4th century. While the festival of Easter occurs on one particular Sunday, its importance is emphasized by the long preparation of Lent, by the solemn services of Holy Week, and by the following fifty days until Pentecost, a period known as Eastertide or Paschaltide. Easter's importance is reflected in the fact that the entire liturgical year of worship is arranged around it, as is the ecclesiastical calendar of movable feasts. There are many allusions to baptism in Easter services, reflecting the early practice of baptizing catechumens during the vigil.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Pacific Islander holidays
Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Epiphany (feast day)
Description: A festival celebrated on January 6 that is one of the three main and oldest festivals of the Christian Church (the other two being Christmas and Easter). The festival originated in the East where it originally celebrated both the birth and baptism of Jesus Christ. Since the 4th century when December 25 became the date of Christmas, the festival in the East has remained the celebration of the manifestation of Christ's divinity at his baptism. The festival spread to the West in the 4th century where it became more associated with visit of the Magi to the infant Christ and specifically the manifestation of Christ to the Gentiles in the form of the Magi. The festival also commemorates Christ's first miracle at Cana in Galilee. Gifts are exchanged on Epiphany instead of Christmas in some countries. The eve of Epiphany is called Twelfth Night in the West.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Precolumbian holidays
Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Feast of the Circumcision
Description: Feast in the Christian calendar, typically observed on January 1. It celebrates an episode in the New Testament in which Jesus is circumcised according to Jewish tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Feast of the Holy Rosary
Synonyms: Feast of Rosary | Feast of Our Lady of the Rosary
Description: Celebrated on October 7, in commemoration the victory of the combined Christian fleet against the Ottoman fleet in 1571.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Feast of the Immaculate Conception
Description: Christian holiday celebrated on December 8, honoring the status of the Blessed Virgin Mary, who was held to have been born without original sin on her soul.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Feast of the Transfiguration
Synonyms: Transfiguration Festival | Transfiguration, Festival of the | Feast of the Transfiguration of Christ
Description: Feast in the calendar of several Christian denominations, observed on August 6. It celebrates an episode in the New Testament in which Jesus is metamorphosed into a radiant in glory upon a mountain.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Feast of the Visitation
Description: Christian holiday celebrated on celebrated on May 31 (earlier on July 2), recognizing the pregnant Blessed Virgin Mary visiting her cousin, Elizabeth, who was pregnant with John the Baptist.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Forty Hours' Devotion (religious period)
Synonyms: Quarant' Ore | Quarantore | Quarante Hore | Quarantor
Description: Said to have Early Christian origins, today it is an annual Roman Catholic period during which continuous prayer is made for forty hours before the Host, which is displayed in solemn exposition.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Good Friday
Synonyms: Good Fridays | Great Friday | Holy and Great Friday | Holy Friday | Sorrowful Friday | Long Friday
Description: Refers to the Friday before Easter during Holy Week. On this day the crucifixion of Jesus Christ is commemorated. It is a day of penance in liturgical churches. Good Friday and the following day (Holy Saturday) are the only days of the years on which mass is not celebrated in the Roman Catholic Church. The rite includes the veneration of the cross and communion with hosts consecrated on Maundy Thursday. Good Friday is kept as a feast rather than as a fast in some Protestant churches and is considered a special celebration of the Lord's Supper. The Three Hours Service is the main service of Good Friday in many denominations. The day is known as Great Friday in Eastern Orthodox churches. Some denominations come together to celebrate Good Friday as an expression of Christian unity.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Halloween
Description: The evening before All Saints’ Day, observed on October 31. It initiates the season of All Saints, which lasts three days and concludes with All Souls' Day. In much of Europe and most of North America, the observance of Halloween is largely nonreligious and has taken on the customs of pre-Christian autumnal festivals, such as the festival of Samhain among the Celts of ancient Britain and Ireland.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Holy Week
Description: In the Christian calendar, the week immediately preceding Easter Sunday.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Holy Year
Description: Year designated by the Pope, during which special indulgences are granted and ceremonies held; in modern times, usually once every 25 years.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Marian feasts
Synonyms: Marian feast | Mary feasts | Mary's feasts
Description: Feasts in the Christian liturgical calendar devoted to the Virgin Mary, for example, the Annunciation to the Virgin, the Assumption of the Virgin, the Nativity of the Virgin, etc.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Maundy Thursday
Synonyms: Maundy Thursdays | Holy Thursday | Green Thursday | Sheer Thursday | Clean Thursday
Description: During Holy Week, the Thursday before Easter that commemorates Jesus Christ's institution of the eucharist at the Last Supper. Maundy Thursday services typically include the consecration of the holy oils for the coming year and a ceremony in which the feet of 12 men are washed in remembrance of Christ's washing the feet of his disciples. The custom in England is for the British sovereign to distribute alms to twelve deserving impoverished people in a different church every year. The variant name 'Green Thursday' refers to the early practice of giving penitents a green branch after completing their Lenten penance and the variant name 'Sheer Thursday' (also 'Clean Thursday') refers to the ceremonial washing of altars that takes place on this day. The name 'Maundy Thursday' comes from a Latin antiphon sung on this feast day in Roman Catholic churches: "Mandatum novum do vobis" ("a new commandment I give to you"; John 13:34).

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Pentecost (Christian feast day)
Description: In the Christian church, a festival observed on the 7th Sunday after Easter, in commemoration of events described in Acts 2 when the Holy Spirit is reported to have descended upon the disciples during the Jewish festival of Pentecost (Shavuot).

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Saint John the Baptist’s Day
Synonyms: John the Baptist's Day | St. John the Baptist Day | Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day | St. John the Baptist’s Day | St. John’s Day | Nativity of St. John the Baptist | Birth of John the Baptist | Nativity of the Forerunner
Description: Christian holiday celebrating the birth of St. John the Baptist, held on June 24. It may include festivities, lights, and fireworks, reminiscent of the earlier feasts of Midsummers for the summer solstice, which occurs at around the same time (on a day between June 21 and 24).

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Saint Nicholas’ Day
Description: Christian feast day celebrating the 4th-century Nicholas of Myra, a bishop and saint from Roman Asia Minor. In the western church, his feast is celebrated on December 6, coinciding with the first days of the Christmas season, which begins with Advent. In some traditions, gifts are given to children on the eve of the Saint Nicholas' Day.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays > Christian holidays > Trinity Sunday
Description: In the western Christian liturgical calendar, the first Sunday after Pentecost; in eastern Christianity, the Sunday of Pentecost. Instituted to honor the Holy Trinity: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > holidays > religious holidays
Synonyms: religious holiday | holidays, religious | holy days | holydays | holy-days
Description: Feast days or other days that are specified for religious observance.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious seasons > Advent
Description: Christian religious season lasting from the fourth Sunday before Christmas until Christmas, observed by some as a period of prayer and fasting.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious seasons > Lent
Description: In the Western Christian church, a period of penitential preparation for Easter that includes 40 weekdays extending from Ash-Wednesday to Easter-eve, observed as a time of fasting and penitence in imitation of Jesus Christ's fasting in the wilderness. In Eastern churches Lent begins Monday seven weeks before Easter and ends the Friday 9 days before Easter. This "Great Lent" allows that Saturdays and Sundays are relaxed fast days.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious seasons > Ramadan
Synonyms: Ramazan | Ramazani | Ramezan | Ramzan | Ramzaun | Rhamadan | Romadan | Ramaḍān
Description: Religious season in Islam, the ninth month of the Islamic year, marking the time during which the Qur'an was revealed to the Prophet. Muslims are enjoined to fast from dawn to dusk during this month and the observance of the Ramadan fast is one of the five 'pillars,' or basic duties, of Islam.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > religious seasons
Description: Designated periods of the year that have religious origins.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > rituals (events) > ablutions > maundy (ritual ablution)
Description: Ritual washing of the feet, derived from hospitality customs of ancient civilizations in which a host would provide water and often a servant to wash the feet of the visitor. Owing to the general use of sandals in Eastern countries, the washing of the feet was almost everywhere recognized from the earliest times as a duty of courtesy. The principle modern example is the ceremony performed by Christian denominations on Maundy Thursday during Lent, following the example of Jesus in Biblical passages.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > rituals (events) > ablutions
Description: Prescribed ceremonial cleansing of part or all of the body or of objects with the intent of purification or dedication, usually but not exclusively performed with water. Usually associated with a religious ritual.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > entertainment events > performances (creative events) > dances (performance events) > dance rituals
Synonyms: dance ritual | dance rites | ritual dance | ceremonial dance
Description: Dances that have a ceremonial or ritual function. Characteristic are movements that are continuously repeated that may reflect a form or inducement of worship, prayer, visionary insights, or healing.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > rituals (events) > ablutions > wudu
Description: Islamic ablutions required before the five daily prayers, involving washing with water the face and nostrils, the head, the hands and forearms, and the feet and ankles.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > rituals (events) > areitos
Description: A term used by Spanish observers of Taino ceremonial rituals. These were gatherings that might include music, song, dance and pantomime applied to religious liturgies, magical rites, and epic narratives of tribal history, but most particularly refers to a type of call-and-response song-poem accompanied by dance.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > rituals (events) > chochoquiztlahpaloa (greeting tearfully)
Description: To greet one another with tears. A ceremonial activity and greeting ritual, common in postclassic central Mexico.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > cultural events > rituals (events)
Description: Generally, practices or patterns of behavior regularly performed in a set, precise manner, for either personal or public purposes. Specifically, particular activities performed during a designated event, often a ceremony.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > journeys > pilgrimages
Description: Journeys, usually long, undertaken as acts of homage or devotion, often to a shrine, birthplace, or sacred site.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Events (hierarchy name) > events (activities) > meetings > conclaves
Description: Assemblies of cardinals who have come together for the election of a Pope. In its present form, the conclave dates from the end of the 13th century.

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Physical and Mental Activities (hierarchy name) > physical activities > <physical activities by specific context> > <public and interactive activities> > athletics (exercises) > yoga (activity)
Description: The systems of physical and breathing exercises, typically combined with meditation, practiced as an activity or discipline for mental and physical health benefits. For the orthodox system of Indian philosophy, use "Yoga (philosophy)."

Hierarchy_Path: Activities Facet > Processes and Techniques (hierarchy name) > <processes and techniques by specific type> > destruction (process) > ritual destruction (process)
Description: Process of deliberately destroying or damaging structures or objects through a ceremonial process; the purpose may be to banish evil, symbolize renewal, or to reinforce group identity.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > archdeaconries
Description: Districts under ecclesiastical control.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > archdioceses
Description: Administrative divisions of a church, under the authority of an archbishop.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > deaconries
Description: Areas of jurisdiction of a deacon.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > dioceses
Description: Administrative divisions of church government under the authority of a bishop.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > ecclesiastical districts
Description: Districts under the jurisdiction of a church.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > parishes (religious divisions)
Description: Divisions of a larger ecclesiastical administrative area committed to the care and authority of one pastor. Also refers to divisions of local government, such as in England and the state of Louisiana, based on or derived from original ecclesiastical divisions.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > patriarchies
Description: The provinces or jurisdictions of a patriarch.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions > prince-bishoprics
Description: Areas of jurisdiction of a prince-bishop, who is a bishop of noble rank.

Hierarchy_Path: Agents Facet > Organizations (hierarchy name) > organizations (groups) > administrative bodies > administrative entities > <political administrative bodies by degree of independence> > divisions (political administrative bodies) > religious divisions
Description: Administrative sections of larger nations, territories, or other districts as defined for religious purposes.
