Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups
US Religious Groups > Baptist
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > "Old" Missionary Baptists Associations
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Old Missionary Baptist Associations are traditional and conservative sects of the Baptist church. They believe in ways of living that are less technologically dependent than modern culture, and are Calvinistic in their beliefs. They trace their roots back to the formation of United Baptists, which took place during the late 1700s as several of the Regular Baptists and Separate Baptists organizations merged.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ)
Description: A structural family category within the Unclassified tradition.

Primary_Label: (German) Reformed Church in the United States
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > (German) Reformed Church in the United States
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | The Reformed Church in the United States (RCUS) is a communion of Christian churches in the Reformed tradition. It was established in the U.S. in 1725, with John Philip Boehm as the first minister. Approximately 20 years later, in 1747, the first Coetus of the church was held in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. This effort to organize was led by Rev. Michael Schlatter. About 100 years later, in 1863, the various Synods and Classes of the church united to form the General Synod. The church was then reorganized in the 1930s; in 1934, this denomination merged with the Evangelical Synod of North America to form the Evangelical and Reformed Church. The part of the church that did not merge continued on with the same name. In 1957, the Evangelical and Reformed Church merged with the Congregational Christian Churches to form the United Church of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > (Original) Church of God
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The (Original) Church of God is a Holiness Pentecostal church founded in 1917 by Reverend Joseph L. Scott, who led a split with the Church of God (Cleveland, Tennessee). The association prizes the autonomy of local congregations; they historically allowed divorced people to enter membership.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism
Description: A structural family category within the Buddhism tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Abhayagiri Buddhist Monastery
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Abhayagiri Buddhist Monastery is the first monastery in the United States that was founded by followers of Ajahn Chan, a master in Theravada Buddhism. Ajahn Pasanno and Ajahn Amaro were chosen as co-abbots at the monastery's founding. The group follows the central elements of Buddha's Path, which include generosity, virtue, mental cultivation, wisdom, and compassion.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups
Description: A structural family category within the Other tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Adidam
Description: Other | Other Groups | Adidam, previously known as Free Daist Communion, was founded by Franklin Jones. The teachings of Adidam are known as the "way of radical understanding." The goal of these teachings is to show there is only one all-comprehensive reality, conscious bliss. The community of Adidam is focused in three areas that Avatar Adi Da Samraj (Franklin Jones) empowered during his life.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism
Description: A structural family category within the Hinduism tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Advaita Fellowship
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Advaita Fellowship was founded by Wayne Liquorman as a tool for spreading Ramesh S. Balsekar's teachings. One teaching of Advaita is that suffering comes from the mistaken idea that human beings are separate entities. Instead, the human soul (atman) and the universal soul (brahman) are the same. With this understanding, ignorance and suffering are dispelled.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Advent Christian Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | Among the first of the Adventist groups to form after the Great Disappointment of 1844 was the Advent Christian Association, which eventually changed its name to the Advent Christian Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Advent Christian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | The Advent Christian Church, originally named the Advent Christian Association, was organized in 1860 and grew out of the Adventist movement initiated by William Miller in the 1840s. The Church adheres to Sunday (rather than Saturday) worship. Another prominent Adventist group, the Life and Advent Union, merged into it in 1964.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist
Description: A structural family category within the Black Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > African Methodist Episcopal Church
Description: Black Protestant | Methodist | The African Methodist Episcopal Church was founded in 1787 by the Rev. Richard Allen and former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church. The new church that Allen started, Bethel Church, was granted full independence in 1816.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church
Description: Black Protestant | Methodist | The African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church was founded in 1796 by former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal
Description: A structural family category within the Mainline Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > African Orthodox Church
Description: Mainline Protestant | Anglican/Episcopal | The African Orthodox Church was founded in 1921 by Bishop George A. McGuire. Bishop McGuire was consecrated by Abp. Joseph Rene Vilatte of the American Catholic Church. The African Orthodox Church was formed in large part around the conviction that African American people should participate in every level of episcopal polity.

Primary_Label: African Union First Colored Methodist Protestant Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > African Union First Colored Methodist Protestant Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The African Union First Colored Methodist Protestant Church is a predominantly black church formed in 1866 from the merging of part of the Union Church of Africans (then the African Union Church) with the First Colored Methodist Protestant Church. It was one of the two churches that emerged around 1865-6 from what had been the Union Church of Africans. The church is congregationally structured with a quadrennial general conference.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Agape Christian Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Agon-shu
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Agon-shu is a form of Japanese Buddhism founded by Seiyu Kiriyama in 1978. Kiriyam serves as the groups "kancho," or leader. Although it draws from Mahayana, Theravada, and the esoteric Buddhism of Tibet, Agon-Shu believes that it has the full teachings of true and genuine Buddhism. The group's teachings are grounded in the Buddha, the dharma, and the sangha.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism
Description: A structural family category within the Judaism tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Agudas Chassidism Chabad
Description: Judaism | Judaism | Currently the largest of the Hassidic groups worldwide, the Lubavitcher movement was almost completely wiped out during the Holocaust, but has experienced phenomenal growth in the last generation after re-establishing itself in Brooklyn, New York, in the 1950s. Much of this growth took place under the leadership of Menachem Mendel Schneerson, the son of Joseph Isaac Schneerson, who assumed leadership of the group in 1950. His efforts included outreach to liberal and non-practicing Jews, and during his tenure the group founded over 900 centers internationally. Additionally, the group developed educational centers and programs for both humanitarian aid and social services. After Menachem Mendel Schneerson's death in 1994, the group became split over beliefs about whether he was the messiah. The main body of the group celebrates the anniversary of his passing every year, and the group's headquarters are still located in Brooklyn, New York.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam
Description: A structural family category within the Unclassified tradition.

Primary_Label: Ahmadiyya Anjuman Ishaat Islam, Lahore, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Ahmadiyya Anjuman Ishaat Islam, Lahore, Inc.
Description: Unclassified | Islam | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Ahmadiyyas
Description: Islam | Islam | A revival movement begun in what is today Pakistan by Mirza Ghulam Ahmad (c.1825-1908). The movement divided in 1914 among those who came to be see Ahmad as a prophet and those who saw him as a religious leader sent by Allah to renew the community but rejected the designation of him as a prophet (and thus the equal of Muhammad). Much of the Sunni community has moved against the Ahmadiyyas as heretics.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ajapa Yoga Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | Ajapa Yoga Foundation was founded and incorporated after Guru Janardan introduced Ajapa yoga to Westerners in 1974. The foundation believes that humans have lost their true identities and are left in a world of pain, want, and illusion. The foundation believes that true identity can be gained by the practice of ajapa yoga, beginning with meditation and specific breathing techniques.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Alaska Moravian Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Alaska Moravian Church is part of the Unitas Fratrum, that is, the Moravian Church broadly. Missionary work began in Alaska in 1885, but it wasn't until 1988 that Alaska became its own province of the Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox)
Description: A structural family category within the Orthodox tradition.

Primary_Label: Albanian Orthodox Archdiocese of the Orthodox Church in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Albanian Orthodox Archdiocese of the Orthodox Church in America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Albanian Orthodox Archdiocese is part of Orthodox Church in America and one of its three ethnic dioceses (the others being Bulgarian and Romanian). Therefore, the 2020 statistics of its congregations and adherents are included in the statistics of the Orthodox Church in America. Christianity has been present in the territory of modern Albania since apostolic times. Historically, Orthodox Church life in Albania was supervised by the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople. After Albania became an independent state (1920), the Ecumenical Patriarchate issued a Tomos of Autocephaly (1937), thereby making the Orthodox Church in Albania a fully independent national Church. Albanian economic immigrants started to arrive in America during the final quarter of the 19th century, settling mostly in the northeast part of the country, with a high concentration around Boston. In 1908, the first ethnic Albanian in America, Fan Noli, became an Orthodox priest and served the first Orthodox Liturgy in the Albanian language. In 1918, Bishop Alexander (Nemolovsky), the ruling bishop of the North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church, created the Albanian Orthodox Mission in America and appointed Fr. Noli to be its administrator. Paralleling his career in the Orthodox Church, Fan Noli was active in Albanian politics. He was elected a member of the Albanian parliament (1921), and for a short period of time was Albania’s prime minister (1924). In 1923, Fr. Fan Noli became a bishop with the title “Bishop of Korka and Metropolitan of Durres.” He had by now become the head of the national Orthodox Church in Albania. In 1932, Bishop Noli returned to the United States and concentrated his efforts on academic pursuits while leading a loosely organized cluster of twelve Albanian Orthodox parishes, most of them in New England. St. George Cathedral in South Boston, MA, became the center of the religious and cultural life of the Albanian Orthodox community in America. After WWII, Albania fell under the Communist regime, and Albanian parishes in America severed their ties with the Church in Albania. In the early 1950s, the Albanian Orthodox Archdiocese in America was formally established under the leadership of Bishop Noli. In 1971, most Albanian Orthodox parishes in the USA merged with the Orthodox Church in America. However, they retained a degree of autonomy and formed the Albanian Archdiocese of the Orthodox Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Albanian Orthodox Diocese of America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Albanian Orthodox Diocese of America was organized in 1950 by His Grace Mark I. Lipa. The church is in communion with the Ecumenical Patriarchate, and its leadership participates in the Assembly of Canonical Orthodox Bishops of the United States of America. Bishop Ilia Katre leads the Diocese now and has done significant work to build seminary education for affiliates of the association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > All World Gayatri Pariwar
Description: Other | Other Groups | All World Gayatri Pariwar was founded by Pandit Shriram Sharma Acharya. He founded the organization in order to deal with the human condition, which he saw as characterized by ignorance, lack of righteousness and joy, insecurities, and infirmities. He wanted to encourage the divinity in humans and a heavenly atmosphere on Earth. The leaders of the movement today include Rev. Dr. Pranav and Rev. Smt. Shailbala Pandya, the daughter of the founders.

Primary_Label: Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection (Original Allegheny Conference)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection (Original Allegheny Conference)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection was founded in 1968 by H. C. Van Wormer, T. A. Robertson, J. N. Markey, F. E. Mansell, and former members of the Wesleyan Methodist Church who rejected its merger with the Pilgrim Holiness Church to form the Wesleyan Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Alliance of Baptists
Description: Mainline Protestant | Baptist | The Alliance of Baptists is a fellowship of progressive Baptist churches formed in 1987 as the Southern Baptist Alliance. It took the current name in 1992. The Alliance sees itself carrying on a traditional Baptist faith that is non-credal and Bible-based in nature.

Primary_Label: Alpha and Omega Pentecostal Church of God of America, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Alpha and Omega Pentecostal Church of God of America, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Alpha and Omega Pentecostal Church of God of America, Inc., was founded my Reverend Magdalene Mabe Philips, a former member of the United Holy Church of America, in 1945.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Amana Church Society
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Communal | The Amana Church Society (Church of True Inspiration) was founded in Germany in 1714 by Eberhard Ludwig Gruber and John Friedrich Rock. Facing prosecution in Europe, the group developed a Committee of Four to find a home for the group in the United States. After settling in New York during the 1840s, the group moved to Iowa in 1855 after outgrowing their land. They continue today as a church consisting of members from seven Amana communities.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Ambassadors Amish Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The origins of the Ambassadors Amish Mennonite date back to 1993, when the Cedar Springs Amish Mennonite congregation was founded in Leitchfield, Kentucky. This group was formed by several families emigrating from Auburn-Franklin, Kentucky, who were part of Beachy Amish Mennonite churches at the time. The new church, Cedar Springs, planted churches and attracted new families. Over time, leaders within the Cedar Springs network became concerned with the Beachy's lack of denominational structure and declining distinctiveness of historic practices. Aided by their larger numbers, they began the process of independence in the early 2000s and became their own denomination in 2008.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > American Anglican Church
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > American Association of Lutheran Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The American Association of Lutheran Churches was formed by conservative Lutheran ministers and lay people who did not wish to participate in the merger that created the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America in 1988.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > American Baptist Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The American Baptist Association grew out of the "landmark" movement among Southern Baptists in the 1850s. The movement, which emphasized the role of the local church, was led by James R. Graves and J. M. Pendleton. The movement remained unorganized until 1905, when some churches from the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) and churches outside of the SBC formed a landmark denomination, called the General Association. A merger of two landmark associations in 1924 created the American Baptist Association.

Primary_Label: American Baptist Churches in the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > American Baptist Churches in the U.S.A.
Description: Mainline Protestant | Baptist | The American Baptist Churches in the U.S.A. continue the oldest organization of Baptists in the United States. Having existed under various names, the present name was adopted in 1972.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > American Baptist Convention
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The American Baptist Convention was the name given in 1950 to the former Northern Baptist Convention. It retained this title until 1972 when it became known as the American Baptist Churches in the U.S.A.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > American Baptist Mississippi Convention
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | No description available.

Primary_Label: American Buddhist Shim Gum Do Association
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > American Buddhist Shim Gum Do Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The American Buddhist Shim Gum Do Association was founded by Chang Sik Kim, a Korean Zen Master. During a retreat, the forms of shim gum do (mind sword path) were revealed to Kim. Through martial arts training, shim gum focuses on attaining a clear mind, thought, action, and enlightenment. Students participate in bimonthly tests, while making their progress through 33 black belt levels. The organization offers daily, weekly, and monthly classes, with separate classes for children starting at age five.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > American Burma Buddhist Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The American Burma Buddhist Association, established in the United States in 1982, follows the meditative practices of the Venerable Mahasi Sayadaw. In 1995, they began to construct the the Lokachantha Universal Peace Pagoda in New Jersey designed after the Great Shwedagon Pagoda of Burma. After many administrative difficulties, the pagoda was completed in 2016.

Primary_Label: American Carpatho-Russian Orthodox Diocese of the USA
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > American Carpatho-Russian Orthodox Diocese of the USA
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The American Carpatho-Russian Orthodox Diocese (ACROD) was established in 1938, when a group of Eastern Rite Catholic parishes (i.e., Catholics following the Byzantine/Orthodox Rite of worship), under the leadership of Fr. Orestes Chornock, joined the Orthodox Church. Back in Europe, the Byzantine Rite Catholic Churches were originally Orthodox Churches that had agreed to recognize the authority of the Roman Pope in the 17th century, but kept their original form of Orthodox worship instead of the Latin Mass. They were also originally allowed to retain a married priesthood as opposed to the celibate clergy of the Roman Catholic Church. However, the attitude of the Roman Catholic bishops in the United States was that the Byzantine Rite Catholics would just have to “fit in” to the predominant Latin Churches, rather than maintain their own unique traditions. This did not sit well with many parishes and caused them to switch back into the Orthodox Church. ACROD is headquartered in Johnstown, PA. Originally founded by immigrants from the Carpatho-Rusyn region of present-day Slovakia, Poland, and Western Ukraine, ACROD today is comprised of peoples of all ethnic backgrounds, many of whom are former Protestants or Roman Catholics who converted to the Orthodox faith. The Carpatho-Russian people brought with them to the United States a unique form of church music called plainchant (“prostopinije”) which has been traced by scholars to the ancient Znameny Chant from early medieval times. This style is quite different from the Greek chant and the Russian choral singing. The Carpatho-Russians also maintained a tradition of communal “congregational” singing in the church, which is not common in other Orthodox Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical
Description: A structural family category within the Catholic tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > American Catholic Church (Syro-Antiochean)
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The American Catholic Church (Syro-Antiochean) was founded during the 1940s by Ernest Leopold Peterson. He started the association in protest to Bp. Percy Wise Clarkson's push to conform the American Catholic Church to the theology of the Liberal Catholic Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > American Episcopal Church
Description: Unclassified | Anglican/Episcopal | The American Episcopal Church was founded in 1968 by a group of former clergy and members of the Episcopal Church and Anglican Orthodox Church. In 1991, it merged with a portion of the Anglican Catholic Church to become the Anglican Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Liberal
Description: A structural family category within the Other tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Liberal > American Ethical Union
Description: Other | Liberal | By the end of the twentieth century, the stance taken by Reform Judaism in America led to a variety of even more liberal approaches to the tradition. The American Ethical Union grew from the thought of Felix Adler, a reform Jewish rabbi who emphasized the cultural aspects of Judaism over its theological unity. Adler, raised Jewish and trained as a rabbi, was inspired by neo-Kantian idealism to push religion toward “pure” ethics and morality. While maintaining a liberal Jewish base, the Union has steadily moved toward humanism as its dominant perspective and has come to include a significant non-Jewish membership and leadership.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Independent Fundamentalist
Description: A structural family category within the Unclassified tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Independent Fundamentalist > American Evangelical Christian Churches
Description: Unclassified | Independent Fundamentalist | The American Evangelical Christian Churches is an independent, interdoctrinal ecclesiastical body founded in 1944. It has tried to remain open to both Calvinist and Arminian theological trends. The association focuses on being evangelical without raising doctrinal disagreements.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > American Evangelical Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The American Evangelical Lutheran Church changed its name from the Danish Evangelical Lutheran Church in 1953. The latter church was founded in 1872. In 1962, it was part of the merger that formed the Lutheran Church in America.

Primary_Label: American Holy Orthodox Catholic Apostolic Eastern Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > American Holy Orthodox Catholic Apostolic Eastern Church
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The American Holy Orthodox Catholic Apostolic Eastern Church was incorporated in 1933 by Cyril John Clement Sherwood. The autocephalous Eastern Orthodox Church became the Eastern Rite Diocese of the Orthodox Catholic Church in America sometime after the death of Clement I (1969).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > American Lutheran Church (1930-1960)
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The American Lutheran Church (1930-1960) was founded in 1930 as a merger between the Ohio (1818), Buffalo (1845), Texas (1851), and Iowa (1854) synods.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > American Lutheran Church (1960-1987)
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The American Lutheran Church (1960-1987) was founded in 1960 as a merger between three Lutheran bodies: the United Evangelical Lutheran Church, the Evangelical Lutheran Church, and the American Lutheran Church (1930-1960). In 1988, this group merged with the Lutheran Church in America and the Association of Evangelical Lutheran Churches to form the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > American Lutheran Conference
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The American Lutheran Conference was a short-lived ecumenical body founded in 1930, soon after the formation of the American Lutheran Church (1930-1960), built to aid the formation of what became the American Lutheran Church (1960-1987).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > American Meditation Society
Description: Other | Other Groups | American Meditation Society was founded by Purushottan Narhinhran, who also known as Gururaj Ananda Yogi, his spiritual name. The society offers basic meditation courses that introduce various ways to meditate. Individuals send photographs of themselves to Gururaji, who then meditates upon them and determines the sound they make with the universe, creating a personal mantram for the individual.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > American Mohammedian Society
Description: Islam | Islam | The American Mohammedan Society was founded in 1907 by Tartar immigrants primarily from Lithuania. In the early 1930s, they purchased an old church and established a mosque in New York City.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > American Moslem Brotherhood Association
Description: Islam | Islam | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > American Muslim Mission
Description: Islam | Islam | Changes occurred within the original Nation of Islam in the years following Elijah's Muhammad's death (1975). Elijah's brother, John, rejected the changes and formed the American Muslim Mission. John Muhammad taught that Elijah Muhammad was the last messenger of Allah. After the American Muslim Mission disbanded in 1975, it was superceded by the ministry of W. Deen Mohammed. Although Mohammed has passed away (2008), the name is still in use.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed
Description: A structural family category within the Evangelical Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > American Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The American Presbyterian Church was founded in 1977 after the dissolution of the Bible Presbyterian Church's Philadelphia presbytery. Three congregations took this opportunity to reorganize, forming the American Presbyterian Church. The new church retained the conservative stance of the Bible Presbyterian Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness
Description: A structural family category within the Unclassified tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > American Rescue Workers
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The American Rescue Workers is a socially active Holiness church founded in 1884 by Thomas E. Moore and former members of the Salvation Army. It originally took the name Salvation Army (the same name as the group it splintered from), but changed its name to American Salvation Army in 1890. In 1913, it took its present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > American Sevashram Sangha of NA
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > American Sikh Council
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Liberal > American Unitarian Association
Description: Unclassified | Liberal | The American Unitarian Association was founded in 1825 by William Ellery Channing as a missionary association. Channing focused on ethics over doctrine. The American Unitarian Association merged with the Universalist Church in America in 1961 to form the Unitarian Universalist Association of Congregations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > American Yoga Association
Description: Other | Other Groups | The American Yoga Association, previously known as Light of Yoga Society, was founded by Alice Christensen. Members of this society practice hatha-yoga and meditation daily. Members also consume a vegetarian diet, practice ahimsa (nonviolence), avoid alcohol, tobacco, drugs, and are restrained in their use of sex.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > American Zen College
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The American Zen College was founded by Zen Master Gisung Shin, Ph.D., with the purpose of studying and practicing religion and philosophy. Since his arrival in the United States, he has established Zen schools and centers in Virginia, Pennsylvania, New York, and the District of Columbia. The organization is based in Maryland with a 7,000 square-foot Zendo and dharma hall.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Amish Groups, undifferentiated
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Amish; Other Groups
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Amitabha Buddhist Societies of USA
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Amitabha Buddhist Societies of USA was founded by Venerable Master Chin Kung and follows the Pure Land study of Buddhism. Pure Land focuses on the law of cause and effect and teaches The Five Sutras and One Commentary of Amitabha Buddha. The organization offers group meditation sessions, distribution of multi-language materials, and broadcasted lectures.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Amrit Yoga Institute
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Amrit Yoga Institute was founded by Amrit Desai. After founding the institute, Desai continued to develop a unique form of hatha yoga, now known as amrit yoga. This type of yoga looks to include both raja yoga (meditation) with hatha yoga in order to create inner stillness combined with effortless outer action.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Amrita Foundation
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Amrita Foundation is an independent organization based on the teachings of Swami Paramahansa Yogananda. The organization emphasizes the original praecepta lessons (instruction by teacher), or home study courses. Instruction includes kriya yoga, meditation, concentration, and physical exercises.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Amritanandamayi, Devotees of Mata
Description: Other | Other Groups | Mata Amritanandamayi, also known as Amma (or Mother) throughout the world for her love and compassion toward all beings, is an Indian spiritual teacher. Her influence in India grew in the 1970s, and since 1988 she has built a number of temples in which four deities are installed to represent the principle of the "Unity of God." Amma made her first trip to the West in 1987, and the Mata Amritanandamayi Center was established in that year as an outrowth of her worldwide ministry. Humanitarian efforts and caring for the needy are also central parts of her work.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Anabaptist Disciples of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ananda Ashram
Description: Other | Other Groups | Ananda Ashram was founded by Shri Brahamanda Saravasti. There are nine stated goals within the ashram, including: experience one's self as the cosmic center of vibrations, establish unity of all beings, promote global togetherness, promote a natural way of education, self-discipline and relations, promote the teachings of Sanskrit, establish modern educational centers, promote natural, spiritual, and psychological methods of healing, experience automatic and spontaneous psychosynthesis and psychoanalysis, and assist individuals in realizing the godhood that is within them.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ananda Ashrama
Description: Other | Other Groups | Ananda Ashrama was founded by Swami Paramananda as an extension of the Vedanta Centre. The Ashrama is a non-sectarian place of worship for all religions. The organization offers classes and services that teach the universal truth in all religions, which they identify as love and service together with study, prayer, and meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ananda Marga Yoga Society
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Ananda Marga Yoga Society was founded by Prabhat Ranjan Sarkar, who began training missionary monks and nuns to spread his teachings of "self-realization and service to humanity." Sarkar also created the concept of neohumanism, meaning all created beings are the veritable expressions of the Supreme Consciousness.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Ananda/Ananda Church of Self Realization
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Ananda Church of Self-Realization was founded by J. Donald Walters, also known as Swami Kriyananda. Ananda's members practice daily meditation using kriya yoga. The goal of the organization is to provide fellowship and inspiration for those who want to find God. The path to finding God is done by using raja yoga techniques for self-realization.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Anglican Catholic Church
Description: Other Christians | Anglican/Episcopal | The Anglican Catholic Church emerged from the mid-1970s Continuing Church Movement among former Episcopalians who rejected the direction the Episcopal Church was taking. Specifically, they rejected the revision of traditional worship and the ordination of women to the priesthood.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Anglican Church in America
Description: Mainline Protestant | Anglican/Episcopal | The Anglican Church in America was founded in 1991 as a partial merger between the American Episcopal Church and the Anglican Catholic Church. The church is theologically conservative and follows the 1928 Book of Common Prayer in its liturgy and teachings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Anglican Church in North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Anglican/Episcopal | The Anglican Church in North America was founded in 2009 by more traditional Anglicans from North America. It is still connected with the Anglican Communion, but it seeks to provide a more traditional expression of Anglicanism than the Episcopal Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Anglican Mission in the Americas
Description: Unclassified | Anglican/Episcopal | The Anglican Mission in the Americas formed in 2000 as an outgrowth of the conservative Continuing Church movement in the United States. It was initiated by the consecration of Revs. Charles H. Murphy and John H. Rodgers as missionary bishops to the United States, who represented Rwanda and Southeast Asia, respectively. The mission sees itself as an Anglican missionary effort in the United States and not a new church. They are open to a wide range of beliefs and practices within Anglicanism.

Primary_Label: Anglican Orthodox Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Anglican Orthodox Church of North America
Description: Unclassified | Anglican/Episcopal | The Anglican Orthodox Church was founded in 1963 by Bishop James P. Dees, a former Episcopal Church priest.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Anglican Province of America
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: Anglican Province of Christ the King
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Anglican Province of Christ the King
Description: Mainline Protestant | Anglican/Episcopal | The Anglican Province of Christ the King dates back to the conservative movement that produced the 1977 congress in St. Louis, Missouri. The Province was formed as a provisional diocese during that time, along with three other dioceses. Its bishop-elect, Robert S. Morse, was consecrated in 1978. In that same year, the Province opposed a new constitution by the group that would become the Anglican Catholic Church. After years of administrative pressures and internal issues, the Diocese of Christ the King reorganized as the Anglican Province of Christ the King in 1991.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Anoopam Mission
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Anoopam Mission was founded as an independent branch of the Swaminarayan movement by Jashbhai, who was also known as Saheb. Originally a part of the BAPS movement, Saheb broke apart when different views on the place of women in the spiritual order arose. He and his followers were asked to leave and reorganized as Anoopam Mission.

Primary_Label: Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of Toledo, and Dependencies in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of Toledo, and Dependencies in North America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Orthodox Church of Antioch is one of four ancient Orthodox Patriarchates. Headquartered in Damascus, Syria, this Church was historically composed of Orthodox Christian Arabs residing in the present-day Syria, Lebanon, Turkey, and Iraq. The Antiochian Orthodox immigrants began arriving in large numbers to America from what was then the Ottoman Empire in the early 1890s. From 1895 to 1915, these Antiochians were united into a “Syro-Arabian Mission” which was part of the Russian Orthodox Church. They were led by the charismatic Raphael Hawaweeny, the first Orthodox bishop consecrated on American soil, who was later (2000) glorified as Saint Raphael of Brooklyn. After Bishop Raphael’s death (1915) and following the Bolshevik Revolution in Russia (1917), the Antiochian Orthodox in America were divided into rival groups. Two major factions were formed in 1936. One, headquartered in New York, was led by Archbishop Anthony (Bashir), who recognized the supremacy of the Patriarch of the Church of Antioch. The second group was centered in Toledo, OH and it was presided over by Archbishop Samuel (David), who maintained closer relations with the Russian Orthodox Church. It has become known as “Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of Toledo and Dependencies.” This group consisted mainly of clergy and parishioners from the Zahle region of Lebanon, and as such was ethnically and regionally defined. As a result, over time, it shrank in size. In contrast, the Archdiocese of New York, under Archbishop Antony, began to introduce the use of English into the church services and thus expanded its horizons and membership. In 1975, the Toledo group ceased to exist and merged with the New York Archdiocese now under the leadership of Metropolitan Philip (Saliba), the successor of Metropolitan Anthony. Together they formed the present-day Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America.

Primary_Label: Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Orthodox Church of Antioch is one of four ancient Orthodox Patriarchates. Headquartered in Damascus, Syria, this Church was historically composed of Orthodox Christian Arabs residing in the present-day Syria, Lebanon, Turkey, and Iraq. The Antiochian Orthodox immigrants began arriving in large numbers to America from what was then Ottoman Empire in the early 1890s. From 1895 to 1915, these Antiochian Orthodox were united into a “Syro-Arabian Mission” which was part of the Russian Orthodox Church. They were led by the charismatic Raphael Hawaweeny, the first Orthodox bishop consecrated on American soil, who was later (2000) glorified as Saint Raphael of Brooklyn. After Bishop Raphael’s death (1915) and following the Bolshevik Revolution in Russia (1917), the Antiochians in America were divided into rival groups. Two major factions were formed in 1936. One, headquartered in New York, was led by Archbishop Anthony (Bashir), who recognized the supremacy of the Patriarch of the Church of Antioch. The second group was centered in Toledo, OH and it was presided over by Archbishop Samuel (David), who maintained closer relations with the Russian Orthodox Church. It has become known as “Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of Toledo and Dependencies.” The group of Archbishop Samuel consisted mainly of clergy and parishioners from the Zahle region of Lebanon. It became ethnically and regionally defined and, as a result, over time, shrank in size. In contrast, the Archdiocese of New York, under Archbishop Antony, began to introduce the use of English into the church services and thus expanded its horizons and membership. In 1975, the Toledo group ceased to exist and merged with the New York Archdiocese now under the leadership of Metropolitan Philip (Saliba), the successor of Metropolitan Anthony. Together they formed the present-day Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America. One of the most significant events in the history of the Archdiocese took place in 1987, when several thousand members of the Protestant “Evangelical Orthodox Church” joined the Archdiocese. This was the largest mass conversion in the history of American Orthodox Christianity. The membership of the modern-day Antiochian Archdiocese is comprised of three different demographic groups: American-born descendants of the original Arab immigrants, Arabs who emigrated from the Middle East in recent decades, and American converts to Orthodoxy who were formerly Protestant or Roman Catholic. As a result, there is a considerable variation in the church and worship practices. Most congregations worship in English, but many also use a great deal of Arabic for the benefit of recent immigrants. Some clergy adhere to very traditional outward practices, such as wearing black cassocks and long beards. Others prefer a more Western appearance. The Antiochian Archdiocese has produced several important organizations for American Orthodox Christianity including Orthodox Christian Prison Ministry which ministers to hundreds of inmates, regardless of religion. Ancient Faith Radio – originally a grassroots enterprise within the Antiochian Archdiocese - has become the largest Orthodox Christian web-based media outlet and publishing house in the U.S.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Apostolic Catholic Orthodox Church
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Apostolic Catholic Orthodox Church (ACOC) is a small independent jurisdiction founded in 1990 by Presiding Bishop Diana C. Dale. The historical roots of the ACOC include the Catholic Church of the Netherlands in the 7th Century, the Old Catholic community of independent Catholic churches in the 1870s, and the North American Old Roman Catholic Church, founded in 1916.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Apostolic Christian Church (Nazarene)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Other Groups | The Apostolic Christian Church (Nazarene) was founded in the early 1900s by former members of the Apostolic Christian Churches of America. It has its origin in Samuel Froehlich's evangelical revival in Switzerland. Due to the influx of Eastern European immigrants (known in their places of origin as Nazarenes) with different customs and doctrine who disagreed with the pre-existing American association on multiple fronts (the growing of mustaches was no small part of the issue), the newcomers separated, forming the Apostolic Christian Church (Nazarene).

Swiss immigrants brought the church to America.

Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Church of Christ in God was formed in 1941 when five elders within the Church of God (Apostolic) decided to split from the church over concerns with the acting presiding bishop, Eli N. Neal. Three churches left with the elders, who established headquarters in Winston-Salem, North Carolina.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Apostolic Church of Jesus Christ
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Church of Jesus Christ was formed in 1928 in Port Arthur, Texas, as two similar Oneness bodies, the Emmanuel's Church in Jesus Christ and the Apostolic Churches of Jesus Christ, merged.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Apostolic Churches of Jesus Christ
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Churches of Jesus Christ was a Oneness church formed in 1925 in Missouri. In 1928, this group merged with a similar group that had formed around the same time - Emmanuel's Church in Jesus Christ - and became the Apostolic Church of Jesus Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Apostolic Episcopal Church
Description: Unclassified | Anglican/Episcopal | The Apostolic Episcopal Church is an Old Catholic body founded in 1925 by Arthur Wolfort Brooks, formerly a minister in the Episcopal Church. It shares leadership with the Holy Celtic Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Apostolic Faith (Kansas)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Faith (Kansas) movement began in 1898 when Rev. Charles Fox Parham, previously a member of the Methodist Episcopal Church, started a home for divine healing in Topeka, Kansas. After establishing a periodical called Apostolic Faith and, two years later, opening Bethel Bible College, Parham spread the message of Pentecostalism in Kansas, Oklahoma, Missouri, and Texas. However, an ensuing split with Pentecostal leadership led him to resign from his role in the Apostolic Faith movement in 1907. Followers remaining in his ministry consolidated into a fellowship in Baxter Springs, Kansas, which is also the site of the group's Bible college. Theologically, the group is similar to the Assemblies of God.

Primary_Label: Apostolic Faith Mission Church of God
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Apostolic Faith Mission Church of God
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Faith Mission Church of God is a predominantly black Oneness Pentecostal church founded in 1906 by Bishop F. W. Williams. It took its present name in 1915.

Primary_Label: Apostolic Faith Mission of Portland, Oregon
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Apostolic Faith Mission of Portland, Oregon
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Faith Mission of Portland, Oregon, is a Holiness Pentecostal church founded in 1907 by Florence L. Crawford.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Apostolic Lutheran Church of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Apostolic Lutheran Church of America is a Finnish-American church founded in 1872 as the Solomon Korteniemi Lutheran Society. In 1929, it was incorporated as the Finnish Apostolic Lutheran Church in America. It adopted its present name in 1962.

Primary_Label: Apostolic Orthodox Catholic Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Apostolic Orthodox Catholic Church of North America
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Apostolic Orthodox Catholic Church was founded in the 1980s by several independent Orthodox bishops, most of whom had formerly been associated with the Western Orthodox Church in America.

Primary_Label: Apostolic Overcoming Holy Church of God
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Apostolic Overcoming Holy Church of God
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Apostolic Overcoming Holy Church of God, Inc., is a predominantly black Oneness Pentecostal church. It was founded in 1916 (though incorporated in 1920) by William Thomas Phillips, formerly associated with the Faith Mission Church of God. Originally, it was known as the Ethiopian Overcoming Holy Church of God, but adopted its present name in 1941.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism)
Description: A structural family category within the Other tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Apostolic United Brethren
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The origins of the Apostolic United Brethren date back to a split in the United Order Effort, which was the largest of the polygamy-practicing groups in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. In 1951, Joseph White Musser became the group's leader upon the death of John Y. Barlow, and he quickly experienced doubts over his leadership and authority from elected elders in the group. Most of the elders of the group resided in Short Creek, Arizona, though Musser ran the group's publishing company in Salt Lake City, Utah. After Musser had a stoke in 1953, he appointed two new members to the church leadership, but internal resistance only increased. At that point, Musser appointed an entire new set of leaders, consisting only of his supporters. This caused a break in the church, with most members resisting and remaining with the church in Short Creek, Arizona. Musser's Chief Assistant, Rulon C. Allred, became the presiding elder of Musser's following in 1954, though it was not until 1975 that the group formally incorporated as "the Corporation of the Presiding Elder of the Apostolic United Brethren.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Appalachian Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Armenian Apostolic Church (Catholicosate of Cilicia)
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Armenian Apostolic Church is one of the so-called Oriental Orthodox Churches (the others being Coptic, Ethiopian, Eritrean, Malankara-Indian, and Syrian) which, unlike Eastern Orthodox Churches, recognize the validity of theological decisions of only the first three Ecumenical Councils. Armenia adopted Christianity as its state religion and became the world’s first Christian nation in 301 AD. The original center of the worldwide Armenian Church is in Etchmiadzin, an ancient city near Armenia’s capital, Yerevan. However, in 1293, a second center of the Armenian Church emerged in the capital of the Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia, situated in what is today southern Turkey. Today, two independent Catholicosates of the Armenian Church - Etchmiadzin and Cilicia - continue to serve their faithful within these different geographic areas. Large groups of Armenian immigrants began to arrive to America in the 1880s–90s. Their influx peaked in the aftermath of the 1915 Armenian Genocide by the Turkish Ottoman Empire. The first Armenian church in the USA was built in 1891 in Worcester, MA: the Church of Our Saviour (Sourp Prgich). In 1898, the Diocese of the Armenian Church for the New World was established by the Catholicos of Etchmiadzin, Mgrdich Khrimian. A growing controversy over relations with Communist Armenia (which had been incorporated into the Soviet Union in 1920) and the credibility of the historic center of the Armenian Church, the Holy Etchmiadzin, split the American Armenian community and the American Armenian Church into political factions. The final break occurred in 1933 after the assassination of the head of the Armenian Church of America, Archbishop Ghevont Tourian, during a worship service in New York City. This division between the two factions of the Armenian Church in United States continues to the present day. A majority of the Armenian churches in America remain under the authority of the Catholicosate of Holy Etchmiadzin, situated in the Republic of Armenia. A smaller group of parishes left this Catholicosate and, in 1957, joined the Armenian Catholicasate of Cilicia. Since 1930, the center of the Catholicosate of Cilicia has been located in Lebanon with most of its faithful living in the Middle East. Various political and social changes in Middle Eastern countries contributed to the growth of the American Armenian parishes affiliated with the Catholicosate of Cilicia. In the 1950s, a wave of immigration was caused by the change of the political regime in Egypt. In the 1970s and 80s, turmoil in Lebanon brought many Lebanese Armenians to America. The new immigrants helped to revive many church traditions that had been lost or diluted. Certain pre-Christian popular traditions that had been maintained in the Middle East, such as water games on the Feast of the Transfiguration and bonfires on the Presentation of the Lord to the Temple, were revived in American Armenian parishes. The best examples of Armenian Churches in America built in a traditional style include St. Garabed Church in Hollywood, CA; St. Gregory Church, San Francisco, CA; St. Sarkis Church, Douglaston, NY; and St. Sarkis Church, Dearborn, MI.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Armenian Apostolic Church (Catholicosate of Etchmiadzin)
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Armenian Apostolic Church is one of the so-called Oriental Orthodox Churches (the others being Coptic, Ethiopian, Eritrean, Malankara-Indian, and Syrian) which, unlike Eastern Orthodox Churches, recognize the validity of theological decisions of only the first three Ecumenical Councils. Armenia adopted Christianity as its state religion and became the world’s first Christian nation in 301 AD. The Armenian Church also sponsored the development of the unique Armenian alphabet. The center of the worldwide Armenian Church is in Etchmiadzin, an ancient city near Armenia’s capital, Yerevan. Large groups of Armenian immigrants began to arrive to America in the 1880s–90s. Their influx peaked in the aftermath of the 1915 Armenian Genocide by the Turkish Ottoman Empire. The first Armenian church in the USA was built in 1891 in Worcester, MA: the Church of Our Saviour (Sourp Prgich). In 1898, the Diocese of the Armenian Church for the New World was established by the Catholicos of Etchmiadzin, Mgrdich Khrimian. A growing controversy over relations with Communist Armenia (which had been incorporated into the Soviet Union in 1920) and the credibility of the historic center of the Armenian Church, the Holy Etchmiadzin, split the American Armenian community and the American Armenian Church into political factions. The final break occurred in 1933 after the assassination of the head of the Armenian Church of America, Archbishop Ghevont Tourian, during a worship service in New York City. This division between the two factions of the Armenian Church in United States continues to the present day. A majority of the Armenian churches in America remain under the authority of the Catholicosate of Holy Etchmiadzin, situated in the Republic of Armenia. A smaller group of parishes left this Catholicosate to join the Armenian Catholicasate of Cilicia, with headquarters in Lebanon. In 1968, the group under Catholicosate of Etchmiadzin built the St. Vartan Armenian Cathedral in New York. Designed in classic Armenian style, the cathedral became a hub of social and civic activity in lower Manhattan. The Armenian Church was vocal in America’s political culture, advocating for the official recognition of the 1915 Armenian Genocide by the US government. The late 1980s and 1990s brought a new wave of Armenian Orthodox immigrants from the former Soviet Union, a mixed result of the expulsion of ethnic Armenians from Azerbaijan, the earthquake in Armenia in 1988, and the fall of the Soviet Union in 1991. Despite the fact that the majority of Armenian families have lived in America for many generations, the sense of a heritage in which religion and ethnic identity are deeply intertwined is surprisingly robust. The Divine Liturgy (the main worship service) is celebrated in the classical Armenian language and is richly melodic, reflecting strong attachments to Armenian culture. Non-Armenian converts are rare in Armenian parishes in America, except in the case of spouses from mixed families.

Primary_Label: Armenian Evangelical Union of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Armenian Evangelical Union of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Armenian Evangelical Union of North America (AEUNA) was formed in 1960 to provide an ecclesiastical association for Armenian conservative Protestant churches in the United States (as well as Canada, Brazil, and Uruguay) of Armenian heritage. Congregations join the AEUNA both as independent churches and as parts of separate denominations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Aro Gar
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Aro Gar is a Western representative of the Tibetan Aro gTer Nyingma lineage. Aro gTer is a small family lineage that was founded by a female visionary in 1909 and is a nonliturgical, nonmonistic tradition that specializes in teaching the practice of Dzogchen. It also states the importance of everyday life as practice.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Arunachala Ashrama
Description: Other | Other Groups | Arunachala Ashrama was founded by Arunachala Bhakta Bhagawat, who was inspired by the life and teachings of Sri Ramana Masharshi. The teachings of Ramana Masharshi were said to be imparted simply by the silence of his presence, which could confer the peace of Self-realization. His oral teachings included the paths of Self-inquiry and Self-surrender.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Asian Classics Institute
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Asian Classics Institute is a Gelugpa Tibetan center that is the focus of a movement that has grown around Geshe Michael Roach. The group follows Tibetan Buddhism, and Roach began teaching classes in New York with the institute emerging as a vehicle of his teaching activity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Assemblies of God International Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Assemblies of God International Fellowship (now known as E4 Ministry Network as of 2019, though retaining its legal name) was founded as the Independent Assemblies of God in 1935 through the merger of three associations of Assemblies of God. Between 1947 and 1948, a division emerged within the church over the Latter Rain movement, which led to the Independent Assemblies of God International, now Assemblies of God International Fellowship.

Primary_Label: Assemblies of God, General Council of the
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Assemblies of God, General Council of the
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The General Council of the Assemblies of God was established in 1914 at a gathering of Pentecostal ministers in Hot Springs, Arkansas.

Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Assemblies of Yahweh (Bethel, PA)
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | In the 1960s, Jacob O. Meyer, a member of the Church of the Brethren, was influenced by the Sacred Name movement and began an independent broadcast ministry, the seed of what became possibly the largest of the Sacred Name denomination, which became known as the Assemblies of Yahweh, based in Bethel, Pennsylvania.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Assembly of Yahweh
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | In the 1920s, some Adventists became convinced that calling the Creator and Savior by their revealed Hebrew names (Elohim, Yahweh, and Yeshohua, rather than God, Lord, and Jesus) was important. Thus was the Sacred Name movement launched. The Assembly of Yahweh in Eaton Rapids, Michigan, an independent congregation in the Church of God (Seventh-day) tradition was the first outpost of the movement and began to issue its first periodical, The Truth. It remains an important center of the movement.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Associate Presbyterian Church
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Associate Presbyterian Church formed in 1753 after a congregation of Seceders (one branch of the Scottish secession movement) in Pennsylvania issued a plea for a minister. Two ministers, Alexander Gellatly and Andrew Arnot, arrived and organized the Associate Presbyterian Church. In 1782, this group merged with the Reformed Presbyterian Church to form the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church. Some members of both groups declined to enter the merger and continued under their previous names.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church grew out of the Seceder Movement that broke with the Church of Scotland in the 1740s. It formed in 1782 as the merger between the Associate Presbyterian Church and the Reformed Presbyterian Church. In 1822, the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church split into northern and southern branches. The southern branch continues today as the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church (General Synod). The church is a member of the International Conference of Reformed Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church (General Synod)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church (General Synod) grew out of the Seceder Movement that broke with the Church of Scotland in the 1740s. It formed in 1782 as the merger between the Associate Presbyterian Church and the Reformed Presbyterian Church. In 1822, the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church split into northern and southern branches. The southern branch continues today as the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church (General Synod). The church is a member of the International Conference of Reformed Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Associated Churches of Christ (Holiness)
Description: Black Protestant | Holiness | The Associated Churches of Christ (Holiness) began in 1915 as a west coast branch of the Church of Christ (Holiness) U.S.A, under the leadership of Bishop William Washington. The new church existed cooperatively with the parent church until 1947, when administrative problems prompted the Associated Churches of Christ (Holiness) to withdraw from the Church of Christ (Holiness) U.S.A. Their doctrine and polity, however, remained the same as the parent group.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Association of Evangelical Lutheran Churches
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Association of Evangelical Lutheran Churches, the newest and the smallest body to enter into the 1988 merger to form the ELCA, was formed in 1976 by ministers and churches that withdrew from the Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Association of Free Lutheran Congregations
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Association of Free Lutheran Congregations was founded in 1962 by Rev. John P. Strand and others who rejected the merger of the Lutheran Free Church into the American Lutheran Church.

Primary_Label: Association of Fundamental Ministers and Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Association of Fundamental Ministers and Churches
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Association of Fundamental Ministers and Churches was founded in 1931 by former members of the Church of God (Anderson, Indiana). These included Rev. Fred Bruffett, who had been disfellowshiped from the church because of fellowshipping with other churches, and his wife, Hallie Bruffett. Additional founding members included Rev. Paul Bennett, Rev. George Fisher, and six other members from the Church of God. Doctrinally, they are similar to the Church of God, but allow for doctrinal disagreement, and they believe that the experience of new birth is necessary for fellowhip.

Primary_Label: Association of Independent Evangelical Lutheran Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Association of Independent Evangelical Lutheran Churches
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Association of Independent Evangelical Lutheran churches was formed in 1988 and incorporated in New York in 2001. It is a small, conservative Lutheran church that affirms the authority of the Bible. It maintains dioceses for Haiti, South America, and South Africa, and is in communion with the Augustana Catholic Church, the Lutheran Orthodox Church, and the Lutheran Evangelical Protestant Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Association of Messianic Congregations
Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | The Association of Messianic Congregations held its first meeting in 2003, uniting like-minded messianic-Jewish congregations in a desire to teach scripture and proclaim Yeshua of Nazareth as messiah. By 2018, after some internal conflict, the largely volunteer-run organization had become an "Identity Affiliation" with less administrative structure and assistance but still bound by its statement of faith.

Primary_Label: Association of Pentecostal Churches in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Association of Pentecostal Churches in America
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Association of Pentecostal Churches in America was formed in 1896 in New York, shortly afterward uniting with the Central Evangelical Holiness Assocation. In 1907, this group merged with the Church of the Nazarene to form the Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene.

Primary_Label: Association of Reformed Baptist Churches of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Association of Reformed Baptist Churches of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Association of Reformed Baptist Churches of America (ARBCA) was formed in 1997 by 24 different churches, all of which ascribed to the Baptist Association of London's 1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith. The Association primarily exists to provide resources and communion to individual congregations, and shortly after its creation the association worked to found the Institute of Reformed Baptist Studies at Westminster Seminary California. The Reformed Baptist Mission Service merged with the ARBCA in 2000, forming its missions arm.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Association of Vineyard Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Association of Vineyard Churches is a conservative evangelical fellowship founded in 1986 by evangelical teacher John Wimber (d. 1997), formerly associated with Calvary Chapel.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Association Zen Internationale (AZI)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Association Zen International was founded in 1970 by Taisen Deshimaru Roshi in France, and was brought to the United States in 1983 by Robert Livingston Roshi after Deshimaru Roshi's death. Today, the group works to establish firm roots of Soto Zen Buddhism in the West.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Atlanta Soto Zen Center (ASZC)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Atlanta Soto Zen Center (ASCZ) was founded under the leadership of Abbot Zenkai Taiun Michael Elliston. The Center provides a group to sit with, a place to sit together with a full zazen schedule, lending library, and experienced teachers. The group also operates a prison outreach and is a member of Soto Zen Buddhist Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Aurobindo Centers
Description: Other | Other Groups | Aurobindo Centers exist mostly independently in the United States, following the teachings of Sri Aurobindo Ghose (1872-1950). Aurobindo was a literary figure and political activist in India. While in jail on sedition charges, he focused his attention on the Hindu scriptures and yoga, soon afterward having a vision of Krishna (the Hindu deity) that prompted him to dedicate his life to spiritual practice. Aurobindo taught "integral yoga," which is based in part on Vedanta and tantra, and works primarily by descent of the shakti into the mind. The Sri Aurobindo Society operates in India, but has an international arm to serve centers outside the country. The are a number of centers in the U.S., with the earliest one being founded in 1951.

Primary_Label: Autonomous Orthodox Metropolia of North and South America and the British Isles
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Autonomous Orthodox Metropolia of North and South America and the British Isles
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Autonomous Orthodox Metropolia of North and South America and the British Isles is an ultraconservative Old Calendarist (also known as “True Orthodox”) Church body which rejects the use of the “New” (Gregorian) Church calendar, condemns ecumenical relations with other – non-Orthodox – Christian Churches, and is not in communion with mainstream Orthodox Churches. Originally, this group was part of the True Orthodox Church of Greece, then it joined another Old Calendarist group called the Holy Synod of Milan. In February of 2011, the Synod of Milan granted the Metropolia an autonomous self-governing status. In April 2011, after the Holy Synod of Milan released a statement about fraternal relations with the Russian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate), the Metropolia ceased communion with and broke entirely from the Holy Synod of Milan. The reason for separation was "the fact that the European Metropolia ('Milan Synod in Europe') broke communion with her sister churches, over her desire to unite with the World Orthodox Patriarchate of Moscow, which has been condemned by the European Metropolia's former Sister Synods in America, Greece, Bulgaria, and Russia." The Metropolia also issued a clergy confession reiterating its full rejection of Ecumenism, Sergianism, Modernism, and the New Calendar. The Metropolia is a member of the International Union of Genuine Orthodox Churches. Headquartered at Holy Name Abbey (West Milford, NJ), the Metropolia has churches in the U.S., Canada, the United Kingdom, and the Philippines.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Avadhuta Foundation
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Avadhuta Foundation grew from the life and work of H.W.L. Poonja, also called Papaji, a teacher of Advaita Vedanta Hinduism. The foundation itself was set by Kamal Ruane, Raj Prabhu, and Billy Pearlman in order to further Papaji's teachings. The primary function of the foundation is to meet regularly and listen to tapes of Papaji's teachings and discuss them.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Avatamsaka Buddhist Lotus Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Avatamsaka Buddhist Lotus Society was founded in 1985, but has its origins in the 1978 visit of Ven. Master Cheng Yi to the United States. Over additional visits to the United States in the late 1970s and early 1980s, Ven. Master Cheng Yi helped catalyze the desire to spread the Buddhist Huayen School and Avatamsaka Sutra. Since its founding in California, the association has since built multiple buildings and founded other groups.
