Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Babaji's Kriya Yoga
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Babaji's Kriya Yoga was founded by S. A. A. Ramaiah and V. T. Neelakantan after perceiving that the founder of Self-Realization Fellowship had neglected Babaji - an alleged immortal master. It is currently led by Marshall Govindan, who was a disciple of Ramaiah and claims to have had two visions of Babaji. Babaji Kryia Yoga follows the ancient teachings of the 18 Siddha Tradition. It includes a series of techniques, or 'kriya,' grouped into five branches. It is thought to accelerate the process of divine consciousness in humans.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Baha'i Faith
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Baha'i Faith is an independent monotheistic religion with its own sacred scriptures, laws, calendar, and holy days. It was founded by Baha'u'llah in 19th-century Persia (today known as Iran). It is not a part of Islam, but its origins cannot be understood apart from the Shi'a Muslim environment in which it was born. The first public mention of the Baha'i Faith in North America was at the 1893 Parliament of Religions in Chicago, IL. The National Spiritual Assembly of the Baha'is of the United States and Canada was incorporated in 1927.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Baptist Bible Fellowship International
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Baptist Bible Fellowship International was founded in 1950 by Reverend Beuchamp Vick with former members of the World Baptist Fellowship. Doctrinally, they hold traditional Baptist beliefs, are strong fundamentalists, and believe in the inerrancy of scripture. The main way they differ from some other Baptists is in their ecclesiology; specifically, they emphasize strong autonomy of the local church and strong authority of the pastor.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Baptist General Conference
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Baptist General Conference was founded among Swedish-Americans in 1852 by Gustaf Palmquist. In 2008, the group changed its name to Converge Worldwide.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Baptist Missionary Association of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Baptist Missionary Association of America was founded in 1950 by former members of the American Baptist Association. It was originally called the North American Baptist Association until it took the present name in 1968.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Baptist Missionary Convention (Triennial Convention)
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Baptist Missionary Convention, formed in 1814, was known officially as the General Missionary Convention of the Baptist Denomination in America. However, because the group met every three years, it was more commonly known as the Triennial Convention. This group was the first formal Baptist missions organization in America, but was not universally supported by Baptists; in fact, it would be the forum in which many issues would be argued, and eventually produced multiple schisms.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Barren River Missionary Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Beachy Amish Mennonite Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Beachy Amish Mennonite Churches started when Bishop Moses Beachy refused to pronounce the ban against some former Old Order Amish members.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Belarusian Autocephalous Orthodox Church
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Belarusian Autocephalous Orthodox Church separated from the Russian Orthodox Church in 1922 in an attempt to create a national Church on the territory of the Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic (then part of the USSR). In the 1930s, under Joseph Stalin's militant atheist regime, the Church was nearly driven to extinction; however, during WWII, the Church was re-established on the territories of Byelorussia which were occupied by German troops. In 1948, bishops and members who managed to escape from the Soviet Union met in Konstanz (Germany) and reorganized their activities and church administration. The Church is currently headquartered in Brooklyn, NY, and has three parishes in the United States.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Believers in Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Berea Amish Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Berea Amish Mennonites formed out of the Old Beachys in the late 1980s out of a desire to use English in services to accommodate outsiders who wanted to join the church. Other more conservative mainstream Beachy Amish Mennonite and Mennonite Christian Fellowship churches also joined.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Independent Fundamentalist > Berean Fellowship of Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Independent Fundamentalist | Berean Fellowship of Churches, formerly called the Berean Fundamental Churches Council, is an independent fundamentalist fellowship of churches founded in 1947 by Dr. Ivan E. Olsen.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Berkeley Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Berkeley Zen Center originated in the Zen Center of San Francisco under the leadership of Shunryu Suzuki Roshi. However, after Suzuki Roshi's death, the organization became independent under Sojun Mel Weitsman, who was Suzuki Roshi's student, with a new emphasis on lay practice. In 1979, the center moved to its present location that is home to a small residential community of priests, lay students, and children.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Bethel Ministerial Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Bethel Ministerial Association was founded in 1934 by the Rev. Albert Franklin Varnell as the Evangelistic Ministerial Alliance. The Association is for ministers and denies the Trinitarian nature of God and practices baptism in Jesus’ name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Bible Church of Christ
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Bible Church of Christ is a Pentecostal church founded in 1961 in the Bronx, New York, by Bishop Roy Bryant, Sr.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Bible Church of Christ, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Bible Church of Christ was founded in 1961 by Bp. Roy Bryant Sr. as a Pentecostal and Trinitarian church under the authority of the Bible. The association developed a ministry in demonology, as well as a Theological Institute.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Bible Fellowship Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Bible Fellowship Church was formed in 1947 by churches withdrawing from the Mennonite Brethren in Christ when the Brethren changed their name to the United Missionary Church and dropped all Mennonite connections. Members see themselves as continuing the tradition of the Mennonite Brethren of Christ. After the split in 1947, the church was allowed to operate under its former name, but it officially adopted the name Bible Fellowship Church in 1959.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Bible Holiness Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Bible Holiness Church, previously the Fire Baptized Holiness Church (Wesleyan), gained its current name in 1995. The association is still active to this day.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Bible Methodist Connection of Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Bible Methodist Connection of Churches formed in 1970 as the merger of the Ohio Wesleyan Connection of Churches and the Alabama Bible Methodists. This merger started at the time of larger merger between the Pilgrim Holiness Church and the Wesleyan Methodist Church, which would create the Wesleyan Church in 1968. The group bases its doctrine on the Twenty-five Articles of Religion provided by John Wesley, which derive from the Thirty-nine Articles of the Church of England.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Bible Missionary Church
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Bible Missionary Church is a Holiness church founded in 1956. The church formed from the Bible Missionary Union started by the Rev. Glenn Griffith and other former members of the Church of the Nazarene after a revival. The Bible Missionary Church remained distinct from the Church of the Nazarene by adhering to strict holiness standards.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Bible Presbyterian Church
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Bible Presbyterian Church is a fundamentalist church founded in 1938 by the Rev. Carl McIntire, formerly a minister in the Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A. and one of the founders of the Orthodox Presbyterian Church (OPC). Once the OPC split in 1937, McIntire founded the Bible Presbyterian Church to pursue his more conservative convictions. The Bible Presbyterian Church was drastically reduced in size (and re-organized) in 1956 when many churches left to form the Evangelical Presbyterian Church, which would become the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod, in 1965 via a merger.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Bible Presbyterian Church (General Synod)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Bible Presbyterian Church (General Synod) is the re-organized remnant of the 1938 Bible Presbyterian Church. They sought to retain the original intent of the association as well as the leadership of Rev. Carl McIntire. The association is presbyterial in structure while preserving congregational autonomy.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Bible Protestant Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Bible Protestant Church was formed by former members of the Methodist Protestant Church in New Jersey who opposed their church’s merger into the Methodist Church in 1939. In 1985, it changed its name to Fellowship of Fundamental Bible Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Bible Standard Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Bible Standard Conference was formed in Eugene, Oregon, by Fred Hornshuh in 1919. It merged with the Open Bible Evangelistic Association in 1935 to form the Open Bible Standard Churches (called Open Bible Churches since 1996).

Primary_Label: Bible Way Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ World Wide, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Bible Way Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ World Wide, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Bible Way Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ World Wide, Inc. is a predominantly black Oneness Pentecostal church founded in 1957 by Smallwood E. Williams and former members of the Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith. Frustrated with Robert Clarence Lawson's leadership of the Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith, the founders of BWCWW sought to establish form of governance less dependent on a personality. In 1997, a conflict over leadership led to the creation of the International Bible Way Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ, which took the majority of BWCWW's congregations.

By blending Western psychology and Zen tradition, it allows students to quickly experience the different aspects of the mind. Following Genpo Roshi's disrobing, Merzel decided to continue to teach "Big Mind" techniques as a purely secular practice.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Blue Mountain Center of Meditation

The center offers programs in an eight-point program of meditation and aligned living skills. The group is not affiliated with any religious group or movement and considers itself to be nondenomiational, nonsectarian, and free from dogma and ritual.

Primary_Label: Bochasanwasi Shri Akshar Purushottam Swaminarayan Sanstha (BAPS)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Bochasanwasi Shri Akshar Purushottam Swaminarayan Sanstha (BAPS)

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Bodhicharya North America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Bodhicharya is a Buddhist movement founded by Ringu Tulku Rinpoche in 1997 in Belgium. It has since spread to the United States, forming officially in 2009 as Bodhicharya North America and receiving tax-exempt status in 2014. The association focuses on three main teachings: "helping, healing, and harmony.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > BodhiPath

Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Buddhist Association of the United States was led by Pres. Lok To and V.P. Chia Teng Shen. The association works to synthesize various Buddhist trends. Ch'an and Pure Land Buddhism are the two most important, and they focus on the worship of Amida Buddha.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Buddhist Churches of America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Outpost of the largest of the many Japanese Buddhist groups, the Buddhist Churches of America was begun by missionaries who arrived in San Francisco in the 1890s. The group was originally called the Buddhist Mission of North America but took their present name in 1944. Having the continental United States as its territory, it is in fellowship with the Buddhist Churches of Canada and the Honpa Hongwanji Mission of Hawaii.

Primary_Label: Buddhist Compassion Relief Tzu Chi Association
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Buddhist Compassion Relief Tzu Chi Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Buddhist Compassion Relief Tzu Chi Association was founded by Cheng Yen, a female dharma. Tzu Chi contains four major missions of charity, medicine, education, and culture. The group has greatly contributed to the rise of a humanistic approach to Chinese Mahayana Buddhism. Tzu Chi is also notable for its female leadership, as the majority of leadership is female at every level.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Buddhist Mission of North America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Buddhist Mission of North America was founded in 1899, and it later changed its name to the Buddhist Churches of America. It remains affiliated with the Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Buddhist Society for Compassionate Wisdom
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Buddhist Society for Compassionate Wisdom, originally known as Zen Lotus Society, was founded by Ven. Samu Sunim. The purpose of the society is to help bring the culture of Buddhism to the West.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Buddhist Society of America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Buddhist Society of America was founded in 1930 by Sokei-an Sasaki Roshi, the only member who remained in the States from the 1906 failed missionary trip sponsored by Ryomokyo-kai Zen Institute of Tokyo. The association incorporated in 1931 and remains active in New York as the First Zen Institute of America (its name as of 1944). The association is currently lay-led but still practices Rinzai Zen.

Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Buffalo Synod was founded in 1845. Theologically, the Synod was influenced by the work of Carl Ferdinand Wilhelm Walther. It was part of the 1930 merger that formed the American Lutheran Church.

Primary_Label: Bulgarian Diocese of the Orthodox Church in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Bulgarian Diocese of the Orthodox Church in America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The first wave of Bulgarian immigrants arrived in America in 1903 in the wake of an unsuccessful insurrection in Macedonia aimed at the overthrow of Turkish Ottoman rule. In 1907, the first Bulgarian Orthodox parish, the Church of the Holy Trinity, was established in Madison, IL. In 1937, the Synod of the Church of Bulgaria created the “Bulgarian Diocese of the Americas” with Bishop Andrey (Petkov) being the first ruling bishop of the Diocese. After WWII, the Diocese was swept up in the political changes in Bulgaria, which became a Communist-ruled satellite of the Soviet Union. Conflicts among clergy and laity resulted in a schism in the parishes. From 1964 on, there existed two rival Orthodox Dioceses in the United States that called themselves “Bulgarian Orthodox.” The first – the “Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Diocese of the USA, Canada, and Australia” – continued to be headed by Bishop Andrey (Petkov) and remained loyal to the Synod of Bulgarian Orthodox Church in Sofia. The second – led by Bishop Cyril (Yonchev) and with an emphasized anti-Communist stance – was called the “Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Church: Diocese of the United States of America and Canada.” It first joined the Russian Church Outside of Russia, and later (1976) moved into the Orthodox Church in America, becoming the “Bulgarian Diocese of the Orthodox Church in America.” The two rival Bulgarian Orthodox Dioceses continue to co-exist in North America to the present day. The Bulgarian Diocese is part of Orthodox Church in America and one of its three ethnic dioceses (the others being Albanian and Romanian). Therefore, the 2020 statistics of its congregations and adherents are included in the statistics of the Orthodox Church in America.

Primary_Label: Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Diocese of the USA, Canada, and Australia
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Diocese of the USA, Canada, and Australia
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Diocese of the USA, Canada, and Australia is part of the Bulgarian Orthodox Church, with headquarters in Sofia, Bulgaria. The first wave of Bulgarian immigrants arrived in America in 1903 in the wake of an unsuccessful insurrection in Macedonia aimed at the overthrow of Turkish Ottoman rule. In 1907, the first Bulgarian Orthodox parish, the Church of the Holy Trinity, was established in Madison, IL. In 1937, the Synod of the Church of Bulgaria created the “Bulgarian Diocese of the Americas” with Bishop Andrey (Petkov) being the first ruling bishop of the Diocese. After WWII, the Diocese was swept up in the political changes in Bulgaria, which became a Communist-ruled satellite of the Soviet Union. Conflicts among clergy and laity resulted in a schism in the parishes. From 1964 on, there existed two rival Orthodox Dioceses in the United States, both calling themselves “Bulgarian Orthodox.” The first - the “Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Diocese of the USA, Canada, and Australia” – continued to be headed by Bishop Andrey (Petkov) and remained loyal to the Synod of the Bulgarian Orthodox Church in Sofia. The second – led by Bishop Cyril (Yonchev) and emphasizing an anti-Communist stance - was called the “Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Church: Diocese of the United States of America and Canada.” It initially joined the Russian Church Outside of Russia, and later (1976) merged with the Orthodox Church in America, becoming the “Bulgarian Diocese of the Orthodox Church in America.” The two rival Bulgarian Orthodox Dioceses continue to co-exist in North America to the present day. In 1991, Metropolitan Joseph was elected as the ruling bishop of the Bulgarian Eastern Orthodox Diocese of the USA, Canada, and Australia. He continues to hold this position up to the present (2023), which makes him the most senior Orthodox bishop in the USA by number of years being a bishop. In 2000, the Diocese requested the Synod of the Bulgarian Orthodox Church to permit non-Bulgarians to serve as priests in parishes of the Diocese. The request was approved, opening the way for a number of American Orthodox clergy and parishes to join the Diocese. Most of the parishes of the Bulgarian Diocese follow the “old” (Julian) church calendar.
