Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Calvary Chapel
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | Calvary Chapel is a non-denominational church founded in Costa Mesa, California. In 1965, Pastor Chuck Smith left his expanding church in Corona, CA, to lead the 25-person congregation in Costa Mesa. His ministry attracted hippies and surfers, grew rapidly, and eventually expanded to an international network of churches that emphasizes a non-denominational identity bound by expository preaching.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Calvary Pentecostal Church, Inc
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Calvary Pentecostal Church was formed in 1931 by a group of Pentecostal ministers in the northwestern United States who longed to inspire a greater reliance on God that they believed was present in former revivals. What was intended as an interdenominational fellowship became a denomination that dissolved sometime after 1970.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Cambodian Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Cambodian Buddhism was founded in the United States by Ven. Maha Ghosananda. He fled to the United States due to trouble in Thailand and became the head of the community in Rhode Island. Today, it is the base for establishing Buddhist communities in refugee communities around the world.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Cambridge Buddhist Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Cambridge Buddhist Association is a nonsectarian center for lay Buddhist practice and studies. The association was founded after Mr. and Mrs. John Mitchell convinced Daisetz Teitaro Suzuki, a Buddhist scholar, and Dr. Shinichi Hisamatsu to establish a Western-style Zendo. The group dissolved in 2011; however, their space is still used by various Buddhist groups in the area.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Canadian and American Reformed Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Canadian and American Reformed Churches form a conservative federation founded in 1950 from immigrants who had previously been part of the Reformed Churches in the Netherlands (Liberated). The federation strictly adheres to the Belgic Confession of Faith, the Heidelberg Catechism, and the Canons of Dort as the foundations of theology.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Catholic Apostolic Church
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Catholic Apostolic Church was founded in reaction to remarkable works of the Holy Spirit witnessed by Rev. John McLoed Campbell, the MacDonalds, and others in and before 1830. In response to these events, and friction with Presbyterian, Congregational, and Episcopal Churches, the Catholic Apostolic Church was formed. They attempted to find signs of the end times, construct Biblical governance, and display gifts of the Holy Spirit. It dissolved slowly after the death of the original leaders with only one church remaining sometime after 1936.

Primary_Label: Catholic Apostolic Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Catholic Apostolic Church in North America
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Catholic Church
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Catholic Church is centered in the Vatican and is headed by the Pope, also known as the Bishop of Rome. Rome is one of the original five Sees of the Christian Church. It became an important center of Christendom after Emperor Constantine's conversion to Christianity. It separated from the Sees of Constantinople, Antioch, Jerusalem, and Alexandria in 1054. The Catholic Church is the largest Christian communion worldwide. The Catholic Church first colonized what was to become the United States in 1565. Through processes of immigration and evangelism, it became the largest denomination in America in the late nineteenth century and remains so today.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Center for American Buddhist Practice
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Center for American Buddhist Practice (CABP) was founded by Sha'ul Hirschmann, Tom Graham, and Jonathan Lungerich, who are now Buddhist priests. The center was developed as a favorable environment for the American English-speaking community and represents diverse Buddhist traditions. The CABP approaches Buddhist practice in a pragmatic way, but has not abandoned traditional Buddhist ceremonies.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Center for Buddhist Development
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Center for Buddhist development initially began with meditation sessions for faculty and students of Arizona State University. The founder and director of this community is Rev. Kennard Dhammapala. The center includes programs in practice in meditation, mindfulness, Dhamma study, and community engagement.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Center for Cultural Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | The Center for Cultural Judaism is a non-theistic group that supports the exploration of historical and modern Jewish identity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Center for Dzoghen Studies
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Center for Dzogchen Studies is a worldwide community that is dedicated to the teachings and practices of the Nyingma lineage. The center teaches the "Nine Yanas" of Nyingma Tibetan Buddhism, in which a nature of mind is gradually introduced to the practitioner.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Center for Spiritual Awareness (CSA)
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Center for Spiritual Awareness (CSA), originally established as the Church of the Christian Spiritual Alliance, was founded by H. Edwin O'Neal along with his wife, Lois O'Neal, and William Arnold Lapp. The group's purpose at its founding was to teach the fatherhood of God and brotherhood of a man in light of modern day experience, and it emerged as a highly eclectic organization that combined Christian, psychic, and Eastern insights. Today, meditation seminars are offered several times a year and focus on the teachings of kriya yoga traditions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Center for Timeless Wisdom
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Center for Timeless Wisdom was founded by Dr. Peter Fenner, a Buddhist scholar who wanted to provide Westerners access to nondualistic Asian Wisdom. The center presents its teachings through dialogues, workshops, and retreats, with the Radiant Mind course at the center of its program.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Central Baptist Association Ministries
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Central Yearly Meeting of Friends
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Central Yearly Meeting of Friends was formed in 1926 by several meetings in eastern Indiana who were protesting the liberalism of the Five Years Meeting. This group is evangelical and conservative on matters of personal holiness.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Chabad Judaism
Description: Judaism | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chagdud Gonpa Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Chagdud Gonpa foundation was founded by Chagdud Tulku Rinpoche. Through the foundation's activities, which include public teachings, retreats, translating, and publishing texts, it is able to uphold the meditation practice of the Nyingma school of Tibetan Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chan Nhu Buddhist Pagoda
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Chan Nhu Buddhist Pagoda was founded by Aya Chan Nhu and is a center seeking a new way for American Buddhists, especially women. Although the temple began as a Vietnamese center, Chan Nhu believed that the temple would be more useful as an open Buddhist space. It has housed a Chinese Pure Land group, a vipassana meditation group, and many more. Other groups use the facilities for classes, retreats, or weekly meditation sessions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Charis Fellowship
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | Charis Fellowship was originally founded in the 1930s by former members of the Brethren Church (Ashland, Ohio). It was known as the National Fellowship of Brethren Churches from 1939-1976 and the Fellowship of Grace Brethren Churches from 1976-2017. In 2017, the group assumed its present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chicago Rimé Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Chicago Rimé Center, now known as the Rimé Foundation, was founded in 1997 by Roberto Sanchez and is a western outpost of the Rimé tradition. The foundation supports the practice, development, and integration of Tibetan Buddhism. The Center offers weekly practice sessions and dharma talks.

Primary_Label: Chinese Buddhist Association of Hawaii-Hsu Yun Temple
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chinese Buddhist Association of Hawaii-Hsu Yun Temple
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Chinese Buddhist Association of Hawaii-Hsu Yun Temple was founded after the suggestion by the Hong Kong Chinese Buddhist Association. The temple was named in memory and honor of Ven. Hsu Yun. The grounds of the temple include a main temple for worship and two memorial halls for worshiping ancestors.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Chinmaya Mission West
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Chinmaya Mission West was founded by Swami Chinayananda, who first began sharing his knowledge with the public in 1951. In the 1960s, he first visited the U.S., and by 1975 the Chinmaya Mission West was incorporated. The group is notable for its Vedantic teachings along with its emphasis on knowledge of the Upanishads and the Bhagavad Gita.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Chishti Sufi Order
Description: Islam | Islam | The Chishti Sufi Order was founded in India by Muin al-Din Chishti (d.1236).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chokling Tersar Foundation USA (CTF)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Chokling Tersar Foundation USA (CTF), originally known as the America center of the Chokling Tersar, was founded by Tulku Urgyen Rinpoche. The foundation has a lineage in Tibetan Buddhism within the Gelugpa school. The Rangjung Yeshe Gome retreat center was established by the foundation and offers practice, study groups, individual retreats, and religious celebrations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chozen-ji Kyudo
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Chozen-Ji Kyudo was developed by Omori Roshi, a dharma successor of the Tenryu-ji lineage, and integrates Rinzai Zen training with martial and fine arts. He transmitted the Chozen-Ji lineage to the United States, with the U.S. center placing an emphasis on Kyudo (Zen archery). The group offers three ways to practice, including sesshin, weekly classes, and live-in training. Chozen-ji opened the institute of Zen Studies to spread Zen practices to the West.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Christ Catholic Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Catholic Liturgical | The Christ Catholic Church is an independent Old Catholic jurisdiction founded in 1965 by the Most Rev. Karl Pruter.

Primary_Label: Christ Catholic Exarchate of Americas and Eastern Hemisphere
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Christ Catholic Exarchate of Americas and Eastern Hemisphere
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | Sometime around the early 1980s, the Old Catholic Archdiocese for the Americas and Europe changed its name to the Christ Catholic Exarchate of Americas and Eastern Hemisphere. Under its new name, the association was led by Peter A. Zurawetzky in New Jersey.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Christ Community Church of Zion
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Christ Community Church, formerly known as the Christian Catholic Church, was founded in 1896 by John Alexander Dowie. It is rooted in evangelical Protestantism, though it has borrowed from several traditions. In 1997, the church approved a name change to Christ Community Church, though it still uses its former name (Christian Catholic Church) to refer to its worldwide fellowship and missions work.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Christ Unity Spiritual Science Church
Description: Other | Other Groups | Unity Worldwide Ministries was founded by Myrtle and Charles Fillmore, originally as the Society of Silent Help (by Myrtle in 1890) and the magazine Modern Thought (by Charles in 1889). Myrtle’s own healing from tuberculosis, brought on by following the teaching of Eugene B. Weeks, catalyzed her foundation of the Society of Silent Help. Emma Curtis Hopkins later influenced the couple; in response, Charles changed his magazine to Christian Science Thought. By 1891, Unity magazine was founded (replacing the former) and the Society of Silent Help was renamed Silent Unity. In 1914, the Unity Tract Society and Silent Unity became the Unity School of Christianity. Unity Worldwide Ministries (named in 2011) was formed as a separate organization for the governance and assistance of Unity’s affiliated religious leaders. Unity offers assistance to people of many faith backgrounds.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Christ's Sanctified Holy Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | Christ's Sanctified Holy Church was organized in 1892. The Church founders separated from the Methodist Episcopal Church over disagreements concerning the influence of holiness-theology on the new group. The church exists today across the southeastern United States. The association has a strong loyalty to the King James Version of the Bible, and they do not administer water baptisms or communion.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Christadelphians
Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | The Christadelphians were founded in 1844 by Dr. John Thomas. They deny the divinity of Jesus Christ and aspire to study scripture accurately in congregations not ruled by employed clergy. In 1898, there was a controversy over an amendment on eschatology in their statement of faith. This has left a lasting schism among Christadelphians in the United States between the Central and Unamended Christadelphians.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Christian and Missionary Alliance
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Christian and Missionary Alliance was founded in 1897 as a merger of the Christian Alliance and the Evangelical Missionary Alliance, both founded by Albert B. Simpson.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Froehlich Churches > Christian Apostolic Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Froehlich Churches | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Independent Fundamentalist > Christian Brethren (Plymouth Brethren)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Independent Fundamentalist | The Christian or Open Brethren is one branch of the movement (popularly called the Plymouth Brethren) begun in England in the 1820s, largely influenced by John Nelson Darby. It has been more open to cooperation with believers in non-Brethren organizations. They do not have central governance and have historically preferred to avoid denominational titles. They were recognized in the United States in the 1926.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Christian Catholic Church
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Christian Catholic Church, also known as the Christian Catholic Rite of Community Churches, was founded in 1896 by John Alexander Dowie. In 1997, the church approved a name change to Christ Community Church, though it still uses this name (Christian Catholic Church) to refer to its worldwide fellowship and missions work.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > Christian Church
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | The Christian Church formed officially from a general convention in 1833 that united churches as old as the 1794 church started by James O'Kelly. The Churches united around desires for non-sectarian unity and identification as merely Christian Churches. In 1931, the Christian Church merged with the Congregational Churches. In 1957, the General Council of Congregational and Christian Churches was part of the merger that formed the United Church of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Restorationists
Description: A structural family category within the Mainline Protestant tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Restorationists > Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)
Description: Mainline Protestant | Restorationists | The Christian Church (Disciples of Christ) is one of several large groups that has grown out of the Restoration Movement begun in the early 19th century by Barton Stone and Thomas and Alexander Campbell.

Primary_Label: Christian Churches and Churches of Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Restorationists > Christian Churches and Churches of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Restorationists | The Christian Churches and Churches of Christ is a decentralized movement derived from the Restoration Movement initiated in the United States during the first half of the 19th century by Barton Stone, Thomas and Alexander Campbell (former Presbyterians), and Walter Scott (a former Baptist). Until recent decades an integral part of the Christian Church (Disciples of Christ), these congregations gradually separated as the Disciples of Christ became more centrally organized in 1968.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Christian Communities
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Christian Congregation in North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Christian Congregation, Inc., The
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Christian Congregation is an evangelical organization which dates to 1798. After a brief association with the Christian Church movement of Barton Stone, it incorporated in 1887.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Restorationists > Christian Connection/Christian Church
Description: Unclassified | Restorationists | The Christian Connection, or Christian Church, was formed around 1810 as the congregations associated with the movements of James O'Kelly, Abner Jones/Elias Smith, and Barton Stone united. At the time, it was still a loose fellowship of churches, and they preferred the name "The Christian Church." In 1832, many of the churches within this group that were led by Barton Stone united with those of Alexander Campbell. This produced the Stone-Campbell movement that would become the Christian Church (Disciples of Christ). The remaining group continued to exist as the non-Stone-Campbell "Christians.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Christian Methodist Episcopal Church
Description: Black Protestant | Methodist | The Christian Methodist Episcopal Church was originally founded in 1870 by black former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. Its prior name was the Colored Methodist Episcopal Church (which was also formerly the Colored Methodist Church). In 1954, the church took on its present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Christian Nation Church U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Christian Nation Church U.S.A., an evangelical Protestant body, dates back to the work of eight young evangelists in central Ohio in 1892. In 1895, the Christian Nation Church was incorporated in Marion, Ohio.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Christian Reformed Church (Korean)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | No description available.

Primary_Label: Christian Reformed Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Christian Reformed Church in North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Christian Reformed Church in North America continues the conservative Reformed tradition of the Netherlands. It was founded in the United States in 1857.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Christian Union
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Other Groups | The Christian Union is an evangelical Protestant body organized in 1864.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Christian Unity Baptist Association
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Christian Unity Baptist Association emerged out of a controversy regarding open communion within the Mountain Union Regular Baptist Association in 1901. The Christian Unity Baptist Association formed from the joining of dissenting congregations from the Mountain Union Regular Baptist Association with the recently (1932) established Macedonia Baptist Association. The newly made Christian Unity Baptist Association strongly supported open communion and practiced congregationally structured polity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Chung Tai
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Chung Tai Chan Buddhist movement was developed by Ven. Wei Chueh. Disciples are expected to live based on three disciplines: cultivation of merit, scriptural understanding through study, and regular meditation. The first center in the United States was founded in 1990s.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Church of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: Church of Christ "with the Elijah Message," Established Anew in 1929
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Christ "with the Elijah Message," Established Anew in 1929
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Church of Christ "with the Elijah Message," Established Anew in 1929 was formed in 1943 after a conflict within the Church of Christ (Fetting/Bronson). Otto Fetting was the leader of the Church of Christ (Fetting/Bronson) until his death in 1933, and during his life he claimed to receive messages from John the Baptist. After he passed, the church received no revelations until another church elder, W. A. Draves, claimed to also receive messages from John the Baptist. Although some of these early messages were accepted by the church, their validity was eventually questioned, and a lawsuit over church assets in 1943 prompted Draves and his followers to leave the church, forming this group.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Christ (Fetting/Bronson)
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Church of Christ (Fetting/Bronson) began in 1929 after a split from the Church of Christ (Temple Lot). Two years earlier, in 1927, one of the Twelve Apostles of the Church of Christ (Temple Lot), Otto Fetting, claimed to receive messages from John the Baptist. The first message was to build the temple, but the twelfth message, which called for members of the church to be (re)baptized, became a matter of controversy and disagreement. The church did not accept this message, and when Fetting and others were baptized anyway, they were disfellowshipped. Thus, the Church of Christ (Fetting) was started by Fetting and his followers, which represented about a third of the Temple Lot church at the time.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Church of Christ (Holiness) U.S.A
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | In 1894, C. P. Jones and Charles H. Mason formed the Church of God in Christ as a Holiness body, following their exclusion from fellowship with black Baptists in Arkansas. Mason took most of the body into Pentecostalism in 1907. Those who remained were organized by Jones as the Church of Christ (Holiness) U.S.A.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Christ (Temple Lot)
Description: Unclassified | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Church of Christ is a denomination of the Latter-day Saint movement headquartered in Independence, Missouri on what is known as the Temple Lot. Members of the church have been known colloquially as Hedrickites, after Granville Hedrick, who was ordained the first leader of the faction in July 1863. The church sees itself as the true continuation of the Church of Christ, which was organized in 1830. After persecutions of the church, migrations, and divisions, the year 1867 marks the return to Independence, Missouri, of four congregations from rural Illinois that had united together and spoke out about errors in the church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Christian Science
Description: A structural family category within the Other Christians tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Christian Science > Church of Christ, Scientist
Description: Other Christians | Christian Science | The Church of Christ, Scientist grew out of the experiences, work, and writings of Mary Baker Eddy. It defines itself as Christian in essence, a major difference between it and most other "metaphysical" churches with which it is often compared.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Church of Daniel's Band
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Church of Daniel’s Band was founded in 1893 by former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church. Despite Wesleyan influences, the church is non-denominational and focuses on evangelizing.

Primary_Label: Church of God (7th Day, Salem, West Virginia)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Church of God (7th Day, Salem, West Virginia)
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | The Church of God (7th Day, Salem, West Virginia) was founded in 1933 by former members of the General Conference of the Church of God (Seventh Day). The catalyzing issue that prompted the formation of the new church was a disagreement over congregational polity; at the general conference meeting in 1933, there was a motion to reorganize the church from a congregational polity to one that maintained a hierarchy of 12 apostles, 70 prophets, and 7 financial stewards. When the move was defeated, those who supported it reorganized as a new congregation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Church of God (Anderson, Indiana)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Church of God (Anderson, Indiana) is a holiness church founded in 1881 by Daniel S. Warner who was expelled from the Churches of God in North America (now the Church of God, General Council). It has since founded congregations in 89 countries with about 8,000 churches worldwide. The Church of God Ministries, Inc. is the legal entity of the General Assembly of the Church of God in the USA and Canada and is synonymous with the Church of God (Anderson, Indiana).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God (Cleveland, Tennessee)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God (Cleveland, Tennessee) was founded in 1886 as a Holiness church, the Christian Union. It assumed its present name in 1907 after members accepted a new Pentecostal perspective.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God (Jerusalem Acres)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God (Jerusalem Acres) began in 1957 when Grady R. Kent, and 300 of his supporters within the Church of God of Prophesy, established a new Church of God based on theocratic government. In worship and service, the group practices "New Testament Judaism," which includes observing the Biblical (Old Testament) calendar along with its holy days. The organizational polity is defined by an appointed leader, 12 apostles, 72 prophets, and 7 men of wisdom.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Church of God (New Dunkers)
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Church of God (New Dunkers) split from the German Baptist Brethren in Indiana. The group, led by George Patton and Peter Eyman, baptized through forward immersion and believed the Bible should provide all the names for sacred groups and activities. The association dissolved in 1962.

Primary_Label: Church of God (Which He Purchased With His Own Blood)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Church of God (Which He Purchased With His Own Blood)
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Church of God (Which He Purchased with His Own Blood) is a predominantly black Pentecostal church founded in 1953 by William Jordan Fizer, formerly a minister with the Church of the Living God (Christian Workers for Fellowship). He was excommunicated for his conviction that the Lord's Supper should be served with grape juice or wine, not water as the Church of the Living God (Christian Workers for Fellowship) believed.

Primary_Label: Church of God and Saints of Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Church of God and Saints of Christ
Description: Other Christians | Judaism | Elder William S. Crowdy, a black cook on the Sante Fe Railroad, claimed to have a vision from God telling him to lead his people to true religion. He left his job and founded the Church of God and Saints of Christ in 1896 at Lawrence, Kansas. The doctrine of the Church of God is a complicated mixture of Judaism, Christianity, and black nationalism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God Apostolic, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God Apostolic is a predominantly black Oneness Pentecostal church founded in 1897 as the Christian Faith Band by Thomas J. Cox. The association was incorporated as the Church of God Apostolic, Inc. in 1919.

Primary_Label: Church of God as Organized by Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Church of God as Organized by Christ
Description: Other | Other Groups | As of 1927, the Church of God as Organized by Christ was located in Quakertown, Pennsylvania, and was attended by 25 people.

Primary_Label: Church of God by Faith, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God by Faith, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God by Faith was organized in 1914 by Elder John Bright and chartered in 1923 at Alachua, Florida. Its doctrine is like that of the Church of God (Cleveland, Tennessee).

Primary_Label: Church of God General Conference (Abrahamic Faith, McDonough, GA)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Church of God General Conference (Abrahamic Faith, McDonough, GA)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | The Church of God General Conference (Abrahamic Faith) is the outgrowth of several independent local groups of similar faith, some in existence as early as 1800; others date their beginnings from the arrival of British immigrants to this country around 1847. These diverse groups shared in general Adventist theology. A national organization was instituted in 1888 and 1889; however, because of strong convictions relating to congregational rights and authority, the national body ceased to function until 1921, when the present general conference was formed at Waterloo, Iowa. In 1991, the group's headquarters and Bible college were moved to Morrow, Georgia, where they remained until 2009. At that point, their headquarters were moved again, this time to McDonough, Georgia.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God in Christ
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God in Christ, the largest of the several predominantly black Pentecostal churches, was founded in 1894 by Charles H. Mason. In 1907, Mason took most of the body into Pentecostalism. Those who remained were organized by Elder Charles Prince Jones as the Church of Christ (Holiness) U.S.A.

Primary_Label: Church of God in Christ Jesus, Apostolic, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God in Christ Jesus, Apostolic, Inc.
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Church of God in Christ Jesus, Apostolic, Inc. was founded in January of 1946 in Baltimore, Maryland, by Randolph A. Carr and Monroe R. Saunders, two former ministers in the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World. The church was organized at a two-day conference for the purpose of establishing greater unity in the fellowship of churches. Theologically, the group maintained similar beliefs to its parent body, though had stricter standards regarding divorce and remarriage. This would eventually lead to internal division that would prompt Carr to break with the church and form the United Church of Jesus Christ (Apostolic).

Primary_Label: Church of God in Christ United
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God in Christ United
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God in Christ United was organized in 1973 as a break from the Church of God in Christ. This movement was led by Bishop James Feltus, Jr., who was previously serving as a Special Representative of the Church of God in Christ in Louisiana East. As conflicts developed in the church, Bishop Feltus began to organize a separate denomination in 1972 around five key principles, which prioritized the Bible, the local church, and Christian fellowship. In 1973, Bishop Felton, Bishop R. A. Campbell, and Bishop Marshall met and formally agreed to unite under the new church name, with Supervisor Hazel L. Feltus as International Supervisor of the Department of Women.

Primary_Label: Church of God in Christ, Congregational
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God in Christ, Congregational
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God in Christ, Congregational was formed in 1932 by Bp. J. Bowe of Hot Springs, Arkansas. Bowe was previously a member of the Church of God in Christ, but was forced out over his assertion that church polity should be congregational rather than episcopal. Nevertheless, the group still remains theologically similar to the Church of God in Christ.

Primary_Label: Church of God in Christ, International
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God in Christ, International
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God in Christ, International was founded in 1969 by Bishop William David Charles, Sr. and other former members of the Church of God in Christ.

Primary_Label: Church of God in Christ, Mennonite
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Church of God in Christ, Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Church of God in Christ, Mennonite, was founded in 1859 by John Holdeman, a former member of the Mennonite Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God of Prophecy
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God of Prophecy is a Holiness Pentecostal church organized by Ambrose J. Tomlinson in 1903. Although it was understood that this group was operating as the Church of God, the General Assembly of 1907 officially adopted the name Church of God. In 1952, “of Prophecy” was added to the name to distinguish this organization from other organizations with similar names.

Primary_Label: Church of God of the Apostolic Faith, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God of the Apostolic Faith, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God of the Apostolic Faith, Inc. was organized in 1914 out of the Pentecostal revivals in Wales and Azusa Street, L.A. The church teaches that sanctification is a definite work accomplished by the Holy Spirit. They have a presbyterial polity.

Primary_Label: Church of God of the Firstborn
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Church of God of the Firstborn
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Church of God, General Assembly
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Primary_Label: Church of God, Mountain Assembly, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of God, Mountain Assembly, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of God, Mountain Assembly is a Holiness Pentecostal church founded in 1906 by Reverends J. H. Parks, Steve Bryant, Tom Moses, and William O. Douglas. In 1911, they changed their name from Church of God to Church of God Mountain Assembly, Inc. to distinguish themselves from other churches with the same title.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Church of God, Reformation Movement
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Church of God, Reformation Movement was founded in 1881 by Daniel S. Warner as a Holiness church. In 1998 the Church of God, Reformation Movement reorganized as the Church of God Ministries, Inc. in Anderson, Indiana. This association is the same as the Church of God (Anderson, Indiana).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Church of Illumination
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Church of Illumination, founded in 1908, is a church associated with the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis to proclaim the arrival of the Manistic Age, a time for the recognition of the inherent equality of men and women.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Jesus Christ (Bickertonite)
Description: Latter-day Saints | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | This church was organized in 1862 at Green Oak, Pennsylvania by William Bickerton.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Jesus Christ (Cutlerite)
Description: Unclassified | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Church of Jesus Christ (Cutlerite) was founded in 1853 by Alpheus Cutler, a Mormon leader at the time Joseph Smith was killed. There appears to be one active congregation left; these congregants affirm the authority of Alpheus Cutler and place little stress on evangelization, instead inviting people who want to move near their church to participate.

Primary_Label: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Description: Latter-day Saints | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The main body of Latter-day Saints is headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah, where the Salt Lake Temple and Tabernacle are located. This church was organized on April 6, 1830 in Fayette, New York, by Joseph Smith. Members believe that Joseph Smith was divinely directed to restore the gospel to the earth, and that through him the keys to the Aaronic and Melchizedek priesthoods and temple work also were restored.

Primary_Label: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (Strangite)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (Strangite)
Description: Unclassified | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (Strangite) was founded in 1844 by James Jesse Strang, a Mormon church leader in Wisconsin at the time Joseph Smith was killed. Joseph Smith appointed Strang as his successor by letter before his death. Strang received a set of revelations which became authoritative for his church.

Primary_Label: Church of our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith, Inc. was founded in 1919 by Robert Clarence Lawson, formerly a minister with the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World. Doctrinally similar to the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World, The Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith relied heavily on the leadership of Robert Clarence Lawson to form its distinct identity in New York City. After his death, the leadership of this church has fallen to the Board of Apostles.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Church of Revelation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Church of Revelation was formed in 1930 at Long Beach, California, by the Rev. Janet Stine Lewis (Wolford). Their teaching centered around what they called the Old Christian Initiate, which claimed scientific truth, spiritual guidance, a pathway to everlasting life, and ways to communicate with the dead. The association apparently dissolved sometime after the last report of membership in 1966.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Church of the Brethren
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Church of the Brethren grew out of the 18th-century Pietist movement in Germany. It was founded in 1723 in the United States by Alexander Mack Sr., Christopher Sauer II, Alexander Mack Jr., and Peter Becker. They are also referred to as Conservative Dunkards.

Primary_Label: Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America is the largest of various the so-called “Old Calendarist” Orthodox (also known as True Orthodox) groups in the U.S. All Old Calendarist groups trace their history to events occurring in early 20th-century Greece. After WWI, various Orthodox churches worldwide began to abandon the “Old” Julian liturgical calendar and adopt the “New” Gregorian calendar, which is 13 days ahead of the Julian and which is also used in the secular world. When this reform was introduced in the Orthodox Church of Greece in 1924, strong opposition arose among some clergy and laity. The calendar question as such was, however, only one of the causes for resistance. The opposition saw the adoption of the new calendar as a symbol of the strengthening ecumenical movement and the growing influence of other, non-Orthodox Christian Churches. In opposition’s view, these changes were compromising the purity of the Orthodox faith. Originally led by Metropolitan Chrysostomos of Florina, those resisting the new calendar became popularly known as Old Calendarists. They formed the Church of True Orthodox Christians of Greece. In the past, Old Calendarists were actively persecuted in Greece (especially in the 1950s), which resulted in their immigration to the U.S., where they founded various Greek Old Calendar churches. The Old Calendar churches are recognizable by the absence of electric lighting and pews, and certain worship practices including traditional Byzantine chant and frequent all-night vigils. Back in Greece, the Old Calendarist community was plagued by internal divisions and eventually fragmented into several groups. Today, most sizeable of these groups is the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of Greece. One of its overseas dioceses is the North American eparchy known as the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America. It has rights of self-governance and broad autonomy, including its own ruling Synod of bishops and Ecclesiastical Tribunal. In 2011-2012, it was strengthened considerably by absorbing many parishes, clergy, and lay members from the Holy Orthodox Church in North America, another Old Calendarist group in the United States. Since 2014, Metropolitan Demetrius (Kyriakou) has been the head of the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America, with his headquarters in the Monastery of St. John of San Francisco in Cobleskill, NY. The liturgical and worship practices of the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America are affected equally by the Greek and Slavic (Russian) traditions. Most parishes use English as the language of worship. Their St. Photios theological seminary is situated in St. Gregory Palamas monastery in Etna, CA, and it is accredited by the state of California.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Church of the Gospel
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Church of the Gospel was formed in 1911 in Pittsfield, Massachusetts by the Rev. and Mrs. C. T. Spike and members of the Advent Christian Church. In 1912, the group incorporated as the Church of God but adopted its present name in 1930 to avoid confusion with other groups. In 1971, there was only a single congregation in Virginia and remnants in the New England area.

Primary_Label: Church of the Living God (Christian Workers for Fellowship)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of the Living God (Christian Workers for Fellowship)
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of the Living God (Christian Workers for Fellowship) is a predominantly black Holiness Pentecostal church founded in 1889 by the Rev. William Christian (1856-1928). Rev. Christian split from the Baptist church to form this association and increase instruction about the important place of black people in the story of the Bible. The church self-identifies as non-denominational and non-sectarian, holds Trinitarian doctrine with Pentecostal influences, and administers water and unleavened bread as the Lord's Supper.

Primary_Label: Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth, Inc. [Lewis Dominion]
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth, Inc. [Lewis Dominion]
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth is a predominantly black Pentecostal church founded in 1908 after the evangelistic work begun in 1903 by Mother Mary L. Tate, a pioneer female Pentecostal evangelist in the South, gained a large following. The church prizes holiness. The House of God Which is the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth, as well as the House of God Which is the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth Without Controversy (Keith Dominion), and the Church of the Living God, the Pillar, and Ground of Truth Which He Purchased with His Own Blood, Inc. all branched off from this association.

Primary_Label: Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth Which He Purchased With His Own Blood, Inc. [McLeod/Jewell Dominion]
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth Which He Purchased With His Own Blood, Inc. [McLeod/Jewell Dominion]
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth Which He Purchased With His Own Blood, Inc. (Jewel Dominion) is a predominantly black Pentecostal church that was part of the 1931 redistribution of the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth founded by Mother Mary Magdalena Lewis Tate. The association was first led by Bishop B.L. McLeod, but after his death in 1936, his widow, Bp. Mattie Lou McLeod, took his place. After her remarriage into the Jewel family, the dominion took her new name as well.

Primary_Label: Church of the Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith (Philadelphia)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Church of the Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith (Philadelphia)
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Church of the Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith is a nontrinitarian Pentecostal church founded in 1933 by Bishop Sherrod C. Johnson. Bishop Johnson was formerly with the Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith but left due to his conviction that women should dress more modestly and not adorn themselves with fashionable accessories. Going as far as to oppose celebrating Lent, Easter, and Christmas, Sherrod Johnson established a lastingly conservative church with an episcopal structure that relies heavily upon lay participation.

Primary_Label: Church of the Lutheran Brethren of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Church of the Lutheran Brethren of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Church of the Lutheran Brethren of America was founded in 1900 by a group of Norwegian-American Lutherans under the leadership of the Rev. Kurt O. Lundeberg.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Church of the Lutheran Confession
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Church of the Lutheran Confession was organized in 1960 at Watertown, South Dakota, by congregations and clergy who had formerly belonged to the various Lutheran denominations that had comprised the Synodical Conference, a Lutheran ecumenical body. With the loss of doctrinal unity within the conference, they felt compelled to leave.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Church of the Nazarene
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Church of the Nazarene is a Holiness church founded in 1908 by Phineas Bresee, a former superintendent in the Methodist Episcopal Church. It was founded as the Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene, and the word "Pentecostal" was dropped in 1919 to avoid confusion with the "speaking in tongues" sects.

Primary_Label: Church of the United Brethren in Christ, USA
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Church of the United Brethren in Christ, USA
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Church of the United Brethren in Christ was founded by conservative members of the United Brethren in Christ who rejected the new constitution adopted by its general conference in 1889. It is sometimes called United Brethren of Christ (Old Constitution).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Restorationists > Churches of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Restorationists | The Churches of Christ (Non-instrumental) is one of the several branches of the Restoration movement begun in the early 19th century by Barton Stone and Thomas and Alexander Campbell, recognized in the 1906 Religious Census as distinct from the Christian Church. Originating primarily among those Restorationist congregations in the South, it has become a national movement. It has been most identified for its disavowal of the use of instrumental music in worship and ultra-congregational organization. Over the years, the decentralized polity has allowed for a number of factions to develop over various beliefs and practices not accepted by the majority of churches. The Churches of Christ (Non-instrumental) represent the largest faction of the movement.

Primary_Label: Churches of Christ in Christian Union
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Churches of Christ in Christian Union
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Churches of Christ in Christian Union is a Holiness church founded in 1909 by former members of the Christian Union.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Churches of God (Greenville, S.C.)
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Churches of God, General Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Churches of God, General Conference was founded in 1825 by John Winebrenner, formerly a minister with the German Reformed Church. It was known for much of its history as the General Eldership of the Churches of God in North America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Churches of God, Holiness
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Churches of God, Holiness is a predominantly black Holiness church founded in 1920 by Bishop King Hezekiah Burruss, formerly with the Church of Christ (Holiness).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Churches of the Cross
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Churches of the Cross were founded in 1834 by congregations that had left the Secession, a group that itself had left the Dutch state church. In 1907, the Churches of the Cross merged with the Ledeboerian Churches to form the Netherlands Reformed Congregations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Clear Water Zendo
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Clear Water Zendo was founded Mary Mocine. The meditation group was originally formed in 1998; however, the center did not official open until 2000. The center is a Soto Zen Buddhist Temple that follows the tradition of Shunryu Suzuki Roshi. The Center offers regular meditation, meditation instruction, classes, one-day sittings, work-practice, lectures, and special workshops.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Colored Methodist Episcopal Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Colored Methodist Episcopal Church was founded in 1870 by black former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. In 1954, the church changed its name to Christian Methodist Episcopal Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Colored Methodist Protestant Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | Founded as the Confederation of Reformed Evangelical Churches in 1997, the Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches switched to its current name in 2011 to prevent association with the Confederacy of the American South. The association seeks to reform the Church through a broader-based collaboration of local churches active in various protestant traditions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Community of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Community of Christ, originally known as the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints, was formed in 1860 by Jason Briggs, Zenos Gurley, William Marks, and followers of Joseph Smith, Jr., who decided against migrating to Utah during the 1850s. The organization changed to its present name in 2001.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Compassion Fellowship
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Compassion Fellowship was founded as an American teaching vehicle for Tulku Orgyen Zengpo Rinpoche. The organization is meant for an American focus for dharma studies, teachings, and retreats. The rinpoche teaches regularly in the United States, while also periodically returning to Tibet.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Concordia Lutheran Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: Conference of United Mennonite Brethren of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Conference of United Mennonite Brethren of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America can be traced to work organized in 1889 by Isaac Peters and Aaron Walls. In that year, they joined their congregations to create the United Mennonite Brethren of North America, though the name was soon changed to Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > Congregational Christian Churches
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | Formed in 1931 by the merger between the National Council of the Congregational Churches and the Christian Church, the General Council of Congregational and Christian Churches lasted until it merged with the Evangelical and Reformed Church in 1957 to form the United Church of Christ.

Primary_Label: Congregational Christian Churches, Additional (not part of any national CCC body)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Congregational Christian Churches, Additional (not part of any national CCC body)
Description: Mainline Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Congregational Holiness Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Congregational Holiness Church is a Holiness Pentecostal church founded in 1921 by the Rev. Watson Sorrow and Hugh Bowling.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Congregational Methodist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Congregational Methodist Church was founded in 1852 by members in Georgia who protested the episcopal structure of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. Their new association sought to change the itinerant preaching system, focus more on local preachers, and provide laypeople a voice through congregational polity. The Congregational Methodist Church prides itself upon holding conservative values to this day.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > Congregationalist Churches
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | Founded in America in 1620 by Pilgrims, the Congregational Churches were a strong spiritual and academic force in early American Christianity. In 1931, the National Council of the Congregational Churches merged with the Christian Church into the General Council of Congregational and Christian Churches.

Primary_Label: Congress of Arya Samajs in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Congress of Arya Samajs in North America
Description: Other | Other Groups | Congress of Arya Samajs in North America follows strong moral and ethical values and focuses on peaceful human living. It is a global organization that is diligently committed to the Universal Truth. The purpose of the North American branch is to maintain communication and ideas with Arya Samaj/Vedic Organizations and spread Hindu-Vedic religion.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Congress of Secular Jewish Organizations
Description: Judaism | Judaism | The Congress of Secular Jewish Organizations most clearly continues the secularized Jewish perspective that emerged in the Jewish phase of the eighteenth-century Enlightenment. As founded in 1969, the Congress offers an alternative to both Reform Judaism and the religious humanism of the Society for Humanistic Judaism. Members seek to affirm their Jewish cultural/ethnic identity apart from any religious affirmations. The Center for Cultural Judaism is a prominent secular Jewish center in New York City that exists independently from the Congress. In 2020, the group changed its name to Cultural and Secular Jewish Organization.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Conservative Amish Mennonite Conference
Description: Unclassified | Mennonite/Amish | The Conservative Amish Mennonite Conference was founded in 1910 as an association of more liberal Amish Mennonite congregations. "Amish" was dropped from the name in 1954.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Conservative Baptist Association of America (CBAmerica)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Conservative Baptist Association of America was founded in 1947 by former ministers and members of the Northern (American) Baptist Convention. In 2019, the organization changed its name to Venture Church Network.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Conservative Congregational Christian Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Conservative Congregational Christian Conference was founded in 1945 by conservative ministers and members of the Congregational Christian Churches who opposed the merger that created the United Church of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Conservative Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | Conservative Judaism formed amid a new wave of immigration of Jews from Europe in the last decades of the nineteenth century. More specifically, it was founded as a movement in 1886 with the formation of the Jewish Theological Seminary Association. The intention of the movement was to retain traditional Jewish practices while inhabiting an ideological position between Reform and Orthodox Judaism, honoring the past while adapting to the present. In 1913, the United Synagogue of America, today called the United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism, began connecting Conservative synagogues. The Rabbinical Assembly unites rabbis within Conservative Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Conservative Lutheran Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Conservative Lutheran Association (CLA) was formed around the late 1980s as congregations began to attach themselves to the World Confessional Lutheran Association. These merged groups initially formed a division with this name, but eventually emerged as a separate Lutheran denomination.

Primary_Label: Conservative Mennonite Churches of York and Adams Counties PA
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Conservative Mennonite Churches of York and Adams Counties PA
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Conservative Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Conservative Mennonite Conference dates back to 1910 when it was originally founded as the Conservative Amish Mennonite Conference. The group changed its name in 1954, dropping the word "Amish," and they usually go by their shortened name, "CMC." The group strives to glorify God by providing leaders and congregations with worship, teachings, fellowship, and service. The group's mission is to mature and multiply churches both locally and globally.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Conservative Yearly Meetings of Friends
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Primary_Label: Convention of Original Free Will Baptists
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Convention of Original Free Will Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Members of the Convention of Original Free Will Baptists are predominately based in North Carolina. After forming in 1961 as the North Carolina Convention of the National Association of Free Will Baptists Inc., these congregations withdrew from the national body primarily over disputes over locations for key activities.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Converge Worldwide
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Converge Worldwide dates back to 1852, when it was founded among Swedish-Americans as the Baptist General Conference. In 2008, the group changed its name to Converge Worldwide.

Primary_Label: Cooperative General Association of Free Will Baptists
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Cooperative General Association of Free Will Baptists
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | After about half Benjamin Randall's Free Will Baptists merged with the Northern Baptist Convention in 1910-1911, a group of remnant churches remained. These groups reorganized into the Cooperative General Association of Free Will Baptists in 1916.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Coptic Orthodox Church
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Coptic Orthodox Church is one of the so-called Oriental Orthodox Churches (the others being Armenian, Ethiopian, Eritrean, Malankara-Indian, and Syrian) which, unlike Eastern Orthodox Churches, recognize the validity of theological decisions of only the first three Ecumenical Councils. The Copts are a Christian ethno-religious group indigenous to North Africa (mostly Egypt). With headquarters in Cairo, Egypt, the Coptic Orthodox Church – also known as the Coptic Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria – is one of the ancient Oriental Christian Churches. It is believed that Christianity was brought to Egypt by St. Mark the Evangelist around the year 43 AD. The most venerated Coptic saint, St. Anthony the Great, is widely recognized as the founder of Christian monasticism. Early Coptic monks from the Egyptian desert provided the first models for the Christian monastic tradition at large. Coptic immigration to the United States from Egypt began after the coup of 1952, when Gamal Abdel Nasser overthrew the monarchy of King Farouk and established a republic. Nasser promoted Arab nationalism, while Copts viewed themselves as pre-Arab, the original people of Egypt who had lived there well before the Arab conquests of the seventh century. Early Coptic immigrants settled in New Jersey and New York. The first Coptic Church in America, St. Mark’s Coptic Church in Jersey City, NJ, opened its doors in 1970. A distinct feature of the Coptic Church administration in America is that there is no single Church center. In addition to seven American dioceses with local Bishops, the Coptic Pope Tawadros III maintains direct authority from Egypt over many parishes in the United States. Because a strong emphasis on the monastic tradition has always been essential to the Coptic Church, it is no surprise that despite a relatively short history of this church in America, several Coptic monasteries exist in the United States today. An important spiritual center of the American Coptic community is St. Anthony Monastery in Newberry Springs, CA (www.stantonymonastery.org). Situated in the Mojave Desert, St. Anthony was founded in 1973, only three years after the first Coptic parish was established. St. Anthony was also the first Coptic monastery outside of Egypt to be officially recognized by the Synod of the Coptic Orthodox Church. Unlike many other Orthodox Churches in America, in the Coptic Church there is no formal process set up for becoming a priest. Oftentimes the candidates for ordination lack the formal theological education and degrees (such as Master of Divinity) that are considered standard in most Orthodox Churches. Rather, entering the priestly vocation is an organic process that starts at the parish level when one member is chosen by the parish for future ordination. The Coptic Church continues to grow rapidly in America. Between 2010 and 2020 alone, the number of parishes increased from 170 to 292, while the membership has nearly doubled (from 92,000 to 180,000).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Covenant Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Covenant Reformed Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Covenant Reformed Presbyterian Church (CRPC) was formed in 1998 from churches and ministers that wanted an ecclesiastical home but that were not content with the larger or smaller conservative Presbyterian bodies. As a result, a new denomination was formed that draws from both the Presbyterian and Reformed traditions. The CRPC subscribes to the original Westminster Confession of Faith and Catechisms.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Cross and the Lotus
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Cross and the Lotus dates back to 1925, when Reverend Mother Yogacharya Hamilton met her guru, Paramahansa Yogananda. She was ordained by him in 1950 and given the title of "yogacharya" (master, or teacher of yoga) in 1951 - one of six people given this title by Yogananda - and the only woman. After traveling to India where Hamilton obtained the complete realization of God, she returned to her hometown of Seattle, Washington, where David R. Hickenbottom became her student. She ordained him in 1984 and later gave him the title of yogacharya. He has continued her work since her death in 1991.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Cumberland Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Cumberland Presbyterian Church began on the Western frontier in the early 19th century. In 1906, the main body of the church reunited with the Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A., now an integral part of the Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.). Those that did not participate in the merger reorganized themselves as the continuing Cumberland Presbyterian Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Cumberland Presbyterian Church in America
Description: Black Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | On May 14, 1874, black members of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church, many of whom had roots in slavery, met at Murfreesboro, Tennessee, and organized what was for many years called the Second Cumberland Presbyterian Church. In 1991, the association ceased efforts to reunify with the parent Cumberland Presbyterian Church; however, these efforts were resumed in 2012.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Cumberland Valley Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Primary_Label: Dagom Gaden Tensung Ling Buddhist Monastery
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dagom Gaden Tensung Ling Buddhist Monastery
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dagom Gaden Tensung Ling Buddhist Monastery was founded by two Tibetan Buddhist teachers: Ven. Kyabje Dagom Rinpoche and Ven. Gonsar Rinpoche. The Monastery acts as a traditional monastery for monks of their lineage, but is also open to lay people of all nationalities. The mission of the monastery is to preserve Buddha's teachings that were transmitted by the Gelugpa lineage of Tibetan Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Danish Evangelical Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Danish Evangelical Lutheran Church was formed in 1872 and changed its name in 1953 to the American Evangelical Lutheran Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Dariusleut Hutterite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Communal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Datta Yoga Center USA
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Datta Yoga Center USA was founded by Sri Ganapati Sachchidananda Swamiji. He teaches kriya yoga as the way to realize the one reality as referred to in his teachings (Advaita Vedanta). His teachings have been described as "universal and unconstrained by religious dogma.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Dawn Bible Students Association
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | In the late 1920s, a group of members of the Pastoral Bible Institute withdrew and launched an expansive radio ministry, which led to the formation of a new national organization proclaiming Christ's second coming, the Dawn Bible Students Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dechen (UK)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Dechen was established in 1975 by Karma Thinley Rinpoche and Lama Jampa Thaye and grew into an international association of Sakya and Karma Kagyu Buddhist Centers in Europe, the United Kingdom, the United States, and Mexico. The association provides instruction in Buddhism and meditation and supports an active nunnery.

Primary_Label: Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America can be traced to work organized in 1889 by Isaac Peters and Aaron Walls. In that year, they joined their congregations to create the United Mennonite Brethren of North America, though the name was soon changed to Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America. In 1937, the name was changed to Evangelical Mennonite Brethren Conference, and in 1987 it adopted its present name, the Fellowship of Evangelical Bible Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Defenseless Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Defenseless Mennonite Church grew out of a spiritual awakening among the Amish of Indiana in 1866. Its founder was Bishop Henry Egly. This group adopted the name "The Defenseless Mennonite Church" in 1898, and became known as the Evangelical Mennonite Church in 1948. In 2003, it took on the name Fellowship of Evangelical Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Devi Mandir
Description: Other | Other Groups | Devi Mandir, also known as the Temple of the Divine Mother, was founded by Shree Maa and Swami Satyananda. Shree Maa teaches devotion to the deities through puja, as well as behavioral code that grows out of divine life. Individuals are encouraged to take refuge in God, cultivate wisdom, develop discernment, and allow their actions to manifest love.

Primary_Label: Dhamma Dena Monastery and Desert Vipassana Community
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dhamma Dena Monastery and Desert Vipassana Community
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Dhamma Dena Monastery and Desert Vipassana Community was founded by Ruth Denison. The center was blessed by Mahasi Sayadaw and his monks, and has served as a Zen and Vipassana meditation center since the 1970s.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dharma Drum Mountain Buddhist Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dharma Drum Mountain Buddhist Association was founded in 1994 by Master Sheng Yen, an established teacher and practitioner of Chan Buddhism. After Master Sheng Yen's death in 2009, the association carried on his mission through working with youth, seeking interfaith conversations, and promoting spiritual, ecological, lifestyle, and social environmentalism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dharma Friendship Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dharma Friendship Foundation was founded in 1985 by Alan Wallace. The association followed the teaching of Gen Lamrimpa until his death and promotes the values of "peace, nonviolence, and compassion." Currently, the foundation is led by Yangsi Rinpoche.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dharma Rain Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dharma Rain Zen Center was established for lay practice by Kyogen Carlson and Gykuko Carlson, a married couple who were Zen priests. The center offers morning and evening meditation, classes, workshops, and residential practice. The center is nationally known for it children's programs as well as the Dharma School.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dharma Realm Buddhist Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dharma Realm Buddhist Association was founded as the Sino-American Buddhist Association by the disciples of Tripitaka Master Xuan Hua. The association is based in the Chan school of Chinese Buddhism, but includes teaching from all five main varieties of Chinese Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dharma Sangha
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Dharma Sangha was founded by Richard Baker Roshi after leaving the Zen Center of San Francisco. There are two centers of activity in the United States; however, the center in Colorado, opened in 1988, became the main focus of the Sangha's activity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dharmata Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dharmata Foundation was founded by Anam Thubten, a Myingmapa teacher. The foundation was created for students who wanted to follow his path and as a vehicle to carry forward his teachings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dhongak Tharling Dharma Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Dhongak Tharling Dharma Center is based in New Orleans and was founded in 1994 by the Ven. Lama Ngawang Tsultrim. It is a Rigdzin Dakgyud Nyingma Center.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Dhyanyoga Centers
Description: Other | Other Groups | Dhyanyoga Centers was founded to share the teachings of Dhyanyogi Mahant Madhusudandasji Maharaj. His teachings emphasize meditation (dhyan), or raja yoga, and kundalini yoga. After Madhusudandasji's death in 1994, Shri Anandi Ma was named his "spiritual heir," and she travels the world offering meditation and yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Diamond Sangha
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Diamond Sangha was founded by Robert Aitken and his wife, Anne Aitken, in Hawaii and is part of the Sanbo Kyodan (Order of the Three Treasures). Robert Aitken Roshi retired in 1996 and was succeeded by Nelson Foster. The Honolulu Diamond Sangha has one center on the island of O'ahu named Palolo Zen Center (PZC). The center has daily zezen, samu, one- to eight-day sessions, and opportunities for residential practice.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Diamond Way Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Diamond Way is considered to be the most "skillful" method of the Buddha, and was established by the efforts of Lama Ole Nydahl and his wife, Hannah Nydahl. The group has a democratic structure, and members share the responsibility of guiding meditation, answering questions, and giving teachings. The daily activities for achieving richness and clarity of the mind include verifiable nondogmatic teachings, meditation, and the means to solidify the levels of awareness.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Diocese of the Holy Cross
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Divine Life Society
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Divine Life Society was founded by Swami Sivananda Saraswati. He formed the group to serve humanity and with the main goal of remolding man in the image of God. The society conducts three two-month courses throughout the year for those interested in Indian culture and to train them in the practice of yoga and Vedanta.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Christian Science > Divine Science
Description: Unclassified | Christian Science | The principles and practice of Divine Science were developed in the late nineteenth century through the work of Aletha Brooks Small, Fannie Brooks James, Nona Lovell Brooks (all sisters), and Malinda E. Cramer. When the sisters, who lived in Denver, Colorado, met Cramer, they all realized they had developed similar ideas independently. They joined forces in 1898 and organized the Divine Science College and the First Divine Science Church of Denver.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Divya Jyoti Jagrati Sansthan
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Divya Jyoti Jagrati Sansthan, also known as Divine Light Awakening, was founded by His Holiness Shri Ashutosh Maharaj Ji. The group was formed in a time of violence with a goal to promote peace. The group has developed programs focused on Krishna worship and cultural activities aimed at presenting spiritual truths through drama, dance, and celebration. They are also active with humanitarian and charitable projects.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Dorje Ling
