Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Evangelical Adventists
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | What is now known as the Adventist movement traces its beginning to the 1830s and the preaching of the imminent return of Christ by Baptist preacher William Miller. Miller predicted Christ's return in 1843, later revised to 1844. When Christ failed to return, believers gathered at a conference in Albany, New York, to consider their future. The amorphous movement took the name Evangelical Adventists and over the next decade several distinct groups began to coalesce and then assume formal organized form, though a variety of otherwise independent evangelists, writers, and periodical publishers continued to exist through the end of the century. Adventists were initially divided by the acceptance of many of sabbatarianism (the belief that Saturday, not Sunday, was the proper day for Christians to gather and worship). Sabbatarians then split over the acceptance of the prophecies of Ellen G. White, whose followers founded the Seventh-day Adventist Church. Among the Adventists who continued to gather for worship on Sunday, a series of new dates were proposed for the second coming, each date provoking the organization of those who accepted it.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > Evangelical and Reformed Church
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | In 1934, the majority of the Reformed Church in the United States merged with the Evangelical Synod to form the Evangelical and Reformed Church. In 1957, the Evangelical and Reformed Church merged with the Congregational and Christian Churches to form the United Church of Christ.

Primary_Label: Evangelical Association of Reformed and Congregational Christian Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Evangelical Association of Reformed and Congregational Christian Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Evangelical Association of Reformed and Congregational Christian Churches was incorporated in 1998. It began forming a few years before that, when members of the United Church of Christ became disgruntled with the spread of more liberal theology in that denomination. The Evangelical Association of Reformed and Congregational Christian Churches is a voluntary association providing partnership, resources, and unity to likeminded congregations who may or may not be part of formal denominations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Evangelical Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Evangelical Church was formed in 1922 through a reunion of the Evangelical Association and a group of churches that had left Association in 1894. It merged with the United Brethren in Christ Church in 1946 to form The Evangelical United Brethren Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Independent Fundamentalist > Evangelical Church Alliance
Description: Unclassified | Independent Fundamentalist | The Evangelical Church Alliance (ECA) is an interdenominational organization of ministers who have united to promote evangelical Christianity throughout the world. The ECA began in 1887 as the World's Faith Missionary Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Evangelical Church of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Evangelical Church of North America was founded in 1968 by former members of the Evangelical United Brethren who rejected that church's merger into the United Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Evangelical Congregational Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Evangelical Congregational Church was formed by members of the United Evangelical Church who did not approve of the merger of that church with the Evangelical Association in 1922 to form the Evangelical Church, which is now a constituent part of the United Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Evangelical Covenant Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Evangelical Covenant Church, a Swedish Pietist church, was founded in 1885 as a merger of the Swedish Lutheran Mission Synod and the Swedish Lutheran Ansgarius Synod to form the Swedish Evangelical Mission Covenant Church of America. In 1937, the "Swedish" was dropped; in 1957, the word "mission" was dropped; and in 1983, the words "of America" were dropped.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Evangelical Free Church of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Evangelical Free Church of America was founded in 1950 by the merger of two Scandinavian independent Pietistic associations of churches which had grown out of nineteenth-century revivals: the Swedish Evangelical Free Church and the Norwegian-Danish Evangelical Free Church Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Evangelical Friends Alliance
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Evangelical Friends Alliance was founded in 1965 as an association of four autonomous Quaker groups. These groups represented the most theologically conservative element in the Friends movement. In 1990, the Evangelical Friends International superseded the Evangelical Friends Alliance.

Primary_Label: Evangelical Friends Church - Mid-America Yearly Meeting
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Evangelical Friends Church - Mid-America Yearly Meeting
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Evangelical Friends Church - Mid-America Yearly Meeting originally began as the Kansas Yearly Meeting in 1872, which formed as Friends moved into Kansas and nearby states. They took on name Mid-America Yearly meeting in 1978, though they changed to their present name in 2001.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Evangelical Friends Church International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Evangelical Friends Church International was originally founded in 1965 as the Evangelical Friends Alliance. In 1990, this group took on the name Evangelical Friends International, and in 2008 the group changed its name to Evangelical Friends Church International. The association works to provide resources and community to congregations that join it through campgrounds and schools (including Friends University, Malone University, and Houston Graduate School of Theology). It changed its name to the Evangelical Friends Church International in 2008.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Evangelical Friends Church, Eastern Region
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Evangelical Friends Church, Eastern Region was formed in 1813 as the Ohio Yearly Meeting of Friends. It took its present name in 1971 and follows a conservative holiness faith.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Evangelical Friends International
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | In 1990, the Evangelical Friends International superseded the Evangelical Friends Alliance. It is theologically conservative and is a member of the National Association of Evangelicals. In 2008, this group changed its name to Evangelical Friends Church International.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Evangelical Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Evangelical Lutheran Church, originally known as the Norwegian Lutheran Church from 1917-1946, was the result of a 1917 merger of the different Norwegian Lutheran bodies established in America in the nineteenth century: the United Norwegian Church, the Norwegian Synod, and the Hauge Synod. In 1960, this group merged with the United Evangelical Lutheran Church and the American Lutheran Church (1930-1960) to form the American Lutheran Church (1960-1987).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Evangelical Lutheran Church in America
Description: Mainline Protestant | Lutheran | The Evangelical Lutheran Church in America continues several varied streams of Lutheran church life introduced to America during the Colonial era. The ELCA was formally constituted in 1988 as a merger of the Lutheran Church in America, the Association of Evangelical Lutheran Churches, and the American Lutheran Church.

Primary_Label: Evangelical Lutheran Conference and Ministerium of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Evangelical Lutheran Conference and Ministerium of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: Evangelical Lutheran Diocese of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Evangelical Lutheran Diocese of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Evangelical Lutheran Synod
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Evangelical Lutheran Synod, formerly known as the Norwegian Synod of the American Evangelical Lutheran Church, was formed at Lake Mills, Iowa, in 1918 by a group of forty pastors and laymen who declined to enter the merger of other Norwegian Lutherans, deciding instead to establish an independent synod. The present name was assumed in 1957.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Evangelical Mennonite Brethren Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Evangelical Mennonite Brethren Conference can be traced to work organized in 1889 by Isaac Peters and Aaron Walls. In that year, they joined their congregations to create the United Mennonite Brethren of North America, though the name was soon changed to Defenseless Mennonite Brethren of Christ in North America. In 1937, the name was changed to Evangelical Mennonite Brethren Conference, and in 1987 it adopted its present name, the Fellowship of Evangelical Bible Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Evangelical Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Evangelical Mennonite Church grew out of a spiritual awakening among the Amish of Indiana in 1866. Its founder was Bishop Henry Egly. This group adopted the name "The Defenseless Mennonite Church" in 1898, and became known as the Evangelical Mennonite Church in 1948. In 2003, it took on the name Fellowship of Evangelical Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Evangelical Methodist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Evangelical Methodist Church is a conservative evangelical church founded in 1946 by a group of former members of the Methodist Church (1939-1968) under the leadership of Dr. J. H. Hamblen. They protested what they saw as the extreme liberalism of the Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Evangelical Presbyterian Church (1956)
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | Evangelical Presbyterian Church was the name taken by the larger segment of the Bible Presbyterian Church following the split of that church in 1956. The name for the larger group had been adopted in 1961 to avoid confusion with Dr. Carl McIntire's smaller group. In 1965, this group merged with the Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America, General Synod to form the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Evangelical Presbyterian Church (1981)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Evangelical Presbyterian Church was formed by conservative Presbyterians in 1981 as the two major branches of American Presbyterianism prepared to merge (1983). The founders rejected the theological liberalism they found in their parent bodies.

Primary_Label: Evangelical Protestant Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > Evangelical Protestant Church of North America
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | The Evangelical Protestant Church of North America, known as the General Conference of Evangelical Protestant Churches since 2000, dates back to the German Evangelical Protestants of late eighteenth century. The group had no denominational structure, however, until a movement to establish one began in 1885. In 1912, the German reference in their name was dropped and they took on the name Evangelical Protestant Church of North America. They participated in a merger into the Congregational Churches in 1924, but retained a separate identity, and eventually reorganized in 1999-2000.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > Evangelical Synod of North America
Description: Unclassified | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | In 1872, the German Evangelical Synod of North America formed as a merger between the German Evangelical Synod of the West, the United Synod of the Northwest, and the United Synod of the East. The word "German" was dropped from the name in 1927, and in 1934, this group merged with the Reformed Church in the United States to form the Evangelical and Reformed Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Evangelical United Brethren
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Evangelical United Brethren Church was the result of a merger between the United Brethren in Christ Church and the Evangelical Church in 1946. In 1968, this group merged with the Methodist Church to form the United Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Evangelical Wesleyan Church
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Evangelical Wesleyan Church was formed in 1963 as the merger of the Evangelical Wesleyan Church of North America and the Midwest Holiness Association. The group follows Free Methodism and is opposed to compromising on doctrines or standards.

Primary_Label: Evangelical Wesleyan Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Evangelical Wesleyan Church of North America
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Evangelical Wesleyan Church of North America was founded in 1958 near Centerville, Pennsylvania, by former members of the Free Methodist Church who desired a stricter interpretation of personal moral codes (e.g., women's hairstyles and the length of dresses). In 1963, this group would merge with the Midwest Holiness Association to form the Evangelical Wesleyan Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Evangelistic Church of God
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Evangelistic Church of God is a Pentecostal church founded in 1949 by Norman L. Chase and former members of the Church of God (Cleveland, Tennessee).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Every Nation
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Falun Gong (Falun Dafa)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Falun Gong (or Falun Dafa, the Great Way of the Law Wheel") was founded in 1992 as a qigong practice group. The founder, Li Hongzhi, is regarded as a master of qigong and the only instructor of the group. The group teaches members to lead lives of moral rectitude and emphasizes the abandonment of selfishness and problematic attachments.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Federated Church
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Federation of Reformed Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Federation of Reformed Churches began in 1990 in Fairfax, Virginia, as the Fellowship of Reformed Churches. In 1992, the name of the denomination changed to its present name, the Federation of Reformed Churches. The group describes its founding motivation as a response to Biblical beliefs and historic practices falling into disfavor; this led a group of pastors to seek a new denomination with the freedom to uphold these beliefs and practices. The church today seeks to serve local churches and allows them to express connection to the Catholic church.

Primary_Label: Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America
Description: Other | Other Groups | After two separate failed attempts to form a united Zoroastrian Association in the States, in 1986 all recorded Zoroastrian associations in the U.S. adopted the constitution of the Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America. This process began in 1985 at the Fifth North American Congress. This association, officially formed in 1987, provides cohesion, cultural strength, and tax-exempt status (as of 1989) to the Zoroastrian community in the United States.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Fellowship of Christian Assemblies
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Fellowship of Evangelical Bible Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Fellowship of Evangelical Bible Churches can be traced to work organized in 1889 by Isaac Peters and Aaron Walls. Prior to 1987, the group was known as the Evangelical Mennonite Brethren Conference.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Fellowship of Evangelical Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Fellowship of Evangelical Churches grew out of a spiritual awakening among the Amish of Indiana in 1866. Its founder was Bishop Henry Egly. This group adopted the name "The Defenseless Mennonite Church" in 1898, and became known as the Evangelical Mennonite Church in 1948. It took its present name in 2003.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Fellowship of Fundamental Bible Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Fellowship of Fundamental Bible Churches was formed by former members of the Methodist Protestant Church in New Jersey who opposed their church’s merger into the Methodist Church in 1939. It was founded as the Bible Protestant Church and adopted its present name in 1985.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Fellowship of Grace Brethren Churches
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Fellowship of Grace Brethren Churches was founded in the 1930s by former members of the Brethren Church (Ashland, Ohio). It was known as the National Fellowship of Brethren Churches from 1939-1976, at which point it took this name until 2017. In 2017, the group changed its name to Charis Fellowship.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Finnish Evangelical Lutheran Church (Suomi Synod)
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Finnish Evangelical Lutheran Church was formed in 1890 in Calumet, Michigan. It used the liturgy of the Church of Finland.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Fire Baptized Holiness Church (Wesleyan)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Fire Baptized Holiness Church (Wesleyan) was established in 1890 by members of the Methodist Episcopal Church of southeastern Kansas. Originally the Southeast Kansas Fire Baptized Holiness Association, the group renamed itself to the Fire Baptized Holiness Church (Wesleyan) in 1945, taking its present title, Bible Holiness Church, in 1995.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Fire-Baptized Holiness Assocation
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The first fire-baptized congregation in the United States was organized at Olmitz, Iowa, in 1895, which prompted several more congregations to form across nine U.S. states in the following years (with two congregations also in Canada). Formal organization of the Fire-Baptized Holiness Association came in 1898 in Anderson, South Carolina. In 1911, this group would merge with the Pentecostal Holiness Church, retaining the name of the latter group.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > First Church of the Nazarene
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The First Church of the Nazarene was founded in 1895 as a single congregation in Los Angeles, California, by Phineas Bresee. Bresee was a former Methodist pastor who had just left the Peniel Mission when he founded the church. After this group grew into a small denomination, it merged in 1907 with the Association of Pentecostal Churches in America to form the Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > First Colored Methodist Protestant Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The First Colored Methodist Protestant Church was formed about 1840 when members of the African Methodist Episcopal Church rejected episcopal leadership and reorganized along the principles of the Methodist Protestant Church.

Primary_Label: First Congregational Methodist Church of the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > First Congregational Methodist Church of the U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The First Congregational Methodist Church of the U.S.A. was founded in 1941 by former members of the Congregational Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > First Zen Institute of America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Formerly the Buddhist Society of America, the First Zen Institute gained its current name in 1944. The lay-led association has a number of publications and offers free introductions to Rinzai Zen.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Fivefold Path Inc./Agnihortra Worldwide
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | FiveFold Path Inc. was founded by Vasant Paranjpe, who had a divine command to come to the United States and teach Kriya yoga, also known as the Fivefold Path. The teachings of the Fivefold Path contain five steps to the purification of the mind. The five steps include Agnihotra, Daan, Tapa, Karma, and Swadhyaya.

Primary_Label: Foundation for the Preservation of the Mahayana Tradition (FPMT)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Foundation for the Preservation of the Mahayana Tradition (FPMT)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Foundation for the Preservation of the Mahayana Tradition (FPMT) was founded by Lama Thubten Yeshe and Lama Thubten Zopa Rinpoche. FPMT has become a worldwide movement and is also active in translating Buddhist texts into English. The foundation is dedicated to preserving and spreading Mahayana Buddhism.

Primary_Label: Free Christian Zion Church of Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Free Christian Zion Church of Christ
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Free Christian Zion Church of Christ is a predominantly black Methodist church founded in 1905 by the Rev. E. D. Brown.

Primary_Label: Free Church of God in Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Free Church of God in Christ
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Free Church of God in Christ is a predominantly black Pentecostal Church founded in 1915 by J. H. Morris and former members of the Church of God in Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Free Church of Scotland (Continuing)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Free Church of Scotland (Continuing) was formed from the Disruption of 1843 when several Scottish congregations split from the state church, gaining the power to appoint their own pastors. After internal conflict, the association was reconstituted in 2000 from the Free Church of Scotland to The Free Church of Scotland (Continuing). This smaller, remaining constituency claims to hold true to the original intentions of the 1843 church.

Primary_Label: Free Magyar Reformed Church in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Free Magyar Reformed Church in America
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Free Magyar Reformed Church in America was founded in 1922 by Hungarian congregations that did not follow the majority into a merger with the Reformed Church in the United States. In 1958, they adopted the name Hungarian Reformed Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Free Methodist Church – USA
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Free Methodist Church – USA, previously called the Free Methodist Church of North America, was founded in 1860 as the result of a split in the Genesee Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church.

Primary_Label: Free Presbyterian Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Free Presbyterian Church of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Free Presbyterian Church of North America began in 1951 in Ulster, Northern Ireland, but first arrived in North America in 1976 (in Toronto, Canada). The group originally formed as a response to perceived modernist threats against the traditional Reformation stance of the church. In May of 2004, the churches in the United States and Canada formally presented a petition to the Irish presbytery to form the North American churches into their own presbytery.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: Free Reformed Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Free Reformed Church of North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Free Reformed Church of North America was started by post-World War II immigrants whose roots were in the Christian Reformed Churches in the Netherlands. The first churches were formed in 1950, with two U.S. congregations joining in the 1960s. Most churches within this organization are located in Canada, which is where a synod of the churches meet annually. A smaller number of churches are located in the United States, and the church also supports mission work in Cubulco, Guatemala.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Friends (Quakers)
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Friends General Conference
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Friends General Conference, founded in 1900, brought together several yearly meetings of the Society of Friends that followed the unprogrammed and free spirit of the Quakers associated with Friends minister Elias Hicks.

Primary_Label: Friends General Conference and Friends United Meeting, dually aligned meetings
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Friends General Conference and Friends United Meeting, dually aligned meetings
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Friends United Meeting
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Friends United Meeting was founded by the more programmed and "orthodox" tradition among Friends in 1902. At first known as the Five Years Meeting of Friends, it assumed its present name in 1965.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Full Gospel Assemblies International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | Full Gospel Assemblies International is a Pentecostal body started in 1972 under the leadership of Dr. Charles E. Strauser.

Primary_Label: Full Gospel Baptist Church Fellowship International
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Full Gospel Baptist Church Fellowship International
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | The Full Gospel Baptist Church Fellowship International was founded out of a 1994 conference of Baptist churches with Pentecostal convictions. Originally led by Bishop Paul Morton Sr., the episcopal structure of the association is now under the leadership of Bishop Joseph Walker III.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Full Gospel Christian Assemblies International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Full Gospel Church Association, Inc.
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Full Gospel Church Association, Inc., was organized by the Reverend Dennis W. Thorn at Amarillo, Texas, in 1952 for the purpose of bringing together a number of small, independent Pentecostal churches and missions, most of them with fewer than 100 members in the South and Southwest.

Primary_Label: Full Gospel Fellowship of Churches and Ministers International
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Full Gospel Fellowship of Churches and Ministers International
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Full Gospel Fellowship of Churches and Ministers International was founded in 1962 by a group of independent Pentecostal ministers under the leadership of Gordon Lindsay. The fellowship has a special emphasis upon divine healing. The organization briefly operated under the name The Fellowship Today before adopting its present name as The Fellowship Network in 2016. Individual churches, groups of churches, and organizations of churches may be recognized with the fellowship.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Fundamental Baptist Fellowship Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Fundamental Baptist Fellowship Association was founded in 1962 and incorporated in 1975. The group's purpose is to promote fellowship between Bible-believing Baptist churches, foster evangelism, and spread the Gospel through education, missions, and testimonies. At their 2018 annual conference, the group dropped "Fundamental" from its name, becoming the Baptist Fellowship Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Fundamental Methodist Conference, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Fundamental Methodist Conference, also called the Fundamental Methodist Church, was formed in 1942 by former members of the Methodist Church who withdrew from the church following the merger in 1939. The new group organized under the leadership of Reverend Roy Keith, and was officially established in 1944 after being joined by other congregations.

Primary_Label: Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (FLDS) is a conservative Latter-day Saints group and the largest of the polygamy-practicing groups. Doctrinally, they are similar to the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints prior to that group's denunciation of polygamy, but they also have rejected most of the Church's changes introduced through the twentieth century. The origin of the church dates back to a council in 1929, though the present name was assumed in 1991 under the leadership of Rulon Jeffs. After Rulon Jeffs passed away in 2002, his son, Warren Jeffs, became the group's leader and married all of his father's wives. Since then, the church center moved to Eldorado, Texas, and became known as the Yearning for Zion Ranch. However, in the years since then, the church experienced numerous raids by governmental authorities, which led to arrests, convictions, and imprisonments of most of the leadership, including Warren Jeffs, who is serving a life sentence. The group is still active, and Warren Jeffs leads the group with communications from prison, though the future of the group is unclear.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Furnace Mountain Zen Retreat Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Furnace Mountain Zen Retreat Zen Center was founded by Zen Master Dae Gak. Dae Gak received dharma transmission in 1994 and continues to lead many of the retreats at Furnace Mountain. He also travels widely to teach across the United States and world.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Gaden for the West
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Gaden for the West was founded by Zasep Tulku Rinpoche and his students in order to support and to foster the study of Gelugpa Tibetan Buddhism in the West. The goals of the group are to organize programs for students of all levels, encourage the exploration of generosity, and arrange a wide range of Sutra and Tantric retreats.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Gangaji Foundation (Spiritual)
Description: Other | Other Groups | Gangaji Foundation was founded by Antoinette Varner after meeting Harivansh Lal Poonja, who was called Poonjaji by his students. Poonjaji confirmed her self-realization and gave her the name Gangaji. He then instructed her to carry his message throughout the West. His message was that human beings are pure consciousness - making them absolutely free - and that spiritual life is not about obtaining freedom, but instead realizing that one is already free.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Gedatsu Church of America
Description: Other | Other Groups | Gedatsu was originally founded by Seiken Okano (posthumously named Gedatsu Kong) in Japan in 1929. It is a nonsectarian spiritual philosophy, though it is influenced by an ancient Japanese tradition, Shugendo, that derives from Shintoism, Buddhism, and other native spiritual practices. In America, Gedatsu began when Kenyu Kiyota - a disciple of Gedatsu Kongo - began teaching Gedatsu in Japanese internment camps during World War II. Kiyota then persuaded Eizan Kishida, another disciple, to join her mission of spreading Gedatsu, and Kishida established the first Gedatsu Church of America in 1950, which was incorporated the following year. Gedatsu offers a way to enlightenment through the development of wisdom, the purification of emotion, and the improvement of willpower. Central to all worship is kuyo, which is humbly giving gratitude to all sources to which one is indebted.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > General Assemblies of Apostolic Assemblies
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The General Assemblies of Apostolic Assemblies was formed in January of 1917 after a number of Oneness ministers were forced out of the Assemblies of God. These ministers included D. C. O. Opperman and Howard A. Goss, who were two early leaders in the Apostolic movement. The group immediately hit organizational difficulties due to the onset of World War I. Specifically, because they were such a new organization, their ministers were not exempt from military service and could not receive clergy rates on railroads, which were the main method of clergy travel at the time. As a result, this group merged into the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World in 1918.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist
Description: A structural family category within the Unclassified tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > General Assembly of Spiritualists
Description: Unclassified | Spiritualist | The General Assembly of Spiritualists was organized in 1897 in New York as the New York State Association of Spiritualists. In 1914, it changed its constitution, bylaws, and name (to the present) to conform to the laws of New York, which also marked the first recognition of Spiritualism as a religion.

Primary_Label: General Association of Davidian Seventh-Day Adventists
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > General Association of Davidian Seventh-Day Adventists
Description: Unclassified | Unclassified | The General Association of Davidian Seventh-Day Adventists was formed originally as the General Association of the Shepherd's Rod Seventh-day Adventists in 1934. This was under the leadership and ministry of Victor T. Houteff. In 1942, when needing to register the organization with the state during World War II, Houteff changed the name to the General Association of Davidian Seventh-Day Adventists.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > General Association of General Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The General Association of General Baptists dates back to the 1820s and the ministry of Benoni Stinson. After Stinson was baptized (1820) and ordained (1821) in Kentucky, he moved to Indiana and began his own church after the Wabash United Baptist Association would not tolerate his Arminian freewill views. This church grew, and soon other churches with Arminian perspectives were formed. In 1824, churches that followed Stinson's teachings organized as the Liberty Association of General Baptists, which then spread south and west. In 1870, the general association was formed.

Primary_Label: General Association of Regular Baptist Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > General Association of Regular Baptist Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The General Association of Regular Baptist Churches grew out of the Baptist Bible Union, which was created by fundamentalist members of the Northern Baptist Convention in 1922. In 1932, the Baptist Bible Union gave way to the General Association of Regular Baptist Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > General Association of Separatist Baptists
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The General Association of Separatist Baptists was formed in 1912 by some independent associations of Separate Baptists in Christ. The origins of this group date back to the eighteenth century and the First Great Awakening.

Primary_Label: General Association of the Shepherd's Rod Seventh-day Adventists
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > General Association of the Shepherd's Rod Seventh-day Adventists
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | The General Association of the Shepherd's Rod Seventh-day Adventists is one of several small groups to grow out of the work of Victor Houteff, a member of the Seventh-day Adventist Church who developed unique views on the imminent end of the worldly order, and called for a return to the pacifist stance of the early Seventh-day Adventist movement. The most famous branch of the Houteff movement was the Branch Davidians, which in 1993 had a violent confrontation with government agents at Waco, Texas, that led to the death of most of its members. The other branches of the Houteff movement were not related to the Branch Davidians at that time.

Primary_Label: General Church of the New Jerusalem
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > General Church of the New Jerusalem
Description: Other Christians | Spiritualist | The General Church of the New Jerusalem (often called "the General Church" or "the New Church") was founded in 1882 by William Benade and other former members of the General Convention of the New Jerusalem.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > General Conference Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The General Conference Mennonite Church was founded in 1860 through the union of more liberal and innovative Mennonite congregations under the leadership of John H. Oberholtzer. In 2002, it merged with the Mennonite Church to create the Mennonite Church USA.

Primary_Label: General Conference of the Church of God (Seventh Day)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > General Conference of the Church of God (Seventh Day)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | The General Conference of the Church of God (Seventh Day) is a sabbatarian church representative of those Adventists of the mid-nineteenth century who rejected the visions and messages of Ellen G. White (founder of the Seventh-day Adventist Church). More specifically, the church traces its roots to believers in Michigan and Iowa in the late 1850s. Some of these Adventists associated together around a periodical, called The Hope of Israel (later known as the Bible Advocate), which was published by the church in Michigan in 1863. The group grew during the nineteenth century, was formally organized in 1884, and was incorporated in Missouri in 1899.

Primary_Label: General Conference of the Evangelical Baptist Church, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > General Conference of the Evangelical Baptist Church, Inc.
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The General Conference of the Evangelical Baptist Church was founded in 1935 as the Church of the Full Gospel, Inc., by William Howard Carter, who died in 1980.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > General Council
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The General Council of the Evangelical Lutheran Church of America, also known by its shorter name of the General Council, formed in 1867 after severing its connection with the General Synod.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > General Six-Principle Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The General Six-Principle Baptists, one of the earliest of Baptist organizations in America, was founded in 1652 in Rhode Island. It draws its name from Hebrews 6:1-2. After its last congregation became an independent Baptist congregation in the 1990s, the denomination virtually ceased to exist. However, the movement was reorganized in 2001, and in 2003 the new organization was officially named the General Association of Six-Principle Baptist Churches, Inc.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > General Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Evangelical Lutheran General Synod of the United States of America, or simply the General Synod, was formed in Hagerstown, Maryland, in 1820.

Primary_Label: Georgian Orthodox Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Georgian Orthodox Church in North America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Georgian Orthodox Church is one of the oldest Christian Churches in the world. Headquartered in Tbilisi, Republic of Georgia, it is the country’s dominant religious institution (85% of the population are Orthodox Christians) and is also highly influential in both the public and political spheres. Christianity had become the state religion of Georgia in 326 AD due to the missionary activity of St. Nino, given the epithet of “Equal to the Apostles.” She came from Jerusalem and was a close relative of St. George, another patron saint of Georgia. Under St. Nino’s influence, Georgian Queen Nana and King Mirian converted to Christianity and asked Byzantine Emperor Constantine to send clergy to Iberia to baptize the nation. Monasteries have flourished in Georgia since the sixth century, serving as important educational and cultural centers. An emphasis on monasticism remains a significant feature of the Georgian Orthodox Church today. The Georgian Orthodox Church follows the same doctrine and teachings as other Eastern Orthodox Churches, but it also retains many distinct ethnic traditions. During the baptism of a baby (natloba), for instance, the godfather (natlia) plays a very important role. He first cuts the hair and nails of the newborn. By doing this, it is believed that the qualities and talents of the godfather are transmitted to the child. The documented Georgian presence in America can be traced to 1890, when Buffalo Bill Cody hired twelve Georgian horsemen to perform with his Wild Congress of Rough Riders. The first wave of immigration from Georgia was result of the Soviet invasion of Georgia in 1921, when about 200 Georgian refugees came to the United States. The second wave of Georgian emigrees arrived in the wake of WWII by virtue of the Displaced Persons Act of 1948 and the Refugee Act of 1953. The third wave of immigration was in the late 1980s-1990s. It was sparked by the breakup of the Soviet Union in 1991, and by the ensuing political turmoil and deep economic crisis experienced by the newly independent Georgia in the early 1990s. Yet the Georgian-American community remains relatively small in numbers and highly dispersed across the United States. Until recently, Georgians in America attended - or even served as clergy - in the other Orthodox churches. The first distinctly Georgian Orthodox parish with Georgian as a language of worship was opened in 2009: the Atskuri St. Mary Church in Philadelphia, PA. Later, the North American Eparchy (Diocese) of the Georgian Orthodox Church was formed. The Diocesan Center is in St. David the Builder Georgian Orthodox Monastery in Ashley, PA. In 2014, Bishop Saba (Intskirveli) was appointed the ruling Bishop of the Diocese.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Froehlich Churches > German Apostolic / Christian Apostolic Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Froehlich Churches | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > German Baptist Brethren
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Girnar Sadhana Ashram
Description: Other | Other Groups | Girnar Sadhana Ashram was established by Sri Punitachariji as a focal point for the dissemination of his of worship of Lord Dattatreya. The ashram was introduced to the United States, United Kingdom, and Germany by Shantibaba. However, in 2008, Shantibaba announced the termination of the foundation's formal operation in the United States. The ashram in India remains active.

Primary_Label: Global Country of World Peace (TM Movement)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Global Country of World Peace (TM Movement)
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Global Country of World Peace (TM Movement) was founded and incorporated in 2000 as a part of the Maharishi Foundation's push to spread Transcendental Meditation (TM) globally. The Global Country of World Peace failed to receive status as a sovereign nation but remains active as the organizing core of the TM movement. It is currently led by Dr. Tony Nader.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Global Methodist Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Global Methodist Connection formed in 2022 as a split from the United Methodist Church. The split came after three years of planning and the inability of the United Methodist Church to approve the "Protocol of Reconciliation and Grace Through Separation" that would have divided the United Methodist Church. The group is a new, theologically conservative Methodist denomination, and the split was led by the Transitional Leadership Council - a 17-member body of theologically conservative Methodist Clergy and laity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Global Organization for Divinity
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Global Organization for Divinity (GOD) was founded in 2007 by Sri Muralidhara Swamiji and flows out of Hinduism. They teach that repeating the Divine names is the best way to accomplish spiritual transformation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Gospel Mission Corps
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Gospel Mission Corps is a Holiness church founded in 1962 by Robert S. Turton III, a graduate of the Pillar of Fire Bible Seminary. It merged with the American Rescue Workers in 1980 due to similar doctrine and membership.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Gospel Spreading Church
Description: Black Protestant | Holiness | The Gospel Spreading Church was founded in 1922 by Lightfoot Solomon Michaux, a minister in the Church of Christ (Holiness). The origins of the church date back a few years prior, to 1919, when Michaux organized a series of tent meetings in Newport News, Virginia, and ultimately saw 150 people accept Christ. This group became the foundation for the Gospel Spreading Church, which broke ties with the Church of Christ (Holiness) after Michaux came into conflict with their founder, C. P. Jones.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Grace Communion International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Grace Communion International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | Grace Communion International began in 2009 as a reorganization of the Worldwide Church of God, which was originally founded in the early 1930s by Herbert Armstrong. After Armstrong's death in 1986, the organization began to change its doctrines, setting in motion a long process of transformation that would eventually culminate in a name change in 2009 to Grace Communion International. Almost a third of the group's membership was lost during this transition, as many of the members aligned with Armstrong's teachings left to form splinter groups. The organization is now in full agreement with the National Association of Evangelicals.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Independent Fundamentalist > Grace Gospel Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Independent Fundamentalist | The Grace Gospel Fellowship is an ultra-dispensationalist fundamentalist association of churches founded in 1944 by Pastor J. C. O'Hair and Charles Baker. Initially, it was only a fellowship for ministers, but was later opened to laypeople. The group provides fellowship for those who believe the truths in their doctrinal statement and promote the Gospel of the Grace of God throughout the world.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Great Plains Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Great Plains Zen Center was founded by Susan Myoyu Anderson Roshi. The Center has two foci, one in Wisconsin that is a rural retreat center and headquarters, and one in Illinois which regularly holds meditation sessions. The center is the center of a small community of Soto Zen Buddhists in the lineage of Taizan Maezumni Roshi, who was the founder of the Zen Center of Los Angeles. The Great Plains Zen Center is a member of the White Plum Asanga, which is a network of Zen centers that follow the lineage of Taizan Maezumni Roshi, and the international Zen Peacemaker Order.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Great Western Vehicle
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Great Western Vehicle was founded by Sotopanna Jhanananda, a self-ordained Western Buddhist monk. He describes the organization as a Fourth Wheel Buddhist tradition, meaning it lies outside the major Theravada, Mahayana, or Vajrayana vehicles (or traditions). The organization was formed as an internet support group for individuals who found wisdom through meditation. As the the group continued to grow, teacher-trainings, ordination programs, and meditation retreats were developed that focused on the attainment of gnosis in meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | With 376,000 adherents (2020), the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese (GOA) is by far the largest Orthodox Christian Church body in the U.S. Administratively, the GOA is a part (“eparchy”) of the worldwide Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople (headquartered in Istanbul, Turkey). The Archdiocesan Center is in New York City, NY. The first documented Greek Orthodox parish in the U.S. was the Holy Trinity Church in New Orleans, established in 1864. Between 1890 and 1920, hundreds of thousands of immigrants arrived from Greece and from the collapsing Ottoman Empire. Consequently, by 1922, the number of Greek Orthodox parishes in America had swelled to 141. These original congregations were self-sufficient and independent of any ecclesiastical authority. Typically, the local Greek community organized a “fraternity” or “society” comprised of compatriots from the same Old-World towns, regions, or islands. The society would obtain a hall, then open a church, and invite a clergyman from their Old-World hometown to emigrate and serve. The Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of North and South America was incorporated in 1921 during a visit of Meletios Metaxakis, the Archbishop of Athens, to the United States. He called the first Clergy-Laity Congress to administer the affairs of the Archdiocese, importantly setting the principle of significant lay involvement with Church administration. In 1937, the Holy Cross Seminary was founded in Brookline, MA, with the goal of training US-born clergy. In 1968, the Seminary became the graduate-level Holy Cross School of Greek Orthodox Theology, and the undergraduate Hellenic College was added. Following WWII, a generation of Greek Orthodox born in the United States began to take their place in Church leadership, but, at the same time, a new wave of economic immigration (approximately 200,000 between 1950–1970) from Greece arrived. This created an ongoing debate about the proper balance between being “Greek” and “American” in Church life. In 1955, the US Congress recognized the Greek Orthodox Church as a “major American religion.” The public profile of the Archdiocese was elevated during the tenure of Archbishop Iakovos Coucouzes (served 1959–1996). In 1965, he made headlines by marching with Martin Luther King, Jr., and other civil rights activists in Selma, Alabama. Friendly with both US political parties, Iakovos was the first American religious leader to offer a prayer at both the Democratic and Republican National Conventions. In the 1970s and 1980s, mixed marriages brought many non-Greeks into the GOA, and more parishes adopted English as the primary language of worship. But in the 1990s, during the tenure of Archbishop Spyridon Papageorge (1996–1999), the GOA experienced the opposite trend. The new leadership opposed the acculturation of the Greek-Orthodox community into “mainstream America,” while an ultraconservative monastic movement spearheaded by Elder Ephraim from Mt. Athos spread and grew quickly. Today, the Archdiocese continues to balance both its Greek and American identities. “Mixed” (Orthodox/non-Orthodox) marriages presently comprise about 65 percent of all marriages in the Archdiocese. Since 2019, Archbishop Elpidophoros (Lambriniadis) has served as head of the GOA. In 2009, the Assembly of Canonical Orthodox Bishops of the USA was established. It serves as a consultative body for all canonical Eastern Orthodox bishops in America. Per the Assembly’s bylaws, the head of the GOA holds the position of Chairman of the Assembly.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of Vasiloupolis
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of Vasiloupolis was an independent Orthodox Church body, not in communion with other Eastern Orthodox Churches. In Greek, “Vasiloupolis” means "Royal City" and referred to Queens, New York, where the Archdiocese was headquartered. It was founded in 1970 by Archimandrite Pangratios Vrionis, a former priest in the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese, who was defrocked on the charges of sodomy. During 1970-1980s the Archdiocese grew by adding parishes which adhered to the so-called “Old Calendarist” movement. The Old-Calendarists reject the “New” Gregorian calendar adopted in 1924 by the mainstream Greek Orthodox Church and use instead the “Old” (Julian) Church Calendar. They also view themselves as being traditionalist and true Orthodox. The Old Calendarists had a large-scale emigration from Greece to the United States in late 1960s because of discrimination by the official Orthodox Church of Greece, which views them as schismatics. In 2002, Pangratios Vrionis was convicted on charges of sexually abusing a 14-year-old congregant, which resulted in eventual disintegration of the Archdiocese.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Gunatit Jyot
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Haidakhan Samaj
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Haidakhan Samj was founded to coordinate the activities of followers of Haidakhan Baba, also known as Babaji. The followers worship him through aarati, morning and evening, and through a fire ceremony called yagya/hawan. However, the form of worship most advocated by Babaji was selfless work that was performed without ego.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Hamsa Yoga Sangh
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Hamsa Yoga Sangh was founded by Gurunath Siddhanath, a member of the Nath lineage and a teacher of kriya yoga. Siddhanath exercises his power of shakipat to free the emergence of kundalini power in individuals; he is also the only master to impart shivapa, in which a student is said to experience soul consciousness of thoughtless awareness. Hamsa Yoga Sangh has centers across the United States, Europe, and Australia.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hanmaum Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Hanmaum Zen Center was founded by Dae Haeng Sunim, a Buddhist woman. The center was founded to teach individuals about their true nature and how to rely on that nature to solve problems. Dae Haeng Sunim was inspired by the Chogye sect, a group based in Korean Buddhism, Zen teachings, and her own insights.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hanmi Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Hanmi Buddhism merges the teachings of Laotzu, Confucius, and Sakyamuni into one lifestyle. Students of Hanmi must learn to forgive, respect, help, and give everyone happiness while handling disaster and pain alone. Hanmi practitioners aspire to know, change, and conquer oneself.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Happy Science
Description: Other | Other Groups | Happy Science, previously known as Kofuku no Kagaku and the Institute for Research in Human Happiness, was founded by Okawa Ryuhu in 1986. It was established in the United States in 1993. Okawa believes himself to be the incarnation of a spirit being known as "El Cantare," and he is known by this title within the movement. At the core of his teachings, he says happiness comes from living with a mind and heart full of love and compassion. The four basic principles of happiness include love, knowledge, development, and self-reflection, and with practice individuals can acquire right-mindfulness. The organization is rooted in Buddhism and emphasizes the three Buddhist treasures: the Buddha, the dharma, and the sangha.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Haredi Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | Haredi Judaism emerged in the early nineteenth century as the most conservative wing of Orthodox Judaism. It opposed reform Judaism and attempted to push the mainstream to ever more conservative adherence to the Jewish Law. It has re-emerged in strength in modern Israel with a goal of re-establishing the boundaries of King David's ancient kingdom. It is often referred to by its critics as the Ultra-Orthodox, and exists through several organizations, among the most famous being the Neturei Karta.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Hariharananda Mission West
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Hariharananda Mission West was founded by Swami Sarveshwarananda Giri following the death of Paramahamsa Hariharananda in 2002. The organization is one of several groups that was founded to continue the lineage of Paramahamsa Hariharananda. The group continues to teach kriya yoga that is passed through the lineage of Self-Realization Fellowship and the Kriya Yoga Centers.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hartford Street Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Hartford Street Zen Center was originally founded as The Gay Buddhist Club in 1980 under the leadership of Issan Dorsey. The group began meeting at the City Center in San Francisco, where Dorsey was the director, but soon relocated to the Hartford Street House, which was inaugurated on December 8th, 1981. The group practices zazen and holds chanting services, lectures, and study hours.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Hauge's Synod (Norwegian)
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | Hauge's Synod (Norwegian) dates back to a religious awakening in Norway at the close of the eighteenth century. This movement was largely led by Hans Nielsen Hauge (b. 1771), and a number of his followers emmigrated to the United States and established community there. Among their leaders was a preacher named Elling Eielsen, who organized this group in 1846, originally known as the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America. After a constitutional revision in 1875 that was approved in 1876, the name "Hauge's Norwegian Evangelical Lutheran Synod" was adopted.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Haven Mennonite Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hawaii Chinese Buddhist Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Hawaii Chinese Buddhist Society dates back to 1953 and was initially led by Rev. Chuen Wai from Hong Kong, though he was joined a few years later, in 1957, by Dharma Master Tsu Yin as co-head of the organization. These men emphasize the Buddhist nature of society, which stands in contrast to the strong Taoist influence found in some Chinese temples. All the statues are considered traditional Buddhist bodhisattvas.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hazy Moon Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Hazy Moon Zen Center was founded by William Nyogen Yeo Sensi and offers programs that support seasoned practitioners along with beginners. The center is focused in the Soto Zen tradition and koan practice, which is associated with Rinzai practice.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Hephzibah Faith Missionary Association
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Hephzibah Faith Missionary Association was founded in 1893 and focused particularly on missions and a Wesleyan theology of piety. The majority of the Association was absorbed into the Church of the Nazarene in 1950.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Heritage Reformed Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Heritage Reformed Churches was formed in 1993 after a split occurred within the First Netherlands Reformed Congregation of Grand Rapids, Michigan. The split was over matters of church order and theological issues, and resulted with the former group dissolving - but continuing its denominational affiliation - and the Heritage Reformed Churches forming.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Hicksite Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | Hicksite Friends were followers of Elias Hicks who first left the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting in 1827. Their separation inspired similar ones throughout the nation. The Hicksite Friends joined the groups that formed the Friends General Conference in 1900.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Higashi Hongwanji Buddhist Church
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Higashi Honganji is the second largest Japanese Shin Buddhist group in the United States - second to only the Buddhist Churches of America. Early work in the United States was led by Shizuka Sazanami in Hawaii in 1899, though Rev. Junjyo Izumida established the church on the mainland shortly afterward, in 1904. In 1917, a merger occured between the Higashi Honganji Buddhist Church and two of its rivals. The work in America is divided into three main districts: North America, Hawaii, and South America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Higashi Hongwongi
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | At the beginning of the seventeenth century, the Jodo Shinshu sect divided. Both branches became substantial organizations, and the Honpa Hongwongi his now the largest Buddhist group in Japan. The group was first established in the U.S. by Rev. Junjyo Izumida, who in 1904 established the Los Angeles Buddhist Mission.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hoa Hao Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Hoa Hao Buddhism was founded by Huynh Phu So, who taught the basic Buddhist ideas and a concept known as the "four debts." The group is considered to be a "reform" branch of Buddhism due to its rejections of various ritual aspects of Vietnamese Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Hohm Community/Hohm Sahaj Mandir
Description: Other | Other Groups | Hohm Community, known as Hohm Sahaj Mandir since 1996, was founded by Lee Lozowick after experiencing a spontaneous spiritual awakening. The community members live a disciplined spiritual life that includes daily meditation, a vegetarian diet, exercise, and study of spiritual classic literature. Other recommended aspects of sadhana (spiritual life) for members include committed to monogamous relationships, conscious child raising, and mutual respect between the sexes.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Holiness Church of Christ
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Holiness Church of Christ was formed in the late 1800s in the southwestern United States. In 1908, it merged into the Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene at an assembly at Pilot Point, Texas; the merged body retained the name Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Holiness Church of God
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Holiness Church of God is a Pentecostal church founded in 1920 by Holiness believers under the leadership of Elder James A. Frost.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Holiness Methodist Church
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Holiness Methodist Church was a result of the "holiness" revival movement that swept the United States in general and Methodism in particular during the late 1800s. The group began as the Northwestern Holiness Association in 1909, but changed its name to the Holiness Methodist Church when the organization realized it played the role of a denomination. In 1969, it joined the Evangelical Church of North America as its North Central Conference.

Primary_Label: Holy Apostolic Catholic Assyrian Church of the East
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Holy Apostolic Catholic Assyrian Church of the East
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Assyrian Church of the East originally developed in the mid-2nd century AD among the Assyrians living in Upper Mesopotamia and northwestern Persia (mostly present day Iran). Its main liturgical language is Classical Syriac, a dialect of Eastern Aramaic. Unlike other (Oriental and Eastern) Orthodox Churches, the Assyrian Church accepts the decisions of only the first two Ecumenical Councils. Therefore, the Assyrians are not in full communion with any other Orthodox church. The East Syrian rite of worship of the Assyrian Church appears to have been an independent development from the ancient Syriac liturgy of Edessa. It may also preserve elements of an ancient Persian rite that has been lost. By the 16th century, the Assyrian Church of the East had been reduced to a small community of Assyrians in what is now eastern Turkey. It was then further weakened by the formation of a Catholic counterpart known as the Chaldean Catholic Church. Individual members and clergy of the Assyrian Church had been present in the States since 1890s, but it was not until 1940s that the Church was firmly established in America. During World War I, the Assyrians suffered massive deportations and massacres at the hands of the Turks who suspected them of supporting the British enemy. About one third of the Assyrian population perished. Most of the survivors fled into Iraq, hoping to be protected by the British. But in 1933, after the end of the British mandate in Iraq, a clash between Assyrians and Iraqi militias ended in another massacre and a further scattering of the community. The Iraqi authorities stripped Assyrian Patriarch Mar Eshai Shimun XXIII of his citizenship and expelled him. He went into exile and eventually settled in Chicago, Illinois. The Patriarch’s relocation to the USA triggered the growth of the Assyrian Church in America. Today, the Assyrian Church in the USA is divided into three dioceses: Eastern United States, Western United States, and California.
