Primary_Label: Holy Eastern Orthodox Catholic and Apostolic Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Holy Eastern Orthodox Catholic and Apostolic Church in North America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Holy Eastern Orthodox Catholic and Apostolic Church in North America was previously known as "American Orthodox Catholic Church" and "American Orthodox Patriarchate." It was established in 1927 by Archbishop Aftimios (Ofeish), a bishop (since 1923, Archbishop) of the Russian Orthodox Church. Born in Lebanon, Aftimios was consecrated as bishop (1917) and appointed to provide spiritual care for those Arab Orthodox faithful in America who attended parishes of the Russian Orthodox Church. However, he had a vision of creating an American multi-ethnic Orthodox Church which would eventually incorporate all of the Eastern Orthodox in North America. In 1927, Archbishop Aftimios was commissioned by the Russian Orthodox Greek-Catholic Church in America (ROGCCA) to form a missionary English-speaking “American Orthodox Catholic Church” which would be unrelated to any particular ethnic group or cultural tradition and would spread Eastern Orthodox Christianity throughout the various American Orthodox Christian communities. Within four years, the American Orthodox Catholic Church consecrated four bishops and, with the charter granted from the ROGCCA, developed a constitution for the new Orthodox Church However, the establishment of the “American Church” inspired a reactionary movement against it within the ROGCCA, which eventually withdrew its support for this initiative. The leadership of other (Greek, Antiochian) Orthodox Church bodies also opposed the creation of the American Orthodox Catholic Church. In 1932, Aftimios's Cathedral was taken from him and given over to the ROGCCA by a decision of a New York State court. The disintegration of the American Orthodox Catholic Church was finalized in 1933, when, in violation of Orthodox Christian canon law, Aftimios married Mariam Namey, a young Syrian girl, which effectively led to his deposition as an Orthodox bishop. Some of his bishops voiced their support of Aftimios's marriage, arguing that while in previous Orthodox traditions and cultures bishops were not allowed to marry, the new Church was separate and free from any particular ethnic or cultural traditions. Ultimately, however, this marriage resulted in Aftimios’ resignation and increasing disagreements among all other bishops. By the summer of 1933, only six parishes remained in the American Orthodox Catholic Church. In 1934, the American Orthodox Catholic Church in its original form became dormant. More than 70 years later it was revived by a church body which was incorporated as "The Holy Eastern Orthodox Catholic and Apostolic Church in North America." This Church claimed be the legal successor to the American Orthodox Catholic Church because it was "held in locum tenens due to lack of clergy," while Ofiesh's widow served on the corporate board until 1999. In 2009, the newly incorporated Church was recognized by the Secretary of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts as the continuation of the earlier Church.

Primary_Label: Holy Greek Pan Orthodox Autocephalous Archdiocese of Canada and America with the Holy Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Archdiocese in Exile (Blessings of Kiev)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Holy Greek Pan Orthodox Autocephalous Archdiocese of Canada and America with the Holy Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Archdiocese in Exile (Blessings of Kiev)
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Holy Greek Pan Orthodox Autocephalous Archdiocese of Canada and America with the Holy Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Archdiocese in Exile (Blessings of Kiev) is an independent Orthodox Church body which is not in communion with mainstream Orthodox Churches. Its history began in 1951 in New York, when Archbishop Palladii (Vydybida-Rudenko) founded the Ukrainian Autocephalous Church in Exile. In 1954, it was recognized by and accepted into the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, thereby granting the Ukrainian Church in Exile a legitimate status in the eyes of the other Orthodox Churches. After the death of Archbishop Palladii (1971), the Church became dormant and by 1980 it ceased to exist, with the majority of its clergy and members joining the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA. Later, however, this Church body was revived and reincorporated under the name Holy Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church in Exile (Blessings of Kiev). In 2006, the current head of the Church, Archbishop Haralambos (Gonatas) issued the statement indicating that, “with the current state of affairs within the Ukraine, it is our position to remain at a distance from the struggle and enjoy our status to be self-governing that was granted to us many years ago by the Ecumenical Patriarchate.” Being ethnically Greek, Archbishop Haralambos is attempting to widen the Church’s reach beyond the Ukrainian community. Hence, the change of the name to the present “Holy Greek Pan Orthodox Autocephalous Archdiocese of Canada and America with the Holy Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Archdiocese in Exile (Blessings of Kiev).”

Primary_Label: Holy Orthodox Church in America (Eastern Catholic and Apostolic)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Holy Orthodox Church in America (Eastern Catholic and Apostolic)
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Holy Orthodox Church in America is an independent Orthodox jurisdiction founded in 1934 by George Winslow Plummer, the former founder of the Anglican Universal Church. It is intimately connected with the Rosicrucian Society in America. The association apparently dissolved sometime after 2008, when only around 100 members were reported.

Primary_Label: Holy Orthodox Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Holy Orthodox Church in North America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Holy Orthodox Church in North America (HOCNA) is an Old-Calendar (following the “old” Julian Church calendar), traditionalist, and conservative Orthodox Church. It was formed in 1986-88 by merging American congregations of the Church of the True Orthodox Christians of Greece and congregations which left the Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia (ROCOR). The Church of the True Orthodox Christians of Greece had been originally formed as a result of the 1935 schism in the Orthodox Church of Greece which occurred over the issue of the adoption of the new (Gregorian) as opposed to the old (Julian) church calendar. The group of bishops who refused to accept changes formed what would eventually become the Church of the True Orthodox Christians. In the late 1960s, the immigration influx from Greece resulted in the establishment of many Greek Old-Calendarist congregations in America. The Holy Transfiguration Monastery and the Holy Nativity Convent in Brookline, MA (near Boston) became spiritual and administrative centers of HOCNA. Holy Transfiguration was the first monastery in North America to follow the so-called Athonite typicon - the rule of life and worship followed by Orthodox monasteries on Mount Athos, Greece. The members of HOCNA consider “ecumenism” as a heresy that undermines the purity and uniqueness of the Orthodox faith. Accordingly, HOCNA perceives the mainstream Orthodox Churches to be enmeshed in this heresy because of their involvement with the World Council of Churches. HOCNA also did not recognize the outcomes of the 1964 meeting of Pope Paul VI and Ecumenical Patriarch Athenegoras of Constantinople which resulted in a mutual lifting of the anathemas of 1054 between the Roman Catholic and Orthodox Churches. HOCNA recognizes as fellow Orthodox Christians only those who hold the same ecclesiology and theological stance as the HOCNA does, and it maintains relations with Old-Calendarist groups in Greece, Russia, Serbia, Bulgaria, Romania, Ukraine, and other countries. Many of the founding fathers of HOCNA are of Greek ancestry. Therefore, most of the local parishes and monastic communities use Byzantine Chant (which is typical for Greek Orthodox) with occasional Russian-style church choir singing. The worship services are held primarily in English. The churches and chapels have very traditional layout: without pews and with very limited use of electrical lighting. HOCNA is known for its publishing work. English translations of various church books are prepared at the Holy Transfiguration Monastery and are used throughout the English-speaking world.

Primary_Label: Holy Ukrainian Autocephalic Orthodox Church in Exile
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Holy Ukrainian Autocephalic Orthodox Church in Exile
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Holy Ukrainian Autocephalic Orthodox Church in Exile traced its roots to the Polish Autocephalous Orthodox Church. It was founded in New York in 1951 by Archbishops Palladii Rudenko and Ihor Huba. After 1954, the Church was an autonomous body under the spiritual supervision of the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople. Its members were primarily post-WWII Ukrainian immigrants to America. In 1980, the Church ceased to exist, when a majority of clergy and faithful joined the Ukrainian Orthodox Church in the USA.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Hongaku Jodo Compassionate Lotus
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Hongaku Jodo Compassionate Lotus is a progressive Buddhist community headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. It was founded by Master Dokyo Chozen, who brought the group to the United States. The group is based in the Pureland tradition of the Tendai Buddhist Community, though it remains a stand alone community. Nevertheless, it incorporates insight from Theravada and Soto Zen Buddhism. Members of the group look to develop deeper compassion and mindfulness.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Honmon Butsuryu Shu
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Honmon Butsuryu Shu is a Buddhist sect founded by Nichiren Shonin following the teaching of the Lotus Sutra. While the sect has international followers, Beikoku Honmon Butsuryushu is an American temple in Los Angeles. The center became a 501(c)(3) nonprofit in 2010.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Honpa Hongwanji Mission of Hawaii
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Honpa Hongwanji sect is one representative of Jodo Shinshu Buddhism in Japan. Shinshu Buddhists began arriving to Hawaii in the late 1800s, and Reverend Soryu Kagahi, a Jodo Shinshu minister from Kyushu, conducted the firs service in 1889. The mission offers spiritual growth in Jodo Shinshu Buddhism along with learning and service to the community. Members of the mission elect their own bishop, who serves a three-year term, and is assisted by a board of directors comprised of lay leaders and ministers.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Honpa Hongwonji
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | At the beginning of the seventeenth century, the Jodo Shinshu sect divided. Both branches became substantial organizations, and the Honpa Hongwonji his now the largest Buddhist group in Japan.

Primary_Label: House of God Which is the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth Without Controversy (Keith Dominion)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > House of God Which is the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth Without Controversy (Keith Dominion)
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The House of God Which is the Church of the Living God, The Pillar and Ground of Truth Without Controversy (Keith Dominion), a predominantly black Pentecostal church, emerged in the 1930s. Following founder Mary L. Tate's death in 1931, the Church of the Living God, The Pillar and Ground of Truth, which she founded, was divided into three districts. One district was headed by Tate's daughter-in-law, M. F. L. Keith. It gradually became independent of the other districts and took its present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > House of God Which is the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of the Truth, Inc. [White Dominion]

Tate) became independent. Subsequently, a new denomination arose. It is not to be confused with the church of the same name that derives from the Church of the Living God (Christian Workers for Fellowship).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Humanistic/Secular Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | By the 1960s, the more liberal wing of the reform movement was moving beyond even Reform theological approaches and adopted a non-theistic Humanist stance. The new secular Jewish movement drew upon a tradition of Jewish leadership in non-theistic forms of philosophy since Spinoza and through the Enlightenment. They have divided over whether they should keep the trappings of the religious tradition, even as they agree in dropping mention of God or revelation. Internationally, they have found a home in the Congress of Secular Jewish Organizations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Hungarian Reformed Church in America
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Hungarian Reformed Church in America was founded in 1922 by Hungarian congregations that did not follow the majority into a merger with the Reformed Church in the United States. They reorganized as the Free Magyar Reformed Church in America, taking the present name in 1958.

Description: Mainline Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Hutterian Brethren
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Communal | Small groups of Hutterites derive their names from Jacob Hutter, a sixteenth-century Anabaptist. Many believers are of German descent and still use their native tongue at home and in church. "Colonies" share property, practice non-resistance, dress plainly, do not participate in politics, and operate their own schools. There are three leuts, or peoples/sects, within the Hutterite Brethren: the Schmiedeleut is the oldest and most conservative, the Dariusleut is the second, the Lehrerleut is the last and most liberal; Hutterites who immigrated to the states but did not join a leut are called prairieleut. Members of these three major groups immigrated to the United States between 1874-1877.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Icelandic Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Icelandic Synod dates back to early Icelandic immigration to America in 1870. The first Icelandic Lutheran services in the U.S. were held in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, in August 1874. The synod joined the United Lutheran Church in America in 1942, but remained as a separate synod; it dissolved in 1962 when the United Lutheran Church in America became part of the Lutheran Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Il Bung Zen Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Il Bung Zen Society was founded by Zen Master Don Gilbert, also known as Ta Hui. He was initiated into the Chogye Order of Korean Zen Buddhism by Seo Kyung-b0 in 1968, and was later commissioned as his successor to the United States. He held services in California and Alabama, and authored two cartoon books on Zen.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Independent Baptist Church of America
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Independent Baptist Church of America was founded in 1927 as a merger of the Scandinavian Independent Baptist Denomination in the U.S.A. and the Scandinavian Free Baptist Society of the U.S.A.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Independent Baptist Fellowship International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Independent Baptist Fellowship International was founded in 1984 by various independent Baptist congregations and their clergy. One of the primary aims of the fellowship was to provide education for ministers, which they accomplished through the Norris Bible Baptist Seminary originally led by Raymond Barber. The fellowship also organized an active missions agency.

In 1922, R. Lee Kirkland organized the American Conference of Undenominational Churches, which changed its name to Independent Fundamental Churches of America in 1930. Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Insight Meditation Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Insight Meditation Society, founded in 1976, is a Buddhist meditation retreat center that also serves as a network between various vipassana meditation groups in the United States; it is not a membership-based association. The founders, Jock Kornfield, Sharon Salzberg, and Joseph Goldstein were among the earliest to bring vipassana meditation to the United States. They offer various levels of meditation experiences.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Integral Yoga International
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Integral Yoga International (IYI) was founded by Rev. Sri Swami Satchidananda. Integral Yoga International is known for teaching all aspects of integral yoga. Specifically, the group teaches hatha yoga, karma yoga, bhakti yoga, jnana yoga, japa yoga, and raja yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > International Babaji Kriya Yoga Sangam
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | International Babaji Kriya Yoga Sangam was founded by Yogi S. A. A. Ramaiah. He founded the original movement in India and brought it to America in the 1960s. By the 1970s, 15 centers had opened. Yogi Ramaiah also established the first shrine to Ayyappa Swami - a figure in Hindu holy books - in Imperial City, California. Each December, members of the sangam make an 800-mile pilgrimage from the shrine to Mount Shasta. The centers practice kriya yoga and were established in North America with the goal of creating a spiritual awakening.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > International Bible Students Association
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | Charles Taze Russell, a Presbyterian influenced by several independent Adventists, founded a periodical advocating his solution to the main questions concerning Christ's return in 1879, which led to his formation of the International Bible Students Association in the 1880s. That association was transformed into the Jehovah's Witnesses organization in 1931 and now exists as the largest group to originate out of the original Millerite excitement.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > International Buddhism Sangha Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The International Buddhism Sangha Association was founded by an American Zen Buddhist monk, Ven. Zhaxi Zhuoma Rinpoche. The association follows the teaching of His Holiness Dorje Chang Buddha III, though His Holiness is not part of the association. The association also abides by the teachings of Sakyamuni Buddha and Buddha-dharma. In 2001, the group gained status as a 501(c)(3) organization.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > International Buddhist Meditation Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The International Buddhist Meditation Center was founded by Thich Thien-An. The worship services follow the syncretic nature of Buddhism. The center was founded as a place where practice and education would integrate and produce a complete practitioner.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > International Buddhist Progress Society (Foguangshan)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The International Buddhist Progress Society (Foguangshan) was originally founded in Taiwan in 1967 by Ven. Hsing Yun. After coming to America in the 1980s, the Xilai Temple was built to serve as a spiritual and cultural center for individuals who wanted to learn more about Buddhism. The group follows the teachings of Ven. Hsing Yun, which emphasize monastic leadership and the presence of bodhisattvas in the Buddhist community.

Primary_Label: International Church of the Foursquare Gospel
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > International Church of the Foursquare Gospel
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The International Church of the Foursquare Gospel is a Pentecostal church founded in 1927 by evangelist Aimee Semple McPherson.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > International Churches of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The International Churches of Christ (ICOC) was founded in 1979 under the leadership of Kip McKean. McKean led his formerly Churches of Christ (Non-instrumental) congregation to begin a rapidly growing international movement built upon one-on-one discipleship of older believers to younger. After accomplishing tremendous missionary goals, the ICOC faced internal chaos in 2000; Kip McKean and his wife eventually stepped down from leadership after their sabbatical, and the association restructured. In the past, some have accused the ICOC of being a cult; these accusations have lessened since the McKean's departure.

Primary_Label: International Communion of the Charismatic Episcopal Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > International Communion of the Charismatic Episcopal Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Anglican/Episcopal | The International Communion of the Charismatic Episcopal Church is a conservative Anglican church that was formed in 1992. They differ from other jurisdictions of the Conservative Church movement in their acceptance and support of charismatic experiences, such as speaking in tongues, divine healing, and prophecy.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > International Council of Community Churches
Description: Mainline Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The International Council of Community Churches was founded in 1950 as an association of interdenominational community (mostly liberal Protestant) congregations. In 1957, after several foreign congregations dropped their affiliation with this group, the word "International" was dropped from the name. In 1969, the name was changed to the National Council of Community Churches. In 1984, however, the new addition of foreign congregations prompted a return to its original name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > International Fellowship of Bible Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The International Fellowship of Bible Churches, Inc. was founded in 1988 as a Wesleyan and Arminian influenced holiness association. Rev. Kenneth Arnold and Dr. Donald E. Hicks were the first CEOs, followed by Dr. William Sillings, who became the first superintendent (the position that later replaced CEO). The association is composed of churches and parachurch ministries.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > International Fellowship of Christian Assemblies
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The International Fellowship of Christian assemblies began in 1927 as a fellowship between Italian-speaking Pentecostals. The group formed in Niagara Falls, New York, and was originally convened as the General Council of the International Fellowship of Christian Assemblies. The group credits its development to Pentecostal awakenings that happened among Italian Christians in Chicago about 20 years earlier. The group is Pentecostal in affiliation, though denounces some of the excesses associated with other churches within the tradition. They consider themselves "Full Gospel," meaning that they embrace every doctrine in the New Testament.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > International General Assembly of Spiritualists
Description: Unclassified | Spiritualist | The International General Assembly of Spiritualists was incorporated in 1936 in Buffalo, New York, by the Rev. Arthur A. Ford (1897-1971), Fred Constantine, and eight other Spiritualist ministers.

Primary_Label: International Meditation Center - USA (Sayagyi U Ba Khin)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > International Meditation Center - USA (Sayagyi U Ba Khin)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The International Meditation Center-USA, founded in 1988, is a branch of the International Meditation Centers, a worldwide Theravada Buddhist Organization that follows the teachings of Sayagyi U Ba Khin. Following Sayagyi U Ba Khin's teachings, the center emphasizes intense practice over the theoretical understanding of enlightenment. The Center offers a range of courses throughout the year, introducing students to the practice of vipassana meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > International Pentecostal Assemblies
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The International Pentecostal Assemblies was formed in 1936 by the merger of the Association of Pentecostal Assemblies (APA) and the National and International Pentecostal Missionary Union. In 1976, it merged with the Pentecostal Church of Christ (PCC) to form the International Pentecostal Church of Christ (IPCC).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > International Pentecostal Church of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The International Pentecostal Church of Christ was founded in 1976 when the International Pentecostal Assemblies and the Pentecostal Church of Christ merged.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > International Pentecostal Holiness Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Holiness Church and Fire-Baptized Holiness Church merged in 1911 and retained the name Pentecostal Holiness Church. It took the current name in 1975.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > International Sai Organization (Sathya Sai Baba)
Description: Other | Other Groups | The International Sai Organization (Sathya Sai Baba) was founded in America by Sathya Sai Baba, who claimed to be Sai Baba of Shirdi's reincarnation, and his followers around the 1970s. The Sathya Sai Baba Central Council of the United States of America was formally established in 1975. Sathya Sai Baba is venerated by the movement, and followers promote five core values: truth, right conduct, peace, love, and nonviolence.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > International Sanatana Dharma Society
Description: Other | Other Groups | The International Sanatana Dharma Society was founded by Sri Dharma Pravartaka after leaving the Hindu Temple of Nebraska. He was known to teach Vishishtadvaita Vedanta, which affirms Brahman as the ultimate single reality.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > International Society for Spiritual Advancement
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The International Society for Spiritual Advancement is a community-focused and programmatically active not-for-profit that organizes the Hari Katha and conducts the Ramcharit Manas Paath every Saturday of the year.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > International Society of Krishna Consciousness
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The International Society for Krishna Consciousness is a representational form of devotional Vaishnava Hinduism that grew from the work of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu - a Bengali saint. It was developed further by businessman A.C. Bhakivedanta Swami Prabhupad, who was initiated into the movement in 1932 and who brought it to the United States in 1965. The group officially formed the next year in New York City. The society is focused around bhakti yoga, which is done by chanting the Hare Krishna mantra and is said to be the process for obtaining pure consciousness of God.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > International Sufi Movement
Description: Islam | Islam | The International Sufi Movement was founded by relatives of Pir Hazrat Inayat Khan who rejected the leadership of the original Sufi Order he had founded and left in charge of a female disciple, Rabia Martin.

Primary_Label: International Zen Institute of America and Europe (IZIA)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > International Zen Institute of America and Europe (IZIA)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The International Zen Institute of America was founded by Ven. Roshi Gesshin Prabhasa Dharma as an organizational umbrella for her work. Two years after her formal resignation from the Rinzai lineage of Myoshin-ji, she received the Dharma Mind Seal Transmission from the Ven. Thich Man Giac of the United Vietnamese Buddhist Churches of America. She then founded the International Zen Institute of America as an organizational umbrella for her work. After her death, the organization spread and affiliation centers were developed in Florida, which is the main IZIA effort in the United States, Germany, the Netherlands, and Spain.

Primary_Label: Interstate and Foreign Landmark Missionary Baptist Association of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Interstate and Foreign Landmark Missionary Baptist Association of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Interstate and Foreign Landmark Missionary Baptist Association of America, also called Faithway Missionary Baptist Association, was formed in 1951 from a split within the American Baptist Association. The Association holds that it is part of an unbroken line of Baptists that goes back to Jesus Christ. The association also affirms Calvinist theology and prizes the authority of local congregations, but ministers are not salaried.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Iowa Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Iowa Synod was founded in 1854. Theologically, the Synod was influenced by the work of Carl Ferdinand Wilhelm Walther. It was part of the 1930 merger that formed the American Lutheran Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Isha Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Isha Foundation was founded by Satguru Jaggi Vasudev. He developed a new yoga form named isha yoga that could lead to a transcending of the body and mind. The foundation offers practice in isha yoga in order to achieve a deep inner transformation. The foundation also supports serval human service projects in India.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Israelite House of David
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | The Israelite House of David was founded in 1903 in Benton Harbor, Michigan, by the very charismatic Benjamin Purnell and his wife, Mary. They believed Purnell to be the seventh messenger in the line of Joanna Southcott and seemed to promote moral purity and very conservative views on sexuality, though Purnell was later accused of many sexual crimes against minors. The site of the group became a tourist attraction for their amusement park and baseball team. After Purnell’s death (1927), there was a schism in the community, which pushed his wife to take her followers to form Mary's City of David.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Jagadguru Kripalu Parishat
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Jagadguru Kripalu Parishat, also known as the International Society of Divine Love and/or the Divine Love Mission, grew from work inspired by Jagadguru Shri Kripalu Ji Maharaj. The group's teachings were initially spread in America by Swami Prakashanand Saraswai, who teaches the bhakti yoga tradition of India. The group's progress was halted in 2007 when Swami Prakashanand Saraswa was accused of, and then arrested for, child molestation. This caused the group to reorganize in the United States and move forward under the leadership of its founder's guru.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Jain
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Primary_Label: Jasper Baptist and Pleasant Valley Baptist Associations
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Jasper Baptist and Pleasant Valley Baptist Associations
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Jehovah Conference
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Jehovah Conference was founded in large part by German clergyman Rev. Wilhelm C.F. Hartwig, a representative of the Lower Hessian Mission Association. The small, six-congregation conference centered around Detroit, Michigan, and prized the Augsburg Confession as the only reliable Lutheran confession. After Hartwig's death in 1927, the Conference faded away.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Jehovah's Witnesses
Description: Jehovah's Witnesses | Adventist | The Jehovah's Witnesses emerged out of the Bible Student movement begun by Charles Taze Russell, who founded the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society in the 1880s. The organization took its present name in 1931.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Jewel Heart
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Jewel Heart was founded by Kyabje Gelek Rinpoche, a Gelugpa Tibetan Buddhist, as a vehicle for his teaching activity. The teachings of Jewel Heart follow the Gelugpa teachings, especially those emphasized by Tsongkhapa (the founder of the tradition).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Jewish Congregations
Description: Judaism | Judaism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Jikoji Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Jikoji was founded by Kobun Chino Roshi and a group of students. It is a Soto Zen temple and retreat center that is maintained by a group of resident practitioners and non-resident sangha members. Jikoji offers regular scheduled meditation practice and four sesshins between October and April.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Jodo Mission of Hawaii
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Jodo Mission of Hawaii, also known as the Hawaii Council of Jodo Mission, dates back to the late 1800s and the arrival of two Jodo Shu clergymen from Japan: Revs. Matsuwo Taijyo and Okabe Gekuwo. They came in response to the poor working conditions of Japanese immigrants on the island and to advocate for their improvement. The first Jodo mission was started in 1896, and soon, 15 temples existed across the Hawaiian islands. In 1969, the Hawaii Council of Jodo Mission was incorporated as a nonprofit; the group is separate from Jodo Shu, which is headquartered in Japan, but cooperates and receives guidance from them. The organization established the Pure Land Institute in 1986, which spreads the teachings of Buddha by providing accessible English translations of important works.

Primary_Label: Jodo Shu North America Buddhist Missions
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Jodo Shu North America Buddhist Missions
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Jodo Shu North America Buddhist Missions is part of the Pure Land School, which is a form of Mahayana Buddhism known as Jodo Shu. The group relies upon the spiritual power of Buddha Amitabha's name in the hopes of reaching the Pure Land where one escapes the cycle of rebirth. The association, which welcomes people of all religions, is based in Los Angeles.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Jogye Order of Korean Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Jogye Order of Korean Buddhism is the largest Buddhism order of Korea and dates back over 1700 years. It uses the teachings of Buddha Sakyamuni as its basis, and has three core principles: transmissional Seon, realization of Buddha nature, and propagation. The Diamond Sutra is foundational to the order as a guiding text, and its most important and widely known practice is hwadu meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kagyu Droden Kunchab
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Kagyu Droden Kunchab was founded in 1974 to propagate Buddha-Dharma in the states. The association follows the bodhisattvas Kalu Rinpoche and Lama Lodu Rinpoche. Kagyu Droden Kunchab seeks to aid all of humanity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kagyu Karma Centres
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | North American Kagyu Karma Centres follow the teaching and leadership of Ogyen Trinley Dorje, the 17th incarnation of the Karmapa, who embodies the works of the buddhas. Ogyen Trinley Dorje was born as Apo Gaga in Tibet, but escaped to India in 2000. Currently, the Karmapa has a large international following, including across North America and the United States; in 1980, the 16th Karmapa made the decision to build the first Karma Kagyu monastery in Woodstock, New York.

Primary_Label: Kalmuck Mongolian Buddhist Center / Tashi Lhunpo Temple
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kalmuck Mongolian Buddhist Center / Tashi Lhunpo Temple
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Kalmuck Mongolian Buddhist Center, in Howell, New Jersey, exists within a community called "Olna Gazur," which approximately translates to "The People's Gathering Place" in English. This community was started by a group of about 20 families who bought land together to build houses on. It is one of three temples in Freewood Acres, which were established in 1958.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kannon Do
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Kannon Do was founded by Shunryu Suzuki-roshi, who also founded the San Francisco Zen Center. The group began to gain momentum after Kobun Chino, a Zen monk, became the resident teacher in 1969. Initially organized as meditation group in 1964, the group officially took its name, Kannon Do, in 1978. Its name means "place of compassion.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kanzeon Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kanzeonji Non-Sectarian Buddhist Temple
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Kanzeonji Non-Sectarian Buddhist Temple was founded by the Rev. Ryugen Watanabe, who was also known as Swami Premananda. He was inspired by Kanzeon Bosatsu, the Buddha of compassion, to bring Buddhism to the United States. The temple offers daily zazen meditation and chanting, and hatha yoga classes. Watanabe understands Zen as the form of meditation practiced by Gautama Buddha, with the objective of forcing the practitioner beyond the sphere of words to an encounter with ultimate truth.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Karma Triyana Dharmachakra (KTD)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Karma Triyana Dharmachakra (KTD) was founded by his Holiness the 16th Gyalwa Karmapa, head of the Karma Kagyu lineage. The Kagyu lineage is one of the four major lineages of Tibetan Buddhism. The organization focuses on Tibetan Buddhism, with their temple building being the largest in North America. The group's Tibetan temple building is surrounded by residences of monastic and lay students.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Karuna Tendai Dharma Center
Description: Other | Other Groups | Karuna Tendai Dharma Center was founded by Tamani Naamon and her husband Monshin (Paul) Naamon. Activities at the center include Wednesday evening meditation service and order of interbeing meditations are held twice a month. After an official visit by Tendai-shu Jiyodan in 2001, the temple was designated the first branch temple of Enryaku-ji in North America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Kashi Church Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Kashi Church Foundation was founded by Ma Jaya Sati Bhagavati, a spiritual teacher who emerged as a result of an intense spiritual experience. The foundation follows a form of Advaita Vedanta, which sees the diversity of the visible world and human experience dissolve in the perception of Oneness of ultimate reality. Since Ma's death, a collective leadership has emerged, and members of the community gather daily for puja (worship ceremony) in the morning and darshan (gathering in spirit with Ma) in the evening.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Kentucky Christian Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Keystone Mennonite Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > KFA/Krishnamurti Foundation of America
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Krishnamurti Foundation of America was founded by Jiddu Krishnamurti to protect his teachings. The foundation's purpose falls under two categories: the first is to spread his teachings, and the second is to provide programs that research the implications of his teachings in everyday life.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Kodesh Church of Emmanuel
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Kodesh Church of Emmanuel is a predominantly black Holiness church founded in 1929 by the Rev. Frank Russell Killingsworth, formerly with the African Methodist Episcopal Church. In 1934, it merged with the Christian Tabernacle Union of Pittsburgh.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Koko-an-zendo
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Primary_Label: Korean American Buddhist Association of the Midwest
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Korean American Buddhist Association of the Midwest
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Korean Presbyterian Church Abroad
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Korean Presbyterian Church Abroad began as the Korean Presbyterian Church in America in 1976. As membership expanded to multiple countries around the world, the group changed to its current name in 2009.

Primary_Label: Korean Presbyterian Church Abroad English Presbytery
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Korean Presbyterian Church Abroad English Presbytery
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Korean Presbyterian Church Abroad English Presbytery formed in 2010 with the purpose of guiding and growing the the churches in North America and abroad where English is spoken as the primary language.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Korean Presbyterian Church in America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The General Assembly of the Korean Presbyterian Church was founded in 1976. It grew out of the migration of numerous Koreans to the United States, especially since the end of the Korean War. The General Assembly represents a group that specializes in serving the Korean-American community. The church was renamed to the Korean Presbyterian Church Abroad in 2009.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Korean-American Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | After the Korean War, many Korean immigrants to the United States were Presbyterians. Once in America, they began to form independent Korean-speaking presbyteries. In 1978, five of these presbyteries came together and formed the Korean-American Presbyterian Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Krimmer Mennonite Brethren Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Krimmer Mennonite Brethren Church was organized by Jacob Wiebe in 1869 as the Crimean Brethren. The Crimean Brethren came to the United States in 1874, settling in Kansas. In 1960, this group (now called the Krimmer Mennonite Brethren Church) was absorbed into the Mennonite Brethren Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Kriya Yoga Ashrams
Description: Other | Other Groups | Kriya Yoga Ashram is the teaching vehicle of Swami Shankarananda Giri, who teaches in the tradition of Paramahansa Yogananda. He began teaching Kriya Yoga in 1974 and began to open centers after traveling widely in European countries.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Kriya Yoga International
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Kirya Yoga International was founded by Swami Hariharananda Giri, a teacher of kriya yoga from the same lineage of Swami Paramahansa Yogananda. The group focuses on breath control, which is said to bring about God realization by turning one's attention to the inner self instead of outward. The group started its Western movement in 1974 when the group was established in Switzerland, and by the end of the decade had organizations established in Europe and New York City.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kuan Yin Temple
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Kuan Yin Temple is the oldest Chinese temple in America and was started by Leong Dick Ying, a monk who brought two gold-leaf statues to Hawaii in 1878. The temple found its permanent home in 1921 in Honolulu. The temple holds various festivals each year, including celebrations of the Chinese New Year and other festivals honoring the Buddha, Bodhisattvas, and deities.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Kundalini Research Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | Kundalini Research Foundation was organized by Gene Kietter in 1970 to spread Gopi Krishna's books and writings and to continue his research. Kundalini is believed to be divine energy that, when awakens, is the biological basis of genius. The foundation's mission and purpose includes promoting the scientific investigation of enlightenment, inspiration, genius, and the evolution of consciousness.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kunzang Palyul Choling (KPC)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Kunzang Palyul Choling (KPC) was founded by Jetsunma Ahkon Lhamo, who was recognized as a reincarnation of a Buddhist lama. It is considered to be a Tibetan Buddhist organization and today offers full programs of teachings in Buddhism and practice sessions in Buddhism meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Kwan Um School of Zen
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Kwan Um School of Zen was founded in order to connect the various temples and centers that were previously founded by Seung Sahn Lee. Seung Sahn emphasized that the two purposes of Zen are to (1) understand the true self and then (2) to assist others to heightened awareness.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Laestadian Lutheran Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: Landmark Missionary Baptists, Independent Associations and Unaffiliated Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Landmark Missionary Baptists, Independent Associations and Unaffiliated Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Laotian Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The present dominant Theravada Buddhism of Laos is dated to the rise of the Laotian kingdom under King Fa Ngum, though other forms of Buddhism had been present in the region in earlier centuries.

Primary_Label: Latvian Evangelical Lutheran Church in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Latvian Evangelical Lutheran Church in America
Description: Mainline Protestant | Lutheran | The Latvian Evangelical Lutheran Church in America was founded in 1939 and came to the United States in 1957. The group originally organized as the Federation of Latvian Evangelical Lutheran Churches in America, but reorganized in 1975 under their present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Laxmi Narayan Dev Spiritual Organization
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Laxmi Narayan Dev Spiritual Organization (southern branch) is one of the two main branches of the Swaminarayan Hindu Movement founded in the nineteenth century. The leadership of the organization is passed through a succession of archaryas and has gone through two upheavals over the years. The first of these took place early in the twentieth century and ultimately caused the creation of the Bochasanwasi Swaminarayan Sanstha (BAPS), which would emerge as the third branch of the Swaminarayana movement. The group differs in leadership but shares beliefs and practices with the northern branch.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Ledeboerian Churches
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Ledeboerian Churches, a Dutch Reformed denomination, was established under the leadership of Reverend Ledeboer. In 1907, the Ledeboerian Churches merged with the Churches of the Cross to form the Netherlands Reformed Congregations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Lehrerleut Hutterite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Communal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Liberal Catholic Church (International)
Description: Other | Other Groups | This church originated in 1916 in Great Britain as a branch of the Old Catholic Church movement that began in Holland in the eighteenth century. It took the name "Liberal Catholic Church" in 1918. It won a legal dispute over the name with the other Liberal Catholic Church that separated in the 1940s.

Primary_Label: Liberal Catholic Church, Province of the United States of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Liberal Catholic Church, Province of the United States of America
Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | The Liberal Catholic Church was established in 1916 through a reorganization of the former Old Catholic Church in Great Britain. The first Bishop was James Ingall Wedgwood, who brought the church to the United States shortly afterward and ordained new priests. After a schism and subsequent legal dispute over the name, the church reincorporated with its current name in 1962, in Maryland, but the world headquarters is in London.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Liberation Methodist Connexion
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Liberation Methodist Connexion officially formed in 2020 after initiating a break from the United Methodist Church in 2019. The church is both highly inclusive and social justice oriented, and works toward the liberation of all individuals from abuse and misuse.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Liberty Church Network
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Liberty Church Network began as the Liberty Baptist Fellowship in 1981. It is an association of fundamentalist Baptist churches and ministers that emerged out of the congregation and television ministry initiated by independent Baptist minister Jerry Falwell and the college and seminary he founded. The organization went by the name Liberty Church Planting Network for a period of time before assuming the present name in 2015. The original name of this organization, the Liberty Baptist Fellowship, is now used for this group's ministry of providing endorsement and care for military chaplains.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Life and Advent Union
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | The Life and Advent Union formed in 1863 and, after a century as a separate body, merged into the Advent Church in 1964.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ligmincha Institute
Description: Other | Other Groups | Ligmincha Institute was founded by Tenzin Wangyal Rinpoche and is focused around the Bon religion of Tibet. Students may enter a seven-year program that is focused in three-week summer retreats where they are trained in the Bon religion.

Primary_Label: Lithuanian National Catholic Church in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Lithuanian National Catholic Church in America
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Lithuanian National Catholic Church was established in 1914 by Bishop Francis Hodur as a separate association apart from the Polish National Catholic Church. Sometime after 1924 and the appointment of Fr. John Grinitas as Bishop, the Lithuanian National Catholic Church was eventually reabsorbed into the Polish National Catholic Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Little Flock Mennonite Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Living Church of God
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | In the 1990s, the Worldwide Church of God rejected a set of distinctive doctrines and practices that had been advocated by its founder Herbert W. Armstrong. As a result, a large percentage of its ministers and members left the church and formed a number of splinter groups (as many as 150 have been counted), though most are very small house churches with but a single small center of activity. The great majority of members adhered to one of the three relatively large groups - the Living Church of God, the United Church of God, and the Philadelphia Church of God - which vary among themselves on the number of former distinctives they have maintained.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Living Compassion
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Living Compassion is a nonprofit organization, founded in 1997, by Cheri Huber. The mission of the organization is to transform lives and end suffering, and it is dedicated to creating a more conscious and compassionate world.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Living Dharma Centers
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Living Dharma Center was founded by Richard Clarke. The goal of the center is the awakening the true self that should be manifested in all of life. Practice combines elements of both Soto and Rinzai traditions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Lost Found Nation of Islam
Description: Islam | Islam | Following the death of Elijad Muhammad, his brother, John Muhammad (1910-2005), formed an independent organization which he also called the Nation of Islam.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Louix Dor Dempriey Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Louix Dor Dempriey Foundation was founded in 2007 as a teaching vehicle for Louix Dor Dempriey, a spiritual teacher whose ministry started in 1996. The group is an educational non-profit and not affiliated with any religion. Followers of Louix Dor Dempriey believe that, while he was an actor, he experienced an awakening of his kundalini energy. His teachings are designed so followers can recognize their own inherent divinity and learn to unconditionally love.

Primary_Label: Lumber River Conference of the Holiness Methodist Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Lumber River Conference of the Holiness Methodist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Lumber River Conference was founded in 1900 by members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South who admired the Holiness movement and had a strong desire to minister to Lumbee and Tuscarora peoples. Due to the participants' particular focus on the Holiness tradition scorned by some Methodists, they officially formed an association apart from the Methodist Episcopal Church. The small conference changed its name seven times since 1900, taking its current name in 1980.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Lutheran Church in America
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Lutheran Church in America was a lasting participant in the dynamic merging of Lutheran churches in the 1900s. It was formed in 1962 as the merger of multiple groups, and it was a participating church in the Lutheran Council in the U.S.A. (which was disbanded in the 1980s). In 1988, the Lutheran Church in America joined with the American Lutheran Church and the Association of Evangelical Lutheran Churches to form the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod (LCMS)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The second-largest Lutheran denomination in the United States was founded in Missouri by German immigrants in 1847. Many of these immigrants had rejected the planned merger of the Lutheran and Reformed churches in Prussia. The group was originally named The German Evangelical Lutheran Synod of Missouri, Ohio and Other States, but the name was shortened to its present form in 1947 on the occasion of their 100th anniversary.

Primary_Label: Lutheran Congregations in Mission for Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Lutheran Congregations in Mission for Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Lutheran Congregations in Mission for Christ formed in 2001 from congregations in the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America who could not endorse the "Called to Common Mission" statement adopted by the ELCA in cooperation with the Episcopal Church. While not created to be an independent denomination, it became one and is congregational in polity. Today, the denomination consists of over 900 congregations in countries across the globe.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Lutheran Council in the U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Lutheran Council in the U.S.A. was formed in 1967 to bring the Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod, the Lutheran Church in America, and the American Lutheran Church together, replacing the old National Lutheran Council. The Missouri Synod was the first to leave in 1977, and the Lutheran Council in the U.S.A. fell apart once the Lutheran Church in America and the American Lutheran Church left to join with the Association of Evangelical Lutheran Churches to form the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Lutheran Free Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Lutheran Free Church was a pietistic synod that valued low-church style worship and emphasized holy living, especially in regard to drink and dancing. In the early 1960s, the Lutheran Free Church merged with the American Lutheran Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Lutheran Synodical Conference
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Synodical Conference was formed in 1872 by the more conservative synods of American Lutherans: Missouri, Ohio, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Illinois, and the Norwegians. It dissolved in 1963.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ma Yoga Shakti International Mission
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Ma Yoga Shaki International Mission was founded by Ma Yoga Shakti Sarawati. The centers offer devotional services, yoga, meditation classes, workshops, and retreats. Her teachings emphasize the unity of bhakti, gyaan, karma, and raja yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Macedonia Baptist Association
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Macedonia Baptist Association was founded in 1932 under the leadership of F.L. Sturgill and Eli Graham. They united the three churches that survived after being barred from the Mountain Union Regular Association in 1901, when that Association forced churches to hold closed communion. In 1935, the Macedonia Baptist Association changed its name to the Christian Unity Baptist Association after adding congregations that had separated from the Mountain Union Regular Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Macedonian Orthodox Church: American-Canadian Diocese
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | Located in the Balkans, the historic region of Macedonia has for centuries been a point of territorial rivalries and claims. Following WWII, a Macedonian Republic was created as a part of former Yugoslavia. In 1991, Macedonia declared itself an independent country (now, the Republic of North Macedonia) with its capital in the city of Scopje. The national Macedonian Orthodox Church today plays a significant role in preserving ethnic identity and uniting Macedonians living throughout the world. The sizeable immigration of Macedonians to the United States and Canada began after WWII. With the steel industry booming, many Macedonian families settled in cities with steel mills. One of the first Macedonian centers in America was Gary, Indiana, the major location of US Steel. In 1963, the first Macedonian Orthodox parish in North America, the Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral, came to life in Crown Point, IN. In 1967, the American-Canadian Macedonian Orthodox Diocese, headquartered in Sterling Heights, MI, was created to oversee the growing number of Macedonian parishes in the USA and Canada. Worship services are conducted in both Macedonian and English. Parochial Sunday schools at each parish provide lessons in the Macedonian language. The Church follows the “Old” Julian liturgical calendar, which is 13 days behind the “Western” Gregorian calendar. One of the very important rituals in the Macedonian Orthodox Church is the “Agiasmos” (Blessing of Holy Water) - a bestowing grace upon the congregation, its members, and their homes. The Greater Blessing of Water is celebrated on the Feast of the Epiphany on January 19. On this day, churchgoers take a bottle of holy water from the church to their homes, where it is kept until the following year.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > MahaSiddha Dharma
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | MahaSiddha Dharma was founded by Buddhist teacher Kali Ma (or pema Khandro), her students, and her husband, Derrick Pawo. The group changed its name to Ngakpa International and is currently known as such.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mahasiddha Nyingmapa Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mahasiddha Nyingmapa Center is a small Nyingma center under the direction of Kyabje Dodrupchen Rinpoche. The center is considered to be the only American outpost of the Longchen Nyingthig tradition of the Nyingmapa School of Tibetan Buddhism. Members at the sole Massachusetts center carry out a daily schedule of meditation and chanting.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mahayana Buddhist Temple Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mahayana Buddhist Temple Association was founded by Mrs. Annie Ying and her husband, Mr. James Ying. The association was initially founded to fulfill the needs of Chinese Buddhists in the greater New York area. The group has three main temples: Eastern States Buddhist Temple of America (the first, founded in 1962), Retreat Mahayana Temple (1971), and the City Campus Mahayana Temple (1997). Today, these temples seek to serve all those who desire a better life on Earth, as well as a better afterlife.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mahayana Zengong Academic Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Mahayana Zengong Academic Society was developed by Chinese Master Miao-Kung and passed through the Buddhayana Order, which he founded in 1911. Followers believe that Miao-Kung was an emanation of the Buddha, incarnated in this world to transmit Buddhist teachings that lead to enlightenment. The group's basic practices include and integrate Zen/Ch'an meditation with Chi kung, a physical practice meant to cultivate and balance one's "life energy.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Mahayog Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Mahayog Foundation was developed to support the teachings of Mahayogi Pilot Bab, also known as Babaji. According to Babaji's teachings, the soul either exists after death and moves to a new form of life or dissolves into the universe. He assists his followers with to finding one's true destination, or inner self.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Malankara (Indian) Orthodox Syrian Church
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Malankara (Indian) Orthodox Syrian Church in the United States is part of the global Malankara (Indian) Orthodox Syrian Church which is headquartered in Kottayam, Kerala State, in South India. It is one of the so-called Oriental Orthodox Churches (the others being Armenian, Coptic, Eritrean, Ethiopian, and Syriac) which, unlike Eastern Orthodox Churches, recognize the validity of theological decisions of only the first three Ecumenical Councils. It is also known simply as the Indian Orthodox Church or the Orthodox Church of India, and it traces its origins to 52 AD, when St. Thomas brought Christianity to India. The church has historically been influenced by the Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church of Antioch. In 1665, the Syrian Patriarch sent a bishop to lead the community of Christians in India on the condition that they accept Syrian Christology and would follow the West Syrian rite. In 1912, however, the Indian Orthodox Church was divided over the issue of the authority of Syrian Patriarch. One faction declared itself an autocephalous (fully independent) Church. The other group remained loyal to the Syrian Patriarch. The first group became what is now the Malankara (Indian) Orthodox Syrian Church. In the 1950s, a number of prominent priests from India came to America for graduate studies in theology. In 1954, a delegation from the Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church attended the Second World Council of Churches held in Evanston, Illinois. Later that year, Bishop Daniel Mor Philoxenos celebrated the Holy Qurbono (Divine Liturgy) at St. John the Divine Episcopalian Cathedral in New York City. This was the first recorded celebration of the Divine Liturgy of the Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church in the United States. In 1965, the new U.S. immigration law made it possible for thousands of professionally qualified individuals to come to America. Many Malankara Orthodox Christians (especially nurses) from Kerala, South India, arrived in the United States during this time. In 1972, the St. Thomas Malankara Orthodox Syrian parish was legally founded in the State of New York. It first used the Lampman chapel at Union Theological Seminary in New York City. In 1979, the Synod of the Malankara Indian Orthodox Church established the Diocese of America with Metropolitan Mor Makarios (former Metropolitan of Bombay) as its first Diocesan Bishop. In 2009, the American Diocese of the Malankara Orthodox Church was divided in two: the Northeast American and the Southwest American. The Divine Liturgy in Malankara Orthodox Indian parishes in the United States is celebrated in Malayalam and English, although at times classic Syriac (an ancient Church language, a dialect of Aramaic) is used. All prayers are sung in the form of chants and melodies. The Malankara Orthodox Church has a tradition of communal “congregational” singing in the church, which is not common in many other Orthodox Churches. A musical notation system has not been developed in the Malankara culture, and melodies are transmitted between generations via oral tradition. Malankara liturgical hymns are chanted antiphonally (“call-and-response” style of singing) by two choirs. In addition, worship services in the Malankara Orthodox Church also incorporate various traditional Hindu elements.

Primary_Label: Malankara Archdiocese of the Syrian Orthodox Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Malankara Archdiocese of the Syrian Orthodox Church in North America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Malankara Archdiocese is an autonomous part of the Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church of Antioch (headquartered in Damascus, Syria) which is one of the so-called Oriental Orthodox Churches (the others being Armenian, Coptic, Ethiopian, and Eritrean). Unlike Eastern Orthodox Churches, Oriental Orthodox Churches recognize the validity of theological decisions of only the first three Ecumenical Councils. Most of the members of the Malankara Archdiocese are immigrants to the US from the State of Kerala in South India who follow the Syriac Orthodox religious tradition. Historically, Orthodox Christians in India shared strong ties with the Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church of Antioch. In 1665, the Syrian Patriarch sent a bishop to lead the community of Christians in India on the condition that they accept Syrian Christology and would follow the West Syrian rite of worship. For centuries, the Indian Orthodox Church functioned as an autonomous church within the Syrian Patriarchate. In 1912, however, the Indian Orthodox Church was divided over the issue of the authority of the Syrian Patriarch. One faction declared itself an autocephalous (fully independent) Church. The other group remained loyal to the Syrian Patriarch. The first group became what is now known as the Malankara (Indian) Orthodox Syrian Church of the East. The second group, those who stayed with the Syrian Patriarch, became the Malankara Syriac Orthodox Christian Church. The Malankara Archdiocese in North America is linked to the second group. The liturgical and cultural practices of both groups are very similar, as the faithful from both groups hail from the state of Kerala in South India and adhere to the West Syrian liturgical tradition. The language of the Divine Liturgy in the parishes of the Malankara Archdiocese is partly Malayalam (the language of many people in Kerala), partly English, and partly classic Syriac (an ancient language, a dialect of Aramaic that is currently used only in the Church). The first Malankara Syrian Orthodox Parish in North America, the Mor Gregorios Syrian Orthodox Church in Staten Island, NY, was formed in 1975. In 1987, a Malankara Council was constituted for the purpose of administrative unification of the American Syrian Orthodox parishes with members from India. In 1993, the Malankara Archdiocese of the Syrian Orthodox Church in North America was established as a self-ruling Diocese under the Patriarch of Antioch and All the East. The present head of the Archdiocese is Archbishop Mor Titus Yeldho with the seat in Pomona, NY.

Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Maningar Shree Swaminarayan Gadi Sansthan
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Maningar Shree Swaminarayan Gadi Sansthan follows the Lord Swaminarayan, whose birth name at his 18th century incarnation was Nilkanthvarni. Gurudev Jeevanpran Shree Muktajeevan Swamibapa visited the United States in 1970 and established the first Shree Swaminarayan Temple in Seattle. Maningar Shree Swaminarayan Gadi Sansthan has grown considerably since then in the United States.

Primary_Label: Mar Thoma Syrian Church of India
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Mar Thoma Syrian Church of India
Description: Unclassified | Anglican/Episcopal | According to tradition, the Mar Thoma Church was established as a result of the apostolic mission of St. Thomas in 52 A.D. It has been in full communion with the Anglican Church since 1954. Commencing as a small prayer group in Queens, New York, in 1972, the first approved parish was recognized in New York in 1976.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Maranatha Amish Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Maranatha Amish Mennonite Church has its origins within the Beachy Amish Mennonites. During the 1980s, concerns among some Beachy leaders over multiple issues within the denomination prompted a call for restructuring. Some progress was made during the early 1990s, prompted by new guidelines proposed by conservative ministers on 18 issues of concern (called "A Charge to Keep, I Have"), but these efforts were soon abandoned. Nevertheless, some church leaders rallied in response, holding a meeting in Tennessee in 1997. This would become the first Maranatha Amish Mennonite meeting, though the development of the group's separate identity from the Beachys would take several years, leading into the early 2000s.

Primary_Label: Meeting in Truth with Isaac (Shapiro) and Meike
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Meeting in Truth with Isaac (Shapiro) and Meike
Description: Other | Other Groups | Meeting in Truth is an online platform founded in 2012 to promote teachers of nonduality and Adviata. Isaac Shapiro and Meike Schuett are two prominent leaders who specialize in self-awareness and teach love.

Primary_Label: Mennonite Brethren Church of North America (Bruedergemeinde)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Mennonite Brethren Church of North America (Bruedergemeinde)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Mennonite Brethren Church of North America (Bruedergemeinde) was founded by Pastor Edward Wuest in 1860 and came to the United States in 1879 under the leadership of Abraham Schellenberg. Today it is known as the U.S. Conference of Mennonite Brethren Churches.

Primary_Label: Mennonite Brethren Churches, U.S. Conference of
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Mennonite Brethren Churches, U.S. Conference of
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The U.S. Conference of Mennonite Brethren Churches was founded by Pastor Edward Wuest in 1860 and came to the United States in 1879 under the leadership of Abraham Schellenberg. This group specifically formed in 2000 when the Canadian and U.S. Mennonite Brethren divested the General Conference into two national conferences.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Mennonite Brethren in Christ
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Mennonite Brethren in Christ formed in 1883 as a merger between the United Evangelical Mennonites and and a small group of River Brethren in Pennsylvania and Ohio. In 1947, this group changed its name to United Missionary Church to reflect its move away from its Mennonite background.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Mennonite Christian Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Mennonite Christian Fellowship, also known as "the Fellowship Churches," is a conservative Anabaptist denomination. Their origins date back to the early Amish revivalist groups in Holmes County, Ohio, which were established in 1958. In the latter half of the 1960s, additional Amish revivalist groups, including a group of Old Order River Brethren, factions of New Order Amish, and factions of Old Beachy Churches, looked to the Holmes County churches for assistance. As they assimilated into the Holmes County group, they became fellowship churches. In the 1970s, though, the Holmes County churches merged with the Beachys, but the fellowship churches outside Holmes County resisted; instead, joined the Mennonite Christian Fellowship, also inheriting the title "Fellowship Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Mennonite Church grew out of the 16th century Swiss Brethren and was named for Menno Simons, one of their Dutch leaders. The first permanent Mennonite colony was established in 1683 at Germantown, Pennsylvania. In 2002, the Mennonite Church merged with the General Conference Mennonite Church to form Mennonite Church USA.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Mennonite Church, USA
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Mennonite Church, USA, is the largest of the Mennonite bodies in North America and was formed in 2002 from a merger between the Mennonite Church and the General Conference Mennonite Church.

Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Methodist Church (1939-1968)
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Methodist Church was formed in 1939 by a reunion of the Methodist Protestant Church, the Methodist Episcopal Church (North), and the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. In 1968, it merged with the Evangelical United Brethren Church to form the United Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Methodist Episcopal Church (1784-1844)
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Methodist Episcopal Church, originally formed in 1784, experienced a schism in 1844 over the issues of slavery and the power of the General Conference to discipline bishops. This schism resulted in two denominations: the Methodist Episcopal Church (northern branch), and the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. It was not until 1939 that this division was healed in a merger that would create the Methodist Church (1939-1968).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Methodist Episcopal Church, North
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Methodist Episcopal Church, originally formed in 1784, experienced a schism in 1844 over the issues of slavery and the power of the General Conference to discipline bishops. This schism resulted in two denominations: the Methodist Episcopal Church (northern branch), and the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. It was not until 1939 that this division was healed in a merger that would create the Methodist Church (1939-1968).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Methodist Episcopal Church, South
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Methodist Episcopal Church, originally formed in 1784, experienced a schism in 1844 over the issues of slavery and the power of the General Conference to discipline bishops. This schism resulted in two denominations: the Methodist Episcopal Church (northern branch), and the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. It was not until 1939 that this division was healed in a merger that would create the Methodist Church (1939-1968).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Methodist Protestant Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Methodist Protestant Church began in 1828 after the Methodist Episcopal Church expelled reformers who persisted in arguing that laymen should be able to participate in the legislative councils of the Church. The reformers organized a convention in 1828, attended by delegates throughout the country, and started a provisional organization under the title of "The Associated Methodist Churches." The group took its present name in 1830. In 1939, some members and ministers of this group - mostly within the Mississippi Conference - declined to enter the Methodist merger and continued on with its present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Metropolitan Church Association
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Metropolitan Church Association is a Holiness church founded in 1894 (as Metropolitan Holiness Church). It took its present name in 1899.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Mevlevi Sufi Order
Description: Islam | Islam | The Mevlevi Sufi Order was founded in what today is Turkey by Jalal al-Din Rumi (1207-1273).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Mid America Dharma Group
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Mid America Dharma Group is a nonprofit umbrella organization that looks to unite members of vipassana sitting groups in the Midwest. The group has a close relationship with the Insight Meditation Society.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mid-America Buddhist Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mid-America Buddhist Association dates back to 1991 when a group of Chinese Americans began meeting in their homes. The group was officially formed in 1999 when land was purchased and Ji Ru (a Chinese Monk) assumed leadership of the association. The association's primary goal is to promote Buddhism in America, with a specific focus in the Midwest.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Mid-Atlantic Mennonite Fellowship
