Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Middlebar Monastery
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Middlebar Monastery was founded by Doki MacDonough and is one of the earliest Zen centers led by an American. Its purpose was to bring traditional Zen training to Americans without the use of Japanese or Chinese language and culture. MacDonough follows a Soto approach to Zen Buddhism that is modified to suit Americans.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Midwest Beachy Amish Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Midwest Beachy Amish Mennonites are an Old Beachy denomination with origins dating back to the 1960s, when the Old Beachys and revivalist Beachys competed for influence within congregations. As the revivalists became the more dominant group, Old Beachys began to withdraw from the denomination, including emigrating to new territories. The Old Beachy churches in Paris, Tennessee, grew rapidly during the 1970s and 1980s, though would be virtually out of the region by 2000, settling in Illinois and Kentucky. These churches remained informally associated, but needed a name for legal reasons after starting the Conservative Anabaptist Service Program for young men. They chose the name Midwest Beachy Amish Mennonite.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Midwest Congregational Christian Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Midwest Congregational Christian Fellowship was founded in 1958 by congregations formerly part of the Congregational and Christian Churches and the East Indiana Association who disagreed with the United Church of Christ's liberal-leaning and more socially-focused teaching. These congregations made their own doctrinal statement and established their conservative denominational identity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Midwest Holiness Association
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Midwest Holiness Association was formed in 1962 in Ansley, Nebraska, as a protest against worldliness and apostasy within the Free Methodist Church. In 1963, this group would merge with the Evangelical Wesleyan Church of North America to form the Evangelical Wesleyan Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Midwest Mennonite Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mindrolling International
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mindrolling International Foundation dates back to when Chogyal Terdag Lingpa established the Og Min Ogyen Mindrolling monastery in 1676. The North American seat of this group, called Mindrolling Lotus Garden and located in Virginia, was founded by Her Eminence Mindrolling Jetsun Khandro Rinpoche in 2003. The center provides a place for study and practice of the Buddha Dharma.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Minnesota Zen Meditation Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Minnesota Zen Meditation Center can be traced back to a group of people in Minneapolis, Minnesota, who began to practice Zen meditation. Dainin Katagiri Roshi became their leader in 1973. Today, their style contains both traditional and contemporary elements of Soto Zen tradition and Zen meditation itself.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Missionary Bands of the World
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Missionary Bands of the World began in 1898 as the Pentecost Bands of the World. This group was formed by the union of branches within a missionary society in the Free Methodist Church, and changed its name to Missionary Bands of the World in 1925. In 1958, the Missionary Bands of the World merged into the Wesleyan Methodist Church (now a constituent part of the Wesleyan Church).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Missionary Church Association
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Missionary Church Association was formed in 1898 at Berne, Indiana, under the leadership of J. E. Ramseyer. In 1969, it merged with the United Missionary Church to form the Missionary Church, Inc. (U.S.).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Missionary Church, The
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Missionary Church is a Holiness church out of the Mennonite tradition. It was founded in 1969 as a merger of the United Missionary Church and the Missionary Church Association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Missionary Methodist Church of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Missionary Methodist Church of America was founded in 1913 in Forest City, North Carolina, by Reverend Henry Clay Sisk and four other members of the Wesleyan Methodist Church. They left the church over disagreement with the church's rules and regulations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Modern Seers, Inc.
Description: Other | Other Groups | Modern Seers, formerly known as the Abhidhyan Yoga Institute, was founded by Anatole Ruslanov in order to interest people in abhidhyan yoga. Students are required to begin a regular practice of meditation and asanas, follow specific moral principles, and find a trustworthy teacher to follow.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mondo Zen
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Mondo Zen, also known as Hollow Bones Zen, emerged from the Mankind Project which was founded by Rich Tosi, Bill Kauth, and Ron Hering. The project requested training and practice structure that promoted Zen Buddhist teachings. Originally an all male movement, women were invited to participate in 2002 with a goal of individual awakening through meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mook Rim Buddhist Society International
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mook Rim Buddhist Society International is a Korean Zen (Chogye) group located in Anaheim Hills, California.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Moorish Science Temple of America
Description: Islam | Islam | The Moorish Science Temple was organized by Timothy Drew (aka Noble Drew Ali) (1886-1929) who took materials from American Spiritualism to publish a "Koran.

Primary_Label: Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum), Alaska Province
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum), Alaska Province
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | The Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum) traces its origins to the reforming activity of John Hus in the 15th century in Czechoslovakia. It was brought to America in 1735 by a group under the leadership of Bishop August Gottlieb Spangenberg. In 1857, they divided into the Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum) Northern Province and Southern province while still sharing full fellowship.

Primary_Label: Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum), Northern Province
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum), Northern Province
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | The Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum) traces its origins to the reforming activity of John Hus in the 15th century in Czechoslovakia. It was brought to America in 1735 by a group under the leadership of Bishop August Gottlieb Spangenberg. In 1857, they divided into the Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum) Northern Province and Southern province while still sharing full fellowship.

Primary_Label: Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum), Southern Province
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum), Southern Province
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | The Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum) traces its origins to the reforming activity of John Hus in the 15th century in Czechoslovakia. It was brought to America in 1735 by a group under the leadership of Bishop August Gottlieb Spangenberg. In 1857, they divided into the Moravian Church in America (Unitas Fratrum) Northern Province and Southern province while still sharing full fellowship.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Morgan Bay Zendo
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Morgan Bay Zendo, originally known as Moonspring Hermitage, was founded by Walter Nowick. It is one of the oldest Zen centers in the United States. The Zendo offers meditation each Sunday and includes elements from Zen, Chan, and vipassana schools of Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Morning Star Zendo
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Morning Star Zendo was founded by Robert Kennedy in 1997 and welcomes people of all faiths and spiritual practices to the organization. Members of Morning Star Zendo considers themselves peacemakers and seek to find common ground in which all beings can co-exist. The group practices zazen, which is the attention to breath in order to free the body of ordinary thinking.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Mother Meera Society
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Mother Meera Society was created by the disciples of Mother Meera, who was believed to be the divine mother. Mother Meera has described her goal as bringing the light of Paramatma to prepare humanity for spiritual transformation.

Primary_Label: Mount Calvary Holy Church in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Mount Calvary Holy Church in America
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Mount Calvary Holy Church in America was founded in 1929 by Bishop Brumfield Johnson in Winston-Salem, North Carolina. Previously, Johnson was a pastor of the United Holy Church of America in Summit, New Jersey. After establishing the new church in North Carolina, Johnson continued to grow the church in other states, including New York, Massachusetts, Ohio, and Wisconsin. Today, the church has a presence in 17 states and 12 countries.

Primary_Label: Mount Sinai Holy Church of America, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Mount Sinai Holy Church of America, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Mount Sinai Holy Church of America is a predominantly black Pentecostal church founded in 1924 by Bishop Ida Robinson, formerly of the United Holy Church of America. Bishop Robinson founded the church in response to a self-proclaimed divine vision to build a Church that promoted women to positions of power. The Church has an episcopal polity and conservative dress policy.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mountain Source Sangha
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Mountain Source Sangha was founded by Taigen Dan Leighton in California. The sangha practices Soto Zen and also maintains fraternal ties with the San Francisco Zen Center. The sangha is currently headed by Layla Smith Bockhorst.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mountain Stream Meditation Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mountain Stream Meditation Center was founded by John M. Travis, who began teaching meditation gatherings in 1986 in the Sierra Foothills of Nevada. The group became a non-profit in 1994, though it was not until 2010 when the group purchased property for the center to be established. The group's mission is to become a resource and support for insight meditation (Vipassana) and to learn and respond to Buddha's teachings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Mountain Union Regular Association
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | Founded in part by Edmund Parsons (d. 1890), the Mountain Union Baptist Association offered advisory functions to preserve communion between participating congregations. In 1901, the Mountain Union Regular Association decided to bar all congregations from their Association that chose to practice open communion. This decision led to the formation of the Macedonia Baptist Association which later became the Christian Unity Baptist Association.

Primary_Label: Mountains and Rivers Order (MRO) of Zen Buddhism
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Mountains and Rivers Order (MRO) of Zen Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Mountains and Rivers Order was founded by Abbot John Daiso Loori and is associated with numerous Zen Buddhist temples and practice centers in the United States. The main function of the order is to maintain the practice of its member organizations, with the main house being Zen Mountain Monastery in New York. In 1984, the group became the first Zen practice group to operate within New York State correctional facilities.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Muridi Sufi Order
Description: Islam | Islam | The Muridi Sufi Order was formed in the mid 1880s by Ahmadu Bamba (1850-1927), a former member of the Qadiri Sufi Order in Senegal.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Muslim Brotherhood
Description: Islam | Islam | A revivalist movement founded in Egypt by Hasan al-Banna (1909-1949). It developed a broad program of charity works while also becoming politically involved in opposition to Western influence in Muslim lands. It was the ultimate source of a variety of radical groups across the Muslim world, including al-Qaeda (also spelled as al-Qaida). Hamas is the Palestinian affiliate of the Muslim Brotherhood.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Muslim Estimate
Description: Islam | Islam | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Nalandabodhi
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Nalandabodhi was founded by Dzogchen Ponlop Rinpoche in response to requests from students who wanted to study with him. Nalandabodhi follows the Vajrayana tradition of the Nyingma and Kagyu schools of Buddhism and provides meditation, study, and mindful activities. The community is dedicated to bringing wisdom, compassion, and awakening into the world.

Primary_Label: Namgyal Monastery Institute of Buddhist Studies
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Namgyal Monastery Institute of Buddhist Studies
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Namgyal Monastery Institute of Buddhist Studies was established as an innovative institute of study and practice for lay as well as ordained men and women. The institute brings programs designed by the Dalai Lama (leader of the largest Tibetan school) to the West as well as supplementary coursework.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Namo Buddha Seminar
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Namo Buddha Seminar was established by Ven. Khenchen Thrangu Rinpoche. The Seminar supports the activities of Thrangu Rinpoche and focuses on the publication of authentic Buddhist teaching from a realized teacher.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Nation of Five Percenters
Description: Islam | Islam | The Nation of Five Percenters was founded in 1964 by Clarence 13X (formerly Clarence Smith) a member of the Nation of Islam who had developed a set of innovative ideas based upon those of the original Nation of Islam. Among is unique ideas was that only five percent of African Americans understood the conditions that produced the present dilemma of the black community and were hence able to lead that community to understand that Black people collectively were Allah.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Nation of Islam
Description: Islam | Islam | The original Nation of Islam was founded by Master Wallace Fard Muhammad in the years following the death of noble Drew Ali, founder of the Moorish Science Temple. It was developed further by Elijah Poole, who was also known as Elijah Muhammad (1897-1975). It began with ideas available in the popular culture and only in the 1980s began to move toward an orthodox Muslim position under the guidance of Elijah Muhammad's son, Warith Deen Muhammad (1933-2008). Under his leadership, the former Nation of Islam changed its name several times and eventually lost its distinctive organizational existence and became part of the larger American Sunni Muslim community.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Nation of Islam (Farrakhan)
Description: Islam | Islam | Changes occurred within the original Nation of Islam in the years following Elijah's Muhammad's death (1975). Louis Farrakhan, one of the most talented ministers in the organization, rejected changes made by Elijah's son, Wallace, and left to establish a group that maintained the beliefs and practices that had prevailed in earlier years.

Primary_Label: National Association of Congregational Christian Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > National Association of Congregational Christian Churches
Description: Mainline Protestant | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | The National Association of Congregational Christian Churches was founded in 1955 by members of the Congregational Christian Churches who rejected the merger of that church into the United Church of Christ.

Primary_Label: National Association of Free Will Baptists
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > National Association of Free Will Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The National Association of Free Will Baptists dates to the ministry of Paul Palmer in the American South in the 18th century. As Palmer's church grew and spread, a yearly meeting with 16 churches was formed in 1752. A general conference formed in 1827 and expanded in 1916. In 1921, a number of churches in the south withdrew and formed the Eastern General Conference. The settlement from this event led to the formation of the National Association of Free Will Baptists in 1935. It operates internationally through the International Fellowship of Free Will Baptist Churches, a structure that has emerged as the missionary program has matured.

Primary_Label: National Baptist Convention of America International, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > National Baptist Convention of America International, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | The National Baptist Convention of America, a predominantly black church, was founded in 1915 by the Rev. R. H. Boyd and his supporters within the National Baptist Convention. In 1987, this group was incorporated in Shreveport, Louisiana, as the National Baptist Convention of America, Inc. (NBCA). It has since added "International" to its name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | The National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc., the largest predominantly black denomination in America, was founded in 1895 when the Foreign Mission Baptist Convention of the U.S.A., the American National Baptist Convention, and the Baptist National Educational Convention merged.

Primary_Label: National Baptist Evangelical Life and Soul Saving Assembly of the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > National Baptist Evangelical Life and Soul Saving Assembly of the U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The National Baptist Evangelical Life and Soul Saving Assembly of the U.S.A., a predominantly African American Baptist church, was founded in 1920 by A. A. Banks as a city-based mission in Kansas City under the National Baptist Convention of America. The growing mission remained part of the National Baptist Convention of America for 15 years until it declared its independent identity in 1936. The association appears to have dissolved.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > National Council of Community Churches
Description: Mainline Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The National Council of Community Churches was formed in 1946 by a number of independent community churches. In 1950, this group was part of a merger that created the International Council of Community Churches.

Primary_Label: National David Spiritual Temple of Christ Church Union (Inc.) U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > National David Spiritual Temple of Christ Church Union (Inc.) U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The National David Spiritual Temple of Christ Church Union (Inc.) U.S.A. was founded in 1932 by Dr. David William Short. Dr. Short split with Baptists to place special emphasis on the temple being the first church. This association was part of the merger that formed the Universal Christian Spiritual Faith and Churches for All Nations in 1952, which was also led by Dr. Short.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > National Evangelical Lutheran Church (Finnish)
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Finnish Evangelical Lutheran National Church of America was organized in 1898 at Rock Springs, Wyoming. It became the National Evangelical Lutheran Church in 1946 and in 1963 it merged into the Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > National Fellowship of Brethren Churches
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The National Fellowship of Brethren Churches was founded in the 1930s by former members of the Brethren Church (Ashland, Ohio). It was known as the National Fellowship of Brethren Churches from 1939-1976 and the Fellowship of Grace Brethren Churches from 1976-2017. In 2017, the group assumed the name Charis Fellowship.

Primary_Label: National Missionary Baptist Convention of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > National Missionary Baptist Convention of America
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | The National Missionary Baptist Convention of America was founded in 1988 as the result of a schism in the National Baptist Convention of America.

Primary_Label: National Primitive Baptist Convention, USA, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > National Primitive Baptist Convention, USA, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | The National Primitive Baptist Convention was founded in 1907 as a fellowship for Black Primitive Baptist associations.

Primary_Label: National Spiritual Alliance of the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > National Spiritual Alliance of the U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Spiritualist | The National Spiritual Alliance of the U.S.A. was formed in 1913 by the Rev. G. Tabor Thompson, previously a medium with the New England Spiritualist Camp Meeting Association, and an advocate of belief in reincarnation, an opinion at variance with the National Spiritualist Association of Churches (NSAC).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > National Spiritualist Association of Churches
Description: Other | Spiritualist | The oldest and largest of the Spiritualist churches is the National Spiritualist Association of Churches (NSAC), formed in 1893 in Chicago. Among its leaders were Harrison D. Barrett and James M. Peebles, both former Unitarian clergymen, and Cora L. Richmond, an outstanding medium and author. The association was formed both for fellowship and to deal with fraudulent mediumship.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Nationwide Fellowship Mennonite Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Primary_Label: Nazarene Christian Congregation (Side A or B)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Froehlich Churches > Nazarene Christian Congregation (Side A or B)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Froehlich Churches | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Nebraska Yearly Meeting of Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Nebraska Yearly Meeting of Friends was an associate of the Friends United Meeting. In 1957, the Rocky Mountain Yearly Meeting split from the Nebraska Meeting.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Nechung Dorje Drayang Ling
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Nechung Dorje Drayang Ling was founded in 1973 in Hawaii by Ven. Nechung Rinpoche, an accomplished master of Gelug and older Nyingma branches of Tibetan Buddhism. Nechung Rinpoche attempts to integrate the practice and teachings of all branches of Buddhism. The center offers lectures, daily meditation sessions, and ceremonies. Tibetan lamas of all lineages have visited and taught there.

Primary_Label: Negro Missions of the Synodical Conference
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Negro Missions of the Synodical Conference
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Netherlands Reformed Congregations
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Netherlands Reformed Congregations was founded in 1907 in the Netherlands when two Reformed groups, the Churches of the Cross and the Ledeboerian Churches, merged. In 1993, a split occurred in the church that formed the Heritage Reformed Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > New Apostolic Church USA
Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | The New Apostolic Church was founded in 1863 in Germany as a split from the Catholic Apostolic Church. The church spread to the United States in the late 1800s, beginning in Chicago, Manhattan, and Buffalo. As of 2015, the church reported 225 congregations in the U.S. and over 10,000 members worldwide.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > New Congregational Methodist Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The New Congregational Methodist Church was founded in 1881 by former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, to protest that church’s episcopal polity. They practiced baptism by full immersion and permitted foot washing during communion. The death of multiple leaders and the loss of certain congregations who left for the Congregational Methodist Church stymied the New Congregational Methodist Church's early success.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > New Hope Baptist Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > New Kadampa Tradition (NKT)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The New Kadampa Tradition (NKT) of Tibetan Buddhism was developed in the west in the mid-1970s by Ven. Geshe Kelsang Gyatso. The Kadampa Tradition is traced back to Atisha (982-1054 CE), who brought Buddhism to Tibet from India, and who emphasized guru devotion and the need for a monastic disciple. The New Kadampa Tradition was created to present the old teachings in a way that appeals to, and connects with, modern Westerners. Today, is it a branch of the Gelugpa Tibetan Buddhist School.

Primary_Label: New Testament Association of Independent Baptist Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > New Testament Association of Independent Baptist Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The New Testament Association of Independent Baptist Churches is a loose association of likeminded congregations formed in 1966 by former members of the Conservative Baptist Association. The association meets once a year, inviting member churches to send one pastor and five messengers. They provide a national identity, tax exempt status, and community to all members.

Primary_Label: New Testament Association of Independent Baptist Churches and other Fundamental Baptists
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > New Testament Association of Independent Baptist Churches and other Fundamental Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Ngakpa International
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Ngakpa International was originally known as MahaSiddha Dharma. The group follows the teachings of MahaSiddha Dharma which are organized in stages that allow for personal engagement in practices, relationship to spiritual community, and relationship with Kali Ma (the founder). There are three stages for followers: community members (beginner), practitioner members (intermediate), or Vajra Sangha (advanced).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Nichiren Shoshu
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The core disciples of Nichiren split soon after his death over the strictness of practice, the split occasioned by what some thought was neglect of Nichiren's relics.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Nichiren Shu
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Nichiren Shonin, a Japanese prophet of the thirteenth century, emphasized the exclusive supremacy of the Lotus Sutra and spread the practice of calling upon the Lotus Sutra with a mantra, "Namu Myoho Renge Kyo," meaning "Devotion to the Law of the Lotus Flower Scripture." He established the Minobusan Kuonji Temple in Minobu, Japan, in 1274, and he lived and taught there until his death. It is considered the most sacred place of worship for his followers.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Nimatullahi Order of Sufis
Description: Islam | Islam | The Nimatullahi Sufi Order is the Western representative of the Nimatullahi Order of Sufis, which is based in Iran.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Nipponzan Myohoji
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Nipponzan Myohoji was founded in 1917 by Nichidatsu Fujii and originates from Nichiren Buddhism. The group actively engages in the peace movement and organizes walks for nuclear disarmament and human rights. The mission of the organization is peace and a nuclear free world.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Nithyananda Vedic Temples
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Non-denominational Christian Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Non-sectarian Bible Faith Churches
Description: Other | Other Groups | Non-sectarian Bible Faith Churches formed around the teaching of Lyman H. Johnson, formerly a Presbyterian minister who increasingly condemned denominationalism as unbiblical and tending toward corruption. Loosely related congregations slowly formed around this ideology after Johnson severed his presbyterian affiliations in 1865. The churches shunned official ecclesial structure and traditional creeds.

Primary_Label: North American and Europe Region of the Knanaya Archdiocese of the Syriac Orthodox Church of Antioch
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > North American and Europe Region of the Knanaya Archdiocese of the Syriac Orthodox Church of Antioch
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Knanaya people constitute an endogamous (i.e., marrying only within their own community) ethnic group. Most of them live in the State of Kerala, in South India. They are descendants of 72 Jewish Christian families who immigrated to India in the year 345 AD from Mesopotamia, the present-day Urfa province in Turkey. Their leader was a merchant named Thomas of Cana (Knai Toma). The term “Knanaya” is derived from his name. Worldwide, the Knanaya presently number about 300,000 and are divided between two major religious groups. The first belongs to the Syro-Malabar Catholic Church. The second group follows the teachings of the Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church of Antioch (headquartered in Damascus, Syria). This latter group recognizes the head of this Church, the Patriarch of Antioch, as their supreme leader, but because of their emphasis on endogamy, and strong ethnic identity and culture, Knanaya churches are united into the autonomous worldwide Knanaya Archdiocese of the Syriac Orthodox Church. Knanaya churches in the USA are part of this Archdiocese. Metropolitan Ayub Mor Silvanos is the head of the Knanaya churches situated in America, Canada, and Europe. The languages of worship in American Knanaya churches are Malalayam (a conversational language used in South India) and English.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > North American Baptist Association
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The North American Baptist Association was formed in 1950 in Little Rock, Arkansas. The group formed primarily in protest to issues with the American Baptist Association, particularly over church representation matters. In 1968, they changed their name to the Baptist Missionary Association of America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > North American Baptist Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The North American Baptist Conference was founded in 1843 as a German-American Baptist Fellowship. It assumed its present name in 1944.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > North American Catholic Church
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Restorationists > North American Christian Convention
Description: Unclassified | Restorationists | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > North American Lutheran Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The North American Lutheran Church was founded in 2010 in Columbus, Ohio, at a Convocation organized by the church reform movement known as Lutheran CORE. The founding members came from several Lutheran bodies, though particularly from the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America (ELCA). These founding members - many of whom were part of Lutheran CORE - felt that the ELCA had drifted away from some of the main priorities of Christian life. Originally, the new group consisted of 17 congregations, though that number had grown to over 400 six years later, in 2016.

Primary_Label: North American Old Roman Catholic Church (Archdiocese of New York)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > North American Old Roman Catholic Church (Archdiocese of New York)
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The North American Old Roman Catholic Church was founded in 1916; the Diocese of New York was formed in 1946. The Church aligned itself with pre-Vatican I Catholicism. The New York Diocese has been the primary diocese for the church since 1958.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > North American Shin Buddhist Association
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The North American Shin Buddhist Association was founded in 2005 to bring together several independent Shin Buddhist Communities. The association follows the Jodo Shinsu Buddhism tradition and is engaged in social activism, especially contemporary social and environmental challenges. The centers across the United States are organized as independent centers, associated by a loose fellowship.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > North Carolina Congregational Conference
Description: Black Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Northeastern Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Northern Baptist Convention
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Northern Baptist Convention began officially in 1907 from the collaboration of generally northern Baptist missionary agencies and congregationally governed churches that did not join the Southern Baptist Convention founded in 1845. Despite theological flexibility within the Convention, two schismatic groups broke away: the General Association of Regular Baptist Churches (1932) and the Conservative Baptist Association of America (1947). In 1950, the Convention changed its name to the American Baptist Convention.

Primary_Label: Northern New Salem Association of Old Regular Baptists.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Northern New Salem Association of Old Regular Baptists.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Primary_Label: Northwest Yearly Meeting of Friends Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Northwest Yearly Meeting of Friends Church
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Northwest Yearly Meeting of Friends Church dates to the movement of Friends into the Willamette Valley of Oregon in the late nineteenth century. They were officially established in 1893 as the Oregon Yearly Meeting of Friends. As they expanded geographically, the group adopted its present name in 1971.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Norwegian Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Norwegian Lutheran Church was the result of a 1917 merger of the different Norwegian Lutheran bodies established in America in the nineteenth century: the United Norwegian Church, the Norwegian Synod, and the Hauge Synod. In 1946, it took on the name Evangelical Lutheran Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Norwegian Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Norwegian Synod was formed in 1853 as Norwegian immigration increased in the United States. It organized around 18,000 Norwegian immigrants. In 1917, most of the synod pastors withdrew to merge with two other synods, forming the Norwegian Lutheran Church (which later took on the name Evangelical Lutheran Church). The remaining pastors reorganized in 1918 as the Norwegian Synod of the American Evangelical Lutheran Church.

Primary_Label: Norwegian Synod of the American Evangelical Lutheran Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Norwegian Synod of the American Evangelical Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Norwegian Synod of the American Evangelical Lutheran Church was formed at Lake Mills, Iowa, in 1918 by a group of forty pastors and laymen who declined to enter the merger of other Norwegian Lutherans, deciding instead to establish an independent synod. In 1957, the group changed its name to the Evangelical Lutheran Synod.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Norwegian-Danish Evangelical Free Church Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Norwegian-Danish Evangelical Free Church Association was formed by immigrants from Denmark and Norway who had been influenced by pietistic revivals in their homelands. A merger of Eastern and Western groups was made in 1909, with the church taking this name. In 1950, this group merged with the Swedish Evangelical Free Church to form the Evangelical Free Church of America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Nyingma Institute
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Nyingma Institute was founded by Tarthang Tulku Rinpoche in 1972. Tarthang Tulku Rinpoche had received extensive Tibetan Buddhist education in Tibet and was the first Nyingma lama to bring the tradition to the United States. The Institute is part of the Nyingma Mandala, through which it is affiliated with the Tibetan Nyingma Meditation Center (founded in 1969), Tibetan Aid Project, Odiyan Country Center, Dharma Publishing, and many other associations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Ohio Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Ohio Synod was founded in 1818 and expanded its ministry into Canada in the early 1900s. In Canada, it provided German speaking pastors and funds to the growing number of settlers moving west. In 1930, the Ohio Synod was part of the merger that formed the American Lutheran Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Ohio Yearly Meeting of Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Ohio Yearly Meeting of Friends was founded in 1813. They worship with a pre-determined structure and an official minister. Theologically, the Ohio Yearly Meeting was very influenced by the Holiness movement after the Civil War.

Primary_Label: Old Apostolic Lutheran Church of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Old Apostolic Lutheran Church of America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old Brethren (California)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old Brethren (Goshen)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old Brethren German Baptist
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Primary_Label: Old Catholic Archdiocese for the Americas and Europe
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Old Catholic Archdiocese for the Americas and Europe
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | Joseph Zielonka, a former priest of the Polish National Catholic Church, established the Old Catholic Archdiocese for the Americas and Europe in 1940.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Old Catholic Church in America
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Old Catholic Church in America is a small independent Catholic jurisdiction founded in 1917 by Archbishop W. H. Francis Brothers. In 1962, the association joined the Russian Orthodox Church, leaving to recreate the Old Catholic Church in America in 1967, and in 1975, the Old Catholic Church of Texas joined the Old Catholic Church in America. Following the 2011 Milan Synod, core parts of the Old Catholic Church in America reorganized into the Autonomous Orthodox Metropolia of North and South America and the British Isles.

Primary_Label: Old Catholic Church in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Old Catholic Church in North America
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Old Catholic Church in North America was established in 1950 by Grant Timothy Billet and several Old Catholic bishops. Priests do not have to be celibate.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old German Baptist Brethren Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Old German Baptist Brethren Church was founded in 1881 as a split from the Church of the Brethren. It represents the conservative wing in the Brethren movement.

Primary_Label: Old German Baptist Brethren Church, New Conference
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old German Baptist Brethren Church, New Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Old German Baptist Brethren Church, New Conference, was formed in 2009 by members of the Old German Baptist Brethren who opposed a resolution adopted at the Brethren's 2009 annual meeting.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Old Mennonite Lancaster Conference Church
Description: Unclassified | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Old Order (Wisler) Mennonite Church
Description: Unclassified | Mennonite/Amish | The Old Order (Wisler) Mennonites, one of several factions of the Old Order Mennonites, was founded in 1870 by Jacob Wisler. As a group, they remain among the most conservative in dress, forms of worship, and social customs.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Old Order Amish Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Old Order Amish Mennonite Church was founded in Switzerland in the seventeenth century by Mennonites under the leadership of Jacob Amman. The Amish first came to America in the eighteenth century and have become known for the horse and buggy culture they perpetuate.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old Order German Baptist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Old Order Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old Order Yorker River Brethren
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The Old Order (or Yorker) River Brethren separated in 1843 from their parent church, the River Brethren (now known as the Brethren in Christ), protesting what they saw as laxity in matters of nonconformity to the world and non-resistance to the military. They are currently the smallest among the River Brethren groups, having only four congregations in southeastern Pennsylvania.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Old Regular Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Old Regular Baptists date back to the aftermath of the Great Awakening, when there was disagreement among Baptists about "old" and "new" doctrinal standards. The new doctrine leaned toward Calvinism, so use of the word "Regular" indicated that a group identified with the older doctrine. Many Baptists in the older tradition came together as "United" Baptists, with the New Salem Association of United Baptists being organized in 1825. In 1854, the word "Regular" replaced "United" in the name, and in 1892, "Old" was added before "Regular." Most of the modern day Old Regular Baptist groups can be traced back to this original group. Today, there are about 16 associations, 300 churches, and 10,000 members in the denomination.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Old Roman Catholic Church (English Rite)
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Old Roman Catholic Church (English Rite), also known as the Roman Catholic Church of the Ultrajectine Tradition, is an Old Catholic jurisdiction founded in 1974 by Bishop Robert W. Lane.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Old Wenger Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > One Drop Zendo
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | One Drop Zendo was founded by Shodo Harada Roshi. The group maintains a schedule of daily morning and evening meditation, monthly weekend retreats, and intensive meditation training twice a year.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Oneness Movement
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Oneness movement, also known as the Kalki Deekshha movement, evolved from the vision of two Indian Spiritual teachers, Sri Bhagavan and Sri Amma. The movement looks to alleviate human suffering by awakening humanity into oneness. The movement also works to erase all divisions and create oneness of self, oneness in family, oneness in society, and oneness in the world.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Open Bible Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Open Bible Churches (known as Open Bible Standard Churches until 1996) is a Pentecostal fellowship founded in 1935 when the Open Bible Evangelistic Association and Bible Standard Conference merged.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Open Bible Evangelistic Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Open Bible Evangelistic Association was founded in Des Moines, Iowa, in 1932 under the leadership of John R. Richey. In 1935, it merged with the Bible Standard Conference to form the Open Bible Standard Churches (called Open Bible Churches since 1996).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Open Gate Sangha (Adyashanti)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Open Gate Sangha was founded by the Zen teacher Adyashanti. His teachings come from Soto Zen, but also has elements similar to Adavaita Vendanta, a Hindu teaching that highlights the oneness of everything. The Open Gate Sangha follows Adyashanti's talks, weekend intensives, and five-night silent retreats.

Primary_Label: Orange County Association for Buddhist Practice
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Orange County Association for Buddhist Practice
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Orange County Association for Buddhist Practice was founded in 1990 and received tax-exempt status in 1992.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Order of Buddhist Contemplatives
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Order of Buddhist Contemplatives was founded by Reverend Jiyu-Kennett, originally known as the Zen Mission Society (1969-1970), with a commission to train and ordain both men and women for Soto Zen priesthood. In 1983, the group was formally established as a monastic order and outgrowth of the previous Zen Mission Society. The group places the most emphasis on their Buddhist heritage, zazen, and teachings on Soto Zen. A study is also done on the teachings of Buddha according to Theravada and Mahayana Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Order of Interbeing
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Order of Interbeing was founded by Nhst Hanh, who was committed to promoting peace in Vietnam. His form of Buddhism was developed from traditional Vietnamese Buddhism that combined Theravada and Zen.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Order of the Prairie Wind
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Order of the Prairie Wind was founded by Rev. Nonin Chowaney (an American Zen Master) and is a Soto Zen Community. The Center became independent of the Minnesota Zen Meditation Center in the 1990s.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Ordinary Dharma
Description: Other | Other Groups | Ordinary Dharma was founded by Caitriona Reed with its practices focused in the Vietnamese tradition that combines vipassama and Zen traditions. The group maintains a rural retreat center named Manzanita Village. Both Caitriona and Michele Benzamin-Miki (co-founders of the retreat) believe Neurolinguistic Programming can accelerate deep transformation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Ordinary Mind Zen School
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Ordinary Mind Zen School was founded by Charlotte Joko Beck who, in the early 1980s, was named one of the four dharma heirs of Taizan Maezumi Roshi of the Zen Center of Los Angeles. After separating from the Zen Center of Los Angeles, she became a Soto Zen teacher in her own right. The group supports the practice and manifestation of "the Awakened Way," which is thought to be universal and can be realized through various mediums and methods.

Primary_Label: Oregon Yearly Meeting of the Friends Church
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Oregon Yearly Meeting of the Friends Church
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Oregon Yearly Meeting of Friends was established by Quaker settlers in the Northwest who were from the Iowa Yearly Meeting. In 1893, they were established as an independent yearly meeting and took on the name Oregon Yearly Meeting of Friends. As this group expanded geographically, they took on the name Northwest Yearly Meeting of Friends.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Original Shree Swaminarayan Sampraday (Ahmedabad)
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Original Shree Swaminarayan Sampraday is the northern branch of the Swaminarayan movement that was founded in the early nineteenth century. Leadership in the northern branch has passed from Ayodhyaprasad (son of the original preceptor, Ramapratap, who was brother to Swami Narayan) through a succession of acharyas. These acharyas included Tejendraprasdji Maharaj, who formally retired in 2004 but continued to assist his successor.

Primary_Label: Original Virginia Conference Old Order Mennonite
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Original Virginia Conference Old Order Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Orthodox Anglican Church
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Orthodox Church in America, The
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | If measuring by number of congregations (560 as of 2020), the Orthodox Church in America (OCA) is the largest American Orthodox Christian Church. Known under its current name since 1970, its history stretches back to 1794, when ten Russian Orthodox monks arrived at Kodiak Island in Alaska. By the beginning of the 20th century, this mission to the native peoples of Alaska evolved into a North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church. It grew considerably, due to the arrival of immigrants not only from Russia, but also from Eastern and Central Europe and the Middle East. At that time, the non-Russian ethnic groups of Orthodox Christians did not have their own bishops in America, so most of them were united under a single North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church. In the wake of the chaos caused by the 1917 Bolshevik revolution in Russia, the head of the Russian Orthodox Church, Patriarch Tikhon, directed all his parishes and dioceses outside of Russia to govern themselves autonomously. In 1924, the North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church became a self-governing Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church in America (also known popularly as "Metropolia"). In 1970, this Church was granted autocephaly (full independence) by the "Mother Church," the Russian Orthodox Church, and was renamed as the Orthodox Church in America (OCA). Unlike most other Orthodox Christian Church bodies in the USA, the OCA does not have an affinity towards any particular ethnic group: typically worship services are in English, the majority of clergy and members are born in the USA, and about half of them are converts to Orthodox Christianity (i.e., former Roman Catholics and Protestants). Founded in 1905, the OCA’s St. Tikhon’s Orthodox monastery is the oldest Orthodox Christian monastery in the United States. The OCA produced many prominent Orthodox theologians and historians, including Fr. George Florovsky, Fr. Alexander Schmemann, and Fr. John Meyendorff. Established in 1938, St. Vladimir Orthodox seminary (Yonkers, NY) became a graduate school of theology and academic center, with students representing various Orthodox Churches, both Eastern and Oriental. Each August, the rich history of the OCA comes alive in the annual pilgrimage to the abode of St. Herman (one of original ten Russian missionary monks and the first American Orthodox saint) on Spruce Island, near Kodiak Island in Alaska.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Orthodox Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Orthodox Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | What today is known as Orthodox Judaism emerged in steps through the nineteenth century as many reacted unfavorably to the development of Reform Judaism and what was seen as the stripping away of the tradition. Attempting to continue traditional belief and practice, modern Orthodoxy assumes its particular cast in its organization to oppose the challenge presented by Reform and then the presence of Hasidism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Orthodox Lutheran Confessional Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Orthodox Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Orthodox Old Roman Catholic Communion
Description: Catholic | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Orthodox Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Orthodox Presbyterian Church was founded in 1936 by J. Gresham Machen, previously an outstanding theologian of the Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A. The church is a member of the International Conference of Reformed Churches. The group initially called itself the Presbyterian Church in America but formally adopted its present name in 1939.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Osho International
Description: Other | Other Groups | Osho, a spiritual mystic born in India and formerly known as Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh, was the founder and leader of what became Osho International. He started official work in the United States in 1981, purchasing land in Oregon for his followers to congregate on. Osho began to spread his teaching in India: enjoy the pleasures of life to the full, but not be attached to them. After a long series of legal trouble, imprisonment in the United States, and international travels, Osho died in 1990. Since the association heavily depended upon his leadership, his followers maintained a decentralized version of the association he founded.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Pacific Yearly Meeting of Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Pacific Yearly Meeting of Friends was founded by Howard H. and Mary Brinton in 1931 as the Pacific Coast Association of Friends. In 1947, the Pacific Yearly Meeting was established within the association.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Pacific Zen Institute
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Padmasambhava Buddhist Centers
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Padmasambhava Buddhist Centers is made up of Nyingma Buddhist practice groups that are connected by the teachings of Khenpo Tse-wang Dongyal Rinpoche and Khenchen Palden Sheerab Rinpoche. The members of the center gather for a retreat once a year.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Palpung Karma Kagyu Lineage
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Palyul Ling International
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The lineage of Palyul Ling International dates back to 1665 in Eastern Tibet. The first center was opened in the United States in 1996 by Khenpo Tsewang Gyatso Rinpoche, an American representative of the branch. The group follows the traditions of Tibetan Buddhism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Particular Regular Baptists
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Particular Baptists emerged in the 1630s as Puritans moved toward a Baptist position. The "Particular" portion of their name comes from belief in "particular" or limited atonement, as in Calvinist predestination. This is in contrast to "general" atonement, or the idea that salvation is possible for all.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Pastoral Bible Institute
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | Following the death of Charles Taze Russell in 1916, the International Bible Students Association (now the Jehovah's Witnesses) went through a period of struggle in the attempt to name a successor. After J. H. Rutherford was confirmed, those who opposed his selection withdrew and in 1918 founded the Pastoral Bible Institute.

Primary_Label: Patriarchal Parishes of the Russian Orthodox Church in the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Patriarchal Parishes of the Russian Orthodox Church in the U.S.A.
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Patriarchal Parishes of the Russian Orthodox Church in the USA are a group of Orthodox parishes subordinated directly to the Russian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) with headquarters in Moscow, Russia. The largest of these parishes, St. Nicholas Orthodox Cathedral in New York, functions as the ecclesiastical embassy (i.e., the representative) of the Russian Orthodox Church in the USA. This Orthodox Church body was formed in 1970, when the Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church in America (also known as “Russian Mitropolia”) was granted autocephaly (full independence) by the Russian Orthodox Church and became the Orthodox Church in America. At this point, a number of parishes declined to enter the new American-centered church. They wished to remain directly under the administration of the Patriarch of the Russian Orthodox Church, thereby forming the Patriarchal Parishes of the Russian Orthodox Church. Until the 1990s, the membership of the Patriarchal Parishes was largely composed of descendants of their Carpatho-Russian founders along with some converts to Orthodoxy. Most of the clergy and members were American-born, and English (instead of Church Slavonic) was increasingly used as the liturgical language. After the break-up of the USSR, the large influx of new immigrants swelled the membership of some of the Patriarchal Parishes. The increased presence of Russian-speaking immigrants, along with a number of new clergy sent from Russia, has transformed the customs and ethos of these parishes into a more “Russian style” of church life. Consecrated in 1902, the St. Nicholas Patriarchal Cathedral is a landmark on the Upper East side of Manhattan, and it continues to be one of the major centers of the Russian community in New York.

Primary_Label: Pentecostal Assemblies of Jesus Christ Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Assemblies of Jesus Christ Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Assemblies of Jesus Christ is an independent association of Pentecostal churches. It was founded in 1931 as a merger between the Apostolic Church of Jesus Christ and the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World. After another merger (followed by a withdrawal) and a period of seven years in which the church was defunct, the group was revived in 1955.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Assemblies of the World
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Assemblies of the World, the first of the Oneness Pentecostal churches, was founded in 1906 by J. J. Frazee. The church has attempted to remain a functionally integrated church through the years.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Church of Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | In 1908, a traveling evangelist, John Stroup, organized churches in southeastern Ohio, Kentucky, and West Virginia. In 1917, in Kentucky, a group of ministers met and organized the Pentecostal Church of Christ, appointing Stroup as bishop. The church was officially incorporated in 1927. In 1976, this group merged with the International Pentecostal Assemblies to form the International Pentecostal Church of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Church of God
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Church of God was founded by Reverend John C. Sinclair in 1919 and took the present name in 1922.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Pentecostal Church of the Nazarene formed in 1907 as the result of the merger between the Association of Pentecostal Churches in America and the First Church of the Nazarene. In 1908, the Holiness Church of Christ also merged into this body in Pilot Point, Texas. In 1919, this group dropped the term "Pentecostal" from its name, becoming the Church of the Nazarene, though the Church of the Nazarene treats 1908 as its official founding.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Church, Inc.
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Church, Inc., began in 1924 when seceding members of the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World made plans to form a new organization, officially chartered in 1925 as the Pentecostal Ministerial Alliance. It functioned under that name until 1932, when it became Pentecostal Church, Inc. In 1945, this church merged with the Pentecostal Assemblies of Jesus Christ to form the United Pentecostal Church International (UPCI).

Primary_Label: Pentecostal Churches of the Apostolic Faith International, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Churches of the Apostolic Faith International, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Churches of the Apostolic Faith International was founded in 1957 by Bishop Samuel N. Hancock. Hancock was originally a bishop within the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World, but growing theological differences between him and the church eventually led to his departure and subsequent formation of a new denomination.

Primary_Label: Pentecostal Evangelical Church of God, National and International
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Evangelical Church of God, National and International
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Evangelical Church of God, National and International was founded at Riddle, Oregon, in 1960.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Fire-Baptized Holiness Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Fire-Baptized Holiness Church was formed in 1918 by those in the Pentecostal Holiness Church, now the International Pentecostal Holiness Church, who wanted stricter standards concerning dress, amusements, tobacco, and association between the sexes. In 1921, the North Carolina Conference of the Pentecostal Free Will Baptist Church joined the association. The association is defunct.

Primary_Label: Pentecostal Free Will Baptist Church, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Free Will Baptist Church, Inc.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Free Will Baptist Church was founded in 1959 as a merger of a number of Free Will Baptist Associations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Holiness Church
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Holiness Church was formed in 1899 in Goldsboro, North Carolina, after Abner Blackmon Crumpler, a Methodist preacher, withdrew from the church and started a new denomination with his followers. In 1911, this group would merge with the Fire-Baptized Holiness Association but would retain its name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Pentecostal Ministerial Alliance
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Pentecostal Ministerial Alliance began in 1924 when seceding members of the Pentecostal Assemblies of the World made plans to form a new organization, officially chartered in 1925 as the Pentecostal Ministerial Alliance. It functioned under that name until 1932, when it became Pentecostal Church, Inc.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Philadelphia Baptist Association
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The Philadelphia Baptist Association, founded in 1707 by five Baptist churches, is the oldest continuous association of Baptist churches in the United States. In 1742, this group adopted the London Confession of Particular Baptists of 1689, which identified them with Calvinist doctrine.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Philadelphia Church of God
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | In the 1990s, the Worldwide Church of God rejected a set of distinctive doctrines and practices that had been advocated by its founder Herbert W. Armstrong. As a result, a large percentage of its ministers and members left the church and formed a number of splinter groups (as many as 150 have been counted), though most are very small house churches with but a single, small center of activity. The great majority of members adhered to one of the three relatively large groups - the Living Church of God, the United Church of God, and the Philadelphia Church of God - which vary among themselves on the number of former distinctives they have maintained.

Primary_Label: Philadelphia Yearly Meeting of the Religious Society of Friends
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Philadelphia Yearly Meeting of the Religious Society of Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Philadelphia Yearly Meeting was founded in the late 1600s in Burlington, New Jersey. The first meeting held in Philadelphia came in 1683 after the adjournment of the Yearly Meeting at Burlington. This group is part of the Friends General Conference, an association of otherwise autonomous yearly meetings in the U.S. and Canada, which was founded in 1900.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Phoenix Buddhist Network
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Phoenix Buddhist Network includes various Buddhist centers and activities located in the greater Phoenix area and across Arizona. The centers include the Arizona Buddhist Temple, Arizona Zen Buddhist Society, and Phoenix Buddhist Association, among others. There is an emphasis on meditation, mindfulness, and service in the community. Bhante Chao Chu, an abbot, serves as the spiritual head of the network.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Pilgrim Holiness Church
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Pilgrim Holiness church began in 1922 when the Pentecostal Rescue Mission affiliated as an autonomous district within the International Holiness Church. It merged with Wesleyan Methodist Church in 1968 to create the Wesleyan Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Pilgrim Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Pillar of Fire
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Pillar of Fire is a Holiness church founded in 1902 by Alma White, the wife of a Methodist minister who preached in revivals and in her husband's pulpit. As her success grew, she organized her converts into independent missions, formally incorporating the missions in 1902 as the Pentecostal Union. The name Pillar of Fire was adopted in 1917, and came from the church's widely-known magazine with the same name.

Primary_Label: Pleasant View Old Order (Virginia) Mennonite Conference
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Pleasant View Old Order (Virginia) Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Primary_Label: Polish National Catholic Church of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Polish National Catholic Church of America
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Polish National Catholic Church of America is an independent Old Catholic jurisdiction founded in the 1890s by former members of the Roman Catholic Church of Polish ancestry under the leadership of Bishop Francis Hodur.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Prabhupada Sankirtan Society
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The mission of the Prabhupada Sankirtan Society is to uphold the original goals of His Divine Grace, A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, their spiritual master. The goals are stated in the original constitution (1966), which aligns itself with following and advancing parampara.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Prana Yoga Centres International
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Prana Yoga Centres International was founded by Swami Sivalingam, also known as Swami Vignanananda. Known for his extensive work, the center was established to organize the other centers he had opened. The centers include Prana Yoga Foundation, Prana Yoga Ashram, the Prana Yoga Center, and the Ayaodhyanagar Retreat.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Pranayama Institute
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Paranayama Institute was founded by Sankara Saranam, a proponent of pranayama, which is a yoga practice that teaches techniques of breathing and concentration. He founded the institute in order to further the belief in Pranayama, and to make its techniques widely available to people. Practicing Pranayama is said to bring a calming, balanced, and focused mind.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)
Description: Mainline Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.), the largest of several Presbyterian churches in America, was founded in 1983 as a merger of the United Presbyterian Church in the United States of America and the Presbyterian Church in the United States.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Presbyterian Church in America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Presbyterian Church in America was organized at a constitutional assembly in December of 1973 and first known as the National Presbyterian Church. It was founded by conservative members of the Presbyterian Church in the United States who rejected that church's merger with the United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A. In 1974, it assumed its current name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Presbyterian Church in the United States of America inherited the tradition of early Presbyterianism in the colonies and is in direct continuity with the first synod organized in 1706. It merged with the United Presbyterian Church of North America in 1958 to form the United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A., now a constituent part of the Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.).

Primary_Label: Presbyterian Church in the United States
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Presbyterian Church in the United States
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Presbyterian Church in the United States arose out of the same controversies that had split the Methodists and Baptists in the years prior to the Civil War. It was started by Southern presbyteries that withdrew from the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in the U.S. to form the Presbyterian Church in the Confederate States (later changed to Presbyterian Church in the U.S.). In 1983, this group merged with the United Presbyterian Church in the United States of America to form the Presbyterian Church (U.S.A).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Presbyterian Reformed Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Presbyterian Reformed Church was formed in 1965 by the joining of two Presbyterian congregations in Canada into a presbytery. John Murray was largely influential in its formation and the initial teaching of many of the denomination's early leaders. The association has close historical ties to Westminster Theological Seminary and the Orthodox Presbyterian Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Primitive Advent Christian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | The Primitive Advent Christian Church developed out of a controversy centering on the preaching of a Reverend Whitman, a minister of the Advent Christian Church in West Virginia. Whitman opposed both footwashing and re-baptizing reclaimed backsliders. Proponents of these two practices organized the Primitive Advent Christian Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Primitive Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | After the American Revolution, a new enthusiasm for missions emerged in the American church. Among the Baptists, this led to the formation of the General Missionary Convention of the Baptist Denomination in the United States for Foreign Missions in 1814. However, a minority of members is this group viewed the missionary movement, as well as other initiatives like Bible societies and theological seminaries, as unnecessary innovations and inventions of men (and thus not warranted from the word of God). Those opposed to such innovations formed the Primitive Baptists, which existed throughout the U.S. by 1840. These groups have a strong congregational form of government and generally have no organization above the loose associations that typically cover several counties.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Primitive Baptists Associations
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Primary_Label: Primitive Baptists, Eastern District Association of
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Primitive Baptists, Eastern District Association of
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Eastern District Association of Primitive Baptists dates back to 1847, when a church was organized in Blackwater, Virginia, in a building previously used as a distillery. This church was called "Blackwater Lick," and the next year, in 1848, the Association was formed with this church and five others.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Primitive Colored Baptist Church
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Primitive Friends
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Primary_Label: Primitive Methodist Church in the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Primitive Methodist Church in the U.S.A.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Primitive Methodist Church grew out of the revivalist impulse in England in the early nineteenth century. It was founded in 1810 by Revs. Hugh Bourne and William Clowes, who were dismissed from the Wesleyan Methodist Connection and whose followers were not welcomed into the Wesleyan Church. Growth was such that in 1812, in Tunstall, England, they became officially organized as The Society of the Primitive Methodists. The group was brought to the United States in the 1820s.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Progressive National Baptist Convention, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | The Progressive National Baptist Convention, a predominantly black Baptist church, was founded in 1961 by former members of the National Baptist Convention who supported the Civil Rights Movement under the leadership of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. (1929-1968). The National Baptists had refused to support the Movement actively.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Progressive Primitive Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | In the early 1900s, there was a split among Primitive Baptists between those who supported the use of instruments, Sunday school, youth ministries, missions, men's brotherhoods, women’s auxiliaries, and non-congregationally based ministries, and those who did not support them. Progressive Primitive Baptists embraced all of these things. The emotional divide between the groups is not as large as it was, but differences still exist.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > Progressive Spiritual Church
Description: Unclassified | Spiritualist | The Progressive Spiritualist Church was founded in 1907 by Rev. G. V. Cordingley.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Protes'tant Conference, The
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Protes'tant Conference is a conservative Lutheran church founded in 1928 by former members of the Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Protestant Reformed Churches in America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Protestant Reformed Churches in America (PRC) originated as a result of controversy in the Christian Reformed Church in 1924 involving the adoption of the "Three Points of Common Grace." Three ministers and their consistories rejected the doctrine and founded the new denomination in 1926.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Pushti Margiya Vaishnav Samaj
