Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Pushti Margiya Vaishnav Samaj was founded over 500 years ago by Jagadguru Shrimad Vallabhacharya Mahaprabhuji. The group is a Hindu sect that serves Shri Krishna as the supreme God through Pushtimarg, "The Path Of Grace," or a voluntary love of the deity. They promote the mantra, "Brahamasambandha." The first temple was founded in the United States around 2010.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Qadiri Sufi Order
Description: Islam | Islam | The Qadiri Sufi Order was founded by the students of Abd al-Qadir al-Julani (1077-1166) a Hanbalite jurist.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Rabbinical Council
Description: Judaism | Judaism | The first of the basic organizations formed to carry the Orthodox cause was the Rabbi Elchanan Theological Seminary (1896) which evolved into Yeshiva University. Two years later it was joined by the national synagogue association, the Union of Orthodox Jewish Congregations in America (1898), which was followed by the Union of Orthodox Rabbis of the United States and Canada (1902). The Orthodox rabbis split in 1935 when the English speaking rabbis formed the Rabbinical Council of America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Real Dharma
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Real Dharma is a nonprofit organization in California led by Hal Blacker. The group is dedicated to helping people find inner freedom through recognizing their true nature. Blacker, the group's founder and leader, teaches in the traditions of Advaita Vedanta and Tibetan Buddhist Dzogchen.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Reconstructionist Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | Reconstructionist Judaism is a movement fueled by the thought of professor Mordecai M. Kaplan, a conservative rabbi who argued for a whole new approach to Judaism as an evolving civilization; it began with the publication of his book Judaism as a Civilization in 1934. In the 1940s and 1950s, the Jewish Reconstructionist Foundation began to take shape, the rabbinical college was founded in 1954, and the Reconstructionist Rabbinical Association was formed by its graduates. Kaplan was excommunicated by Conservative Jews for his edits to the prayer book in 1945. His followers remain socially progressive and have drawn near to Reform Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Reform Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | Beginning at the time of the European Enlightenment in the 18th century, Jewish leaders began to search for a form of Judaism more attuned to modern culture. German Reform leaders began to articulate a way of being fully religious as a Jew that both fit into modern cultural and intellectual expectations and which emphasized the more universal affirmation of Judaism and abandoned what were seen as mere ancient cultural attributes. The reform movement found its organizational unity through Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati, Ohio, the Central Conference of American Rabbis, and the Union of American Hebrew Congregations (1873). Reform Jews practice a Judaism that is open to change and adaptation with the times. They view the Torah as divinely inspired (but a product of human hands), are committed to the equality of men and women, ordain women rabbis, and accept gays and lesbians into all areas of Jewish life. Globally, they are a minority movement, though they maintain a signficant presence in North America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Reformed Baptist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Reformed Baptist Church was formed by preachers who left the Primitive Baptists in 1888 as the Holiness movement gained influence. The church later combined with the Wesleyan Church USA in 1966.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Catholic Liturgical > Reformed Catholic Church
Description: Catholic | Catholic Liturgical | The Reformed Catholic Church was founded in 2000 by Robert J. Allman. It was established to perpetuate the beliefs and practices of Roman Catholicism but has opened its doors to those who have had difficulties participating fully in the Roman Catholic Church. The church experienced an upheaval in 2007 and dissolved in 2009, but was reorganized in 2014 under the leadership of Bishop Marcus Heckman.

Description: Mainline Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Church in America, the oldest continuously existing Protestant church in the United States, perpetuates the religious life of the Dutch Reformed settlement of New Amsterdam (New York) in the seventeenth century.

Primary_Label: Reformed Church in the United States
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Church in the United States
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | In 1934, the majority of the Reformed Church in the United States (formed in the 1720s) merged with the Evangelical Synod to form the Evangelical and Reformed Church (now a constituent part of the United Church of Christ). At that time, the Eureka Classis (based in South Dakota) refused to join the merger and continued as the Reformed Church in the United States and laid claim to the history of that body from the eighteenth century. The Church is a member of the International Conference of Reformed Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Anglican/Episcopal > Reformed Episcopal Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Anglican/Episcopal | The Reformed Episcopal Church is an independent Anglican jurisdiction founded in 1873 by Right Reverend George David Cummins and former members of the evangelical wing of the Episcopal Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Reformed Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Reformed Mennonite Church, a very conservative Mennonite body known for its use of shunning (or avoidance) of deviating members, was founded in 1812 by John Herr and former members of the Mennonite Church. They emphasize the exclusive claims of their distinct faith, practices, and community. They dress plainly and live in plain surroundings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Reformed Methodist Church
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Reformed Methodist Church was founded in Readsborough, Vermont, by a group of Methodists led by Pliny Brett, a local preacher. It joined the Churches of Christ in Christian Union in 1952.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Reformed Methodist Union Episcopal Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Reformed Methodist Union Episcopal Church is a predominantly black church founded in 1885 by former members of the African Methodist Episcopal Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Reformed New Congregational Methodist Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Reformed New Congregational Methodist Church was organized in 1916 by the Reverend J. A. Sander and the Reverend Earl Wilcoxen, a minister in the Congregational Methodist Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Presbyterian Church General Assembly
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Presbyterian Church General Assembly traces its origin to 1983 when congregations in Georgia withdrew from the Presbyterian Church in America. By 1990, after two name changes, the group formed by these congregations was called the Reformed Presbyterian Church in the Americas. However, after internal disruption of this group in 1990-1991, one of this group's four presbyteries dissolved and another became independent, forming the Reformed Presbyterian Church (Hanover Presbytery). The other two presbyteries reorganized as this group in 1991.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Presbyterian Church Hanover Presbytery
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Presbyterian Church General Assembly traces its origin to 1983 when congregations in Georgia withdrew from the Presbyterian Church in America. By 1990, after two name changes, the group formed by these congregations was called the Reformed Presbyterian Church in the Americas. However, after internal disruption of this group in 1990-1991, one of this group's four presbyteries dissolved and another became independent, forming the Reformed Presbyterian Church (Hanover Presbytery). The other two presbyteries formed the Reformed Presbyterian Church General Assembly.

Primary_Label: Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America, General Synod
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America, General Synod
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Presbyterian Church, General Synod was formed in 1833 as Reformed Presbyterians split over the issue of participation in government, such as whether members could vote or hold office. The "New Lights," who allowed such participation, formed this group, which in 1965 merged with the Evangelical Presbyterian Church (1956) to form the Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod. This group would then merge into the Presbyterian Church in America in 1982.

Primary_Label: Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Presbyterian Church of the United States was founded after congregations in Georgia withdrew from the Presbyterian Church in America in 1983. Those congregations first took the name Covenant Presbytery, but became the Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States in 1985. The group took the name Reformed Presbyterian Church in the Americas in 1990 before internal disruptions cause four presbyteries to either dissolve, become independent, or merge. The present name was resumed in 1992.

Primary_Label: Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America (Covenanters)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America (Covenanters)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America traces its history to the 1643 Solemn League and Covenant, a document issued by a group (the Covenanters) which separated from the Church of Scotland. The Church remains as the only Covenanter branch that has not merged into one of the other Presbyterian churches. In 1833, Reformed Presbyterians split over the issue of participation in government, such as whether members could vote or hold office. The "New Lights," who allowed such participation, formed the Reformed Presbyterian Church, General Synod, while those who did not allow such participation continued as this group. The Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America is a member of the International Conference of Reformed Churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod was formed in 1965 by a merger of the Evangelical Presbyterian Church and the Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America, General Synod. This group merged into the Presbyterian Church in America in 1982.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Reformed Zion Union Apostolic Church
Description: Black Protestant | Methodist | The Reformed Zion Union Apostolic Church was founded in 1869 by Reverend James Howell as a split from the African Methodist Episcopal Church. It was originally named the Zion Union Apostolic Church but was reorganized in 1882 under its present name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Regular Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Regular Baptists have their roots in the Great Awakening in the 1740s, when new Baptists were divided into Regular Baptists and Separate Baptists. Despite differences between these groups, they spent much of the late 1700s attempting to reunify. In 1765, the first Regular Baptist Church was formed in Virginia and given the name Ketoctin. The Regular Baptists began a move to reconstitute themselves in the middle of the 19th century, and in 1854, the New Salem Association of United Baptists changed their name to the New Salem Association of Regular Baptists. This group rejected the innovations introduced among American Baptists during the nineteenth century. In 1870, they adopted the name Old Regular Baptists.

Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Reiyukai was founded by Kakutaro Kubo and Kimi Kotani as a lay Nichiren organization and has no priests.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Reiyukai America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Reiyukai was founded by Kakutaro Kubo and Kimi Kotani in the 1920s as a lay Nichiren organization. Reiyukai America was established in 1972 in California. The organization has no priests and encourages personal development for the benefit of all humanity. Their practice includes daily recitation of the Blue Sutra, which is said to enhance individual awareness and actions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Religious Society of Friends (Kansas Yearly Meeting)
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Kansas Yearly Meeting was formed in 1872 as Friends moved into Kansas and then Oklahoma, Texas, Nebraska, and Colorado. In 1978, they changed their name to the Mid-America Yearly meeting.

Primary_Label: Religious Society of Friends (Mid-America Yearly Meeting)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Religious Society of Friends (Mid-America Yearly Meeting)
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Mid-America Yearly Meeting originally began as the Kansas Yearly Meeting in 1872, which formed as Friends moved into Kansas and nearby states. They took their new name in 1978, though they added "Evangelical Friends Church" to their name in 2001.

Primary_Label: Religious Society of Friends (Philadelphia and its Vicinity, for the Relief of Colored Freedmen)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Religious Society of Friends (Philadelphia and its Vicinity, for the Relief of Colored Freedmen)
Description: Unclassified | Friends/Brethren | The Friends Association of Philadelphia and Its Vicinity, for the Relief of Colored Freedmen, was founded in 1863. Its purpose was to provide relief and education to former enslaved people during and after the Civil War. The name was changed around 1873 to Friends' Freedmen's Association, and in 1982 the Association was dissolved.

Primary_Label: Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints was formed in 1860 by Jason Briggs, Zenos Gurley, William Marks, and followers of Joseph Smith, Jr., who decided against migrating to Utah during the 1850s. The organization changed its name in 2001 to the Community of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Restoration Branches Movement
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Restoration Branches Movement began in the 1980s as a conservative split within the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (now known as the Community of Christ). Dissent within the church had been developing for two decades prior to the split, with key issues being the church's change in school curriculum and the ordination of women. Dissenting individuals within the church began to organize and separate, forming autonomous branches (congregations) that remained within the church but operated independent of its hierarchy.

Primary_Label: Restoration Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) > Restoration Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Description: Other | Latter-day Saints (Mormonism) | The Restoration Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints was formed in 1991 by former members of the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The separating members perceived the church to be drifting toward liberal Protestant theology and away from the early teachings of Joseph Smith, Jr., in the 1830s and 1840s. The church has a theology similar to the Reorganized Church prior to changes introduced in the 1970s.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Rhema
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | Rhema" is the name of a set of ministries developed by Kenneth E. Hagin Sr., a former pastor with the Assemblies of God. Hagin left his post as pastor to become a traveling evangelist, starting his ministries in the 1960s, which would eventually evolve into Rhema Bible Church in Tulsa, Oklahoma, in 1978. The group's beliefs are very similar to those of the Assemblies of God.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Rigpa Fellowship
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Rigpa Fellowship is an association under Tibetan Buddhism meditation centers that is headed by Sogyal Rinpoche. The fellowship teaches Dzogchen meditation to bring precise experiences of the awakened state.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Rigpe Dorje Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Rigpa Dorje Foundation was founded by His Eminence Jamgon Kongtrul Rinpoche. The organization's primary location is in Canada, but there are outposts in the United States, Europe, and Hong Kong, where projects of education, medical, social, and cultural development are supported by his followers. He believed that the combination of Eastern wisdom and Western knowledge could solve many contemporary problems, and initiated the Buddhism and Psychotherapy Conference in New York. The primary meditation practice, called shamata, looks to awaken the nature of the mind.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Rime Buddhist Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Rime Buddhist Center was founded by Lama Chuck and Chaplain Mary Stanford. The Center encourages both wisdom and compassion with a primary goal of teaching Buddhism and Tibetan culture through monks, lamas, and other Tibetan teachers. The center's objective is to promote a harmonious relationship of understanding between both Tibetans and Westerners.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Rimé Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Rimé Foundation, formerly the Chicago Rimé Center, was founded in 1997 by Roberto Sanchez and is a western outpost of the Rimé tradition. The foundation supports the practice, development, and integration of Tibetan Buddhism. The Center offers weekly practice sessions and dharma talks.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Rinzai-Ji, Inc.
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Rinzai-Ji, Inc., was founded by Kyozan Joshu Sasaki Roshi and is an association of Zen centers in the Rinzai tradition. Each center offers a program of zazen (sitting with the master) and periodic sesshin (extended sitting meditation) and all are ran by individuals trained by Sasaki Roshi.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Rissho Kosei Kai
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Rissho Kosei Kai was founded in 1938 by Nikkyo Niwano and Myoko Naganuma, both former members of Reiyukai. It is also a lay-led group. The group follows Niwano's interpretation of Buddhism, with attention focused on the three Hokke Sutras. Worship includes the chanting of the Lotus Sutra, the daimoku, and a sermon.

Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Rochester Zen Center grew out of the experience of Philip Kapleau. Kapleau published the Three Pillars of Zen, which has a strong emphasis on koan work and zazen meditation from his Rinzai training.
