Primary_Label: Romanian Orthodox Episcopate of America (Diocese of the Orthodox Church in America)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Romanian Orthodox Episcopate of America (Diocese of the Orthodox Church in America)
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Romanian Orthodox Episcopate is a semiautonomous part of the Orthodox Church in America. Historically, Romanian ethnic identity has been closely identified with the Orthodox Christian faith. Orthodox Christians comprise about 90% of the present-day Romanian population. The initial Romanian immigration to the U.S. occurred at the beginning of the 20th century and gravitated largely to industrial centers such as Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, New York, and other “Rust Belt” cities. Typically, the Romanian immigrants were accompanied by Romanian Orthodox clergy who came to America to minister to them. The first Romanian Orthodox parish in the U.S. was opened in 1904, in Cleveland, OH. In 1929, the Congress of clergy and laity held in Detroit established the “Autonomous Missionary Episcopate,” which united all the scattered parishes. The Congress also asked the Synod of the Romanian Orthodox Church in Bucharest to send a bishop. In 1935, the first Romanian Orthodox Bishop, Policarp Moruşca, arrived in America. That same year, the Episcopate purchased the Grey Tower Farms in Grass Lake, Michigan, which is near Detroit. Known as the Vatra Romaneasca (Romanian Hearth), it served from its establishment as the center of the Romanian Church and culture in the United States. After WWII, the Communist takeover in Romania divided Romanian communities in America along political lines. The question of either remaining loyal to the Patriarchate of Bucharest or separating from the Communist-controlled Church in Romania provoked a schism among Romanian Orthodox parishes. Since 1951, two rival Romanian Orthodox church bodies have existed in America. One was the Romanian Orthodox Archdiocese of the Americas, which remained loyal to the Patriarchate of Bucharest and has become the present-day Romanian Orthodox Metropolia in the Americas. The second was the Romanian Orthodox Episcopate of America, which declared itself completely separated from the Church in Romania in both spiritual and administrative affairs. The Episcopate also became the legal successor to the original Missionary Episcopate. As such, it retained the historical headquarters at Vatra Romaneasca. The first Episcopate’s leader was a former lay theologian, Viorel Tiofia, who was consecrated as a bishop. In 1960, the Romanian Episcopate merged with and became semi-autonomous diocese of the Orthodox Church in America. The local Romanian Orthodox parishes, their Church choirs, their youth dance troupes, and summer festivals continue to play a key role in keeping Romanian culture alive in the United States. With the fall of Communism in Romania in 1989, a massive influx of new immigrants changed the demographics of Romanian churches in America, making them both larger and more ethnically-centered. The Romanian Episcopate is part of Orthodox Church in America and one of its three ethnic dioceses (the others being Albanian and Bulgarian). Therefore, the 2020 statistics of its congregations and adherents are included in the statistics of the Orthodox Church in America.

Primary_Label: Romanian Orthodox Metropolia of the Americas
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Romanian Orthodox Metropolia of the Americas
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Romanian Orthodox Metropolia of the Americas (formerly known as the Romanian Orthodox Archdiocese in the Americas) is an autonomous part of the Romanian Orthodox Church headquartered in Bucharest, Romania. The Metropolia consists of the Archdiocese of the USA and the Diocese of Canada. Romania is called sometimes a “Latin country in a sea of Slavs,” because the Romanian language descended from the language of Roman soldiers who occupied Dacia following its conquest by Emperor Trajan in 106 AD. Historically, Romanian ethnic identity has been closely identified with the Orthodox Christian faith. Orthodox Christians comprise about 90% of the present-day Romanian population. The initial Romanian immigration to the U.S. occurred at the beginning of the 20th century and gravitated largely to industrial centers such as Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, New York, and other “Rust Belt” cities. Typically, the Romanian immigrants were accompanied by Romanian Orthodox clergy who came to America to minister to them. The first Romanian Orthodox parish in the U.S. was opened in 1904, in Cleveland, OH. In 1929, the Congress of clergy and laity held in Detroit established the “Autonomous Missionary Episcopate,” which united all the scattered parishes. The Congress also asked the Synod of the Romanian Orthodox Church in Bucharest to send a bishop. In 1935, the first Romanian Orthodox Bishop, Policarp Moruşca, arrived in America. From the beginning, the Romanian Church in North America had a significant administrative autonomy from its “Mother” Church, the Patriarchate in Bucharest. After WWII, the Communist takeover in Romania divided Romanian communities in America along political lines. The question of either remaining loyal to the Patriarchate of Bucharest or separating from the Communist-controlled Church in Romania provoked a schism among American Romanian Orthodox parishes. Since 1951, two rival Romanian Orthodox church bodies have existed in America. One was the Romanian Orthodox Archdiocese of the Americas, which remained loyal to the Patriarchate of Bucharest and has become the present-day Romanian Orthodox Metropolia in the Americas. The second was the Romanian Orthodox Episcopate of America, which (in 1960) became a semi-autonomous diocese of the Orthodox Church in America. The local Romanian Orthodox parishes, their Church choirs, their youth dance troupes, and summer festivals continue to play a key role in keeping Romanian culture alive in the United States. With the fall of Communism in Romania in 1989, a massive influx of new immigrants changed the demographics of Romanian churches in America, making them both larger and more ethnically-centered.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Rosicrucian Fellowship
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Rosicrucian Fellowship was founded in 1909 by Max Heindel (Carl Louis Van Grasshoff), a student of Rudolf Steiner, the founder of the Anthroposophical Society.

Primary_Label: Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia, The
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia, The
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia (ROCOR) is a US-based church which – with rights of broad autonomy and self-governance – is part of the Russian Orthodox Church - Moscow Patriarchate (headquartered in Moscow, Russia). ROCOR was founded in 1921 in Sremski Karlovci, Yugoslavia by Russian Orthodox bishops who had escaped from Russia after the 1917 Bolshevik revolution. After WWII, its worldwide headquarters moved from Yugoslavia to New York. A Russian banker, Sergei Semenenko, donated to ROCOR the historic Baker Mansion at 75 East 93rd St. in Manhattan. It became a Synodal Cathedral of Sign and a center for both ROCOR and the Russian emigrant community in New York. The staunchly monarchist political orientation of its clergy and members separated ROCOR from another Russian Orthodox church body in the U.S.: the Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church in America (also known as the “Russian Metropolia”) which became the Orthodox Church in America in 1970. A very important figure in ROCOR history in America was Archbishop John (Maximovitch), who was later glorified as a Saint. From 1934 to 1949, he served as an Orthodox bishop for the sizeable Russian émigré community in Harbin, China, where exiled soldiers and officers of the Anti-Bolshevik White army had settled. With the Communist takeover of China, in 1949, Archbishop John led the exodus of these Russian émigrés from China to the U.S. He played a key role in successfully lobbying the American government to amend the immigration law to allow thousands of Russians to enter the United States. In the 1950s, the arrival in America of thousands of displaced persons from the Soviet Union sparked further growth in ROCOR parishes. During Archbishop John’s tenure, the Russian Orthodox Holy Virgin Cathedral - Joy of all Who Sorrow was built on Geary Boulevard in San Francisco. To this day, it remains a spiritual and social center for the Russian community there. From the very beginning, ROCOR had a strong emphasis on preserving Russian culture, the Slavic style of Orthodox Christian worship (with Church Slavonic as the principal language of services), traditional church order, a conservative theological outlook, staunch anti-ecumenism with regard to non-Orthodox Christian Churches, and a firm anti-Communist political stance. The fall of the Communist regime and collapse of the Soviet Union (1991) led to gradual reconciliation between ROCOR and the Russian Orthodox Church - Moscow Patriarchate. In 2007, the two groups reunited, but ROCOR retained broad autonomy in its affairs and administration. During the same period, many old and declining ROCOR parishes were revitalized by an influx of new immigrants from the former USSR, who came to the United States in the 1990-2000s. They typically preferred to join ROCOR, because it retained the Russian cultural appearance and Slavic character of Orthodox worship that was familiar to them.

Primary_Label: Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church of America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church of America was a predecessor of the current Orthodox Church in America. Historically, it began in 1794 with the arrival of ten Orthodox monks from Russia to Kodiak Island in Alaska, then part of Russian America. By the beginning of the 20th century, this mission to the native peoples of Alaska had evolved into the North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church. It grew considerably due to the arrival of immigrants not only from Russia, but also from Eastern and Central Europe, and from the Middle East. At that time, the other – non-Russian – Orthodox Christian ethnic groups did not have their own bishops in America, and most of them were united under a single North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church. In the wake of the chaos caused by the 1917 Bolshevik revolution in Russia, the head of the Russian Orthodox Church, Patriarch Tikhon, directed all his parishes and dioceses outside of Russia to govern themselves autonomously. In 1924, the North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church became a self-governing Russian Orthodox Greek Catholic Church in America (also known popularly as "Russian Metropolia"). In 1970, this Church was granted autocephaly (full independence) by the "Mother Church," the Russian Orthodox Church, and was renamed the Orthodox Church in America (OCA).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Ryumonji Zen Monastery
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Ryumonji Zen Monastery is a Soto Zen Buddhist temple founded by Katagiri Roshi. The center offers various practice opportunities for both lay and ordained people. The monastery provides an environment and practice tradition that emphasizes peace and harmony with all beings. The mission of the center is to emphasize silent reflection and compassionate living.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sacha Dham Ashram
Description: Other | Other Groups | Sacha Dham Ashram was founded by Maharajji Hans Raj Swami. He gave very few verbal teachings; instead, devotees sat in his presence in order to surrender to the unconditional love of the guru and contact the limitless love of Being itself. In the early 1990s, Maharaj was brought to the West by ShantiMayi, who studied under Maharajji Hans Raji Swami.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Saddhamma Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Saddhamma Foundation, a Buddhist nonprofit organization, was founded by Venerable Sayadaw U Pandita in order to spread the teachings of Dhamma throughout the world. The foundation believes that Vipassana meditation is the only way to achieve enlightenment as taught by the Buddha.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sadhana Ashram
Description: Other | Other Groups | Sadhana Ashram was founded after Shankar Das had a vision of the Divine Mother. The daily routine includes morning chanting, meditation, and shaktipat. Sundays are dedicated to the Divine Mother and can include a fire ceremony.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sadhu Vaswami Mission
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Sadhu Vaswami Mission was founded by Sadhu T.L Vaswani. It was originally composed of all women who found a new meaning in their lives by serving Sadhu Vaswani. The organization's service programs in social, educational, cultural, and humanitarian domains have benefited the province of Sindh. The organization has several locations in the United States, including Georgia, Florida, New Jersey, and Los Angeles.

Primary_Label: Sae Taw Win II Dhamma Foundation
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sae Taw Win II Dhamma Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Sae Taw Win II Center was founded by Dr. Thynn Thynn in California as a modern representation of the Shwe Gyun lineage in Burmese Buddhism. The Center is designed for both teaching and residential community for Theravada Buddhism.

Primary_Label: Sahaja Yoga, World Council for the Advancement of (WCASY)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sahaja Yoga, World Council for the Advancement of (WCASY)
Description: Other | Other Groups | The World Council for the Advancement of Sahaja Yoga was founded by Shri Mataji. The group was founded in order to continue the growth of Sahaja Yoga and support all the national collectives; it consists of 34 members designated as world leaders. Sahaja Yoga looks to achieve a state of meditation called thoughtless awareness and looks to awaken the consciousness called the Kundalini.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sai Baba of Shirdi Temple
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Saiva Siddhanta Church
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Saiva Siddhanta Church was founded by Master Satguru Sivaya Subramuniyaswami, and was originally known as Subramuniya Yoga Order along with various other names before taking its current name in the 1970s. The church is built around the worship of Siva, which is considered to be the absolute reality, both immanent and transcendent.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sakya Monastery of Tibetan Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Sakya Monastery of Tibetan Buddhism is considered to be a place to learn from highly qualified Tibetan lamas in a traditional setting. The purpose of the group is to share and preserve Tibetan Buddhism and culture. The main meditation practice focuses on the cultivation of loving kindness and compassion.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Salafism
Description: Islam | Islam | A reform movement begun in Egypt by Jamal al-Din al-Afghani (d.1897) and Muhammad Abduh (d.1905) which looked to a conservative and literal reading of the Quran.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Salvation Army
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Salvation Army was founded in 1865 (as the Christian Mission) in England by William Booth. Though best known for its social work and its military organization, it is also a Holiness denomination.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > San Francisco Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | San Francisco Zen Center was founded by Shunryu Suzuki Roshi and his American students. Today, the center follows Suzuki Roshi's style of warm hand and heart to warm hand and heart. The center offers daily meditation, classes, lectures, and retreats. It also participates in outreach activities including food distribution to the homeless, street outreach, mindfulness training in prisons, and more.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sanbo Kyodan (Japan)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sangha of Master Kosen
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sant Shri Asarmaji Ashram
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Sant Shri Asharamji Ashram was founded by Param Pujya Sant Shri Asharamji, also known as Bapu. He has emerged as a teacher of kundalini yoga and imparts shaktipat on his followers. Bapu also emphasizes the existence of One Supreme Conscious in every human.

Primary_Label: Sardis Association of Old Regular Baptists.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Sardis Association of Old Regular Baptists.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Satmar Hassidism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | The variety of Hassidic groups in America were imported from Eastern Europe. Among the oldest and largest in America, the Satmar groups were founded relatively recently, in 1904, in Hungary, by Rebbe Yoel Teitelbaum (1887-1979). In 1946, Rebbe Yoel Teitelbaum settled in Brooklyn, New York, with a few Satmar survivors from the Holocaust. The headquarters remain there until this day, though leadership has factioned since the death of Rabbi Moshe Teitelbaum (Yoel Teitelbaum's nephew and successor), who took office in 1980 and passed away in 2006. Today, Satmar Hassidism is one of the largest Hasidic movements in the world. Members include the largest concentration of Jews opposed to the state of Israel and vocal anti-Zionists.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Satsang with Robert
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Satsang with Stuart
Description: Other | Other Groups | Satsang with Stuart was founded by Stuart Schwartz after the death of his teacher, Robert Adams. Stuart followed Adam's teaching style, which included Ramana's teachings of Advaita Vedanta along with his methods of sitting in silence and engaging with students based on the questions they asked.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Satyananda Ashram/Institute of Holistic Yoga
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Institute for Holistic Yoga, also known as Satyananda Ashram, was founded by Swami Brahmavidyananda Saraswati. Swami Brahmavidyananda Saraswati was a disciple of Swami Satyananda Saraswati, who was the founder of Bihar School of Yoga. The institute continues the beliefs and practices of the Bihar School.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Scandanavian Augustana Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Scandinavian Augustana Synod originated in 1851 when the Synod of Illinois was established by Lutheran immigrants in the Midwest. Around 1860, the Swedish and Norwegian elements in the Illinois Synod withdrew and formed the Scandinavian Augustana Synod. In 1962, the Augustana Synod was part of the merger that formed the Lutheran Church in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Schmiedeleut Hutterite Group 1
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Communal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Schmiedeleut Hutterite Group 2
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Communal | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Schwenkfelder Church in America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Other Groups | The Schwenkfelder Church in America, a small free church built around the teachings of German mystic Caspar Schwenkfeld, emerged at the time of the Protestant Reformation. It was brought to the United States by followers who arrived in 1734.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Science of Identity Foundation
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Science of Identity Foundation was founded in 1977 by Jagad Guru Siddhaswarupananda, born Chris Butler, who was a follower of His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada. Jagad Guru Siddhaswarupananda founded the association in Hawaii and has gathered an international following. The foundation is part of the Brahma Madhva Gaudiya Sampradaya line of Yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Scottish Covenanter Presbyterians
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Self Enquiry Life Fellowship
Description: Other | Other Groups | Self Enquiry Life Fellowship was founded by His Holiness Swami Vidyadhishananda after the death of master Paramahamsa Swami Hariharananda. The Fellowship follows the practices and beliefs of the Self-Realization Fellowship along with other organizations that have grown out of Hariharananda's work.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Self-Realization Fellowship
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Self-Realization Fellowship was founded on the work of Mahavatar Babji, who focused on a system of meditation that induced a deep state of tranquility and communication with God, also known as kriya yoga. The fellowship teaches the way to ananda (bliss), or self-realization or God realization.

Primary_Label: Self-Revelation Chapter of Absolute Monism/Mahatma Gandhi Memorial Foundation
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Self-Revelation Chapter of Absolute Monism/Mahatma Gandhi Memorial Foundation
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Self-Revelation Church of Absolute Monism was founded by Swami Premananda and operates independently of the Self-Realization Fellowship. Along with teaching kriya yoga (self-realization), the church also focuses on the life and work of Gandhi. The church also operates the Mahatma Gandhi Memorial Foundation as an affiliate educational and cultural center.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Separate Baptists in Christ
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Separate Baptists in Christ trace their history to the "separation" of people from the Congregational Church in the early 18th century because of their acceptance of Baptist doctrines. Although many of the Separate Baptists were absorbed by other Baptist groups through mergers, a few survived, and in 1912, some of these independent groups organized the General Association of Separatist Baptists.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Serbian Metropolitanate of New Gracanica
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Serbian Orthodox Church in North America has its origins in the immigration of Serbs, starting in the mid-19th century, from various regions of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, as well as Dalmatia and Montenegro in the Balkans. St. Sava Church in Jackson, California, is the oldest Serbian Orthodox church in America. It was founded in 1894 by Archimandrite Sebastian Dabovich, the first US-born Serbian Orthodox priest and missionary, who was later (2015) glorified as a Saint. Originally, Serbian Orthodox parishes in America were under the supervision of the multi-ethnic North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church. In 1921, in the wake of the Communist revolution in Russia, Serbian Orthodox parishes formed the Diocese of the Serbian Orthodox Church under the administration of the Patriarchate in Belgrade, Yugoslavia. In 1927, Bishop Mardarije (Uskokovich) became the first bishop of the Serbian Diocese of America and Canada. After WWII, the Orthodox Serbians in America were affected and divided by the political changes in their homeland, Yugoslavia, ruled by the Communist government of Marshal Josip Tito. In 1963, disagreements over whether to remain faithful to the Mother Church in Serbia or whether to separate themselves from the Patriarchate of Belgrade (allegedly manipulated by the Communist state) resulted in a final split between the two factions. A group of parishes led by Bishop Dionisije (Milvojevic) formed a new, independent American Serbian Orthodox Church body – the Serbian Metropolitanate of New Gracanica headquartered in New Gracanica Monastery at Third Lake, IL. This monastery is an impressive architectural replica of the Old Gracanica of Kosovo, the famous church that was continually destroyed and rebuilt in the course of the history. In 1998, the unity between the two groups was restored. In 2009, the Serbian Orthodox Church in the United States was restructured so that now it consists of three dioceses: Eastern America, New Gracanica – Midwestern America, and Western America.

Primary_Label: Serbian Orthodox Church in North and South America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Serbian Orthodox Church in North and South America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | Serbian Orthodox parishes (congregations) and dioceses in the United States are part of the Serbian Orthodox Church (headquartered in Belgrade, Serbia). The Serbian Orthodox Church in North America has its origins in the immigration of Serbs, starting in the mid-19th century, from various regions of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, as well as Dalmatia and Montenegro in the Balkans. St. Sava Church in Jackson, California, is the oldest Serbian Orthodox church in America. It was founded in 1894 by Archimandrite Sebastian Dabovich, the first US-born Serbian Orthodox priest and missionary, who was later (2015) glorified as a Saint. Originally, Serbian Orthodox parishes in America were under the supervision of the multi-ethnic North American Diocese of the Russian Orthodox Church. In 1921, in the wake of the Communist revolution in Russia, Serbian Orthodox parishes formed the Diocese of the Serbian Orthodox Church under the administration of the Patriarchate in Belgrade, Yugoslavia. In 1927, Bishop Mardarije (Uskokovich) became the first bishop of the Serbian Diocese of America and Canada. After WWII, the Orthodox Serbians in America were affected and divided by the political changes in their homeland, Yugoslavia, ruled by the Communist government of Marshal Josip Tito. In 1963, disagreements over whether to remain faithful to the Mother Church in Serbia or whether to separate themselves from the Patriarchate of Belgrade (allegedly manipulated by the Communist state) resulted in a final split between the two factions. A group of parishes led by Bishop Dionisije (Milvojevic) formed a new, independent American Serbian Orthodox Church body – the Serbian Metropolitanate of New Gracanica headquartered in New Gracanica Monastery at Third Lake, IL. In 1998, the unity between the two groups was restored. In 2009, the Serbian Orthodox Church in the United States was restructured so that now it consists of three dioceses: Eastern America, New Gracanica – Midwestern America, and Western America. Many Serbian church buildings in America are examples of classic Serbo-Byzantine architecture that include unique elements from the Middle Ages. The New Gracanica Monastery in Third Lake, IL, is an impressive architectural replica of the Old Gracanica of Kosovo, the famous church that was continually destroyed and rebuilt in the course of the history. Like the Russian Orthodox Church, the Serbian Orthodox Church adheres to the “Old” (Julian) liturgical calendar, which is 13 days behind the “New” (Gregorian) secular calendar that is used by most Christian Churches around the world. A noteworthy characteristic of Serbian Orthodox chant is that it frequently incorporates folk melodies. Many of these melodies are now sung in the English language and are being used in worship by other American Orthodox communities as well.

Primary_Label: Seventh Day Baptist General Conference of the United States and Canada
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Seventh Day Baptist General Conference of the United States and Canada
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Seventh Day Baptist General Conference was organized in 1802 and continues the practice of sabbatarianism among English-speaking Baptists.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Seventh Day Baptists (German)
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | Under the leadership of Johann Conrad Beissel in 1728, a group of German Baptists separated from their brethren over the issue of the sabbath. They formed a self-sufficient, monastic community at Ephrata, Pennsylvania. This monastic community eventually dissolved with the death of the last member in 1900, but a small group still meets on the property, and the Salemville church within this tradition continues to host a small but active congregation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Seventh-day Adventist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Adventist | The Seventh-day Adventist Church grew out of the work of William Miller, who predicted the Second Coming of Christ in 1843/44. After the failure of the prophecy, many attracted to his message reorganized. Some who continued as Adventists and also accepted sabbath worship found new leadership in the persons of Ellen G. White and James White. The church was formally organized in 1965.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Seventh-day Adventist Reform Movement
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | When organized on the heels of the American Civil War, the Seventh-day Adventists had a strong commitment to pacifism. Several generations later, that commitment began to slip and members were allowed to participate in World War I. This change of policy caused a break in fellowship, primarily in Europe, when a number of members who still adhered to the pacifist position were unable to affect a return to the church's traditional position. In 1925, they founded the Seventh-day Adventist Reform Movement.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Seventh-Day Baptists
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | Seventh-Day Baptists began in London in 1617 under the leadership of John Trask. The group worshipped on both Saturdays and Sundays, but eventually the Sabbatarians (those who practiced Saturday worship) were in control. In 1664, the Reverend Stephen Mumford, who was a member of a Seventh-Day church in London, came to America and affiliated with Baptists in Newport, Rhode Island. He began to raise the issue of Saturday worship - to both support and opposition - and formed his own congregation of Seventh-Day Baptists in 1671. This was the first of such churches in America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Shadhili Sufi Order
Description: Islam | Islam | The Shadhili Sufi Order, which exists in a number of loosely associated branches, looks to Abu al-Hasan Ali al-Shadhili (d.1258) as its founder.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Shambhala International
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Shambhala International was founded by Chogyam Trungpa Rinpoche, a Tibetan teacher of the Kagyu lineage, who fled Tibet in 1959 and came to North America in 1970. Chogyam Trungpa claimed to share pre-Buddhist teachings (Shambhala); these teachings outlined a way of warriorship to enable fearless and direct living. The group was originally known as Vajradhatu, but after Chogyam Trungpa's death in 1987, his son succeeded him and later changed the group’s name to Shambhala International.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shanti Mandir
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Shanti Mandir was founded by Swami Nityananda after withdrawing from Siddha Yoga Dham and renouncing vows as a sannyasin. In December of 1989, Nityananda reaffirmed his sannyasin vows along with his commitment to Muktananda and to God's work. Conflict arose as members of Siddha Yoga Dham challenged his authority; however, he was ultimately inducted into the association and became the youngest mahamandaleshwar.

Primary_Label: Shanti Yoga Institute and Yoga Retreat
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Shanti Yoga Institute and Yoga Retreat
Description: Other | Other Groups | Shanti Yoga Institute and Yoga Retreat was founded by Shanti Desai, who received shaktipat (conferring of spiritual power in a student by guru) in India. When he returned to the United States, he opened the center in New Jersey. He has published several books and designed his instruction of yoga for a Western audience.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Shaolin Temple
Description: Other | Other Groups | Shaolin Temple is the birthplace of martial arts and Chan Buddhism. The first outpost of the temple came in 1995, in Flushing, New York, where many first generation Chinese Americans live. The work was initiated by the Ven. Shi Goulin, the leader of the temples overseas mission. It has since created new centers in Manhattan and Brooklyn, and in 2013, a new national headquarters was opened in Northport, New York.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Shingon Mission of Hawaii
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Shingon Mission of Hawaii is a Buddhist school that studies and shares the faith of Shingon esoteric Buddhism. The mission follows the original tenets established by Kobo Daishi. The temple is the largest esoteric Buddhist temple in Hawaii.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Shingon Mission USA
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Primary_Label: Shinjinkai: The Japanese Martial Arts Society
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Shinjinkai: The Japanese Martial Arts Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Shinjinkai: The Japanese Martial Arts Society was founded by Meido Moore Sensei and a year later was ordained in the Zen Buddhist tradition. The main focus of Shinjinkai is the intensive study of Japanese warrior arts and to follow shungyo, or deep mind-body training to live fully and benefit others.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Shinnyo-En
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Shinnyo-en, a lay Buddhist order, was founded by Shinjo Ito and his wife, Tomoji Lto. The two mastered a meditation training known as sesshin to help trainees understand the teachings of Buddha and apply them to their surroundings. Followers are encouraged to be well-rounded members of society and express the spirit of Buddhism in all things.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Shinshu Kyokai Mission of Hawaii
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Shinshu Kyokai Mission of Hawaii was founded as a independent group of Jodoshinshu faithful. The group is devoted to the life and teachings of Shinran Shonin, who lived a life of gratitude and reverence based on Amida Buddha's vows.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Shinto
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Primary_Label: Shree RamKibir Bhakta Samaj of USA
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Shree RamKibir Bhakta Samaj of USA
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Shree RamKibir Bhakta Samaj of USA, originally known as Bhakta Association until 1992, was founded by Chaganbhai Bhakta. The organization emphasizes eight essential teachings: humans are spiritual beings and an integral part of God; divine love is essential to all creation; ahimsa (non-violence); one God is the source of all creation; the guru's grace is the path to ultimate self-realization; the purpose of life is bhaki; all humans are equal in God's eyes; love should lead to service.

Primary_Label: Shree Swaminarayan Agyna - Upasana Satsang Mandal
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shree Swaminarayan Agyna - Upasana Satsang Mandal
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shree Swaminarayan Gurukul, Rajkot, USA
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Shree Swaminarayan Gurukul, USA was founded when Swami Dharmajivandas left the southern branch of the Swaminarayan movement. Dharmajivandas's new organization, the Swaminarayan Gurukul, was considered a separate entity; however, it maintained a close relationship to the southern branch of the Swaminarayan movement. Swaminarayan Gurukul became fully free from both the North and South branches after neither side was willing to perform the formal installation ceremony for the temple room.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shree Swaminarayan Sidhhant Sajivan Mandal
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shree Swaminarayan Vadtal Gadi
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Shree Swaminarayan Vadtal Gadi was founded after an Almighty Supreme God (Bhagawan) incarnated himself as Bhagwan Shree Swaminarayan. He founded Shree Swaminarayan Vadtal Gadi on the principles of "pure Upasana and Bhakti" by remaining on the path of Dharma. The group is considered the organization of the original Shree Swaminarayan Sampraday, the Southern diocese of Shree Laxminarayan Dev.

Primary_Label: Shri Krishna Pranami Association of U.S.A. and Canada
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shri Krishna Pranami Association of U.S.A. and Canada
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Shri Krishna Pranami Association of U.S.A. and Canada was founded by Shri Devchandraji. The organization is the North American representative of the Pranami religion, which is a form of Hinduism. The group's holy book is known as Tartam Sagar and contains 18,000 verses written by Prannathji.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Shri Ram Chandra Mission
Description: Other | Other Groups | Shri Ram Chandra Mission was founded by Sri Ram Chandraji Maharaj of Shahjahanpur, also known as Babuji. The mission was founded in memory of Babuji's master, Samarth Guru Mahatma Shri Ram Chandraji Maharaj of Fatehgarh. The mission follows the sahaj marg system of meditation, which is embodied by 10 maxims that lay out a daily schedule. Each day the disciples' goal is to complete oneness with God.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Shri Shivabalayogi International Maharaj Trust
Description: Other | Other Groups | Shri Shivabalayogi International Maharaj Trust was founded by Shivabalayogi. He began initiating students into the practice of meditation with the goal of darshan, the vision of the realized person. He taught his works through silence and experience. He also gave concentrated vibhuti (ash), believed to heal the body and mind, and trained disciples to achieve bhava samadi (divine trance). There were various ashrams dedicated to Shivabalayogi throughout India, with his first Western centers appearing in London and then the United States throughout the 1980s-1990s.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Shri Surya Narayan Mandir
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Shri Surya Narayan Mandir was founded by Dharmacharya Pt. Hardowar Panday. The primary goal of the group is to bring out the essence, philosophy, and teachings of Shastras through services, programs, and classes in an understandable way. The hope of the organization is that all members use their teachings to reach the ultimate goal of mankind, Moksha.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Sivananda Yoga Vedanta
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Sivananada Yoga Vedanta was founded by Swami Vishnudevananda and named after his guru, Swami Sivananda. The center's mission is to spread peace, health, and joy through yoga. The group has made yogic teachings easily understandable and breaks down yoga teachings in five points: (1) proper exercise, (2) proper breathing, (3) proper relaxation, (4) proper diet, and (5) positive thinking and meditation.

Primary_Label: Slovak Evangelical Lutheran Synod of the United States of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Slovak Evangelical Lutheran Synod of the United States of America
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | Formerly named Slovak Evangelical Church of the Augsburg Confession in the United States of America (1902-1903) and Slovak Evangelical Synod of the Augsburg Confession in Pennsylvania (1903-1913), the Slovak Evangelical Lutheran Synod of the United States of America was renamed to the Synod of Evangelical Lutheran Churches in 1946. It aligned theologically with the Lutheran Synodical Conference and joined the Missouri Synod in 1971.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Slovak Zion Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Slovak Zion Synod was formed in 1919 as the Slovak Evangelical Lutheran Zion Synod. Although it joined the United Lutheran Church in America in 1920, it remained a separate synod and continues as a separate synod of the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America today. It is a non-geographic synod of the church, and their ministries are spread throughout the U.S. and Canada.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Social Brethren
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Social Brethren church was a congregational association of churches founded in 1867 by people who had left several different evangelical churches. One of the main aims of the association was to welcome Christians who fell on both sides of the debate over slavery; therefore, they did not support discussing politics from the pulpit. It is unclear what happened to this association after 1975.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Society for Humanistic Judaism
Description: Judaism | Judaism | By the 1960s, the more liberal wing of the reform movement was moving even beyond even Reform theological approaches and adopted a Humanist stance. They differed from both the American Ethical Union in their desire to maintain the religious trappings of the synagogue led by a rabbi. That stance led to the formation of the Society for Humanistic Judaism founded in 1963 by Rabbi Sherwin Wine who proposed the idea of continuing a Jewish religious life apart from any affirmation of the existence of god.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Society of Abidance in Truth
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Society of Abidance in Truth (SAT) is focused on the teachings of nonduality as revealed by Bhagavan Sri Ramana Maharshi. Nonduality teaches that the self is infinite and eternal, and places an emphasis on self-knowledge. The group maintains a center in California where individuals can come learn about the teaching, practice it, and strive to attain self-knowledge.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Soka Gakkai
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Soka Gakkai was founded in 1930 as a lay organization of Nichiren Shoshu. It was first established in the United States in 1960, and formally separated from Nichiren Shoshu in 1991. A highly evangelical group, it has become the largest Buddhist group in the United States and in many European countries.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Soka Gakkai International–USA (SGI–USA)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Soka Gakkai (literally, "Society for the Creation of Value") was founded in Japan 1930 by Tsunesaburo Makiguchi upon the publication of his work "Soka kyoikugaku taikei" (The System of Value-Creating Pedagogy). The organization responds to the needs of individual happiness and empowerment of peace, culture, and education. It is exclusively a lay membership that follows the teachings of the Nichiren school of Mahayana Buddhism. Although founded in 1930, it was established in the United States during a visit by its third president, Daisaku Ikeda, in 1960. The group's name at the time was Nichiren Shoshu Soka Gakkai of America (NSA), and an international group formed in 1975. The group took on its present name when it formally separated from Nichiren Shoshu in 1991. A highly evangelical group, Soka Gakkai has become the largest Buddhist group in the United States and in many European countries.

Primary_Label: Solid Rock Association of Old Time Baptists.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Solid Rock Association of Old Time Baptists.
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sonoma Mountain Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Sonoma Mountain Zen Center was founded by Jakusho Kwong Roshi in order to preserve his zen lineage. The center focuses on the teachings of Zen and has various programs including zazen (meditation), sesshin (extended retreats), seminars, and sittings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Soto Mission (United States)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Soto Mission was founded by Rev. Hosen Isobe. In 1932, the mission was appointed a subsidiary of two main temples in Japan. Shortly after, the general head office was established to manage the Soto Zen Buddhism in North America. The group focuses on Soto Zen Buddhism and its education center works to establish Zen Buddhism in the Western world. In 2014, Soto Zen temples and centers were registered in by the Soto head office in Japan, although most of these centers are independent.

Primary_Label: Sought Out Church of God in Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Sought Out Church of God in Christ
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Sought Out Church of God in Christ was founded in 1947 after Mother Mozella Cook, previously a member of the Church of God in Christ, felt a divine calling to found a church. The church was established in Brunswick, Georgia.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Source School of Tantra Yoga
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Source School of Tantra Yoga was founded by Charles Muir. Muir encourages students to express themselves as both spiritual and physical beings. The organization believes that sex and spirit are connected, and that sexual activity is both a means of meditation and a spiritualizing force of the universe.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > South Atlantic Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Southeastern Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Southern Baptist Convention
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Southern Baptist Convention was founded in 1845 by Baptist congregations in the American South who rejected the anti-slavery tendencies in the Northern Baptist Convention and withdrew to carry forward independently.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Southern Illinois Christian Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Southern Methodist Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Southern Methodist Church was founded in 1940 by members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, who rejected that church's merger with the Methodist Episcopal Church and Methodist Protestant Church to form The Methodist Church in 1939.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Southwide Baptist Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Southwide Baptist Fellowship was founded in 1956 at a conference at Dr. Lee Roberson's church in Chattanooga, Tennessee. The group follows the beliefs in premillennialism and demands separation from all forms of modernism, especially the National Council of Churches. The group was originally known was the Southern Baptist Fellowship until 1963, when the current name was adopted.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Sovereign Grace Churches
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Sovereign Grace Ministries, formerly known as People of Destiny International (until 2003) and Sovereign Grace Ministries (until 2014), dates back to the early 1970s and a ministry by Larry Tomczak and C. J. Mahaney. The ministry efforts of Tomczak and Mahaney led to the establishment of Covenant Life Church in Gaithersburg, Maryland, in 1978. In 1982, they founded People of Destiny International (PDI) to provide resources and training for churches, primarily through commissioning apostles to work with individual churches. In 1998, Tomczak left PDI over theological differences, and the group changed its name to Sovereign Grace Ministries. In 2014, they again changed their name to better reflect their identity as a family of churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Spirit Rock Insight Meditation Center
Description: Other | Other Groups | Spirit Rock Insight Meditation Center is a spiritual training organization that is grounded in Buddha's teachings. The center's mission is to provide teachings that manifest both wisdom and compassion, which is achieved through dharma teachings and retreats. The practice of insight meditation develops mindfulness, which allows one to pay attention to each moment of life and see clearly the truth of each experience.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Spiritual Realization Institute
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Spiritual Realization Institute was founded by Geoffrey Giuliano, who legally changed his name to Jagannatha Dasa. After recommiting himself to his religious faith in 1990, he and his wife established Sri Puri Dhama Vaishnava, which was then legally incorporated in 1996 as The Spiritual Realization Institute (SRI). The Institute describes itself as an "international resource for those interested in the Vedic arts and sciences as well as a fully functioning educational and cultural institution.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Springwater Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Springwater Center, originally known as Genesee Valley Zen Center until 1986, was founded by Toni Packer after leaving the Rochester Zen Center. The center was founded because of Packer's desire to no longer rely on traditional koans and practices and instead work with the raw materials of everyday life. The center has held retreats since 1985, with no rituals or ceremonies.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sravasti Abbey
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Sravasti Abbey was founded in 2003 by the Venerable Thubten Chodron. Established in Washington state, the Abbey follows the tenants of Tibetan Buddhism and the Dalai Lama. As of 2021, the Abbey had 16 nuns and 1 monk.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sree Rama Dasa Mission
Description: Other | Other Groups | Sree Rama Dasa Mission was founded by Brahmashree Neelakanta Gurupada. After three years, he personally named his successor Swami Sathyananda, who was an advocate for various paths of yoga. The group is a registered charitable society that serves in the fields of education, training, and other social services.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Sri Chaitanya Sanga
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Sri Chaitanya Sanga, previously known as Gaudiya Vaishnava Society, was founded by B. V. Tripurari Swami who was a previous leader in the International Society for Krishna Consciousness (ISKCON). The Sanga is at one with the ISKCON in belief and practice. However, the society now flows from the lineage of Sridhara Maharaj, a lineage not found in ISKCON.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Sri Chaitanya Saraswati Mandal
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Sri Chaitanya Saraswat Mandal was founded by Bhakti Rakasaka Sridhara Deva Goswami, the godbrother of A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, who was the founder of International Society of Krishna Consciousness (ISKCON). It is considered a separate organization from ISKCON, and after Prabhupada's death, some of his disciples turned to Sridhara for guidance and broke with the society. Sridhara then founded Sri Chaitanya Saraswat Mandal as the American branch of Sri Chaitanya Sarawati Math.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sri Chinmoy Centre
Description: Other | Other Groups | Sri Chinomy Centre was founded by Sri Chinomy Kumar Ghose, who encouraged an active, dynamic life of service to the divine in humanity. He encouraged his followers to engage in regular meditation, living and working in the world, vegetarianism, and celibacy. He guided his students in meditative discipline and spiritual growth.

Primary_Label: Sri Lanka Sangha Council of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Sri Lanka Sangha Council of North America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Sri Lanka Sangha Council of North America, also known as the Sri Lanka Buddhist Sangha Council of the United States and Canada, was formed by a group of Sri Lankan-American monks. Some of the most prominent centers include Dharma Vijaya Buddhist Vihara Society and the California Buddhist Vihara Society, both located in California.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sri Mathrudevi Vishwashanthi Ashram Trust
Description: Other | Other Groups | Sri Mathrudevi Vishwashanthi Ashram Trust (SMVA Trust) was founded in 1995 as an international association to administer various forms of free health care, aid, and education to those in need. The association is led by Amma Sri Karunamayi and is a NGO of the Department of Public Information of the United Nations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Sri Premananda Centers
Description: Other | Other Groups | Sri Premananda Center was founded by Sri Premananda, who dedicated his life to the divine and helping people understand the true purpose of their existence.

Primary_Label: SRV Associations of Oregon, San Francisco, and Hawaii
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > SRV Associations of Oregon, San Francisco, and Hawaii
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekanda (SRV) Associations of Oregon, San Francisco, and Hawaii were founded in 1993 by Babaji Bob Kindler as independent associations closely tied to the Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekananda Association of the West founded by Lex Hixon. The Associations follow the teachings of Sri Ramakrishna, Sri Sarada Devi, and Swami Vivekananda, and practice various forms of non-dualistic yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Stauffer Mennonite (independent groups)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Stauffer Mennonite Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Stauffer Mennonite Church was founded by a conservative group led by Jacob Stauffer who believed the "ban" against deviating members should be applied more severely. In protest they withdrew from the Mennonite Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Strict Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Strict Baptists formed primarily in England as a loose association of churches with Calvinistic theology and restricted views of communion (closed table). They gradually strengthened through the 1860s and 1870s as some Particular Baptists denied the full deity of Christ. Strict Baptists united around the Gospel Standard magazine where they promoted the full deity of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Sufi Order International
Description: Islam | Islam | The Sufi Order International was founded by Pir Hazrat Inayat Khan (1882-1927) a member of the Chishti Sufi Order who worked in Europe and North America beginning in the second decade of the twentieth century. He originally left the order in the hands of a female disciple, Rabia Martin. She led the order to become a part of the following of India teacher Meher Baba. In the 1960s, Khan's son, Pir Vilayat Khan assumed his father's mantle and reestablished the Sufi Order.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Swaminarayan Mandir Vasna Sanstha
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | Swaminarayan Mandir Vasna Sanstha was founded Swami Devandandasji, also known as H. D. H. Bapji. Devandandasji acknowledged the lineage of Swaminarayan Mandir Vasna Santha's founder, Muktajeevan, along with the divine status of Abji Bapashree. The group originated in India, but a small group of members migrated to the United States where they founded a center in New Jersey.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Spiritualist > Swedenborgian Church
Description: Other Christians | Spiritualist | Founded in North America in 1792 as the Church of the New Jerusalem, the Swedenborgian Church was organized as a national body in 1817 and incorporated in Illinois in 1861. Its biblically-based theology is derived from the spiritual, or mystical, experiences and biblical studies of the Swedish scientist and philosopher Emanuel Swedenborg (1688-1772).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Swedish Evangelical Free Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Swedish Evangelical Free Church was formed in 1884. It was composed of congregations that preferred an association of autonomous congregations rather than a typical denominational structure. In 1950, it merged with the Norwegian-Danish Evangelical Free Church association to form the Evangelical Free Church of America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > SYDA Foundation/Shree Muktananda Ashram
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | SYDA Foundation was established in order to make the teachings of Siddha Yoga available to individuals around the world. The foundation oversees the Siddha Yoga curriculum, which focuses on self-realization, as well as the publication of books and magazines. The foundation is headed by the board of directors.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Synod of Evangelical Lutheran Churches
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | Lutherans from Czechoslovakia began to migrate to the United States in the 1870s and early congregations were formed in Illinois, Pennsylvania, and Minnesota. Attempts to organize began in the 1890s, and the Slovak Evangelical Lutheran Synod was finally established at Connellsville, Pennsylvania, in 1902. After taking the name Synod of Evangelical Lutheran Churches (1946-1971), it merged into the Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod in 1971.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Syriac-Greek Antiochian Orthodox Catholic Church
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Syriac-Greek Antiochian Orthodox Catholic Church has its origins in what was previously known as the American Orthodox Catholic Archdiocese. Originally formed in 1892, it was one of the first Orthodox Church bodies in America to offer the Divine Liturgy and other worship services in the English language. During the following decades, the worship practices of this Church were affected both by the Syrian Orthodox (i.e., Oriental Orthodox) as well as Russian and Greek Orthodox (i.e., Eastern Orthodox) traditions. Later, the church also operated under the name Syro-Russian Orthodox Catholic Church. The current name was adopted in 2008. This was done with the intention to better describe the origins of this Church: both the Syriac (“Oriental”) and Greek (“Eastern”) Orthodox Patriarchates of Antioch. Also, in 2008, the Synod of Bishops voted unanimously to return to the original mandate of this Church: to be an American Orthodox Church which uses a Western-style Liturgy.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church of Antioch
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church in the United States is part of the global Syrian (Syriac) Orthodox Church of Antioch headquartered in Damascus, Syria. It is one of the Oriental Orthodox Churches (the others being Coptic, Armenian, Ethiopian, Eritrean, and Malankara-Indian) which, unlike Eastern Orthodox Churches, recognize the validity of only the first three Ecumenical Councils. The Syrian Church traces its origin to the ancient Patriarchate that was established in Antioch (present-day South Central Turkey) by St. Peter the Apostle. Most members of this Church in America have their ancestral origins in Syria, Iraq, Lebanon, Turkey, and India. The first influx of Syrian Orthodox Christians to the U.S. dates back to the late 1800s, when religious persecution in the Ottoman Empire forced their emigration. In 1907, the first Syrian Orthodox priest, Fr. Hanna Kourie, arrived in the United States. In 1927, the Virgin Mary Syrian Orthodox church in West New York, NJ, was opened. It was the first church building in America constructed originally as a Syrian Orthodox Church. In 1957, the Archdiocese of the Syrian Orthodox Church in the United States and Canada was established. The diocesan see, St. Mark Cathedral, was in Hackensack, NJ, before relocating (1994) to a new complex in Teaneck, NJ. The 1960s-1980s brought a new wave of Syrian Orthodox immigrants to America, caused by continued unrest in the Middle East and religious discrimination against Christians in mostly Islamic countries. In 1995, the Syrian Orthodox Church in the USA was divided into two Archdioceses: Eastern United States and Western United States. The majority of Syrian Orthodox Christians in America are first-generation immigrants, with most clergy still coming from overseas. In worship services, their parishes primarily use the Classical Syriac language (a dialect of an ancient Aramaic language). Several cultural and educational church-affiliated institutions have been established, such as the Aramaic American Association and the American Foundation for Syriac Studies.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Tampico Amish Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | The Tampico Amish Mennonites date back to the Amish-Mennonite movement of the late 1800s. The group organized around the teachings of John Kauffman, a layman Amish-Mennonite who preached in a trance. Though the broader Amish-Mennonite movement rejected this practice, Kauffman started a separate congregation with his own followers. As a group, they maintain many of the conservative practices of the Amish but do allow for some modern conveniences. The teachings of Kauffman are used alongside scripture, as members consider him a prophet of his time.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Tao
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Tara Mandala
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Tara Mandala is a Tibetan Buddhist retreat center founded by Tsultrim Allione Tsultrim. The center was inspired by the western retreat center and designed to create a place for the reemergence of the sacred feminine. The center aligns with the Buddhist tradition of partnerships with earth, animals, family, and respect for other wisdom traditions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Temple of Kriya Yoga
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Temple of Kriya Yoga was founded by Goswami Kriyananda and followed the yoga systems of Yogananda. Kriyananda teaches the oneness of law, spirit, and love and their identity with God. The goal of the temple is to affirm the meaningfulness of the universe and to achieve illumination and fulfillment through kriya yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > Temple Society
Description: Unclassified | Communal | The Temple Society was founded by Christoph Hoffman in 1861 in Wurttemberg, Germany. Hoffman desired for people to live in such a way as to create the "kingdom of God" on earth, and sought settlement in the Holy Land. Eventually, six Temple Societies flourished in Palestine between 1869 and 1948, when the state of Israel was founded. Templars since spread to other countries, such as Australia, Russia, Germany, and the United States. Australia is home to the largest and most active group of Templars (with their Temple Society founded in 1950), though there are no known Templars left in the United States.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Tendai-shu
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Tergar International
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Tergar International was founded in 2009 and is a partner of Tergar Asia. The association is under the teaching and leadership of Yongey Mingyur Rinpoche and his primary lineage from the Kagyu school of Tibetan Buddhism. Yongey Mingyur Rinpoche, a charismatic leader, integrates many traditional teachings with modern science.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Texas Buddhist Association
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Texas Buddhist Association was founded by Chinese American lay Buddhists who were under the leadership of Ven. Jan Hai. The work is focused in its two active centers along with the Chinese-speaking community. Ven. Jan Hai still serves as the president today and has opened three other centers.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Texas Synod
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The Texas Synod was founded in 1851 and was part of the 1930 merger that formed the American Lutheran Church.

Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > The Movement Center
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Movement Center was founded after Swami Rudrananda's death, as his followers reorganized as the Nityananda Institute. The reorganization was led by Swami Chetanananda, who was head of the ashram in Bloomington, Indiana. After the headquarters were relocated to Cambridge, Massachusetts and then Portland, Oregon, the group adopted its current name (in 2008). The center includes a community of followers living the spiritual life of Swami Chetanananda. The center's goal is to make spiritual life accessible to Westerners.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > The Sambodh Society, Inc.
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Sambodh Society, Inc., was founded in 1997 as part of the pre-existing Sambodh Foundation; both are led and were started by H. H. Swami Bodhananda Saraswati, who is part of the Advaita Vedanta tradition. The Society is based out of the Sambodh Center for Human Excellence (Kalamazoo, MI).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Theosophical Society in America
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Theosophical Society in America is the American Branch of the international Theosophical Society headquartered in Adyar, Madras, India. It was founded in 1875 by Helena Petrovna Blavatsky, Col. Henry S. Olcott, William Q. Judge, and others. It promotes studies of a wide range of academic and spiritual disciplines to uncover Divine Wisdom.

Primary_Label: Theosophists, Independent Society of New York
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Theosophists, Independent Society of New York
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Independent Society of New York was founded in 1899, out of the original Theosophical Society (1875) by J. H. Salisbury and Donald Nicholson. The association later attracted H. W. Percival, whose publication, The World, outlived the Independent Society and remains active. This association is distinct from the Australia-based Independent Theosophical Society.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Three Treasures Zen Community
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Three Treasures Zen Community was founded by McMahon Roshi. The community's goal is to create an open and compassionate environment where Zen teaching can be accessible to lay and monastic students.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Thubten Dhargye Ling
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Thubten Dhargye Ling, a name meaning "land of increasing Buddha's teachings," was founded by Geshe Tsultim Gyletsen, a teacher of Tibetan Buddhism. Geshe Gyletsen also founded two affiliated centers, one named Mahakaruna Tibetan Buddhist Meditation Center, located in Alaska, and one small center in Colorado.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Tibetan Buddhist Learning Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Tibetan Buddhist Learning Center, originally known as Lamaist Buddhist Monastery until 1984, was founded by Kalmuck-Mongolian in central New Jersey. The center's main task is to teach its students Tibetan Buddhism and to covey basic knowledge of the many facets of Tibetan Buddhism.

Primary_Label: Tibetan Center for Compassion and Wisdom
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Tibetan Center for Compassion and Wisdom
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Tibetan Center for Compassion and Wisdom was founded by Arjia Rinpoche and is dedicated to preserving Tibetan Culture and sharing Buddhism in the West. The center offers a place where both members and the public can explore and practice Buddhist teachings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Tibetan Mongolian Buddhist Cultural Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Tibetan Mongolian Buddhist Cultural Center was founded by Thubten Jigme Norbu. The Center was originally known as Tibetan Cultural Center, but in 2007 the group changed to its current name. The mission of the organization is fostering and preserving Tibetan and Mongolian cultures in the United States.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Tijaniyya Sufi Order
Description: Islam | Islam | Tijaniyya Sufi Order was founded by Ahmad b. Muhamad as-Tijani (1737-1815) around 1784 in Algeria.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Todaiji Hawaii Bekkaku Honzan
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Todaiji Hawaii Bekkaku Honzan was founded by Bishop Tatsusho Hirai. The center follows Kegon, a traditional form of Buddhism, in which nature is said to exist in opposite attributes including: universality/specialization, integration/differentiation, and similarity/diversity. The center located in Hawaii is the only center in the United States.
