Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Triratna Buddhist Community
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Triratna Buddhist Community was founded by Ven. Maha Sthavria Sangharakshita, and up until 2010 the group was known as the Friends of the Western Buddhist Order. The community focuses on expressing Buddhism in a modern way while emphasizing the unity of Buddhism through teachings.

Primary_Label: Triumph the Church and Kingdom of God in Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Triumph the Church and Kingdom of God in Christ
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | Triumph the Church and the Kingdom of God in Christ is a predominantly black Pentecostal church founded in 1902 by Elder E. D. Smith, who had a private revelation concerning the Pentecostal baptism of fire. The association believes they bring peace and are set apart from other Christian churches which they call the Church Militant.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Truc Lam Bamboo Forest Zen
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > True Buddha School
Description: Other | Other Groups | The True Buddha School was founded by Grand Master Shen-Yen, who teaches a path to enlightenment similar to the Gelugpa Tibetan tradition. Daily practice includes meditation, chanting, the repetition of mantras, and the visualization of one's personal deity.

Primary_Label: True Orthodox Church of Greece (Holy Synod in Resistance): American Exarchate
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > True Orthodox Church of Greece (Holy Synod in Resistance): American Exarchate
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The American Exarchate of the True Orthodox Church of Greece was part of the Holy Synod in Resistance (also known as the “Metropolitan Cyprian Synod”), one of several Orthodox Old Calendarist groups in Greece which existed between 1986 and 2014. After WWI, many Orthodox churches moved away from the “Old” Julian liturgical calendar, instead adopting the “New” Gregorian calendar, which is 13 days ahead of the Julian and is also used in the secular world. In 1924, this reform was introduced in the Orthodox Church of Greece which caused strong opposition among some clergy and laity. The calendar question as such was, however, only one of the causes for resistance. The opposition saw the adoption of the new calendar as a symbol of the strengthening ecumenical movement and the growing influence of other, non-Orthodox, Christian Churches. In this opposition view, the purity of the Orthodox faith was being compromised. Originally led by Metropolitan Chrysostomos of Florina, those opposed to the new calendar became known as the Old Calendarists, or the Church of True Orthodox Christians of Greece. In the past, the Old Calendarists were actively persecuted in Greece (especially in the 1950s), which resulted in their immigration to the U.S., where they founded various Greek Old Calendar churches. They are recognizable by the absence of electric lighting and pews, and certain practices including traditional Byzantine chant and frequent all-night vigils. Back in Greece, the Old Calendarist community was plagued by internal divisions and ultimately fractured into several groups. One of the most sizeable was the Holy Synod in Resistance, also known as Metropolitan Cyprian Synod. Formed in 1985, this group has followers in the U.S. who were united into the American Exarchate (later, Archdiocese of Etna), with its center in the monastery of St. Gregory Palamas in Etna, CA. Unlike most other Old Calendarist groups, the group of Metropolitan Cyprian did not consider mainline Orthodox Churches to be “schismatic” and were willing to share sacraments with them, but still considered the adoption of the New Calendar a grave error. In 2014, after long negotiations, the Synod in Resistance merged with the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of Greece (most sizeable of all Old Calendarist groups) and ceased to exist. Most churches and monasteries in the American Exarchate joined the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of America, which is a semi-autonomous part of the Church of the Genuine Orthodox Christians of Greece.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Truevine Baptists Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Truth Consciousness/Sacred Mountain Ashram/Jyoti Mandir
Description: Other | Other Groups | Truth Consciousness was founded by Swami Amar Jyoti. The group is devoted to his perception of truth, along with the transformation of human consciousness into divine consciousness. The group offers year round programs, including satsang held twice a week, guru aarati, weekly group meditations, and weekend retreats.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Turkish Orthodox Church in America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Turkish Orthodox Church in America was an independent Orthodox Church body loosely affiliated with the Autocephalous Turkish Orthodox Patriarchate (also known simply as the Turkish Orthodox Church). The latter was established in 1922, in Kayseri, Turkey, by the priest Pavlos Karahisarithis, a staunch supporter of the nationalistic ideas of Mustafa Kemal Ataturk. Karahisarithis became the Patriarch of the newly founded Church and took the name of Papa Eftim I. In the United States, in 1966, some 20 congregations formed the Turkish Orthodox Church in America under the leadership of Archbishop Civet Kristof, an African-American physician originally named Christopher M. Cragg. The Turkish Orthodox Church in America continued to exist throughout the 1970s, but in the early 1980s Archbishop Civet moved to Chicago and opened a health clinic. The association was eventually dissolved.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Twelve Tribes
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Two-Seed-in-the-Spirit Predestinarian Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Two-Seed-in-the-Spirit Predestinarian Baptists began as followers of Daniel Parker's teaching, spread first through his 1826 pamphlet, “Views on the Two Seeds.” He preached that one’s eternal destiny is determined by which seed is planted in a person at birth: the good seed by God or the bad seed by Satan. His followers became more ardent than Parker and persisted in small numbers through the turn of two centuries. Besides their unique doctrine, they are similar to primitive Baptists.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > U.S.A. Shaolin Temple
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The U.S.A Shaolin Temple was founded by Shifu Shi Yan Ming. At the temple, Shifu teaches Chan Buddhism, which is considered to be the core of Shaolin Martial arts, and is known as the action meditation. It is one of several centers in the United States that perpetuates the traditions of the Shaolin Temple that was founded in the Song Mountains of central China.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Udumbara Zen Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Udumbara Zen Center was established in the 1980s as an outpost of the Zen Center of San Francisco, later becoming an independent center. The director and head priest of Udumbara is Nehan Myoshin Tricia Teater, who received Dharma transmission from Roshi Diane Martin in 2012. The center features a diverse community of Buddhist practitioners who are particularly devoted to the practice of Zen.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Ukrainian Orthodox Church of America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The first distinctly ethnic Ukrainian Orthodox parish in America, Holy Trinity, was founded in 1915 in Chicago by Fr. Gregory Chomycky. Later, it became the St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cathedral on Cortez Street. In the early 1900s, the Ukrainian immigrants in America were increasingly aware of their particular ethnic identity – a process which was reinforced by the short-lived independence of the Ukraine as a state (1918-21) before it was absorbed into the Soviet Union. In 1924, Archbishop John (Theodorovich) arrived from the Ukraine and assumed the leadership over the newly formed American-Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA. Under his administration, the Church saw rapid growth. However, Archbishop John had been sent to America by the Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church, which only existed in the Ukraine during 1921-27, by which time the Communist government had arrested all its bishops and most of the clergy. Because this Autocephalous Church had not received canonical recognition by other Orthodox Churches throughout the world, the validity of Archbishop John’s Episcopal ordination was questioned by many clergy and church members. As a result, in 1929, a rival Ukrainian Orthodox Church body was formed: the Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America. In 1936, it was recognized and accepted by the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, which solidified its status as a “canonical” (“lawful”) Church. The two Ukrainian Orthodox groups existed until the arrival (1947) of Archbishop Mstyslav (Skrypnyk), a bishop of the Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church, which had been re-established in the territories of Eastern Poland and Western Ukraine during the period of occupation by German Nazis. Archbishop Mstyslav was instrumental in merging most parishes of Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America into the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA (1950). Still, some Ukrainian Orthodox clergy and parishes refused to join the unified Church. As a result, the Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America, although much smaller, continued to exist. In 1995, the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA was also accepted into the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople. This event finalized the recognition of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA by other Orthodox Churches and led to the complete absorption of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America.

Primary_Label: Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the U.S.A.
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA (UOC) is a US-based autonomous church under the spiritual supervision of the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, headquartered in Istanbul, Turkey. The history of the UOC goes back to 1915, when the first Ukrainian Orthodox parish, Holy Trinity, was founded in Chicago by Fr. Gregory Chomycky. Later, it became the St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cathedral on Cortez Street. The Ukrainian immigrants in America were increasingly aware of their distinct ethnic identity – a process which was reinforced by the short-lived independence of Ukraine as a state (1918-21) before it was absorbed into the Soviet Union. Some ethnically Ukrainian parishes and clergy from other Orthodox and Eastern Catholic (“Uniate”) groups decided that they should have their own Church. In 1924, Archbishop John (Theodorovich) arrived from Ukraine and assumed the leadership over the newly formed American-Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA. Under his administration, the Church saw rapid growth. However, Archbishop John had been sent to America by the Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church, which only existed in the Ukraine during 1921-27, by which time the Communist government had arrested all its bishops and most of the clergy. Because this Autocephalous Church had not yet received canonical recognition by other Orthodox Churches throughout the world, the validity of Archbishop John’s Episcopal ordination was questioned by many clergy and lay church members. As a result, in 1929, the second rival Ukrainian Orthodox Church body was formed, which was called the Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America. In 1936, it was recognized and accepted by the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, which solidified its status as “canonical” (“lawful”) Church. The two Ukrainian Orthodox groups existed until the arrival (1947) of Archbishop Mstyslav (Skrypnyk), a bishop of the Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church, which had been re-established in the territories of Eastern Poland and Western Ukraine during the period of occupation by German Nazis. Archbishop Mstyslav was instrumental in merging most parishes of Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America into the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA (1950). In 1995, the UOC of USA was accepted as an autonomous Church into the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, an event that finalized the recognition of the UOC of the USA by other Orthodox Churches. The present-day membership of UOC is very diverse: second- and third-generation Ukrainian-Americans, new immigrants from the post-Communist Ukraine, and American converts to the Orthodox faith (former Roman Catholics and Protestants). The center of the UOC of the USA is in Bound Brook, NJ. The extensive complex includes the Consistory administrative offices, St. Andrew Memorial Church, cemetery, mausoleum, St. Sophia Seminary, museum, the Archive and Research Center, and the Ukrainian Cultural Center. The best examples of traditional Ukrainian church architecture in the United States include St. Katherine Parish, Arden Hills, MN; St. Andrew Cathedral, Silver Spring, MD; St. Andrew Parish, Boston, MA; St. Andrew Parish, Los Angeles, CA; St. Vladimir Cathedral, Parma, OH; St. Mary Protectress, Rochester, NY; St. Mary Cathedral, Southfield, MI; St. Luke Parish, Warners, NY; and Sts. Peter and Paul Parish, Youngstown, OH.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Unaffiliated Amish-Mennonite (Conservative)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Unaffiliated Amish-Mennonite (Spring Garden network)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Unaffiliated Conservative Mennonite
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Unaffiliated Conservative Mennonite (Grandview fellowship)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > Unaffiliated Friends Meetings
Description: Mainline Protestant | Friends/Brethren | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Unaffiliated Mennonite (Wisler Christian Fellowship)
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Unfettered Mind
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Unfettered Mind was founded by Ken McLeod and follows much of the Kagyu Tradition, as well as practices from Mahayana Buddhism. His pioneered approach to teaching was used to effectively address spiritual development in a modern culture. The organization is now a loosely organized network for teachers and students.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Unification Church
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Union American Methodist Episcopal Church
Description: Black Protestant | Methodist | The Union American Methodist Episcopal Church, formed in 1865 as the African Union American Methodist Episcopal Church in the United States of America and Elsewhere, is one of two denominations which grew out of The Union Church of Africans, a movement within the Methodist Episcopal Church led by two African American members, Peter Spencer and William Anderson. They maintained an identity separate from what would become the African Union First Colored Methodist Protestant Church because they chose to be governed by episcopal polity.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Union Church of Africans
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Union Church of Africans, founded in 1813 when Peter Spencer and William Anderson left the Asbury Methodist Episcopal Church, was the first church in the United States to be originally organized by, and afterward wholly under the care of, black people. The association suffered long-term division over polity structure, culminating in the 1865/1866 formation of the Union American Methodist Episcopal Church and the African Union First Colored Methodist Protestant Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Union of American Hebrew Congregations
Description: Judaism | Judaism | The Union of American Hebrew Congregations was formed in Cincinnati in July of 1873 through the leadership of Rabbi Isaac Mayer Wise (a Reform Judaism leader in North America) and delegates of 28 reformed congregations. At the time, the primary objective of this new group was the founding of a seminary. Accordingly, two years later, in 1875, the Hebrew Union College opened in Cincinnati, Ohio, with Rabbi Wise as its president. In 2003, the group changed its name to the Union for Reform Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Union of Messianic Jewish Congregations
Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | The Union of Messianic Jewish Congregations was formed in 1979 as the first congregational association of among Jewish followers of Jesus, normally called by his Hebrew name, Yeshua. The goals of the group are to advocate for Messianic Judaism, develop Messianic synagogues, and train Messianic leaders. In 1981, the Union adopted the statement of faith of the National Association of Evangelicals, though modified the terminology for Jewish Christians.

Primary_Label: Union of Orthodox Rabbis of the United States and Canada
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Union of Orthodox Rabbis of the United States and Canada
Description: Judaism | Judaism | The first of the basic organizations formed to carry the Orthodox cause was the Rabbi Elchanan Theological Seminary (1896) which evolved into Yeshiva University. Two years later it was joined by the national synagogue association, the Union of Orthodox Jewish Congregations in America (1898), which was followed by the Union of Orthodox Rabbis of the United States and Canada (1902). The group is also known the Agudat HaRabbonim. The Orthodox rabbis split in 1935 when the English speaking rabbis formed the Rabbinical Council of America.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Liberal > Unitarian Universalist Association
Description: Other Christians | Liberal | The Unitarian Universalist Association was formed in 1961 by the merger of the American Unitarian Association and the Universalist Church in America. The merger represents the coming together of the two oldest segments of the liberal tradition. Unitarian Universalism is a creedless religion with Judeo-Christian roots. It draws also from Eastern, humanist, and other religious traditions, and encourages its members to seek religious truth out of their own reflection and experience.

Primary_Label: United American Free Will Baptist Church General Conference
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > United American Free Will Baptist Church General Conference
Description: Unclassified | Baptist | The United American Free Will Baptist Church General Conference was incorporated under its current name in 1968. It arose from the 1898 Negro General Conference of the Free Will Baptists; Free Will Baptists' first congregation was formed in 1727. The United American Free Will Baptist Church General Conference is theologically Arminian and has altered the Apostle's Creed in certain places.

Primary_Label: United American Free Will Baptist Denomination, Inc.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > United American Free Will Baptist Denomination, Inc.
Description: Black Protestant | Baptist | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > United Baptists
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The United Baptists movement was founded in 1787 by a merger of several Regular Baptists' and Separate Baptists' associations. Most of the groups within this tradition have dropped "United" from their names, and they exist within larger Baptist bodies, like the Southern Baptist Convention. However, there are still United Baptist associations in existence in Kentucky, West Virginia, and Missouri.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > United Brethren in Christ
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The United Brethren in Christ formed in 1800 as a result of the work of Philip Otterbein, a German Pietist, with the help of Martin Boehm. Otterbein and Boehm began evangelistic work among the German immigrants in Pennsylvania. In 1946, this group merged with the Evangelical Church to form the Evangelical United Brethren.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > United Catholic Church
Description: Catholic | Other Groups | The United Catholic Church is a fellowship of independent Catholic churches founded in 1996 under the leadership of Bishop Robert M. Bowman.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > United Christian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The United Christian Church was founded in 1877 by George W. Hoffman and former members of the United Brethren in Christ. The activities of the church include an annual camp meeting, missions work, and services in prison and elderly homes.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) > United Church of Christ
Description: Mainline Protestant | Congregationalists (United Church of Christ) | The United Church of Christ was founded in 1957 as a merger of the Congregational-Christian Churches and the Evangelical and Reformed Church. It continues the American Congregational tradition.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > United Church of God
Description: Other Christians | Adventist | In the 1990s, the Worldwide Church of God rejected a set of distinctive doctrines and practices that had been advocated by its founder Herbert W. Armstrong. As a result, a large percentage of its ministers and members left the church and formed a number of splinter groups (as many as 150 have been counted), though most are very small house churches with but a single small center of activity. The great majority of members adhered to one of the three relatively large groups - the Living Church of God, the United Church of God, and the Philadelphia Church of God - which vary among themselves on the number of former distinctives they have maintained.

Primary_Label: United Church of God in Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Church of God in Christ
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The United Church of God in Christ was founded in 1980 by Marshall Carter III, his wife, and other former members of the Church of God in Christ (COGIC). The movement to form a new organization began several years earlier when Carter began to disagree with the national church on various matters, but especially the matter of successorship. These disagreements prompted Carter's Lynwood Park congregation to withdraw from COGIC, and they were soon joined by other churches, which united together to form this group in 1980. The church still maintains many of the beliefs and practices of its parent body.

Primary_Label: United Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The United Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Ground of Truth was founded in 1946 by Bishop Clifton Okley in Los Angeles, California. Although raised a Baptist, Okley was a minister in the Church of the Living God, the Pillar and Group of Truth (Jewell Dominion) before a disagreement with the church prompted him to leave and start a new denomination with his followers.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Covenant Churches of Christ
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The United Covenant Churches of Christ began as the Pentecostal Churches of Christ, which was formed in 1992 in Cleveland, Ohio, under the leadership of Bishop J. Delano Ellis. Under the leadership of Ellis's successor, Bishop Larry Trotter, the church grew in numerous ways, eventually prompting a name change in 2008 to United Covenant Churches of Christ.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > United Danish Evangelical Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The United Danish Evangelical Lutheran Church was formed in 1896 by the merger of two Danish immigrant groups: the Blair Synod and and the North Church.

Primary_Label: United Episcopal Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > United Episcopal Church of North America
Description: Mainline Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > United Evangelical Lutheran Church
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The United Evangelical Lutheran Church was originally formed in 1896 as the United Danish Evangelical Lutheran Church. This resulted from the merger of two Danish immigrant groups: the Blair Synod and and the North Church. In 1945, the group dropped "Danish" from their name.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Holy Church of America
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The United Holy Church of America is a predominantly black Pentecostal church founded in response to a revival led by Reverend Isaac Cheshier in North Carolina in 1886, after which many participants struggled to work with pre-existing denominations. It was first named the Holy Church of North Carolina, then the Holy Church of North Carolina and Virginia. The Church assumed the unincorporated form of its present name in 1916, which it incorporated in 1918 in Durham, North Carolina. The Church gradually became international and suffered internal division from 1977 to 1998, at which point the church reunified.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > United House of Prayer
Description: Black Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Primary_Label: United House of Prayer for All People
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United House of Prayer for All People
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The United House of Prayer for All People was founded in the mid-1920s by Bishop Charles Manuel Grace, popularly known as "Sweet Daddy" Grace. The church was officially incorporated in Washington, D.C., on June 20th, 1927.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > United Lutheran Church in America
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The 1918 merger of the General Synod, the General Council, and the General Synod of the South formed the United Lutheran Church in America, the largest Lutheran body through much of the twentieth century.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > United Lutheran Church of America (Norwegian)
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The United Norwegian Lutheran Church in America was formed in 1890 as the merger of the three synods: the Norwegian Augustana Synod, the Norwegian-Danish Evangelical Lutheran Conference, and the Norwegian Anti-Missouri Brotherhood.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > United Methodist Church
Description: Mainline Protestant | Methodist | The United Methodist Church, the third-largest church in America, continues the Methodist movement founded in the 18th century in England by John Wesley. In 1968, a long process of consolidation of various Methodist churches in the United States culminated in the formation of the United Methodist Church by the merger of the Methodist Church (1939-1968) and the United Evangelical Brethren.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > United Missionary Church
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The United Missionary Church began as the Mennonite Brethren in Christ, which was formed in 1883 by a merger between the United Evangelical Mennonites and a small group of River Brethren in Pennsylvania and Ohio. In 1947, the present name was assumed to reflect a movement away from its Mennonite Background. In 1969, it merged with the Missionary Church Association to form the Missionary Church, Inc. (U.S.).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Pentecostal Church International
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The United Pentecostal Church International, a Oneness Pentecostal church, was founded in 1945 as a merger of the Pentecostal Church, Inc., and the Pentecostal Assemblies of Jesus Christ. It is the largest of the predominantly white Oneness Pentecostal churches.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Pentecostal Churches of Christ
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The United Pentecostal Churches of Christ was formed in 1992 in Cleveland, Ohio, under the leadership of Bishop J. Delano Ellis. Under the leadership of Ellis's successor, Bishop Larry Trotter, the church grew in numerous ways, eventually prompting a name change in 2008 to United Covenant Churches of Christ.

Primary_Label: United Pentecostal Council of the Assemblies of God
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > United Pentecostal Council of the Assemblies of God
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | The United Pentecostal Council of the Assemblies of God was founded by George A. Phillips and Sidney J. Davis. The original group consisted of African Americans from the Caribbean and several people from North Carolina. The council is a Trinitarian Pentecostal body that confirms the authority of the Bible and faith in God. Divine healing is stressed and all believers are said to seek and expect spiritual gifts. The council is considered to be an international association of congregations and missions.

Primary_Label: United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The United Presbyterian Church of North America and Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A. merged in 1958 to form the United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.

Primary_Label: United Presbyterian Church of North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > United Presbyterian Church of North America
Description: Unclassified | Presbyterian/Reformed | The United Presbyterian Church of North America was formed in 1858 by a merger of the Associate Presbyterian Church and the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church.

Primary_Label: United Reformed Churches in North America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > United Reformed Churches in North America
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | The United Reformed Churches in North America was founded officially in 1996 by former members of the Christian Reformed Church (CRC) who were unhappy with the latter’s developing positions on scripture, Arminianism, women in ministry, and evolution. Congregations previously unassociated with the CRC joined the United Reformed Churches in North America. This conservative association has eight classes, and the synod meets at least once every three years.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > United Seventh-Day Brethren
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | The United Seventh-Day Brethren, a small Sabbatarian Adventist church, was founded in 1947. Local churches acted autonomously while affirming that souls are not immortal and certain "unclean" meats should not be consumed. As of 1980, there were 4 congregations; it is unknown what happened since then.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Communal > United Society of Believers (Shakers)
Description: Unclassified | Communal | The United Society of Believers was founded in 1747. In the 1950s, Mother Ann Lee, who had received visions from God after the death of her 4 infant children, began to assume control of the Quakers previously influenced by James Wardley and his wife. The group, popularly called Shakers for their ecstatic dance in worship, came to America in 1774 after suffering persecution in England. The group is pacifistic and celibate; the only remaining Shakers live in the Sabbathday Lake Village in Maine.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > United Synagogue of America
Description: Judaism | Judaism | In the early 1900s, two champions of the Conservative Judaism movement were Solomon Schechter and Cyrus Adler. One of these men, Solomon Schechter, organized the United Synagogue of America in 1913, though it is now known as the United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism (name change in 1992). The group serves as the association of Conservative Jewish congregations in North America and maintains a network of nearly 600 congregations. The group's main mission is to strengthen synagogues and invite others into Conservative Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > United Synod South
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The United Synod South dates back to 1863 when synods from various southern states withdrew from the General Synod because of the Civil War and what they perceived as offensive resolutions adopted by the General Synod with respect to the war. The group had multiple names before adopting a doctrinal bases in 1884, resulting in the the organization of the United Synod in the South.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > United Trungram Buddhist Fellowship
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The United Trungram Buddhist Fellowship was founded by His Eminence Trungram Gyaltrul Rinpoche to share his teachings of Buddhism and to preserve the Tibetan heritage. The group is focused on spreading their principles of nonviolence, social justice, and mutual understanding.

Primary_Label: United Wesleyan Methodist Church of America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > United Wesleyan Methodist Church of America
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The United Wesleyan Methodist Church of America, a predominantly African American fellowship, was founded in 1905 by former members of the Methodist Church in the Caribbean and the Americas who had migrated to the United States. In 1978, there were four congregations in New York city.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Friends/Brethren > United Zion Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Friends/Brethren | The United Zion Church, originally known as United Zion's Children, was founded in 1855 by Matthias Brinser and other former members of the Brethren in Christ. In 1967, the Brethren in Christ asked the United Zion Church for forgiveness for the action of the council in 1855. The following year, the United Zion Church offered a formal statement of complete forgiveness. The two groups still remain separate denominations, and these resolutions became the basis for the mission field and higher education.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Unity Churches, Association of
Description: Other Christians | Other Groups | The Association of Unity Churches, known since 2011 as Unity Worldwide Ministries, traces its origin back to the Unity movement founded in the 1880s by Charles S. Fillmore and his wife, Myrtle Fillmore. After a number of developments in the movement, the first Unity convention was held in 1923, and in 1925, a Unity annual conference was established to govern teachings and regulate leaders of Unity groups. Although this group was chartered in 1934, it went through several reorganizations before the Association of Unity Churches emerged as a distinct organization in 1966. In 2011, the group changed its name to Unity Worldwide Ministries.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Unity of the Brethren
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Methodist | The Unity of the Brethren, previously the Evangelical Union of Bohemian and Moravian Brethren, is related to the Moravian Church. It was founded by A. Chumsky and H. Juren in the mid-19th century and took its present name in 1919. The group practices infant baptism and open communion with all Christians.

Primary_Label: Universal Christian Spiritual Faith and Churches for All Nations
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Universal Christian Spiritual Faith and Churches for All Nations
Description: Unclassified | Pentecostal | The Universal Christian Spiritual Faith and Churches for All Nations came together in 1952 as the National David Spiritual Temple of Christ Church Union (Inc.) U.S.A., St. Paul's Spiritual Church Convocation, and King David's Spiritual Temple of Truth Association merged. Dr. David William Short, former leader of the National David Spiritual Temple of Christ Church Union, controlled the new association from the beginning alongside a national executive board. They do not affirm that speaking in tongues is the only indication that one has been granted the Holy Spirit.

Primary_Label: Universal Fellowship of Metropolitan Community Churches
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Universal Fellowship of Metropolitan Community Churches
Description: Mainline Protestant | Other Groups | The largest of the several churches serving the homosexual community is the Universal Fellowship of Metropolitan Community Churches, founded in 1968 in Los Angeles by Pentecostal minister Troy Perry.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Liberal > Universalist Church in America
Description: Unclassified | Liberal | The Universalist Church in America was founded in 1793. In 1961, it merged with the American Unitarian Association to form the Unitarian Universalist Association of Congregations.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > USA Devanand Yoga Center
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The USA Devanand Yoga Center was founded by Guruji Luis F. Rivera. The center believes in the importance of humanity's physical-mental-spiritual balance. The center also promotes attaining world peace through individual peace, and trains students in the importance of integrating, strengthening, and harmonizing all aspects of their personality. It also teaches the ancient technique of Mantra Yoga Meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Vairotsana Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Vairotsana Foundation was founded by Bhakha Tulku Rinpoche. The foundation is dedicated to propagating and preserving the spiritual teachings of the Nyingma tradition. The foundation is considered a teaching vehicle in the West of the Ven. Bhakha Tulku Rinpoche, who resides in Bhutan at the Tamshing Gonpa in Bumthang Vat.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Vajrakilaya Centers of North America
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Vajrakilaya Centers of North America was established by Orgyen Kusum Lingpa. The centers are named for the Tibetan deity Vajrakilaya, who is the supreme destroyer of obstacles to the attainment of enlightenment.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Vajrayana Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Vajrayana Foundation was founded by Lama Tharchin Rinpoche. The foundation follows the Dudjom Tersar Myingma tradition. The foundation also offers Ngorndro practice, which is considered the gateway to the advanced practices of Dzogchen meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Valley Zendo
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Valley Zendo was established as an outpost of Antaiji, a Soto Center in Japan. Kosho Uchiyama Roshi sent his students to serve as resident teachers with a strict schedule of practice and retreats maintained.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Unclassified > Vanguard Presbytery
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Unclassified | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Vedanta Center
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Vedanta Centre
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Vedanta Centre was founded by Swami Paramananda. The center follows Vedanta and teaches that Vendanta's tenets are that truth or God is one without a second, an individual's real nature is divine, that all paths lead to the same goal, and that the purpose in life is to realize God is within one's own soul.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Vedanta Societies (Vivekananda)
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Vivekananda Vedanta Society, known formerly as the Vedanta Society, is a branch of the Ramakrishna Math and Mission and dates to 1893, when Swami Vivekananda arrived in Boston and taught Vedanta. Swami Vivekananda was a follower of Sri Ramakrishna. The first branch of the society was established in New York (1894), with subsequent branches forming in San Francisco (1900) and Boston (1909). It is the oldest Hindu association in the United States of America, and it focuses on the divine within persons. It does not have a centralized organizational structure.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Vedanta Society
Description: Other | Other Groups | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Vedantic Center
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Vedantic Center was founded by Alice Coltrane. She teaches that the purpose of human life is to advance spiritually. The center emphasizes to its members that Satya Sai Baba is the avatar of this age.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Veerashaiva Samaja of North America
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Veerashaiva Samaja of North America was founded in 1978 to support Indian Americans who follow Shaivite Hinduism, many of whom left India in the 1960s. Shaivite Hinduism is also known as Veerashaisism or Langayatism. Veerashaivites follow five Pancharas ("rules of conduct") using eight Ashtavaranas ("sheilds"); they value the agamas over the Vedas. The association now has chapters across the United States.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Venture Church Network
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | Venture Church Network, originally known as the Conservative Baptist Association of America, was founded in 1947 by former ministers and members of the Northern (American) Baptist Convention. The organization changed to its present name in 2019.

Primary_Label: Vicariate for the Palestinian and Jordanian Orthodox Christian Communities in America
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Vicariate for the Palestinian and Jordanian Orthodox Christian Communities in America
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Vicariate for the Palestinian and Jordanian Orthodox Christian Communities is a semi-autonomous church body within the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America (the 2020 data on the Vicariate's parishes and membership are included in the data of the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese). Its administrative center is at St. George Orthodox Church in San Francisco, CA. The Vicariate was formed in 2009, after a long negotiation between the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople (headquartered in Istanbul, Turkey, and represented in the U.S. by the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America) and the Patriarchate of Jerusalem (headquartered in Jerusalem, Israel). The two Patriarchates disputed the ecclesiastical status of a group of Palestinian and Jordanian Orthodox Christian communities in America. The Patriarchate of Jerusalem is one of the four original ancient Patriarchates of Eastern Orthodox Christianity (along with Constantinople, Alexandria, and Antioch). It was established in 451 AD at the fourth Council of Chalcedon. Today, most members of the Patriarchate of Jerusalem are Orthodox Christian Arabs living in Israel, Jordan, and the territory under the control of the Palestinian Authority. During the 20th century, Orthodox immigrants originating from the territory of the Patriarchate of Jerusalem established parishes in America. However, their governance from the distant Jerusalem had always been difficult; for practical reasons, oversight for these communities was eventually given to the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, to be governed locally through the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America. The last attempt to form a separate Church body in America linked to the Patriarchate of Jerusalem was made in 1993, when seventy lay leaders from the parishes that would later form the Vicariate traveled to Jerusalem to meet with Diodoros, Patriarch of Jerusalem at that time. Despite this effort, in 2009 the two Patriarchates agreed to give pastoral supervision of these parishes to the Ecumenical Patriarch via the Archbishop of the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America. As a result, the present Vicariate was formed. The Vicariate uses the "Old" (Julian) church calendar, which is 13 days behind the “New” (Gregorian) calendar that is also used by the secular world. Worship services are conducted primarily in English and Arabic. The Vicariate has a rather unique practice of ordination to priesthood. Instead of seeking ordination for themselves, candidates for pastoral work are nominated and presented to the bishop by their fellow parishioners. It is believed that this practice fosters a high degree of cooperation and mutual accountability between clergy and laity and contributes to the atmosphere of trust within each parish family. Despite the Palestinian-Jordanian origins of the parishes, their membership is increasingly diverse and includes people from a wide range of Orthodox ethnicities as well as converts to the Orthodox faith.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Volunteers of America
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Volunteers of America movement was founded in 1896 by Ballington and Maud Booth, both former leaders in the Salvation Army. They practice baptism and communion as well as broadcast worship services.

Primary_Label: Vrindavan Institute for Vaisnava Culture and Studies
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Vrindavan Institute for Vaisnava Culture and Studies
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Vrindavan Institute for Vaisnava Culture and Studies (VRINDA) was founded in 1984 by Srila Bhakti Aloka Paramadvaiti Maharaja. VRINDA has many centers around the world. They claim to be "under the divine protection of Sri Sri Guru Gouranga Radha Vrajesvara," practice Bhakti Yoga, and encourage chanting the Maha Mantra.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Islam > Wahabbism
Description: Islam | Islam | A reform movement based on the teachings of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (d.1792), whose approach aligned closely with the Hanbalite School of Sunni Islam and opposed both Shi'a and Hanafi emphases. It has been most influential in Saudi Arabia and played a role in Arabia's separation from the Hanafi-dominated Caliphate.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Washington-Franklin Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Primary_Label: Way of the Cross Church of Christ
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > Way of the Cross Church of Christ
Description: Black Protestant | Pentecostal | The Way of the Cross Church of Christ was founded in 1928 by Henry C. Brooks. Brooks was an independent black minister who founded a church in Washington, D.C., that became part of the Church of Our Lord Jesus Christ of the Apostolic Faith. However, when the latter group's founder, Robert Clarence Lawson, wanted Brooks's congregation to join another, Brooks decided to leave Lawson's jurisdiction and start a new organization.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Baptist > Wayne Trail Missionary Baptist Association
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Baptist | The Wayne Trail Missionary Baptist Association was formed in 1961 as an advisory association for participating Baptist churches. Congregations send messengers to the meetings in proportion to their size. The association uses the King James Version of the Bible and promotes evangelism.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Weaverland Mennonite Conference
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Wesleyan Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Holiness | The Wesleyan Church is a Holiness church that continues the tradition of the Wesleyan Methodist Church, which was founded in the 1820s by former members of the Methodist Episcopal Church. In 1968, the Wesleyan Methodist Church merged with the Pilgrim Holiness Church to form the Wesleyan Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Wesleyan Holiness Association of Churches
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Wesleyan Holiness Association of Churches is a conservative Holiness denomination founded in 1959 by Reverend Glenn Griffith, previously the founder of the Bible Missionary Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Holiness > Wesleyan Methodist Church of America
Description: Unclassified | Holiness | The Wesleyan Methodist Church of America arose in 1843 as an abolitionist denomination. Led by Orange Scott, this new group of Wesleyan Methodists called for an end to slavery, planted anti-slavery churches in the south, and were active in the Underground Railroad. They were also among the earliest proponents of the ordination of women. In 1968, this group merged with the Pilgrim Holiness Church, which was very similar in both theology and governance, to form the Wesleyan Church.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Mennonite/Amish > Western Conservative Mennonite Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Mennonite/Amish | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) > Western Orthodox Christian Church
Description: Orthodox | Eastern Liturgical (Orthodox) | The Western Orthodox Christian Church is an independent Church body which is not recognized by the mainstream Orthodox Churches as “canonical” (“lawful”). The Church was founded in 1911, when Archbishop Arnold Harris Mathew, representing the Old Catholic Church in Great Britain, converted to the Orthodox faith. The first leader of this Church in America was the missionary bishop from Scotland, Rudolph de Landas Berghes. The Church was originally known under the name the Old Roman Catholic Western Orthodox Church. In worship practices, the Western Orthodox Church uses the Western Rite, namely the Liturgy of St. Gregory (also known as Tridentine Mass), celebrated either in English or Latin.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Presbyterian/Reformed > Westminster Evangelical Presbyterian Church
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Presbyterian/Reformed | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Lutheran | The Wisconsin Synod began with the organization of German Lutheran immigrants in Wisconsin in the 1850s. It spread as similar synods from other states associated with it. It is among the most conservative of Lutheran bodies.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Won Buddhism
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Won Buddhism was founded by Pak Chungbin, better known by his pen name, Sot'aesa. Won Buddhism is a form of Korean Mahayana Buddhism that focuses on the worship of one-circle figure rather than any image of Buddha. The organization looks to bring ancient Buddhist truth into contemporary society so the teachings can be used for practical purposes.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > World Agudath Israel
Description: Judaism | Judaism | World Agudath Israel was established as a political expression of the Haredi Movement as an alternative to the recently formed Zionist movement.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Lutheran > World Confessional Lutheran Association
Description: Unclassified | Lutheran | The World Confessional Lutheran Association was founded in 1965 in Cedar Rapids, Iowa, as Lutherans Alert National by a group of conservative Lutheran pastors. The pastors and associated lay people were concerned about trends in the American Lutheran Church and other synods that they felt were contrary to the historical beliefs and confessions of Lutheranism. In 1984, the association adopted the title World Confessional Lutheran Association in order to represent the growing international members.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Pentecostal > World Evangelism Fellowship
Description: Evangelical Protestant | Pentecostal | World Evangelism Fellowship is a reorganization of ministries following the break between Pentecostal pastor Jimmy Swaggart and the Assemblies of God. The break followed a scandal in which the Assemblies of God instituted a suspension on Swaggart, but Swaggart did not accept the penalty. Instead, he reorganized his ministries in 1988.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > World Zen Fellowship
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The World Zen Fellowship was founded by Pohwa Sunim who was a Korean Ven. Zen Master. The organization follows the teaching of "lineage Zen." This is a special transmission from Buddha to lineage masters that occurs outside of doctrinal teachings.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Adventist > Worldwide Church of God
Description: Unclassified | Adventist | Among those who participated in the founding of the Church of God (Seventh-day, Salem, WV) with its emphasis on sabbaterianism and the celebration of Jewish festivals rather than Christian holidays, was Herbert W. Armstrong. In 1934, he founded a radio ministry out of Eugene, Oregon, which he named the Radio Church of God. That ministry grew into the most successful branch of the sabbatarian Church of God, and was eventually renamed the Worldwide Church of God. Over the years, Armstrong proposed a number of innovative doctrines, all of which were rejected in the 1990s by his successors. By the end of the 1990s, the Worldwide Church of God had left its Adventist roots and aligned to the contemporary theologically orthodox Evangelical movement. In 2009, the group would formally accept a new identity and name, becoming Grace Communion International.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Yasodhara Ashram
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Yasodhara Ashram Society was founded by Swami Sivananda Rahda. She was considered one of the most important leaders on kundalini yoga, and created a ceremony called the Divine Light Invocation. The group offers courses in various types of yoga.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Yeshe Khorlo USA
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Yeshe Khorlo USA (or wheel of wisdom) follows the teachings of Padma Linpa, who created the Drugpa branch of the Kagyu school of Tibetan Buddhism. The Center is located in Crestone, Colorado, is considered to be the main center in the United States.

Primary_Label: Yeshe Nyingpo (His Holiness Shenphen Dawa Rinpoche)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Yeshe Nyingpo (His Holiness Shenphen Dawa Rinpoche)
Description: Other | Other Groups | Yeshe Nyingpo was founded in 1976 by His Holiness Dudjom Rinpoche, who was believed to be a reincarnation of one of Buddha's personal disciples. He was also believed to be a reincarnation of Cheuchung Lotsawa, a disciple of Padmasambhava who brought Buddhism to Tibet. This broader group has multiple locations in the U.S. In 1980, Dudjom Rinpoche established Orgyen Cho Dzong, a spiritual retreat center in New York, under the guidance of his son, Shenphen Dawa Rinpoche. In 1983, the Southern California Region Yeshe Nyingpo was formed (originally known as Yeshe Nyingpo Los Angeles) and was led by the Venerable Gyatrul Rinpoche. It took its present name in 1993.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Yeshe Nyingpo (Venerable Gyatrul Rinpoche)
Description: Other | Other Groups | Yeshe Nyingpo was founded in 1976 by His Holiness Dudjom Rinpoche, who was believed to be a reincarnation of one of Buddha's personal disciples. He was also believed to be a reincarnation of Cheuchung Lotsawa, a disciple of Padmasambhava who brought Buddhism to Tibet. This broader group has multiple locations in the U.S. In 1980, Dudjom Rinpoche established Orgyen Cho Dzong, a spiritual retreat center in New York, under the guidance of his son, Shenphen Dawa Rinpoche. In 1983, the Southern California Region Yeshe Nyingpo was formed (originally known as Yeshe Nyingpo Los Angeles) and was led by The Venerable Gyatrul Rinpoche. It took its present name in 1993.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Yin Shun Foundation/Bodhi Monastery
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Bodhi Monastery was founded in 2000 by Master Jen-Chun in New Jersey. The Monastery is non-sectarian but follows the teaching of Master Yin-Shun and Pali Buddhism.

Primary_Label: Yoga in Daily Life, American Association of
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Yoga in Daily Life, American Association of
Description: Other | Other Groups | Yoga in Daily life was founded by Paramhans Swami Maheshwarananda. The organization is centered around a few main principles, such as building physical, mental, and spiritual health, and respect for life. Additionally, their principles include tolerance for all religions, protection of human rights, the protection of the environment, and preservation of nature.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Other Groups > Yoga Research Foundation
Description: Other | Other Groups | The Yoga Research Foundation was founded by Swami Jyotirmayananda. The foundation was originally known as Sanatan Dharma Mandir until 1969. Jyotirmayananda teaches integral yoga, with the foundation offering classes in yoga philosophy, ancient Hindu text, hatha yoga, and meditation.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Yogi Divine Society (Illinois)
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Yogi Divine Society (Illinois) is guided by H. D. H. Hariprasad Swamiji and its main goals are to serve individuals, families, and society with humanitarian and spiritual activities based on faith in Lord Swaminarayan.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Hinduism > Yogi Divine Society (New Jersey)
Description: Hinduism | Hinduism | The Yogi Divine Society was founded by His Divine Holiness Guru Haroparasad Swamiji. The society was formed as a result of the excommunication from the Bochasanwasi Swaminarayan Sanstha (BAPS). The group is similar to BAPS; however, there is a more open approach to the participation of women.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Yogi Tsoru Dechen Rinpoche Foundation
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Yogi Tsoru Dechen Rinpoche Foundation (YTDR) was founded by the Ven. Tulku Tsori Rinpoche. The main goal of this organization is to provide access to Buddha Dharma (path of awakening) and give compassion and wisdom to all human begins; Tulku Tsori also has a great devotion to children's issues.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Yongey Buddhist Center
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Yongey Buddhist Center is an organization dedicated to supporting the teachings and work of the seventh Yongey Mingyur Rinpoche. The group began after a meeting with Mrs. Mei Yen Chen Ladle and Yongey Mingyur Rinpoche, and serves as the base for Yongey Mingyur Rinpoche's global teaching work.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Judaism > Young Israel
Description: Judaism | Judaism | Some younger leaders associated with emerging Conservative movement found Orthodoxy more appealing and founded young Israel as a new association of orthodox believers. The association began in 1912 at Jewish Theological Seminary and was started by two professors, Israel Friedlander and Mordecai M. Kaplan, and Rabbi Judah Magnes. The goal of the group was to unite Orthodox Jewish youth of the Lower East Side of New York City. In 1926, the National Council of Young Israel was incorporated, and has since expanded through the Jewish community. Today, the group claims over 25,000 member families and approximately 150 branch synagogues throughout the United States, Canada, and Israel.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Yun Lin Temple
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Yun Lin Temple was founded by Prof. Thomas Lin-Yun and is a center of Black Sect Tantric Buddhism of Tibet. The older Bon practices of the Black Sect included healing practices, magic, and philosophy. The Yun Lin Temple attempts to rethink tradition and incorporate modern thinking.

Primary_Label: Zen Buddhist Order of Hsu Yun (ZBOHY)
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Buddhist Order of Hsu Yun (ZBOHY)
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Zen Buddhist Order of Hsu Yun (ZBOHY) is an exclusively electronic ministry on the internet. It was founded in 1997 by Grandmaster Jy Din Shakya of Hsu Yun Temple. The priests of this group follow the teaching of Hui Neng, Lin Chi, and the modern teachings of Hsu Yun.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Buddhist Temple of Chicago
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Zen Buddhist Temple of Chicago was founded in 1949 by Soyu Matsuoka Roshi and is the largest Zen center in the Midwest. The main focus of the group is meditation service, including a lecture by one of the priests.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Center of Los Angeles
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Zen Center of Los Angeles was formed under the leadership of Hakuyu Taizan Maezumi Roshi, who was a seminal figure in the development of Zen Buddhism. Their mission is to know the self, maintain the precepts, and serve others through the teaching, training, and transmission of Zen Buddhism. The center supports activities such as Zazan, weekly lectures, and beginning classes.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Com. of NY
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Community of Baltimore
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Zen Community of Baltimore, also known as Clare Sangha, was founded by Janet Jinne Richardson, Roshi, and Rosaline Jishin McQuaide, Sensei. The community's purpose is to cultivate wisdom and compassion through zazen. The Zen Community of Baltimore is affiliated with the White Plum Asanga and is a member of Zen Peacemaker Sangha.

Primary_Label: Zen Life & Meditation Center of Chicago
Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Life & Meditation Center of Chicago
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | Zen Life and Meditation Center of Chicago, originally known as Zen Center of Oak Park, was founded by Sensei Robert Joshin Althouse. The group's practices include zazen, samu work practice, study, koans, classes, workshops, and sesshins (retreats). Generally, the group's focus falls into both Soto and Rinzai practice and traditions.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Mission Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Zen Mission Society was founded by Reverend Jiyu-Kennett, with a commission to train and ordain both men and women for Soto Zen priesthood. The present-day outgrowth of this group is the Order of Buddhist Contemplatives.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Peacemakers
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Buddhism > Zen Studies Society
Description: Buddhism | Buddhism | The Zen Studies Society was founded to help the work of Daisetz Teritaro Suzuki. The group practiced without a Zen master until 1965, when Eido Tai Shimano assumed the leadership of the Society. Eido Tai Shimano then shifted the focus of the society away from its intellectual study to the practice of zazen (Zen meditation).

Hierarchy_Path: US Religious Groups > Methodist > Zion Union Apostolic Church
Description: Unclassified | Methodist | The Zion Union Apostolic Church was founded in 1869 by a group led by Rev. James Howell that left the African Methodist Episcopal Church. Conflict ensued after Howell was elected as bishop for life in 1874, but this was resolved in 1882 with the institution of 4-year presidential terms – a rule that has since been abandoned – and the renaming of the association to the Reformed Zion Union Apostolic Church.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree
World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism
Description: Judaism emerged in Palestine in a time before written records and distinguished itself from its neighbors by the development of a belief in monotheism. Under King David, Jerusalem was established as the capital of Israel, with Judaism being its established religion. Worship became focused in the temple built by David's successor, Solomon. Under Solomon's successor, Rehoboam, the kingdom divided into Israel and Judah. Israel was destroyed and ceased to exist after being overrun by the Assyrians c.722 BCE. Judah continued to exist until it was overrun by the Babylonians c.587 BCE. At that time, the temple in Jerusalem was leveled and with the deportation of the Jewish religious elite, Judaism underwent significant changes. Following its conquest of Babylon in 539 BCE, the Persians allowed the Jews to return to Jerusalem and to rebuild the Temple, completed c. 515 BCE. Judaism again underwent significant changes and would continue to evolve as its land became a prize for competing monarchs century by century. Eventually, the Romans leveled the city and destroyed the temple again in 70 CE. The Roman action destroyed a variety of groups that had emerged in Israel--Sadducees, Zealots, etc. The history of modern Judaism really began after the dispersion of the community and the end of temple-centered worship. After 70 CE, Jews were scattered around the Mediterranean Basin, but two particularly important communities eventually gathered on the Iberian Peninsula and in northeastern Europe from Germany to Poland and Lithuania to Russia. The traditional Jewish community, defined by its allegiance to the Hebrew Bible and its following of the old Oral law as written in the Talmud and Misash, would be challenged on two sides in the nineteenth century by a liberalizing movement influenced by the Enlightenment (Reform) and by the most strict of traditionalists (Hiradi movement). What has emerged is modern Orthodoxy, which attempts to stay true to tradition while also attempting to engage the modern world.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Sepharidic Judaism
Description: Sepharidic Judaism : Sephardim is the name given to the Jewish community that emerged in Spain and Portugal, especially during the years of Muslim rule. It was largely destroyed in 1492-93, when Jews were expelled from the two countries. Sephardic Jews would find their way to the Americas and establish themselves in Brazil and eventually build some of the first centers in North America, but most would migrate into Europe and lose much of their religious (if not ethnic) uniqueness as they identified with Ashkenazi Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Hassidism
Description: Hassidism : Hassidism emerged as a pietistic devotional movement in eastern Europe, built around rabbis that were seen as embodying the teachings they offered and assigned attributes as healers, wonder workers, and bearers of supernatural powers (termed "rebbes" or "tzadiks"). The charismatic Hassidic leaders challenged traditional rabbinic authority but became a major force across eastern Europe-Lithuania, Poland, Ukraine, and Russia. Largely wiped out by the Holocaust, groups moved to Western Europe, the US, and Israel and a few like the Satmar and Lubavicther (currently the largest Hassidic group) have thrived.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Reform Judaism
Description: Beginning at the time of the European Enlightenment in the 18th century, Jewish leaders began to search for a form of Judaism more attuned to modern culture. German Reform leaders began to articulate a way of being fully religious as a Jew that both fit into modern cultural and intellectual expectations and which emphasized the more universal affirmation of Judaism and abandoned what were seen as mere ancient cultural attributes. The reform movement found its organizational unity through Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati, Ohio, the Central Conference of American Rabbis, and the Union of American Hebrew Congregations (1873). Reform Jews practice a Judaism that is open to change and adaptation with the times. They view the Torah as divinely inspired (but a product of human hands), are committed to the equality of men and women, ordain women rabbis, and accept gays and lesbians into all areas of Jewish life. Globally, they are a minority movement, though they maintain a signficant presence in North America.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Orthodox Judaism
Description: What today is known as Orthodox Judaism emerged in steps through the nineteenth century as many reacted unfavorably to the development of Reform Judaism and what was seen as the stripping away of the tradition. Attempting to continue traditional belief and practice, modern Orthodoxy assumes its particular cast in its organization to oppose the challenge presented by Reform and then the presence of Hasidism.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Orthodox Judaism > Haredi Judaism
Description: Haredi Judaism emerged in the early nineteenth century as the most conservative wing of Orthodox Judaism. It opposed reform Judaism and attempted to push the mainstream to ever more conservative adherence to the Jewish Law. It has re-emerged in strength in modern Israel with a goal of re-establishing the boundaries of King David's ancient kingdom. It is often referred to by its critics as the Ultra-Orthodox, and exists through several organizations, among the most famous being the Neturei Karta.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Reform Judaism > Conservative Judaism
Description: Conservative Judaism formed amid a new wave of immigration of Jews from Europe in the last decades of the nineteenth century. More specifically, it was founded as a movement in 1886 with the formation of the Jewish Theological Seminary Association. The intention of the movement was to retain traditional Jewish practices while inhabiting an ideological position between Reform and Orthodox Judaism, honoring the past while adapting to the present. In 1913, the United Synagogue of America, today called the United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism, began connecting Conservative synagogues. The Rabbinical Assembly unites rabbis within Conservative Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Reform Judaism > American Ethical Union
Description: By the end of the twentieth century, the stance taken by Reform Judaism in America led to a variety of even more liberal approaches to the tradition. The American Ethical Union grew from the thought of Felix Adler, a reform Jewish rabbi who emphasized the cultural aspects of Judaism over its theological unity. Adler, raised Jewish and trained as a rabbi, was inspired by neo-Kantian idealism to push religion toward “pure” ethics and morality. While maintaining a liberal Jewish base, the Union has steadily moved toward humanism as its dominant perspective and has come to include a significant non-Jewish membership and leadership.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Orthodox Judaism > Haredi Judaism > World Agudath Israel
Description: World Agudath Israel was established as a political expression of the Haredi Movement as an alternative to the recently formed Zionist movement.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Reform Judaism > Conservative Judaism > Reconstructionist Judaism
Description: Reconstructionist Judaism is a movement fueled by the thought of professor Mordecai M. Kaplan, a conservative rabbi who argued for a whole new approach to Judaism as an evolving civilization; it began with the publication of his book Judaism as a Civilization in 1934. In the 1940s and 1950s, the Jewish Reconstructionist Foundation began to take shape, the rabbinical college was founded in 1954, and the Reconstructionist Rabbinical Association was formed by its graduates. Kaplan was excommunicated by Conservative Jews for his edits to the prayer book in 1945. His followers remain socially progressive and have drawn near to Reform Judaism.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Traditional Rabbinic Judaism > Reform Judaism > Humanistic/Secular Judaism
Description: By the 1960s, the more liberal wing of the reform movement was moving beyond even Reform theological approaches and adopted a non-theistic Humanist stance. The new secular Jewish movement drew upon a tradition of Jewish leadership in non-theistic forms of philosophy since Spinoza and through the Enlightenment. They have divided over whether they should keep the trappings of the religious tradition, even as they agree in dropping mention of God or revelation. Internationally, they have found a home in the Congress of Secular Jewish Organizations.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism
Description: Ancient Judaism : No description available.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Samaritans
Description: Samaritans followed an Abrahamic religion which claims to continue the ancient religion of the Israel prior to the Babylonian Exile. The Samaritans emerge as the local opposition to the building of the second temple in Jerusalem, thrive under various Greek and Roman rulers of the Holy Land, and have survived to the present.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Ancient Judaism > Karaites
Description: Karaite Judaism dates to the Jewish community in Baghdad in the 7th century BCE. It is distinguished by its use of Hebrew Bible alone as the supreme legal authority relative to Jewish law and theology. It rejects the old Oral law, later codified in the Talmud. It is also one of the few ancient variants of Judaism to survive into the present era.

Hierarchy_Path: World Religions Family Tree > Indian Buddhism > Central Asia
Description: Buddhism spread through the kingdoms of Central Asia following the missionary activity of Asoka in the third century BCE and became established in Afghanistan by the first century. The Mahayana Buddhism that emerged gradually became the dominant form of Buddhism. It dies out after the arrival of Islam and its establishment as the state religion.
