The term Asatru, or troth of the Aesir, is used by many groups and organizations. "Odinism", properly speaking, would refer only to worship of Odin ... but in practice is used mostly by groups which define themselves along racial lines; Norse religion"; Heathenism is non-racist) | subtitle (A collection of Heathen carols) | dust jacket (Contemporary Heathenry; Loki's place in modern Heathenry); page 43 (In Heathenry today ...) | about (Heathenry, Asatru and Norse Paganism are names for a religion rooted in history and relevant in the modern age. Revived after a nearly one-thousand year long sleep, our faith represents an outlook and a way of life whose time has come again) | (Heathenry (new religious movement). Heathenry, also termed Heathenism, contemporary Germanic Paganism, or Germanic Neopaganism, is a modern Pagan [or Neopagan] religion. Scholars of religious studies classify it as a new religious movement; typically polytheistic, centering on a pantheon of deities from pre-Christian Germanic Europe; A central division within the Heathen movement emerged surrounding the issue of race. Older groups adopted a racialist attitude - often termed "folkish" within the community - by viewing Heathenry as an ethnic or racial religion with inherent links to a Germanic race ... and often combine the religion with far right-wing and white supremacist perspectives; A larger proportion of Heathens instead adopt a "universalist" perspective, holding that the religion is open to all, irrespective of ethnic or racial background; the term Heathenry is used widely to describe the religion as a whole); Terminology (Academics studying the religion have typically favoured the terms Heathenry and Heathenism to describe it, for the reason that these words are inclusive of all varieties of the movement)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion
Description: A set of beliefs concerning the nature, cause, and purpose of the universe, especially when considered as the creation of a superhuman agency. It usually involves devotional and ritual observances and often a moral code for the conduct of human affairs. (Random House Collegiate Dictionary, rev. ed.)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Beliefs
Humanities > Religion > Religious Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Prayer
Humanities > Religion > Buddhism
Description: The teaching ascribed to Gautama Buddha (ca. 483 B.C.) holding that suffering is inherent in life and that one can escape it into nirvana by mental and moral self-purification. (Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Buddhism > Buddhist Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Anthroposophy
Description: Knowledge of the nature of man. A spiritual and mystical doctrine that grew out of theosophy and derives mainly from the philosophy of Rudolph Steiner, Austrian social philosopher (1861-1925). (Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Islam
Description: A monotheistic religion promulgated by the Prophet Mohammed with Allah as the deity.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Islam > Islamic Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Islam > Muslims
Humanities > Religion > Judaism
Description: The monotheistic religion of the Jews based on the teachings of the Hebrew Scriptures (Torah).

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Judaism > Jewish Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Religious Missions
Description: Programs or projects established by religious groups or institution to support and provide missionary activities, including sharing faith or providing educational, medical, or other assistance.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Theology
Description: The study of religion and religious belief, or a particular system or school of religious beliefs and teachings (from online Cambridge Dictionary of American English, 2000 and WordNet: An Electronic Lexical Database, 1997)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Medicine
Description: The interrelationship of medicine and religion.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Medicine > Religious and Spiritual Coping
Humanities > Religion > Religion and Medicine > Spiritual Coping
Humanities > Religion > Religion and Psychology
Synonyms: Psychology and Religion | Psychology, Religion | Religion, Psychology
Description: The interrelationship of psychology and religion.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Science
Description: The interrelationship of science and religion.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Sex
Description: The interrelationship of religion and sexual practices and beliefs.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies
Description: Sets of beliefs on the nature of the universe or Man.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies > Taoism
Description: A Chinese philosophy and system of religion that is based on the teachings of Lao-tsu in the 6th century B.C. and on subsequent revelations. (from American Heritage College Dictionary, 4th ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies > Intelligent Design
Description: Assertion that the presence of complex life on earth is best explained by the creative actions of an intelligent designer, not the chance consequences of random mutation and differential survival that natural selection proposes.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity
Description: The religion stemming from the life, teachings, and death of Jesus Christ: the religion that believes in God as the Father Almighty who works redemptively through the Holy Spirit for men's salvation and that affirms Jesus Christ as Lord and Savior who proclaimed to man the gospel of salvation. (From Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Christian Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Stigmata
Humanities > Religion > Hinduism
Description: A complex body of social, cultural, and religious beliefs and practices evolved in and largely confined to the Indian subcontinent and marked by a caste system, an outlook tending to view all forms and theories as aspects of one eternal being and truth, and the practice of the way of works, the way of knowledge, or the way of devotion as a means of release from the round of rebirths. (From Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Hinduism > Hindu Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Religion and Medicine > Spirit Possession
Description: The idea that abnormal behavior or a medical condition is evidence that a person is under the control of a supernatural power or spirit. This belief was most commonly held in religion and medicine of the medieval period and is also held in many tribal cultures today.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Psychology > Spirituality
Description: Sensitivity or attachment to religious values, or to things of the spirit as opposed to material or worldly interests. (from Merriam-Webster's Collegiate Dictionary, 10th ed, and Oxford English Dictionary, 2nd ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Psychology > Pastoral Care
Description: Counseling or comfort given by ministers, priests, rabbis, etc., to those in need of help with emotional problems or stressful situations.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religion and Psychology > Pastoral Care > Pastoral Psychology
Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies > Confucianism
Description: A school of thought and set of moral, ethical, and political teachings usually considered to be founded by Confucius in 6th-5th century B.C. China. (from Cambridge Dictionary of Philosophy, 1995)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies > Mysticism
Description: A philosophy based upon spiritual intuition that is believed to transcend ordinary sensory experiences or understanding.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies > Spiritualism
Description: Religious philosophy expressing the fundamental belief that departed spirits may be contacted by the living through a medium.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Religious Philosophies > Yoga
Description: A major orthodox system of Hindu philosophy based on Sankhya (metaphysical dualism) but differing from it in being theistic and characterized by the teaching of raja-yoga as a practical method of liberating the self. It includes a system of exercises for attaining bodily or mental control and well-being with liberation of the self and union with the universal spirit. (From Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Catholicism
Description: The Christian faith, practice, or system of the Catholic Church, specifically the Roman Catholic, the Christian church that is characterized by a hierarchic structure of bishops and priests in which doctrinal and disciplinary authority are dependent upon apostolic succession, with the pope as head of the episcopal college. (From Webster, 3d ed; American Heritage Dictionary, 2d college ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Catholicism > Roman Catholic Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Catholicism > Roman Catholics

Primary_Label: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Description: A group of religious bodies tracing their origin to Joseph Smith in 1830 and accepting the Book of Mormon as divine revelation. (from Merriam-Webster's Collegiate Dictionary, 10th ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints > Mormons
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy
Description: The name given to the religion of the body of modern churches, including among others the Greek and Russian Orthodox, that is derived from the church of the Byzantine Empire, adheres to the Byzantine rite, and acknowledges the honorary primacy of the patriarch of Constantinople. (from American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language, 4th ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy > Eastern Orthodox Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy > Armenian Orthodoxy
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy > Greek Orthodoxy
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy > Russian Orthodoxy
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy > Serbian Orthodoxy
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Eastern Orthodoxy > Syrian Orthodoxy
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Jehovah's Witnesses
Description: Members of a religious denomination founded in the United States during the late 19th century in which active evangelism is practiced, the imminent approach of the millennium is preached, and war and organized government authority in matters of conscience are strongly opposed (from American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language, 4th ed). Jehovah's Witnesses generally refuse blood transfusions and other blood-based treatments based on religious belief.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism
Description: The name given to all Christian denominations, sects, or groups rising out of the Reformation. Protestant churches generally agree that the principle of authority should be the Scriptures rather than the institutional church or the pope. (from W.L. Reese, Dictionary of Philosophy and Religion, 1999)

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Protestant Ethics
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Seventh-Day Adventists
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Protestants
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Anglican Church
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Baptist Church
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Episcopal Church
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Lutheran Church
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Methodist Church
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Presbyterian Church
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Protestantism > Society of Friends
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Christian Science
Description: A religion founded by Mary Baker Eddy in 1866 that was organized under the official name of the Church of Christ, Scientist. It includes the practice of spiritual healing.

Hierarchy_Path: Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Christian Science > Christian Scientists
Humanities > Religion > Christianity > Saints
Description: Persons officially recognized or acknowledged as pre-eminent for consecration, holiness, and piety, especially through canonization by a branch of the Christian church. (From Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies
Description: Mystical, religious, or spiritual practices performed for health benefit.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Exorcism
Description: Practice of evicting demons or other spiritual entities from a person or area believed to be possessed.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Spiritual Healing
Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Meditation
Description: A state of consciousness in which the individual eliminates environmental stimuli from awareness so that the mind can focus on a single thing, producing a state of relaxation and relief from stress. A wide variety of techniques are used to clear the mind of stressful outside interferences. It includes meditation therapy. (Mosby's Medical, Nursing, and Allied Health Dictionary, 4th ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Meditation > Transcendental Meditation
Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Shamanism
Description: An intermediate stage between polytheism and monotheism, which assumes a Great Spirit, with lesser deities subordinated. With the beginnings of shamanism there was the advent of the medicine man or witch doctor, who assumed a supervisory relation to disease and its cure. Formally, shamanism is a religion of Ural-Altaic peoples of Northern Asia and Europe, characterized by the belief that the unseen world of gods, demons, ancestral spirits is responsive only to shamans. The Indians of North and South America entertain religious practices similar to the Ural-Altaic shamanism. The word shaman comes from the Tungusic (Manchuria and Siberia) saman, meaning Buddhist monk. The SHAMAN handles disease almost entirely by psychotherapeutic means; he frightens away the demons of disease by assuming a terrifying mien. (From Garrison, An Introduction to the History of Medicine, 4th ed, p22; from Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Therapeutic Touch
Description: Placing of the hands of the healer upon the person to be cured with the intent of spiritual energetic healing.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Therapeutic Touch > Laying-on-of-Hands
Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Magic
Description: Beliefs and practices concerned with producing desired results through supernatural forces or agents as with the manipulation of fetishes or rituals.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Mental Healing
Description: The use of mind to cure disease, particularly physical illness.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Faith Healing
Description: The use of faith and spirit to cure disease.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Faith Healing > Prayer Healing
Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Radiesthesia
Description: Therapeutic cult concerned with intangible energies surrounding the living body and based on the detection of these intrinsic radiations by dowsing, or divining, or the use of more elaborate instruments (radionics).

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Medicine, African Traditional
Synonyms: African Medicine, Traditional | African Traditional Medicine | Medicine, Traditional African | Traditional African Medicine | Traditional Medicine, African
Description: A system of traditional medicine which is based on the beliefs and practices of the African peoples. It includes treatment by MEDICINAL PLANTS and other MATERIA MEDICA as well as by the ministrations of diviners, MEDICINE MEN, witch doctors, and sorcerers.

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Medicine, African Traditional > Medicine, African
Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Witchcraft
Description: An act of employing sorcery (the use of power gained from the assistance or control of spirits), especially with malevolent intent, and the exercise of supernatural powers and alleged intercourse with the devil or a familiar. (From Webster, 3d ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Witchcraft > Sorcery
Therapeutics > Complementary Therapies > Spiritual Therapies > Reiki
Description: A technique involving a trained practitioner who places their hands on, or above a patient with the intent to guide a flow of energy into the patient. Depending upon beliefs, the goal of the procedure can include energetic healing, SPIRITUAL HEALING, or both forms of healing. While Reiki sessions may be offered on a massage table, it is distinct from MASSAGE THERAPY, which involves the physical manipulation of muscles and tissues.

Hierarchy_Path: Social Sciences > Sociology > Sociological Factors > Secularism
Description: Indifference to, or rejection of, RELIGION or religious considerations. (From Merriam-Webster's Collegiate Dictionary, 10th ed)

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel
Description: Persons that live and work according to a religious vocation, or calling, to a consecrated or ordained life.

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel > Monks
Description: Members of a religious community of men typically living under vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience.

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel > Nuns
Description: Members of a religious community of women typically living under vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience.

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel > Nuns > Abbess
Description: A woman who is head of an abbey or convent of nuns.

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel > Nuns > Prioress
Description: A woman who is head of a house of certain orders of nuns, and who is next in rank below an abbess.

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel > Clergy
Description: Persons ordained for religious duties, who serve as leaders and perform religious services.

Persons > Occupational Groups > Religious Personnel > Clergy > Ministers

Description: A nursing specialty involving programs designed to bring wholeness and healing to a particular faith community through addressing the health needs of body, mind, and spirit. They are coordinated by registered NURSES and may involve HEALTH EDUCATION and counseling, facilitation, referral, PATIENT ADVOCACY, and health care plan interpretation, as influenced and defined by the unique needs of the congregation.

Hierarchy_Path: Persons > Missionaries
Description: Persons sent into an area to evangelize or to perform acts of service, such as medical care, EDUCATION, or SOCIAL JUSTICE.

Description: Health personnel using systems of medicine based on cultural beliefs and practices handed down from generation to generation, including mystical and magical rituals (SPIRITUAL THERAPIES); TRADITIONAL MEDICINE; PHYTOTHERAPY; HERBAL MEDICINE; and other treatments which may not be explained by modern medicine.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Intercessory Prayer
Description: A petition made to a Transcendent/Higher Power that is said with benevolent intention on behalf of someone else. Examples: Praying for improvements in health condition

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Intercessory Prayer > Distant Intercessory Prayer
Description: A petition made with benevolent intention for others to a Transcendent/Higher Power that is said on behalf of someone else, but the recipient is not present. Examples: Praying for improvements in health condition

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Intercessory Prayer > Proximal Intercessory Prayer
Description: A petition made with benevolent intention to a Transcendent/Higher Power where the recipient is present and aware of receiving prayer. Examples: Prayer in small groups (Christianity) One-to-one prayer support

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Intercessory Prayer using religious items and rituals.
Hierarchy_Path: Intercessory Prayer > Intercessory Prayer using religious items and rituals.
Description: A petition made with benevolent intention to a Transcendent/Higher Power where the recipient is present and aware of receiving prayer that is conducted as part of a religious ritual or used as a religious item. Examples: Laying of hands Using items that are perceived as ‘blessed’ to pray: Chaplain to perform healing service

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Intercessory Prayer > Requesting Intercessory Prayer
Description: An individual requesting a petition be made with benevolent intention for themselves to a Transcendent/Higher Power by an intermediator. (Code also religious social support/congregational support/ religious leader support if it’s from there). Examples: Requesting intercession for healing from patron saints that are affiliated with certain illnesses Requesting religious community to pray for you

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Personal Prayer
Description: A personal communication with a Transcendent/Higher Power Examples: Inward prayer (Prayers concerned with self-understanding and self-change) Outward prayer (Prayers concerned with how to enhance our relationships with others and external realities (e.g., seeking direction and guidance) Upward prayer (Seeking identification with a Transcendent/Higher Power)

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Personal Prayer > Private Prayer
Description: A personal communication with a Transcendent/Higher Power conducted in a private setting. Examples: Inward prayer (Prayers concerned with self-understanding and self-change) Outward prayer (Prayers concerned with how to enhance our relationships with others and external realities (e.g., seeking direction and guidance) Upward prayer (Seeking identification with a Transcendent/Higher Power)

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Personal Prayer > Contemplative Prayer
Description: A practice where the individual perceives they are in the presence of a Transcendent/Higher Power and reflect on their relationship with a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Encourage an individual to create a specific time where they engage in private prayer to reflect on their relationship with a Transcendent or Higher Power.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Engaging with items perceived/representative of religion during private prayer
Hierarchy_Path: Personal Prayer > Engaging with items perceived/representative of religion during private prayer
Description: Using and or praying with physical items of religious meaning or connotation to help interact and bring around closeness to a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Prayer beads Rosary prayer beads Wooden cross

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Personal Prayer > Reading pre-written prayers
Description: Using a template that has been created by an religious leader with the intention to facilitate private prayer, such as reciting well-known prayers Examples: Template prayers written as part of a religiously integrated intervention

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Arts and Literature
Description: Creative use of arts and literature to interact, get closer to, or focus on a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Religious painting, colouring, drawing, creating prayer cards, poetry, photographs. Creating a religious journey blueprint that can document how far they have come and how they wish to progress. The blueprint can incorporate all of the individual’s senses almost like a mood board.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Arts and Literature > Creative Religiosity
Description: Expressing perceptions of religious themes or worship through arts, writing, and photography. Examples: Religious painting, colouring, drawing, creating prayer cards, poetry, photographs. Creating a religious journey blueprint that can document how far they have come and how they wish to progress. The blueprint can incorporate all of the individual’s senses almost like a mood board.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Arts and Literature > Reading religiously inspired text
Description: Reading religiously creative writings/biographies. Examples: Provide a religiously creative writing or biography that is relevant to the health context of the individual.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Arts and Literature > Discussing religiously inspired text
Description: Informal discussion about religiously creative writings/biographies. (Code also reading religiously inspired texts). Examples: Prompt individual to think about how the religiously inspired texts relates to them, and how they can apply it to their situation

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Media
Description: Watching or listening to the streaming of religious material via television, radio, or internet. Examples: Watching [or participating in] an online service Watching religious TV channel Listening to a religious radio channel. Listening to spiritual/religious podcast.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Media > Religious Media
Description: Watching or listening to religious material via television, radio, internet, or mobile phone app Examples: Watching [or participating in] an online service Watching religious TV channel Listening to a religious radio channel. Listening to spiritual/religious podcast.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Meditation
Description: A mind-body exercise using a variety of techniques that require emphasis and focus on breathing. Examples: Vipassana meditation

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Meditation > Mindfulness meditation
Description: Cultivating moment-to-moment, non-judgmental awareness of one’s present experience. Examples: Vipassana meditation

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Meditation > Movement Meditation
Description: Cultivating a meditative state of mind by focusing awareness on the body, whilst engaging in a prescribed or spontaneous set of movements with focused breathing. Examples: Standing dikhr Labyrinth walking Yoga

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Meditation > Concentrated Meditation
Description: Using a stimulus to direct concentration towards during meditation; such stimulus can be a physical item or unchanging repetitive word. Examples: Mantram repetition Meditating on religious text

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Meditation > Religious/spiritual meditation
Description: A silent meditation during which the individual reflects on the silence and seeks to connect to a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Focusing on images, representations of religion/spirituality whilst individual uses all of their senses to guide their meditation.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Involving the religious leader
Description: Religious leader being actively involved in the delivering, participation, and/or supporting of a healthcare intervention (Items in this category should only be enacted by ordained religious leaders). Examples: Discussing the importance of self-examination (in cancer) and applying religious text as a way to validate why self-examination is important health behaviour to engage with.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Delivering a health focused religious service
Hierarchy_Path: Involving the religious leader > Delivering a health focused religious service
Description: Religious leader will deliver a religious service that applies their religious knowledge to a specific health matter. (Consider coding use of religious text in a healthcare intervention). Examples: Discussing the importance of self-examination (in cancer) and applying religious text as a way to validate why self-examination is important health behaviour to engage with.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Involvement of the religious leader within a healthcare intervention
Hierarchy_Path: Involving the religious leader > Involvement of the religious leader within a healthcare intervention
Description: Utilising the highly respected/ authoritative positionality of the religious leader to deliver a healthcare intervention within a place of worship. Examples: Developing a healthcare intervention that has no religious content but is delivered by the religious leader as a way to reach the congregants

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Establish a new religious ruling to change health behaviour
Hierarchy_Path: Involving the religious leader > Establish a new religious ruling to change health behaviour
Description: Religious leader enforces a religious practice that must be followed by congregation in relation to health behaviour. Examples: ‘A new religious legal ruling aimed at teenagers that prohibits the initiation of smoking. The ruling was written by a panel of leading rabbis, representing several streams of the ultra-orthodox religious community.’ (Knishkowy et al., 2012 [Judaism])

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling
Description: Discussing religious/spiritual issues with the patient that are a cause of solace, distress or well-being. NB. Resolution may not be feasible, and so this is not the outcome. Examples: Discussing how the patient views The Transcendent/Higher Power and discuss the potential implications of this view point for health and well-being.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Discussing patient’s perception of a Transcendent/Higher Power
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Discussing patient’s perception of a Transcendent/Higher Power
Description: Discussing with the patient how they perceive a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Discussing how the patient views The Transcendent/Higher Power and discuss the potential implications of this view point for health and well-being.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Discussing the health benefits of Religion/spirituality.
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Discussing the health benefits of Religion/spirituality.
Description: Discussing evidence-base ways in which health and well-being can be facilitated through engagement with religion/spirituality. (NB code also the techniques that you signpost to, or encourage the individual to uptake). Examples: Discussing evidence-based health benefits of R/S

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing theological misinterpretations
Description: Identifying and discussing religious resources and struggles in order to fortify the former, and challenge the latter. Examples: Exploring an individual interpretation of a religious worldview and discussing an alternative interpretation to identify and challenge the discrepancy.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Addressing religious/spiritual struggles related to a Transcendent/Higher Power
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing religious/spiritual struggles related to a Transcendent/Higher Power
Description: Discussing a specific conflict an individual has directly with a Transcendent/Higher Power (Code also Discussing patients perception of a Transcendent/Higher Power). Examples: Discussing abandonment, anger towards The Transcendent/Higher Power.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing internal/intrapsychic religious/spiritual struggle
Description: Discussing an inner conflict that a patient has about their own religion/spirituality. (Consider also coding Addressing religious/spiritual struggles related to a Transcendent/Higher Power). Examples: Discussing unsettling feelings about individuals self that may be related to religious/spiritual constructs (i.e., being self-critical and describing self as a sinner and deserving of a negative outcome).

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing interpersonal/communal religious/spiritual struggles
Description: Discussing an external conflict that a patient has with family member, clergy, friends, place of worship community about religion. Examples: Discussing feelings of abandonment by a religious community.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing demonic oppression
Description: Discussing perceptions and beliefs around the influence of evil and demons. Examples: Discuss demonic oppression and how this relates to an individual’s health condition or outcome.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing guilt and shame
Description: Discussing feelings of regret, shame and responsibility that impacts engagement with religiosity/spirituality. Examples: Discussing guilt and shame and how this relates to a health condition or outcome and explore how to resolve these feelings.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing religious fatalism
Description: Discussing religious fatalistic beliefs that may limit health seeking behaviours Examples: Discussing health and well-being as predetermined or purposed by a Transcendent/ Higher Power and not within the individual's control.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Addressing religious meanings of mortality
Description: Discussing religious/spiritual concerns associated with death Examples: Discussing what the meaning of death is. Discussing the role of a Transcendent/Higher Power in death.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Receiving spiritual guidance
Description: The process of receiving wisdom that is believed to be from a Transcendent/Higher Power, which directs the individual’s life. Examples: Mentorship Facilitating a discussion around purpose/calling over one’s life.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious counselling > Mapping religious development
Description: Charting a map of habits, thoughts and actions that will be practiced and engaged with following the end of a healthcare intervention. Examples: Religious goal-setting Developing a storyboard of how an individual will continue to apply religious themes in a healthcare intervention.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Imaginative activities
Description: To facilitate expression of religious beliefs, perceptions and teachings through imagination/simulation based activities. Examples: Learning the virtue of forgiveness, practice a situation where you need to forgive and role play how the situation should play out if forgiveness has happened.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Role play of religiously motivated virtues
Hierarchy_Path: Imaginative activities > Role play of religiously motivated virtues
Description: An opportunity to perform/practice a newly learned religious virtue in a simulated setting. Examples: Learning the virtue of forgiveness, practice a situation where you need to forgive and role play how the situation should play out if forgiveness has happened.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Revisioning/reconstructing personal constructions of a Transcendent/Higher Power
Hierarchy_Path: Imaginative activities > Revisioning/reconstructing personal constructions of a Transcendent/Higher Power
Description: An imagery exercise which begins with exploring how the patient perceives a Transcendent/Higher Power, with the aim of facilitating the development of a more positive construction of a Transcendent/Higher Power. (Code also Discussing patient’s perception of a Transcendent/Higher Power). Examples: Explore how an individual currently views a Transcendent or Higher Power and how they would like to view them. Prompt exploration of how the ideal view could replace current view from the individual’s perspective.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious music
Description: Music that is composed, and or performed for religious purposes or religious use. Examples: Listening to Kirtans/Bhajans; Gospel Music; Hymns

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Listening to religious music with lyrics
Hierarchy_Path: Religious music > Listening to religious music with lyrics
Description: Listening to music where the lyrics are an expression of religious/spiritual teachings. This can be done privately or as part of religious ritual. (Consider also coding attending a religious service if this is part of a religious service). Examples: Listening to Kirtans/Bhajans; Gospel Music; Hymns

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Listening to instrumental religious music (without lyrics)
Hierarchy_Path: Religious music > Listening to instrumental religious music (without lyrics)
Description: Listening to instrumental religious music that evokes emotions, reflection of The Transcendent/Higher Power and their own religiosity. Examples: Listening to Sitar music

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious music > Singing/chanting religious songs
Description: Singing/chanting music that has been composed for religious/spiritual purposes and with religious/spiritual influence. This category can also include singing chanting verses from sacred writings as songs. Examples: Sufi Music Gospel music

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Engaging in physical activity to religious music
Hierarchy_Path: Religious music > Engaging in physical activity to religious music
Description: Conducting physical activity to music that has been composed for religious purposes or with religious influence. Examples: Chair aerobics to gospel music

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Using religious texts within interventions
Description: This category discusses how religious texts (sacred writings that are regarded as central to a religion comprising the key teachings and beliefs) are used within healthcare interventions. Examples: Reading a religious text as part of an intervention session or homework associated with a session.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Using religious texts within interventions > Reading religious text
Description: To read religious text(s) that are regarded as core to the religious belief and typically comprise the practices and teachings central to the religion. (Code also Using religious themes within religious texts in intervention sessions). Examples: Reading a religious text as part of an intervention session or homework associated with a session.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Using religious texts within interventions > Listening to religious text
Description: Listening to audio recordings of passages from a religious text or listening to someone read passages from a religious text out loud. Examples: Listening to recitals of religious texts.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Using religious themes within religious texts in intervention sessions
Hierarchy_Path: Using religious texts within interventions > Using religious themes within religious texts in intervention sessions
Description: The application of religious themes from religious texts to intervention content. This can be added for relatability purposes, and/or to prompt consideration and discussion of how religious themes can also be applied to health. (If religious text is read individually then code also Reading religious text). Examples: Using a parable/story/fable from within a religious text and applying it to a session within a healthcare intervention

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Using religious texts within interventions > Reciting passages from religious texts
Description: Verbally speaking out a passage/section from a religious text aloud. Examples: Reciting a verse to commit it to memory.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development
Description: An attempt to develop and apply characteristics which are highly valued in religious/spiritual teachings to daily life. NB: the development or enactment of these may be an outcome of engagement with other practices listed in the taxonomy. Examples: Explore how an individual perceives themselves in comparison to how their religious/spiritual worldview perceives them. Consider the differences and how self-esteem can be perceived in light of their religious/spiritual worldview. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Self-Esteem
Description: Using one’s religious/spiritual values to inform how the one evaluates their self-worth. Examples: Explore how an individual perceives themselves in comparison to how their religious/spiritual worldview perceives them. Consider the differences and how self-esteem can be perceived in light of their religious/spiritual worldview. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Religious Humility
Description: An appropriate awareness of one’s identity relative to a Transcendent/Higher Power, knowledge, and goodness of a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Create a programme where participants can have an opportunity to develop religious humility. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Love
Description: Being motivated to love others and oneself, by considering religious/spiritual teachings. Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Compassion/Empathy
Description: Motivated to be [empathic] for others by considering religious/spiritual teachings. Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Honesty
Description: Motivated to be honest by considering religious/spiritual themes. Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Equanimity
Description: Cultivating ‘an even-minded mental state or dispositional tendency toward all experiences or objects, regardless of their affective valence (pleasant, unpleasant or neutral) or source (Desbordes et al. 2015).’ Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Morality
Description: Cultivating a perception of right and wrong through considering religious/spiritual teachings. Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Patience
Description: Cultivating the ability to accept or tolerate a delay in problems because of belief in religious/spiritual teachings. Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Kindness
Description: In response to religious/spiritual teachings, cultivating an attitude which can be defined by acts of generosity, consideration and concern for others without expecting praise or reward. Examples: Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue. An altruistic act as a form of religious worship

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Wisdom
Description: Cultivating the development of wisdom from religious/spiritual teachings through applying religious spiritual principles to guide daily life. Consider coding also reading religious text. Examples: Using religious text to understand a daily life experience.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Faith
Description: Cultivating a centring process that derives from trust in a Transcendent/Higher Power to create a quiet assurance of a desired outcome that protects against challenges and facilitates well-being. Examples: Trusting a Transcendent or Higher Power for an expected outcome in relation to health and well-being. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Religious Hope
Description: Cultivating optimism that is based on religious/spiritual teachings that can facilitate mean-making. (See also religious meaning-making and faith). Examples: Prompting the individual to hold a positive view of the future due to their belief in a Transcendent/Higher Power. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Religious gratitude
Description: A recognition of fortitude in one’s life or positive happenstances that are attributed to be from a Transcendent/Higher power. Differs from secular gratitude that depends on physical agents carrying out tangible acts to prompt feelings of gratefulness which can only occur in an interpersonal context (Rosmarin et al., 2011). Examples: Being thankful to a Transcendent/ Higher Power for the day ahead. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated character development > Worship
Description: An attitude of positive reflection on the characteristics, attributes and actions of a Transcendent or Higher Power. Examples: Encourage an individual to say out loud the positive characteristics of a Transcendent or Higher Power when they are experiencing negative emotions. Consider keeping a journal that maps how an individual has cultivated this character virtue.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated forgiveness
Description: Attitudes that facilitate engagement with forgiveness (a conscious or deliberate decision to release feelings of resentment injustice). Examples: Explore why an individual views themselves as unforgivable, and consider discussing a religious worldview of forgiveness to prompt decisional forgiveness of self. This could be through a reflective exercise.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated forgiveness > Decisional forgiveness of self
Description: A decision based on religious/spiritual teachings wherein you forgive yourself for betrayal of one’s values or participating in behaviours contrary to one’s moral code (perpetration). Examples: Explore why an individual views themselves as unforgivable, and consider discussing a religious worldview of forgiveness to prompt decisional forgiveness of self. This could be through a reflective exercise.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated forgiveness > Decisional forgiveness of others
Description: A decision based on religious/spiritual teachings wherein you forgive others for misdeeds that have caused you to perceive hurt or offence. Examples: Formulate a decisional forgiveness contract where it outlines that an individual has contractually agreed to make a decision to forgive a person who has cause hurt or offence.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated forgiveness > Confession
Description: An admittance of wrong-doings with the intention to seek forgiveness in accordance to religious/spiritual values. (See also Repentance). Examples: Sacrament of reconciliation]

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religiously motivated forgiveness > Repentance
Description: A commitment to change wrong-doings and misdeeds that have been previously committed. (Code also Confession) Examples: Sacrament of reconciliation

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Pilgrimage
Description: A journey undertaken for religious/spiritual purposes Examples: Pilgrimages for religious transformation Pilgrimages for healing

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Pilgrimage > Pilgrimage
Description: A journey undertaken for religious/spiritual purposes (Code also Retreats if applicable) Examples: Pilgrimages for religious transformation Pilgrimages for healing

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Retreats
Description: A definite time spent away from ones normal life for the purpose of connecting with a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Organised retreats (e.g., a church organises a retreat for members or informal members organise retreats for religious community). Sweat lodges

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Retreats > Retreats
Description: A definite time spent away from ones normal life for the purpose of connecting with The Transcendent/Higher Power. (Code also pilgrimage if applicable) Examples: Organised retreats (e.g., a church organises a retreat for members or informal members organise retreats for religious community). Sweat lodges

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious social support
Description: The receipt and/or provision of social support to/from a religious/spirituality community. Examples: Religious communities providing resources and support to help deal with life events.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious social support > Congregational support
Description: The receipt and/or provision of informational, emotional, esteem related, social network support and tangible support from a religious/spirituality community. Examples: Religious communities providing resources and support to help deal with life events.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious social support > Religious Leader Support
Description: The receipt of informational, emotional, tangible, financial support from the religious leader. (Consider coding other techniques that may be an outcome of receiving religious leader support) Examples: Providing spiritual guidance Pastoral visiting Religious leader may decide to role model a health behaviour that is being addressed in a healthcare intervention.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious social support > Testimonials
Description: Either giving or receiving a public recounting of a religious experience to encourage and support others Examples: Sharing your own experience with someone as a source of encouragement.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious coping
Description: Drawing upon religious resources to cope with life events. Examples: Encourage the individual to think about their current situation and make two lists the first a list of things they can control, and the second a list of what they cannot control. Then have a discussion around the second list to explore barriers preventing the individual from letting go of what they cannot control.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious coping > Surrendering to the Transcendent/Higher power
Description: Releasing the desire to control and practicing acceptance within a religious/spiritual framework when coping with challenging life situations. NB. This may be viewed negatively where passive religious fatalism limits uptake (or use) of timely healthcare. Examples: Encourage the individual to think about their current situation and make two lists the first a list of things they can control, and the second a list of what they cannot control. Then have a discussion around the second list to explore barriers preventing the individual from letting go of what they cannot control.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious coping > Journaling
Description: Writing thoughts and feelings about life, work, relationships, health and well-being and how these may fit within a religious/spiritual framework. Such can also be a personal written testament, request stories or notes written in private with the view to inspire support from The Transcendent/Higher Power. (For gratitude journals code both journaling and gratitude). Examples: Writing in a personal diary Writing prayers Two-way journaling

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious coping > Cognitive reframing using religious/spiritual viewpoint
Description: Reappraisal of negative, unhelpful thoughts and beliefs using religious/spiritual texts. (Consider also coding reading religious text). Examples: Using a religious text from the patient’s religious/spiritual tradition to reframe an unhelpful thinking style

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious coping > Religious meaning-making
Description: Applying religious/spiritual beliefs to interpret or accept life events. (Consider also coding cognitive reframing using a religious/spiritual viewpoint). Examples: Prompt an individual to apply their religious/spiritual worldview to change/amend their global meaning of their life.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Reflecting on characteristics of a Transcendent/Higher Power.
Hierarchy_Path: Religious coping > Reflecting on characteristics of a Transcendent/Higher Power.
Description: Consciously thinking about the positive characteristics affiliated with a Transcendent/Higher Power to cope with a life event. (Consider coding also surrender) Examples: Reflecting on the goodness of God’s Character (e.g., His love, hope, freedom (Christianity [Lycett et al., 2016]).

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Abstinence/prohibition
Description: To willingly give up something in alignment with religious/ spiritual beliefs or in honour of a Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Vegan [Seven Day Adventists, Jainism]

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Abstinence/prohibition > Dietary restrictions
Description: The practice of restraining from eating certain foods and drinks based on religious/spiritual teachings and personal expression of religious/spiritual faith. Examples: Vegan [Seven Day Adventists, Jainism]

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Abstinence/prohibition > Abstinence from stimulants
Description: A complete avoidance of using drugs or naturally occurring stimulants in accordance with religious/spiritual teachings and personal expression of religious/spiritual faith. Examples: Discuss the benefits of sobriety from stimulants, and then develop a behavioural contract where individual specifies they will not consume stimulants for a specific period that can be reviewed periodically.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Abstinence/prohibition > Abstinence from alcohol
Description: A complete avoidance of alcohol and related products in accordance with religious/spiritual teachings and personal expression of religious/spiritual faith. Examples: Discuss the benefits of sobriety from alcohol, and then develop a behavioural contract where individual specifies they will not drink alcohol for a specific period that can be reviewed periodically.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Abstinence/prohibition > Abstinence from narcotics
Description: A complete avoidance of narcotics and related products in accordance with religious/spiritual teachings and personal expression of religious/spiritual faith. Examples: Discuss the benefits of sobriety from narcotics and then develop a behavioural contract where individual specifies they will not consume narcotics for a specific period that can be reviewed periodically.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Abstinence/prohibition > Fasting
Description: The willing abstinence or reduction from pleasured activities. (This definition is not limited to food and drink but can include prolonged abstinence from physical things beyond food and drink e.g., social media). (Consider also coding sensing the Transcendent and/or a Higher Power, and Contemplative Prayer). Examples: Ramadan Social media detox

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Communal gatherings
Description: An assembly of individuals that are meeting for religious/spiritual purposes. Examples: Consider how frequently services are attended to explore health benefits of the component.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Communal gatherings > Attending religious services
Description: To attend a religious service that is conducted at a place of worship or temporary setting (Zoom, outdoors). Examples: Consider how frequently services are attended to explore health benefits of the component.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Communal gatherings > Religious Dancing
Description: An expression of religiosity through the form of dancing with the intention to worship and/or connect to The Transcendent/Higher Power Examples: Whirling Dervish Navaratri Garba Praise Dance

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Expression of Commitment
Description: Asking the individual to affirm or reaffirm their intention to change and/or maintain religious beliefs and attitudes or actions with the intention to bring the closer to a Transcendent/higher power. Examples: A verbal or written commitment where individuals agree to adapt programme ethos into their daily life. Religious conversion Religious deconversion

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Expression of Commitment > Expression of Commitment
Description: Asking the individual to affirm or reaffirm their intention to change and/or maintain religious beliefs and attitudes or actions with the intention to bring them closer to the Transcendent/higher power. Examples: A verbal or written commitment where individuals agree to adapt programme ethos into their daily life. Religious conversion Religious deconversion

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Conducting a religious/spiritual assessment.
Description: Using an established clinical framework to identify how religious/spiritual dimensions relate to the patients’ health and well-being needs. Examples: See ‘Spirituality integrated psychotherapy’ by Ken Pargament for examples of validated frameworks.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Conducting a religious/spiritual assessment. > Conducting a religious/spiritual case history/assessment
Description: Using a validated clinical framework to identify how religious/spiritual dimensions relate to the patients’ health and well-being needs. Examples: See ‘Spirituality integrated psychotherapy’ by Ken Pargament for examples of validated frameworks.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious rituals
Description: Activities that are performed as prescribed by a religious/spiritual faith tradition. Examples: Ask participants whether they have any rituals they may perform as part of their religious worldview.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Primary_Label: Rituals around the time of death
Hierarchy_Path: Religious rituals > Rituals around the time of death
Description: Activities that are performed by the patient, family members or ordained religious leader around the time of death as prescribed by their religious/spiritual faith tradition. (Consider also intercessory prayer as a part of a religious ritual, and intercessory prayer with religious item). Examples: Ask participants whether they have any rituals they may perform as part of their religious worldview.

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious rituals > Religious rituals to address ‘evil’
Description: Activities that are performed by an ordained religious leader for the purposes of removing ‘evil’ supernatural influence over the patient. Examples: Exorcism Deliverance Smudging

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Experience
Description: An experience where the individual reports a beneficial religious/spiritual encounter with a Transcendent/Higher Power. This can be described as an ethereal feeling that conveys a sense of unity and completeness, along with new knowledge and positive emotions such as joy and bliss (Greeley, 1974). (NB this may be facilitated by the techniques identified above, but it can also naturally occur). Examples: Feeling in awe of nature as an expression of the Transcendent/Higher Power’s creative ability Attributing unlikely coincidences or specific events as acts of a Transcendent/Higher power Religious visions

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Experience > Sensing The Transcendent/Higher Power
Description: The reported experience of sensing a Transcendent/Higher Power. NB. These would be reflective of normative experience as perceived by a particular religious/spiritual community rather than pathological hallucinations and delusions. Examples: Feeling in awe of nature as an expression of the Transcendent/Higher Power’s creative ability Attributing unlikely coincidences or specific events as acts of a Transcendent/Higher power Religious visions

Source_System: Religious Health Interventions in Behavioral Sciences Taxonomy
Hierarchy_Path: Religious Experience > Religious harmony
Description: The reported experience of being at peace with The Transcendent/Higher Power. Examples: Encourage the individual to identify where they feel most at peace with a Transcendent or a Higher Power. Consider engaging with religious activities where participants can experience this reported feeling more.
